four men and a woman — well, three men, a woman and a boy — sat around the green felt table and stared hard at their cards. A few feet
‘away, the bartender studiously ignored the players' conversation while polishing an already gleaming countertop. A row of bottles and
Glasses was arrayed like stalagmites in front of the bar mirror, which, incidentally, held in its reflection both the swinging doors at the
Aront of the saloon and the set of large windows located on cither side. There were other tables, also set up for cards, but empty now in
the heat of the afternoon. Out front, the players' horses nudged each other in futile efforts to dominate the water trough; upstairs, a

) patron snored away a hangover.

ae

chairs were wooden and mismatched; most looked like they'd been liberated from some train's dining car, with faded red cushions
nd scratched wooden backs. A chandelier dangling from the ceiling was dark now, waiting patiently for night.

The Beer players noticed none of this. The first, a muscular man in a faded U.S. Cavalry uniform, scowled intently at his dog-eared

} Bright-faced from the sun, he sported a drooping red mustache and a face craggy enough to serve as a bas-relief map of the

+ wildlands. He'd been the last to arrive, and the others had noticed he'd walked with a distinct limp. That in and of itself was enough to
furrow brows and excite speculation; it took some kind of beating to permanently maim one of their kind. However, none of the others
were so rude as to ask him about the matter; they were sure Jonas Moon, ISth New York Cavalry (ret.) — as he'd introduced himself —
would tell the tale in his own time.

On Moon' right sat the lone woman of the group, fanning herself with her cards to break the midday heat. She had the build and grace
of a dancer, but her palms were calloused from hard work. Black-haired and black-cyed but with fair skin, she'd been the first of the five
to show up; the rest had sauntered in to find her already seated and sipping a glass of beer Learns-the-Olld-Ways (she had told them that
her human friends call her Janice) wore man' clothing, buckskins and leathers that looked like they'd been through more than one
owner. Some sort of Indian charm hung around her neck, but none of the others recognized it.

The kid had been the dealer the ast round, and he'd dealt out the cards like he'd worked in a saloon for years. That was the end of his
professionalism, though. He sat, eyes shaded by a hat several sizes too larrge, and stared a little too hard at his hand. All of his clothes
said one thing: Easterner. They were too new, too clean, too fresh to have been worn for long on the frontier, and they just didn't hang
right. Every so often he fidgeted with a button or missed a pocket in a way that said he wasnt used to these clothes.

The kid — that's how he'd introduced himself, actually, though the others were sure he'd wanted "kid" capitalized — had guns, of course.
Shiny, spanking-new guns in leather holsters so new they still squeaked like old furniture when the kid sat down. When the kid had
walked in, Learns-the-Old-Ways had quietly bet her left-side neighbor the guns had never been used.

The other players had already pegged the kid as a rotten bluffer, and at seeing his attempts at nonchalance, Moon had already folded. So
did the man Janice made her bet with, a slick-looking fellow whose vest, pants, ote and gloves were all suspiciously free of trail

dust. An unkind man might have called the fellow a dandy. A sharp-eyed man might have noticed the notches on the fellows gun barrels,
and refrained from the insult. Drooping blond mustachios fell well past his lip, and his eyes were a piercing, dead blue.

During the course of the game's pleasantries, he'd let slip (in a gentleman's Georgia drawl) that he went by Aaron Walks-

With-Steel. Moon had muttered later that he didn't care what a fellow walked with, no man or wolf on iss green Earth

could possibly be that good a card player. At that, Aaron had merely smiled a wolfish grin and raked in the pot from

hand. Magnanimously, he had left five dollars in the pot to cover the next rounds antes. He often did that, as a
won most every hand, he could afford to.


The last man at the table, a smal! fellow with the shifty cyes and quick fingers of a practiced cardplayer, had actually spent most of the
afternoon losing. His clothes, all mismatched shades of brown, clearly done on the cheap, had one unusual feature to them: His vest was
faded into the color of dirty sand, except one patch on the left side — a patch in the shape of a six-pointed star

That last man hadn't bothered to introduce himself. When pressed, he'd mumbled something about doing some work for the
Pinkertons, and fet the matter drop. The bartender seemed to know the man, and so no one had thought to bring the matter up again.
Nobody ever wants to drive the worst player away from the table, after all.

The bartender had watched them all come in and settle down with only the mildest of curious twinges. It wasnt unusual for a certain sort
of special folks to use his saloon for all sorts of meetings — He day or night. As a matter of fact, he was sort of proud of the reputation
his saloon enjoyed among his kin; word from Texas to the S4th Parallel was that Old Stone's in Shilo was a good place for "family"
business.

And if that family business looked like it was going to get ugly, well, what he kept down in the root cellar kept even the hottest heads
more or fess cool. There are some things that are just more important than fighting, you know?

He'd known from the start that this was going to be one of those special mectings, one that he'd have to keep an eye on, for a lot of
reasons, While the bartender hadn't gone through First Change himself (and never would), he had watched plenty of others, and the kid
looked like he'd just gone through it solo. Even worse, the kid had all the signs of having read too many dime novels about the frontier
— and believed every last one of ‘em.

Then there was the Indian necklace the lupus woman was wearing: the bartender had no problem with Indians (Icast not ones who paid
their tabs), but sometimes a whole pack that didnt understand the delicate arrangements he'd made with the local Uktena would come
into town whoopin' and hollerin' and generally trying to bust the place up on account of what was down in the root cellar Most of
them were kin to the Comanche, but there had been a few others, and they'd worn charms like the one that lupus woman

had on. Maybe she was a scout for a different pack that was fixing to take another whack at the caern he
watched over. It could be, it could be.

Plus, there was the fact that the fancy fellow with the sad mustache we }

was cheating, but Stone didn't worry so much about that. ames LE
After all, if the others werent wise to the . ol :
sharp, who was he to spoil the a : A Ps

man's business? al 1 ns

"Three cards," said Learns-the-Olld- Ways, "and don't
try to look at them this time." The kid blushed and
laid the three cards down on the green felt, even as
she favored him with a predatory grin. With
calloused fingers she drew them into her hand, and
her expression quickly shifted to a frown. "Crap."

"Folding again, dear lady?" Aaron, the Georgian, inquired.
"None of your damn business."
Ah, I forget my manners. Forgive me."

"Shut up and let her play," interjected the kid. The others turned to see that he'd haff-risen from his chair One hand was on the table,
the other was half-way to his holster. The cards were scattered everywhere. Slowly, Aaron raised his gaze to mect the dealer'.

id, do you really think you're fast enough? Hell, do you even think what you're packing is going to slow me down?" Aaron's eyes
ed onto the kid's, even as his fingers crept toward his own holsters.

be not, but you're not going to hassle her any more. And I'll take my chances with you, fancy dan." The kid's tone was defiant, but
ds were shaking. Aaron's smile grew wider.

Not like in the dime novels, is it, kid?" Moon said softly. "It's okay. You can sit back down and we can get on with the game."
*Not until he apologizes to Janice." The kid demanded.

Moon jerked his head sideways, his gaze traveling past the obviously annoyed Learns-the-Old- Ways to the dandy. "Aaron, would you
mind apologizing so we can get on with this?"

‘Walks With-Steel shook his head, slowly. "I would love to, my dear Colonel, but I'm afraid I cant. Should word get out that I gave in
to this punk, I would get grief at every table from here to Sacramento. Now, mind you, | will allow him apologize to me, We can even
make it as formal as anyone likes, and then get on with things. I'd really like that, as a matter of fact."

"Like hell | will! Put your hands where I can — uuurrrkk!"

And suddenly, the kid was sitting back down with both his hands on the table. Learns-the-Old-Ways later claimed that she'd actually
seen the stranger hook the kid's ankles with his foot and pull him back down into his chair, but the others didn't see so much as a blur.

"Kid," said the Pinkerton in a voice of infinite weariness, "sit down and shut up."

"But he—"

"T said, shut up." The kid lapsed into sullen silence, while Aaron didn't even bother trying to hide a smirk across the table. "Now let me
explain a few things to you about what you just did. First of all, you see that nice man behind the bar who's got his hands where you can't
see ‘em? He's the bartender, and the reason you can't sce his hands is ‘cause they're both down where he keeps his pet shotgun. If'n you'd
continued making a fuss, he'd have introduced the back of your head to one Mac Ain't that right, Zeke?" Tight-lipped, the
bartender nodded and returned to polishing glasses.

"Second, you never, ever drop your cards in the middle of a hand, ‘specially if you're the dealer. There's a lot of money on the table; you
want to figure out who that paper belongs to? In less friendly company, I've seen men shot deader ‘n nails for doin' what you
just did."

"Hell, I had three kings; it would've been mine anyway." The kid's tone was whiny and petulant; suddenly he looked much
than his 18 years.


"That ain't the point, and with your cards all over the floor it's going to be damn hard to prove it anyway. Cards are sacred out here. You
understand that? And another thing..."

"How long since your First Change, kid?" It was Learns-the-Old-Ways who spoke, staring intently across the table. "A month? Two?"
"T-I-L-1 don't know what you're talking about."

Learns-the-Old-Ways ignored him, turned to Moon. "Weaver's bird, Moon, the kid cant have changed more than a few weeks ago. And
with no one there to help him... I don't want to think about it."

"A lost cub..." the stranger muttered. "Ain't no wonder I was drawn here."

"It can't have been pleasant. So Aaron Stick-Up-Orifice, or whatever you're calling yourself, settle down." Moon scowled heavily and
turned to face the fid "Son, you have two choices. You can let us help you, or you can be a tinhorned pain in the ass, get your dander up
and stomp off. In that case, I predict you'll be dead inside of a week. Much as the local Indians dont like our looks, their friends like
our true faces even less."

The kid's face scrunched up in a confused frown. "Colonel, I have no idea what you're talking about, sir. No idea at all, and if you don't
mind, I'll be taking my leave." He started to stand but was cut short by a comment of Aarons.

"Let me guess. A couple of months ago, you started having dreams. Strange ones. Ones about running, hunting, killing — and then one
night, a couple of weeks ago, you found that it wasn't a dream. You had a face full of blood and something dead lying at your feet. And
you could have run home to Mommy and Daddy — they're rich, arent they? Easy to tell — but you got scared. You didn't want your
precious mommy and daddy to sec you with the guts of something, maybe someone, you'd killed all over your face. So you ran, took
some of Daddy's money and ran all the way out here, because you'd read a couple of dime novels and thought you could handle the big
bad frontier, didn't you? How much of Daddy's money did you steal, anyway, kid? How much is left, hmm?"

Moon's hand came down on the table like thunder. "That's enough, Walks-With- —
Steel, enough. Can't you see the boy is terrified?" "

It was true. The kid had sunk back into his seat, wide-eyed and trembling. "It wasnt
like that," he whispered, over and over again. "I swear it wasnt like that."

Learns-the-Old-Ways glanced over at Aaron with sheer contempt. "Brilliant. Were | Reward Offered for his Return!

"Son, let me tell you a little bit about who we arc." that his son was a well-mannered boy
who had-no énemies, and was well-liked by all who


"Nov, son, the first thing I have to tell you is what we are. We're what you'd call werewolves, Garou in our own parlance. Does that
explain a bit of what happened the other night? Somehow, I thought it might.

"Anyway, you can forget all that penny dreadful stuff you read about what werewolves are, and what they do, and whatnot. They're no
More accurate than the quarterlies you read about the frontier. We're not monsters, and we ain' the sort that gocs around wrecking cabins

and slaughtering homesteaders. Instead, we've got a mission — orders, if you will — and doing what we do is the only way to carry them
out.

5 Right now, you're probably feeling pretty sorry for yourself. You're thinking that your life is ruined, that you'll never see your folks
again, that you're going to spend the rest of your days on the run. You're thinking that what you've discovered about yourself is that
Ive been cursed.

fou've been given one of the greatest gifts that Gaia — or God, if that's what you want to call Her — can lay down on a mortal soul.
You've got the strength of a Samson, the knack of all sorts of magical trickeries, and the ability to talk to the spirits that are all around
Us. And at what cost? Not much of one, son, not much. At least, not much if you're a soldier.

"You see, if you think the War Between the States was something, well, you're in for a treat. What we're fighting for — and mind you,
son, this transcends Yank and Reb, Old Country and New, and maybe even white and Indian — is the world. We've been charged, sec,
with saving the world from something that wants to wreck it, sure as Sherman wrecked Georgia." Aaron coughed at that last.

"The whole world? That's impossible! I mean, how could anyone do anything to the entire world?" The kid was incredulous, but some
of the color had crept back into his pale cheeks.

"Did I say someone, boy? No. I said something. Hmmm...it looks like I've got to back it up a step of two.

"There are three powers in the world, son, with Gaia—"

"Think of Her as Mother Nature, and it'll go down easier," chimed in Learns-the-Old-Ways.

"—above them all," continued Moon. "But just below her are those three powers; we call them the Triat. There's the Weaver, who stands
for order. She weaves patterns into the ahi Then you've got the Wyld, which is the spirit of chaos — but in a good way, mind you. It's
Creativity, spontaneity — that sort of spark or fire you see in a good soldier's eyes. Finally there's the Wyrm, which is destruction.

"Now, the way things are supposed to work is that the Wyld dreams something up, the Weaver makes something useful out of it, and the

‘Wyrm gets rid of it when it isnt of use any more. Now, mind you, that's the way it's supposed to work. It doesn't work that way any
more.

"No one's real sure as to what happened, but I heard that the Weaver got a little bit out of control and tried to weave the Wyrm into her
doings. The Wyrm, being an ill-mannered sort at the best of times, didnt take kindly to this and resolved that it was going to

>* go out and wreck everything. And the Wyld, well, it wasn't paying much attention at the time. Come to think of it, I've never
Known a time when it did. ,

"So that's where we are right now. The Weavers trying to sew up the whole frontier, while the Wyrm's trying to wreck it all,
Tim not sure the Wyld's even noticed."


"I may be slow, sir, but I'm still not understanding you. You say a big worm's trying to cat the world? Well, | haven't seen too much of
things, but I haven't seen any giant worms in my travels, and you'll forgive me if I doubt that I ever will."

Moon sighed. "Son, I think you're misunderstanding me here, perhaps deliberately. The Wyrm — spelled with a "y," mind you — isa
spirit. An idea, sorta, but an idea that moves and thinks and eats all on its own. And while you're not going to sec that spirit out there
knocking down houses, you're going to see the things that get their marching orders from it. People who give in to greed or hate, lesser
spirits, but evil ones — we call ‘em Banes. Folks who get possessed by these Banes and turn into monsters — in the Old Country we'd
call ‘em fomori, after something out of legend, but around here they're called mockeries. And all these people and critters and things
half-way in between, well, they may think they're doing what they want to do, but every time they kill an honest man, or burn a
homestead, or poison a well, or kill a few thousand head of buffalo for no good reason and let the corpses rot, they're doing the Wyrm's
work for it. Now do you understand?"

Silent, the kid nodded. Almost as quietly, the stranger cleared his throat. "I think you've wandered a bit from the topic, Moon. Mind if
Tcut in?"

The soldier spread his hands in a gesture of surrender. "No offense taken. I'm not a storyteller, or even much of a reporter. Mind you, I
was there the day Meade had that fellow from the Richmond paper whipped out of camp, but that' another story."

"You were with Meade? And Grant?"the kid cut in. "Oh, I'd love to hear about that!"
"Well, I wasn't with Meade all that long—
"Moon." The stranger's voice cut across the soldier's like a whipcrack. "Your turn's done. Quict."

Caught in mid-sentence, Moon looked for all the world like a landed fish sucking ait "Of course, you're right, terribly sorry," was all
he managed to mutter before collapsing into a crestfallen silence.

The stranger smiled a thin-lipped smile. "Thank you kindly, Colonel. Now, if there are no further interruptions?" He looked around
the table. Learns-the-Old-Ways met his gaze with a challenge of her own; none of the rest looked up from the table. "Right. That's that,
then. Now, for a little practical learning."

"Now, kid, you've got some of the basics under your belt. It's my job to give you the rest of them, because Moon cant keep his stories
straight," said the stranger. There was a choked cough from across the table, but nothing more. "There are rules and regs to what we are,
and yall have to learn them afore we turn you loose. Otherwise we're just sending you out to your death, and I don't want your ghost
haunting me none."

> The Georgian cardsharp raised a wary eyebrow. "Strider? Should have guessed. Your kind does s0 love playing cryptic."

"Yep. Silent Strider. You have a problem with that? Didnt think so. Now, first order of business is to let the kid know what in
Srttion that means." The lawman swiveled his gaze; fixed it on the kid again. "Kid, first thing you have to learn about being Garou is
that we aint all the same. We're broken down into tribes, same as the Indians. A long time ago we sort of split up according to our
special feclings and interests and whatnot, and more or less broke ourselves down into 13 tribes."

"Sixteen," interjected Learns-the-Old-Ways. "Give him the full story."

The stranger tipped his hat. "Sixteen it is. Sorry, ma'am. Anyway, we broke down into 16 tribes, though two or three of ‘em are gone now.
Now, cach tribes got its own little piece of the pic, its own little specialty. My kind, the Silent Striders, well, we're wanderers. Go from
place to place, can‘ really settle down. We tend to carry messages and news a lot, things like that. ‘Course, there are a few other things

about bein' a Strider, like the fact that you generally get ghosts on you like fleas on a dog, but that's all part of the territory." For a
second, he glanced down at the patch where his badge had been. "Its a tradeoff, I guess. But it's the way it is."

"In any case, we're not the only tribe out there. There's the Fianna, who're mostly Irish and whatnot, and who usually" — he shot a
significant glance at Moon — "usually tell the best stories; the Bone Gnawers, who're really sort of the scum o' the earth, but at least
don't whine about it much; the Iron Riders, who're a little too tight in with the Weaver if'n you ask me; and so on."

"Black Furies, Bone Gnawers, Red Talons, Iron Riders, Fianna, Shadow Lords, Silent Striders, Stargazers, Silver Fangs, Get of Fenris
and Children of Gaia." The dancer's tone was clipped and terse. "Each with its own little...quirks, pecadilloes, nasty habits."

"But that's just II," the kid protested. "You said 16 or 13, not II."
Aaron's head whipped around, a surprised expression on his face. "That's fast counting. Good with numbers, are you, kid?"
The kid just shrugged. "I didn't think it was anything big. Just counting, that's all."

The cardsharp pursed his lips. "You know, kid, if you are good with numbers | just might have a proposition for you when this game
breaks up."

"Really? Well, | only worked in my father's offices for two years, but he did say that—" A withering glance from Janice and an unsubtle
"Harumph" from Moon were sufficient to wind the kids enthusiasm down to silence.

The ex-lawman sighed and cocked an eyebrow at the kid. as if to say, "You finished yet?" When the kid nodded, shamefaced,


the Strider held his gaze for a long minute before continuing. "There's two other tribes walking around, but they're not real happy with
our kind. Call themselves "Pure Ones," for no good reason I can see, and they're born out of Indian and native wolf stock. They dont
much like our kind, not that I blame ‘em much, but th' way I see it, you can't stand in the way of progress. The Wendigo and the Uktena
— that's the names they've given themselves — dont so much see it that way. So that's the 13 who are still around."

"And the others? What happened to them?"

Aaron let out a bark of bitter laughter. "Dead, or worse, my boy. Dead or worse. I don't know all the stories, but | know enough. The way
T heard it, the Croatan — Pure like the Wendigo — sacrificed themselves a few centuries ago, and now they're just a howl and a
memory. Now, my understanding is that they did it to board up some -beast or other — if they could have seen what their lands are
now, well, I suspect that they might have changed their minds." Learns-the-Old-Ways shot the cardsharp a horrified glance, but the man
went on. "Oh, and mind you, all our kind cares about is the fact that Virginia Dare went missing too, but remember, it's the winners that
write the history books. Then there's the White Howlers. They went over to the other side, well, it must be nine or 10 centuries ago, but
who's counting? They're called Black Spiral Dancers now, and you should pray you never meet one, kid. If you think we're scary, well,
wait until you meet one of their kind."

"Not quite the way I'd have put it, but more or less the truth." The Silent Strider sighed heavily. "We're far from perfect. Gaia knows I
get reminded of that every night. But in any case, those are the tribes. Each tribe has its own particular sort o' humans — or wolves — it
associates with. That's right, kid, we come from both sides. Need to keep mixin' in human and wolf blood or things get kind of stale.

"Now, mind you, not everyone's got the touch. Stone back there, well, he's got the bloodlines for it, but the moon just never leaned down
and kissed him right. So instead he's what we call Kinfolk, same as your folks. We protect our Kinfolk, work with ‘em, have families
with ‘em, and even if Stone himself aint going to make the change, well, just maybe one of his kids will.

"Course, we sort of have to keep having families with wolf-kin or human-kin — dont turn your nose up at wolves, son. You run around
on four fect at the right time of year, you're going to find the notion pretty darn appealing. Dont believe me? You will. But where was 1?"

"Kinfolk," the dancer supplied with a smirk, "And that delicate talk the boy's father should have had with him years ago."

"Why thank you, Janice." The dancer flushed, and the lawman grinned. "We have to look outside the pack for mates, ‘cause you're simply
not allowed to look inside. One Garou wants to settle down with another, it's trouble, trouble of the worst kind. Kids get born all
twisted " and sterile; we call ‘em metis. And since being cruel to a cub is about the worst thing you can do, life ain‘ too easy on the
parents of the poor deformed cub, cither. So if you had any notions about rescuing Lrastie tt Wars from the evil nasty poker player
and riding off into the sunset with her, forget ‘em." A blush from the kid told the Strider he'd been on the mark.

"But why?" The kid's face was still brighter than Moon', though the red glow was slowly fading. "I mean, you want to raise the best
horses, you breed the best with the best. You want to have the best fighters, why not, err, umm...." He trailed off into silence and blushed

again.
"Cause that's the way it is, and that's the only reason, kid. There's no other way to say it. And that's another important lesson to learn
about being of our kind: You obey the rules, no matter how strange they seem. They're rules, after all, and that's part of the price you've

got to pay. Now, we've got all the rules down in something called the Litany, which you're going to set to learning. It's a song, really, but
someone's taken to writing it down these days, which makes it easier on new cubs like you.

"Anyway, the basic rules are as follows: Garou Shall not Mate with Garou, which I've just told you about; Combat the Wyrm Wherever it
Dwells and Whenever it Breeds, which strikes me as plain common sense—"

"Banes riding it, most likely," said Moon. The other men nodded agreement, but Learns-the-Old-Ways shook her head. "No," she said

into the distance.

Learns-the-Old-Ways laughed. "Hardly. You haven't learned anything about spirits or totems or the Umbra. The first thing you have to
understand is that everythings got a spirit to it. Trees, rocks, animals, emotions — they've all got spirits. If you play your cards right, you
can talk to those spirits, maybe learn things from them, maybe get them to help you. If you're real lucky, you can talk one into taking up
residence in an obyect for a while; we call that a fetish, and it's something powerful to have. Don't expect to get your paws on one for
years, kid — but its important that you know they're out there, so that when you see an Oglala shaman call lightning down with a stick,
you know what's actually going on.

"Now, some spirits have a bit more affinity for our kind than others, and some more power than others. There are a few who take a
special interest in us, and help out from time to time. These spirits are our totems, and each pack gets adopted by one.

"That's not to say the spirits do all this out of the goodness of their hearts. You have to bargain with them, and it's anyone's
guess what they'll want. A colored stone, a war-song — it could be anything. Spirits may not afl be that bright, but they're
sharp cnough when it comes to dealing.


"Another part of the Gare is Gifts. You get Gifts from your auspice, you get Gifts from your breed, from your tribe, and half the time
I think from your hair color. Totems give you other help, but you get your Gifts first and foremost from who you are, and you get them
starting with your Rite of Passage."

"Well, that's just great," said the kid, "but there's one thing missing here. What are Gifts?"

"Gifts are magic, more or less," said Learns-the-Old-Ways. "Special ways of doing things like purifying water or talking to spirits or
calling down storms or whistling up the wind. Depending upon who you are — no — more what you are, you get certain talents for doing
things. The Gifts you're offered, I suspect, are going to have to do with war and fighting. Its all part and parcel of being an Ahroun."

"That's wonderful to know," the kid said bitterly. "Any way of knowing what these Gifts are, or do I have to wait uatil Christmas?"

"You'll just know, kid. That's all I can tell you. A time will come when you'll need to know how to do something, something your brain
tells you is impossible, and somehow you'll just find a teacher who'll give you exactly what y'nced. It's what happened to me; I was lost
and near dying of thirst when I ran across Cenc ote, Had a full canteen, he did, but wouldnt offer me none uatil I'd learned
from him how to call the waters for myself." She smiled ruefully at the memory. "Took me damn near a day, and I near died ‘for I got it
right. And did the old bastard offer me a drink once? Not a one. Gaia bless ‘im for teachin' me, though."

"I guess I sec." The kid's face furrowed into a frown. "That doesn't mean | have to like it, though, does it?"

"Not at all," Learns-the-Old-Ways reassured him. "It's just like medicine. Now, for your last lesson: the Umbra. Just as this is the real
world, there's a spirit world. Actually, there are spirit worlds, but the one we deal with we call the Umbra. It's where the spirits dwell, and
it looks a lot like, well, a lot like this place to look like. I mean, if a place has been tainted by hate or fear, in the Umbra it looks
run-down and foul. Ifa place has been ordered and tamed, there'll be Weaver spirits all over it in the Umbra. They look like spiders,
mostly, and there'll be webs everywhere.

"And if a place has a caern in it, well, in the Umbra it shines. We call going into the Umbra "reaching," which is as good a name as any
for it. It cuts down on travel time, but it's not easy and it can be dangerous. That, among other reasons, is why I own a horse." She smiled
ruefully. "Not as natural-fecling, but it raises fewer questions."

"She's not telling you the whole story again, son." Moon interjected with a worried look on his face. "What she's talking about is the

that tells me that there's a storm about to break in the real world as well."

O my brothers and sisters, my children and grandchildren, listen to me. I have walked
in strange places in my dreams, and I have seen terrible things. I have met with powerful
spirits, who showed me horrible visions of tomorrow, and of seasons not yet born. | tell
you now that these things are very important, and you must listen closely. The world is
going to change soon, like the weather, but the change may be forever.

I sing of a time when the Weaver will threaten to bind all of the People and all of the
land in her webs.

Where her gaze falls, her hollow-eyed children will swarm, and they will break mountains
with their woven thunder. The mountains and rocks will fly into the air, fall down in little
pieces, and the hollow-eyed ones will pick through the pieces to see what is to their liking.

She will spin powerful weapons for her children, and fill them full of Weaver-magic.

They will be tools with no spirits, yet they will spit death as surely as any fetish,
Her metal webs will stretch even into the physical world, and great Weaver-beasts will
run swiftly along the strands. She shall spin even into the sacred places, breaking them
and leaving them hollow.

‘eo She is coming = "GD

I have seen these things, and they make me afraid.
I sing of a time when the Wyrm will once again drag its stinking coils across the lands
we know as Pure.

Wherever its filthy scales touch, there will be sickness, and our families will weaken
and die from the strength of its poison. Spirits of plague will dance above dying tribes,
and not even the strongest of our kin will be safe.

Many men from afar will come with the Wyrm in their hearts and mouths. Their
tongues will be made of lies, and their bellies will always be empty; they will eat whatever
they can take, and it will never satisfy them, They will drink fire, and it will make them
ever thirsty, so they will drink even more. And the Wyrm in their mouths and under their
skins will touch the People, and the People will also hunger and thirst like dying men.

The Wyrm will come like a mad beast among the herd, and it will kill the animals and
leave their bodies to rot. There will be great heaps of bones and rotting meat, and the
people will have nothing to eat.


And when the Wyrm's hand tightens enough around the hearts of these strangers,
they will be filled with cruelty. They will run across the land with their long, long legs,
killing whoever they meet. They will be eaten up with greed, and they will kill. They
will be eaten up with hatred, and they will kill. The Wyrm will make a powerful and
terrible war against the people, and it will try to drive us into the waters and under the

mountains until we are gone. :

It is coming.

Ihave seen these things, and they make me afraid.

The worst of all things will also happen. The others, the long-vanished wolf-changers

who are like us and very much not like us, they will return. They will run with the
empty-bellied people and the evil people and the hollow-eyed people, and they will
fight the children of the Pure Lands. They will say that they are not of the Weaver or

the Wyrm, and yet they will blindly break the old seals and angrily fight us for our
sacred places. Only after many seasons will they see how foolish they have been, and
by then the Pure Lands will be no more. The land will be sick, and our people will be

hungry and cold.

CD Chey are coming. "CT

I have seen these things, and they make me afraid.
Listen to me and do not forget my words, O my tribes and people. The Wyrm is
coming, and he brings his people. The Weaver is coming, and she brings her people.
They come to slay the Wyld, and even I cannot say whether they shall succeed.

— The Lament of Voice-of-Winter, last Theurge of the Croatan

So these are the words that have been given to me by my parents, and that they
heard from their parents, and that were spoken by Middle Brother in his last breath. I
see your eyes shine; you hear the words and you know Middle Brother spoke true. The

hollow-eyed people have come, and their Garou families have come with them. They
kill the buffalo and steal the caerns, and we have not the numbers to fight them.
We are strong, but too few. We must be wise as well. I do not know if all these
strangers are evil; perhaps too many are like foolish children, who do as they like
because they have learned no better.

I will see if they hear the voice of the Land. If they do, then they must be told to
behave, or else our Mother will take sick from their strange illnesses, and the End
Times will be upon us. If they do not listen, then surely we must fight them until they
are driven back over the waters or are dead, for the Mother cannot tolerate their harsh
hands. | will offer them peace if they will have it. If not, then I will give them war until
the stars fall from the sky.

— Proud Speaker, Uktena Galliard

This is the West. Things are different out here. There's only
one rule — winner take all. And I intend to take it.

— Styles, Tall Tale

Welcome to a place where the frontier's split wide open,
where danger is as common as tumbleweed and as unfathom-
able as a spirit that devours thunderstorms to feed itself. It's a
time of war, a place of conflict. This isa place where Death runs
by night on four swift legs, a place of fire and steel and silver.
It's scene out of a tall tale, and yet it's a land easily lost in the
shadows of an emerging nation. Welcome to the Savage West.

Werewolf: The Wild West is a game of storytelling; agame
about mystique and wonder, visceral action and intense spiri-
tuality. It's a game that allows you to spin great tales of glory,
loss, horror and triumph. If you haven't done this sort of thing
before, don't worry — it will come naturally soon enough.

The setting for this game is the Savage West, a blend of

primordial horror and genuine historical action. The

& Wild West of the World of Darkness mirrors our own,

but is filled with brutal monsters that slip between the frontier
towns at night. Here conflict and savagery ride side by side with
spirituality and mysticism. There are few proper "good guys" as
we know them— even the heroes of this time, the werewolves,
are monsters themselves. But even though they're supernatural
terrors in their own right, the Garou (for so they name
themselves) are also people, admittedly of a different sort. And
as terrible as they are, they face an impending catastrophe that
threatens to sweep across this dark frontier and devour every-
thing in its path. This is a place that's about to be consumed by
killing winds and metal monsters, and it'll be all the inhabit-
ants can do to keep their territory safe — if they can.

And you're invited to step inside the tales of the Savage
West as a player and participant.

Chapter One: Introduction 25


SSS

ZLLPz

Storytelling

Storytelling is an ancient form of entertainment — and
even as such, it manages to be much more than simply a way
to while away the hours. It can convey information, polish
imaginations, and broaden horizons. The tall tales of any folk
culture say volumes about the people who tell them; that's
why they've always been worth repeating.

Storytelling, of course, comes in its highest form when we
all participate, not just listen. Are you content to simply hear
somebody else's tall tales, or read another man's dime novels
— or do you want to devise your own? Do you want to hear
the legends of the most terrible person (or thing) in
Utah, or do you want to take the title for
yourself? Do you want to hear about
the struggle on the frontier, or do
you want to put yourself there?

That's what this game is about.
Werewolf: The Wild West takes a
long look at old legends, and gives us.
all a chance to redefine them to suit
our own tastes, to tell our own stories
with these ancient archetypes. With
this game, you can look at your own
culture and yourself in a new context
— or you can, as the saying goes, walk
amile in another man's moccasins. Along
the way, you can see new things and
discover new perspectives, all in the
company of your friends. You might
just find it well worth your time.

=
Roleplaying
Evenso, this game isn't precisely 100%
storytelling, at least not by the usual
book definition. In most cases
of yarn-spinning, the tale-
teller knows how his story's
going to end. However,
Werewolf: The Wild West
deals in fluid plots and genu-
ine suspense — the final
outcome depends on your charac-
ters' actions, and is usually in
doubt. Just like improvisa-
tional theater, you have a
set to act on and foils to
react to — but you don't have a
script, and you make up the lines as you go along.
Not that roleplaying's all that difficult. Kids
do it all the time, tying towels around their necks
and acting out their super-deeds or miming gunfights. In
effect, by trying on someone else's shoes for a while, kids

exercise their imaginations and get a feel for just how many
possibilities the world has to offer. This game is just a more
sophisticated version. There are rules (so you know how
many bullets that bullyboy'll take before he falls over, and
so the events don't seem completely arbitrary) and a setting
(so everybody knows where the story is set, and can guess
where it might go). That's about it.

You can play Werewolf: The Wild West with as many
friends as you can pack around a table, but it tends to go
smoother with six or fewer players. This way, you still can
have a decent-sized social gathering, but it's small

enough that everyone can have their say.

The Storyteller

No game board in the world's big enough
or detailed enough to properly represent
the Savage West, the Storm Umbra, and
all their residents and features. Nota one.
The stage for this show has to be set in
your imagination, and the Storyteller is
the director and set designer.

Note that we didn't say
playwright — it's not the
Storyteller's job to put
words in the player
character's mouths. It's not
even her job to tell the charac-
ters where to go and what todo.
What she provides is a support-
ing cast, arunning (but flexible)
storyline and a set packed with
props. It's up to the players to
work out how the villain gets it in
the end (ifhe gets it at all), but they
obviously can't shoot him
with a gun that hasn't
been brought on stage
one way or another.
The Storyteller tells
the players what their
characters' surroundings
are, and describes what hap-
pens to the characters as a
result of what the players
say and do. The Story-
teller has the power to
judge whether a character
succeeds or fails, whether his
fortune's good or bad — even if circum-
stances call for him to die. The Storyteller
shapes the very world of the game:

not an idle undertaking, but about

as rewarding as you could ask.


Naturally, the best yardstick for measuring the quality of a
Storyteller is the enjoyment her players get out of the game.
That doesn't mean every story should have a happy ending,
but the tales her group spins should at least be good ones. To
this end, the Storyteller remains realistic, impartial and
creative; and if she's able to improvise at the drop of a hat, all
the better. What's more, she keeps an eye on internal logic
and consistency — the occasional outrageous coincidence
certainly preserves the flavor of the genre, but too much freak
chance and the story loses its plausibility, becoming less real
for everyone involved. The Storyteller sets the odds,
but doesn't deal from the bottom of the deck
(at least not without good reason).

So there you go. A Storyteller wears two
hats (at least): that of the judge, who inter-
prets and enforces the game rules to keep
things fair and flowing; also that of the nar-
rator, talespinner and entertainer. It sounds
like a hell of a juggling act at first, but this
book exists to help you out. In particular,
Chapter Three: Storytelling goes
into much more detail on the
Storyteller's various roles,
and offers advice on how
best to go about the job.

The Players ({-»)
Most of the gaming group are :
players, the ones who act out the

parts of the story's protago-
nists. Although they have
less work ahead of them,
they still have a responsibil-
ity to do what they can to
keep the game enjoyable.
Being a player involves taking
on the persona of a character, typi-
callyawerewolf,and then roleplaying
thatcharacter's reactions to herenvi-
ronment. Youpick where yourcharacter
goes, what she does, who she likes
and who she dislikes. What you
say, she says (unless you're
asking the Storyteller a
question or describing
her actions). The
more fully developed
her personality, the more
believable and fun she is to play.
Often, after describing the actions
"you" want to take, you must roll dice
(the ever-popular element of

© chance) to see if your character

successfully carries out those actions. Your character's Traits —
numeric descriptions of her strengths and weaknesses — dictate
how well she can perform certain actions. Actions are the basic
elements of Werewolf; they describe how characters affect their
environment and the story's course.

Just as in fiction and movies, the protagonists are the focus
of the story. They alter, direct and energize the plot's progress,
and often add new elements to the story. Players should feel
free to offer ideas for the betterment of the story, which the
Storyteller may adapt or reject as he sees fit. He may like your

suggestion that the familiar-looking ranch hand's

arelative of your character, or he may feel
that would hurt the story. Whatever's
most appropriate should be your guide.

Characters

We all have a multitude of different per-
sonality traits, some of which are frequently
dominant. A person uses a thousand voices, a
thousand facial expressions — all different,
but all still the same person. Your
characters are a literary variant of
this; they capture certain elements
of your personality, and logically
grow from there. Over the course
ofthegame, yourcharactersevolve
just as people do. They become bet-
ter, stronger people, or they change for
the worse; but just as in real life,
their experiences shape them.
Werewolf characters are
fairly easy to create; once you
have a fair idea of what you
want, it shouldn't take more
than half an hour to select the
appropriate Traits. However,
turningacollection of statistics intoa
living, breathingcharactergenerally takes
a bit more time and effort. Everything you
need to give your character life, that vivid
spark of authenticity, is already inside
you. All you need to do is tap it.
(Interestingly enough, most writ-
ers admit that their best-developed
characters often develop a life of
their own, and tend to write themselves.
Thiscan happen in roleplaying, too—a well-
defined charactercan wind updoingand saying
things that are perfectly appropriate for his
personality, but that the player didn't see coming at all! If this
happens to you, congratulations; when your character develops this
spark of life, the game becomes all the more exciting.)
Character creation is discussed in greater detail in Chapter Five.

Chapter One: Introduction a7


Endgame

So when does the game end, and who wins? Well, this is
pretty freeform; the game ends when appropriate, and there
aren't any clear-cut winners. The players aren't in competition
—far from it. Achieving the story's goals is usually only possible
if the players cooperate. Even if your character dies along the
way, you still haven't "lost," at least not if you had a good time.

If your character survives, and manages to improve his lot
(either through self-improvement or by successfully accom-
plishing any personal goals she has), then you're about as
close to winning as it gets. What's more, your pack earns
certain victories over the antagonists arrayed against you —
if you root out the vampire cult in the sleepy Arizona town
and destroy the bloodsucker in charge, you've "won" an-
other sort of victory. If he winds up controlling or killing the
entire pack, then the characters have certainly lost the
battle. Once more, packmates are encouraged to learn to
trust one another and work well together. A house divided
against itself cannot stand, especially in the Savage West.

What You Need

Werewolf: The Wild West was designed to be played
around a table. You don't need a board or cards — just this
tulebook, pencils, paper, character sheets (photocopy the
one in the back, or make your own) and dice. This game
calls for 10-sided dice, which you can find in any game store.
The Storyteller may also want to have paper on hand in
order to sketch out a setting or a rough map (making it easier
to describe places to the players), visual aids such as old
photos or daguerreotypes, and any other props that spring to
mind (poker decks, oil lamps, feathers — whatever makes
the experience more vivid for the group).

The Werewolf

Werewolves aren't easily described as humans that can
turn into wolves, or vice versa — they're something else
entirely. In human form, they look like you or me, but
they're different on a fundamental level. Half-mortal and
half-spirit, they call on mystical abilities that Gaia has
given only to her chosen children.

‘W Multiple Forms: A werewolf isn't limited to two forms;
he can walk as a man or wolf, as well as take more combat-
oriented shapes. Most terrible of these is the half-wolf,
half-human Crinos form, although the massive near-human
and dire wolf shapes are nearly as potent.

‘w Regeneration: Although werewolves can be cut, shot or
mangled; they supernaturally heal their injuries at incredible
speed (one Health Level per turn). Only wounds fatal to normal
humans can slow down a werewolf for long. However, wounds
caused by silver weapons, fire and the claws and teeth of their
own kind (or other supernatural beasts) are harder to heal.

ZLLLs

4

‘e Gifts: As messengers of the spirit world, werewolves
learn great supernatural powers that they call Gifts from
the spirits.

‘ Perception: All werewolves have the sharpened senses
of an animal; their hearing and smell are almost without
compare, particularly when in wolf form.

‘w The Delirium: The Crinos war-form floods humans
who view it with blind, instinctive terror. Nobody wants to
admit that such monsters exist, and most folks eventually
forget what exactly it was that they saw. Werewolves see this
selective amnesia, the Veil, as both a shield and a weapon —
many think nothing of killing or terrifying those humans who
become bothersome.

‘w Frenzy: Werewolves are practically the embodiment
of anger, particularly in a justified cause like the defense of
what's theirs. When their emotions get the better of them,
they practically go berserk, lashing out at whatever is in
their way. The tales of man-eating wolves attacking on the
full moon are usually spawned by these rages. A frenzied
Garou instinctively changes into her war-form, and forgets
everything about being human or even wolf — the demands
of her anger are all that matter.

Although a werewolf begins life as a human or wolf, she
soon finds out how different she truly is. This game deals with
what she and her kin do after this revelation; how they react
to the horrors lurking under the skin of the Savage West, and
who or what they choose to fight for. And fight they must, for
the storms boiling in the future aren't about to spare anyone.

Darkness in the West

The overall mood of Werewolf: The Wild West isn't
that of the old Saturday morning serials, with white hats and
unquestionably upstanding sheriffs. This fictional West is
one that could have happened, and that doesn't ignore the
atrocities that were a part of our own history. Here, inno-
cent women and children die at the ends of cavalry bayonets
or war hatchets. White men try everything short of geno-
cide (and sometimes even that) to take the lands they feel
are rightfully theirs, and the natives resist in whatever ways
they feel necessary. To make matters worse, there's trouble
coming on the spiritual front, trouble that normal men and
women don't stand a prayer against.

This is where the Garou come in. These warriors are ina
volatile atmosphere, one where compromise is almost never
an option, where treaties are more commonly broken than
upheld. And werewolves have the most sensitive hair trig-
gers of them all. A werewolf can explode at any minute,
possibly even slaughtering an entire mining town in a
berserk fury. All it takes is being pushed a little too far —
and in this rough atmosphere, such provocation comes easy.
The Garou are monsters, creatures that hunt and kill by
night. Anywhere they go, someone stands a good
chance of dying.

Werewolf: The Wild West


However, even werewolves overcome their taint of fury
and rise to become heroes, legends in their own right.
What's more, the characters are expected to do just that;
without the efforts of heroes, the state of the Savage West is
only going to become worse. Only through virtue, courage
and unity do the Garou stand a chance of survival. It's a lot
toask, and just about everything rides on whether the Garou
can meet the challenge or not.

Lexicon

Werewolves can instinctively communicate with wolves,
and most learn at least one or two human languages. How-
ever, Garou also have their own tongue, and even their own
jargon for specialized terms that don't arise in humanspeak
(at least with any degree of accuracy). The following lexicon
contains terms broadly used by most werewolves. The sheer
number of cultures the Garou exist in (or watched crumble)
contributes greatly to the etymological diversity of their
language. However, these terms are a key insight into how
werewolves define themselves.

Common Jargon

These words are commonly used by native and immigrant
werewolves alike.

Anchorhead: A spirit gate between the Middle Lands and
Heavens; see Membrane.

Apocalypse: The age of destruction, maybe even the end
of Gaia herself — a time that werewolves prophesy will come
when the Wyrm tries to swallow Gaia forever. Most Garou
feel this will be the final battle — and many look to the
ravages of the Storm Umbra as proof that the Apocalypse is
coming, if not already here.

Auspice: The phase of the moon under which a particu-
lar Garou is born; held to influence personality and sacred
duty. The auspices are: Ragabash (New Moon; Trickster),
Theurge (Crescent Moon; Seer), Philodox (Half Moon;
Judge), Galliard (Gibbous Moon; Moon Dancer), Ahroun
(Full Moon; Warrior).

Bane: A generic term for an evil, corrupt spirit; the Wyrm's
own offspring. The variety among Banes is astounding, and
they often have "breeds" of their own.

Bawn: A boundary area around a caern, where the Garou
keep a careful eye on local mortals.

Blight: Any corrupted area in either the spirit world or
physical reality.

Breed: The ancestry of a werewolf, whether wolf, human or
other Garou.

Caern: A sacred place, a meeting spot where the Garou
contact the spirit world.

Celestines: The greatest spirits; the closest things to
gods that werewolves acknowledge. Examples are

® Luna (the Moon) and Helios (the Sun).

Concolation: A great multitribal moot, called to discuss the
gravest and most wide-reaching concerns. Almost no
concolations have been called in the last century.

Concord, The: The agreementall the tribes reached nearly
9,000 years ago; the end of the Impergium. The traditions it
established are still obeyed today.

Crinos: The half-wolf, terrifying war-form of the Garou.

Deep Umbra: Another term for the Heavens.

Delirium: The madness that seizes humans unlucky enough
to see a werewolf in Crinos form.

Domain: A mini-Realm in the Middle Lands, usually con-
nected to a larger Realm in the Heavens.

Feral: Slang term for Lupus.

Gaffling: A simple spirit servant of a Jaggling, Incarna or
Celestine. Gafflings are rarely sentient.

Gaia: The universe, both physical and spiritual (usually when
referring to the beautiful portions); Mother Earth.

Garou: What many werewolves call themselves; sometimes
disdained as an unnecessary euphemism.

Gauntlet: The barrier between the physical world of Earth
and the spirit world of the Umbra. It is strongest around
technological, Weaver-ridden places, weakest around caerns
and in the heart of the wilds.

Heavens, The: The far-off realms of the Umbra; the lands
that lie outside the Membrane. Reality becomes less cohesive
the farther into the Heavens one travels.

Hispo: The huge dire wolf or near-wolf form of the Garou.

Homid: A Garou of human ancestry, sometimes a point of
disdain to lupus —e.g., "I never saw a homid that could follow
a track." When capitalized, refers to the human form all
werewolves may shapeshift into.

Impergium: The 3,000 years immediately following the
birth of agriculture, during which werewolves culled settle-
ments to keep human populations in check.

Incarna: Powerful spirits; weaker than the Celestines, but
still great spirits by any measure.

Jaggling: A spirit servant of an Incarna or Celestine.

Kenning: A form of howl with emotional subtext.

Kinfolk: Humans and wolves who are related to the Garou,
and who are resistant to the Delirium.

Klaive: A specially crafted fetish dagger or sword, usually of
great spiritual potency and usually made of silver.

Litany, The: The werewolves' traditional and ancient code
of laws.

Lupus: A werewolf born of wolf blood; when capitalized,
the wolf form all Garou may assume.

Membrane, The: The barrier between the Middle Lands
and Heavens; usually breachable only through the Membrane
or the Dream Zone.

Metis: The sterile, deformed and typically reviled offspring
of two werewolves.

Chapter One: Introduction 28


oR

Middle Lands: The spirit world surrounding the Gaia
Realm; also called the Near Umbra.

Mockeries: Bane-possessed humans or animals that gain
strange powers from their "benefactors," but are completely
corrupt.

Moon Bridge: A gate between two caerns; most often called
during moots.

Moot: A sept or tribal conclave called at a caern.

Mule: Slang for metis.

Near Umbra: See Middle Lands.

Pack: A small group of Garou socially and spiritually bound
to each other by ties of friendship or mission as opposed to
culture.

Penumbra: "Earth's shadow," the spirit world directly sur-
rounding the physical world; many, but not all, Penumbral
terrain features are the same.

Protectorate: The territory claimed and patrolled by a pack
or sept.

Pure Lands: The mostly native Garou term for the Ameri-
cas; refers to the belief that the Garou had banished the
Wyrm's influence from the land previous to the Europeans'
arrival.

Pure Ones: The Garou of the Pure Lands; the Croatan,
Uktena and Wendigo.

Realms: The worlds of "solid" reality within the Tellurian.
Earth is referred to as the Realm.

Sept: The small community of werewolves that lives near
and tends an individual caern.

Stepping Sideways: Entering the spirit world. Also Reaching.

Storm Eater, The: A great spirit, part Weaver and part
Wyrm, that devours the energies of the Wyld to feed itself.
Some see its awakening as a sign of the Apocalypse.

Tellurian: All of reality; generally used to refer to bad as
well as good.

Totem: A spirit joined toa pack or tribe and representative
of its inner nature. Tribal totems are Incarna, while pack
totems are Incarna avatars (usually about Jaggling-strength).

Triat, The: The Weaver, the Wyld and the Wyrm; the
trinity of primal cosmic forces.

Tribe: A larger community of werewolves, bound by a
common totem, similar lifestyles and often blood relations.

Umbra: The spirit world.

Veil, The: The amnesia that claims most people who fall
prey to the Delirium.

Ways, The: The traditions, lore and secrets of werewolf
society.

Weaver, The: The purest, greatest manifestation and sym-
bol of order and pattern. Its influence on Earth manifests in
logic, technology and most other forms of "progress."

Wyld, The: Manifestation and symbol of pure change,
chaos, creativity, metamorphosis and elemental force. Often
associated with the wilderness, and as such venerated by
werewolves.

Wyrm, The: Once the cosmic force of balance, now the
manifestation of entropy, corruption and decay. It feeds on and
spreads murder, desecration and pollution, among other evils.

Wyrmcomer: Uktena and Wendigo term for Europeans,
who the natives see as having brought the Wyrm to the Pure
Lands.

Wyrmhole: A locale that has been spiritually defiled by the
Wyrm; invariably a place of powerful corruption.

Slang

These terms are in favor with young Garou, who prefer
"plain speech" to fancy formality. Elders typically look on such
as disrespectful.

Ape: Slang for human or homid; uncommon among Pure
Ones. "Monkey" implies even more contempt.

Lone Wolf: A werewolf who has chosen or been forced to
leave Garou society, forfeiting all social standing and commu-
nal respect. The old form version is Ronin.

Run: A ritual hunt or revel that takes place at the conclu-
sion of a moot.

Sheep: Humans.

Storm Umbra: The wild and chaotic Penumbra of the
American West; also used to describe the areas in the Middle
Lands that have suffered from the Storm Eater's taint.

Throat: To best another in ritual combat; used as a verb.

Tick: One of many derogatory terms for vampires; here used
in the "worthless parasite that's hard to kill" sense. Other
common terms include Leech, Cadaver, Deadskin and Carcass.

Old Form
These terms were spoken in the oldest days and are kept
alive by lorekeepers; most display a distinct Fianna turn of
phrase. Werewolves use this "fancy-speak" only infrequently,
but virtually all know these words and their definitions.
Adren: A pupil or student who learns from a mentor.
Airts: The magical paths within the spirit world (Spirit
Tracks, Moon Paths, etc.).
Aisling: A journey into the spirit world; also, a visionquest.
Anamae: "Soul-friend"; most often a bond with a pack

totem.


Anruth: A Garou who travels from caern to caern but is bound to none
of them; a dangerous practice in the Savage West.

Athro: Teacher; mentor.

Brugh: Any sort of mystic place, whether caern, untouched
spiritual glade or even Wyrmhole.

Charach: A Garou who mates or has mated with another Garou; an
insult.

Chiminage: Offerings or services made to appease spirit instruc-
tors orallies; also, a term describing a request that a sept can make
of any Garou who uses its caern.

Cliath: A young Garou, not yet of any standing rank.

Fomori: Mockeries.

Fostern: Your pack brothers and sisters; a werewolf's
family by choice rather than blood.

Gallain: Kinfolk.

Harano: An enigmatic, almost supernatural depression
that claims some werewolves. Said to be mourning for the
Earth Mother's suffering.

-ikthya: "Of the Wyrm"; a suffix appended to a name.

Inceptor: A Garou who guides another through a rite;
more frequently called ritemaster.

Kinain: The family relationship among blood-related
werewolves; a term of endearment and pride, never used when
referring to metis.

Pericarp: The section of the Near Umbra around each
Realm.

Phoebe: An Incarna of Luna, representing the Moon.

Praenomen: A pack's totem spirit.

-thya: "Greater in station"; a suffix appended to a name.

Ronin: See Lone Wolf.

Urrah: Garou who hold too much to civilization and"
"progress"; also, the tainted ones.

-yuf: "Honored equal"; a suffix appended to a name.



Brothers — My people wish for peace;

the red men all wish for peace; but where the
white people are, there is no peace for them,
except it be on the bosom of our mother.

— Tecumseh, Shawnee ca. 1811

The Savage West

Unlimited potential, limitless dangers, the Savage West looms
larger than life. A land of sprawling plains, towering mountains
and vast, unfathomable canyons, it offers little mercy to the weak
and untold glory and renown to the strong and dedicated. In this
bold century of discovery and expansion, European and American
explorers brave the strange and forbidding wilderness. An inexo-
rable wave of settlers follows in their wake, claiming lands for their
homes and placing boundaries where none have been before.

"Manifest Destiny" becomes the cry as the onward rush of
civilization brings cities, railroads and armies in its wake.

The long-time inhabitants of the lands west of the Missis-
sippi watch this migration of pale-skinned strangers with
curiosity, at first, but soon find themselves driven from their
best hunting grounds and fertile fields. Their anger and outrage
grows, ultimately sparking a series of battles they cannot win
against foes who are too numerous, too well armed, and who

don't play by the natives' "civilized" rules.

In this era fraught with war — between humans and the
wilderness, settlers and natives and, most tragically, between
indigenous and immigrant Garou — another conflict rages,
one fought in both this and the spirit world, as a great and evil
spirit breaks free, ravaging all in its path.

Chapter Two: Setting 33


"This was once the Pure Lands. Back before any European set
foot on the Americas, the protectors of Gaia triumphed over the
Wyrm, binding its minions with a web of power anchored by the
might of their caerns. The Croatan, Uktena and Wendigo tribes
held the webs taut while exercising minimal interference with
the people and creatures under their watchful eye.

The coming of the Europeans changes that. The Croatan sacri-
ficed themselves to stop the Wyrm-creature knownas Eater-of-Souls,

Father of Waters and the mighty Pacific before the end of the 1800s.

The Wilderness

From the vast prairies that blanket the Midwest to the towering
ranges of the Rockies, the land west of the Mississippi has long
remained a wellspring of the Wyld. The stark, majestic beauty of the
Grand Canyon and the barren splendor of the Great Salt Desert
radiate with power from a closeness to the Umbra that has not yet
succumbed to the bindings of the Weaver or the encroachments of
the Wyrm. Along the western coast, the great Pacific crashes against
shoresbordered by sandy beachesand virgin forests. Few humans live
here, but those who do have come to terms with their natural
surroundings and attune their lives and
daily patterns to the spirit brothers
and sisters that
dwell all
around
them.

Native Americans

We gave up some of our country to the white men, thinking that then we
could have peace. We were mistaken. The white men would notlet us alone.

— Chief Joseph, Nez Perce

The descendants of the Pure Ones have prospered in the Pure
Lands, making their homesin the fertile prairies, following the great
herds of bison, and growing cor in the vast southwestern deserts.
Asearlyasthe 16th century, when the Spanish landed in California
and Mexico, the natives of western North America found their
lives changed by the introduction of horses, guns and Christianity.

Before 1830, numerous tribes of Native Americans made the
western half of North America their home. The Great Plains,
which encompass the land between the Mississippi and the Rocky
Mountain plateau, served as hunting grounds for the nomadic
plainstribes: the Pawnee, Blackfeet, Cheyenne, Kiowa, Comanche,
Osage, Oto, Sauk, Fox, Crow, Arapaho and Sioux. The NezPerce,
Paiute, Cayuse, Shoshone, Flathead and Utes dwelled throughout
the region formed by the Great Basin between the Sierra and
Rocky Mountains and the Columbia River plateau. Along the
Pacific coastal region of California, the Miwok, Chumash, Yokut,
Yurok, Hupa, Wyot, Tipai-Ipai, Gabrielino and other tribes were
already diminishing after two centuries of Spanish presence. In the
Southwest, the Hopi, Pima, Dineh (or
lived in the harsh desert lands and amid the rocky canyons north
of Mexico. Along the rainy, forested northwestern coast lived the
Tlingit, Tsimshian, Salish and other peoples.

By the end of the century, most of these tribes find them-
selves confined to reservations — some far from their native
homes. Decimated by diseases introduced by
contact with white settlers,


traders and soldiers, deprived of their hunting grounds and
farmlands, and angered by the lack of faith demonstrated by the
pale-skinned newcomers, some refuse to go quietly to their
appointed fate. Violent clashes between natives and settlers —
or, more often, with cavalry troops stationed along the frontier
to protect American interests — become frequent.

Waves of Exiles

By the early 1800s, the new U.S. government, eager to
expand its borders, adopts a general policy of opening up its
western boundaries for settlement and eventual incorporation
into the Union. The presence of native populations proves
troublesome and detrimental to expansion. Despite the treaties
that guarantee the sanctity of native homelands, the govern-
ment begins the general relocation of the native peoples to lands
west of the Mississippi. In the most infamous of these mass
relocations, the Trail of Tears, the U.S. government exiles the
vast majority of Cherokee from their southeastern home, reset-
tling them in Indian Terr' *ry, the future Oklahoma.

Unfortunately for the newcomers, their new homes are not
uninhabited. Otherexiles, including their formerneigh-
bors, the Choctaw and Creeks, have preceded

them. But even before them, there were

other tribes that lived on the
lands" given" by the

U.S. govern-

ment to its a
deportees.

Wolves

Unaware of the doom that lies before them, wolves flourish
in the lands west of the Mississippi. The abundance of herd
animals and other game provides ample food for the packs of red
and gray wolves which, along with the mountain lion and
coyote, maintain the balance between predator and prey. Viewed
as fellow predators by the native tribes, wolves are rarely hunted.
Indeed, many tribes hold the wolf as one of their sacred or totem
animals, and in some cases as a messenger from the Great Spirit.

They are not wrong in their assessment, for many of the
strongest wolves are Kinfolk of Gaia's spirits-made-flesh, the
Garou. Cherished for their strength, endurance, loyalty and
cunning, these special wolves form the backbone of the lupus
(or wolf-born) strain of the Wendigo and Uktena.

With the coming of ranchers and sheep farmers, the exist-
ence of roving packs of wolves becomes a liability to the
economy of the booming livestock industry. Wolves recognize
no boundaries, fences do not stop them, and cattle and sheep

seem no different to them than the

bison or antelope that serve as
their natural prey. Wher-
everlivestock becomes

an important

business, the
wolves
are soon
hunted
to near-
extinction.

Te RS

The Cities and Civilization

Asearlyas the 16th century, Spanish explorers and conquis-
tadors made their presence felt in California, Mexico and the
Southwest. Spaniards established settlements and missions for
the dual purpose of funneling wealth and resources to Spain and
for converting the native populations to the worship of Christ.
In 1803, the Louisiana Purchase opened the lands west of the
Mississippi to westward expansion by the new government of
the United States of America, compelling others to brave the
unknown territory that beckoned from the far shores of the
great river that divides the continent. The Lewis and Clark
expedition blazed the trail across the newly acquired lands, and
it reo~hed the Pacific in 1805. By 1830, the move west by
settlersand homesteaders heralds the onset of a wave of
settlements that soon link the bustling cities of the eastern
United States with the boom towns, mining communities and
frontier towns of the West.

During the next 60 years, pioneers transform the face of
the land and displace the Native American tribes who dwell
there. The coming of the railroad, transcontinental tele-
graph, the discovery of gold in California and the Range Wars
that pit sheep farmers and cattle ranchers against each other
in a vicious battle for grazing rights leave little room for the
people and the creatures who were there first. Backed by the
U.S. Cavalry and a network of forts, homesteaders and
ranchers, entrepreneurs and prospectors, missionaries and
educators spread across the land. Where they go, towns and
settlements soon arise.

Missionaries, Homesteaders
and Prospectors

Jim Bowie lay dying, his blood and his powder run dry

But his knife had him ready to take him a few in reply

Ah, young Davy Crockett lay laughin' and dyin'

The blood and the sweat in his eye

For Texas and freedom a man was more willin' to die

— Donovan Leitch, "The Alamo"

The westward expansion by settlers and immigrants in-
cludes a wide range of people, each with his own motivations
for claiming the unknown wilderness for his mother country
or for himself. The Spanish establish missions, forts and
colonies along the California coast and in Mexico, south of
the Rio Grande. Their cities, however, remain small and
serve primarily as trading posts and bases for their military
presence. After the war that transfers Spanish lands in Cali-
fornia and north of Mexico to the United States, the western
coast beckons to hardy American settlers. The discovery of
gold in 1849 transforms the town of San Francisco into a
major city almost overnight.

In Texas, a growing dissatisfaction with the Mexican
government (or lack thereof) sparks a war between Texas
and Mexico that results ina brief period of independence for

Texas. By 1853, the United States has nearly solidified its
hold on the lands west of the Mississippi. America's borders
now stretch from the Atlantic to the Pacific.

The temptation of cheap land, bountiful and fertile,
proves irresistible. The growing population in the East
demands ever greater quantities of food, and the newly
acquired prairies seem ideal for grain and livestock. Cattle
ranching, already established by the Spanish in the south-
west, grows into a major business. Other livestock farmers
see the opportunity to raise sheep for wool and mutton. The
Range Wars escalate between the cattle ranchers, who need
the open range for their mobile herds, and the sheep ranch-
ers, who rely on fenced-in pastures.

Many see this time as one of fiscal opportunity, and seize
the chance to make their fortunes. Some, blessed with capital
and backed by the interests of the East, settle in the growing
cities of San Francisco, Los Angeles, Santa Fe and Houston.
The Gold Rush brings waves of hopeful prospectors, land
speculators and fortune hunters to California. In their wake
come the necessary "support" industries to help these new
investors spend their windfalls: saloons, banks and gambling
halls. Soon the West develops a reputation as a land of
gamblers and show girls, gunslingers and lawmen. The native
peoples, where they still maintain a visible presence among a
sea of white faces, are relegated to the status of curiosities and
local color, giving rise to many stereotypes — among them,
that of the "cigar-store Indian."

The Telegraph

Following the trails blazed by the pioneers and later by the
Pony Express, a new kind of trail stretches across 3,000 miles
of skyline from New York to San Francisco in 1861. This
single, fragile wire opens the door to rapid communication
between the civilized East and the expanding West. Before
the telegraph, news from the East can take four months to
reach California. The Pony Express can get the mail through
in a few weeks — provided riders avoid any hazards along the
way. With the opening of the first transcontinental telegraph,
information flashes across the breadth of the country in mere
minutes. Suddenly, the West is no longer an isolated enclave,
but a part of continent-wide America.

In the wake of lumbering wagons piled with surveyors'
tools, poles and wires, over 27,000 wooden poles tower
across the plains, mountains and deserts, strung with iron
wire, cutting a swath of strangely bare-looking "trees"
across the landscape. The humming electrified wires spook
animals, with the exception of bison, who find the poles
convenient scratching posts and often send them crashing
to the ground.

Many of the Plains Indians are initially highly suspicious of
the telegraph wire, due to the builders' penchant foradmin-
istering electrical shocks to them when they approach

and ask what it is. Told that it isa great beast that eats *

Werewolf: The Wild West


lightning, many Garou Kinfolk carry the story back to their
Uktenaand Wendigo brethren. Though many donot believe the
wire isa living beast, they certainly recognize itasa manifestation
of the Weaver's unwanted presence fouling lands already reeling
from European invasion. While the native tribes eventually
realize that the rapid communication made possible by the
telegraph helps their enemies plan military campaigns against
themand begin cutting wires todisruptsuch plans, the werewolves
pull down poles and break wires in an attempt to limit the
Weaver's passage through their lands. They will soon have to
face a far worse incursion, however, as the iron tracks of the
railroad are laid, cutting through hunting and burial grounds,
tunneling through mountains and eroding caerns, gripping the
land firmly in the Weaver's cold, rigid grasp.
