The need to establish swift cross-country travel and communica-
tiongivesrise tothestagecoach, which carries both mail and passengers
from St. Louis, Chicago and other eastem cities across the continent.
For two years, the Pony Express provides rapid mail delivery, and the
telegraph takes over in 1861. In 1863, a new project begins: the
construction ofa transcontinental railroad. By 1869, a road of iron rails
stretches across the West. By 1883, a network of railroads links the
westem coast of the United States to points east, south and north. Prior
totherailroad, travelers endure weeks ina jolting stagecoach, cross the

MINORITIES In THE West

Despite what the average Western depicts, white Christian males aren't the only people of
importance in the West, nor do they represent the lion's share of homid Garou. Blacks, Chinese,
Indians, Jews, Mormons, women in general, and other "minority" groups figure prominently in the
struggle to pioneer and survive in the Wild West.

Chinese physicians practice acupuncture and herbal healing. Though patronized almost exclusively
by their countrymen, they represent some of the more advanced medical practitioners in the cities of
California and along the railroad line. Various religious communities fleeing persecution move to the
West, among them the Mormons, who settle in Utah, and the Jews who bring a passion for education
and higher learning that opens the West to greater diversity and better schooling.

Free blacks (and later, escaped slaves or newly freed ones) make up a significant portion of the pioneers
and homesteaders seeking a better life and land to call their own. According to a Spanish census, people
of African descent comprised 18% of California's population by 1800. The black soldiers of the 9th and
10th Cavalry, called "buffalo soldiers" by the Indians because their curly hair reminds them of the curly
pelts of the bison, serve in some of the roughest areas of the frontier, hunting down desperadoes and bandits
and attempting to establish law in a lawless land. A born leader, ex-slave Benjamin Singleton engineers
the Exodus of 1879 which brings over 40,000 black pioneers up the Mississippi tosettle in the western lands.
Many blacks become cowboys, making up approximately 25% of those tough range riders.

Despite the limitations imposed upon them by social mores and legal restrictions, women fill as many
(if not more) positions as do men. Stagecoach driver Charlotte "Charley" Parkhurst, disguising herself as
a man, votes in state and federal elections, exercising a privilege available only to males. Annie Oakley,
ace shooterand rodeo star, makesa name for herself proving thata keen eye, a firm hand and askillful trigger
finger know no gender. Besides filling the expected positions of frontier wives, mothers, school teachers,
saloon or dance-hall girls and missionaries, women also join the ranks of prospectors, miners, healers,
ranchers, business owners and outlaws. Among native tribes, older women are valued as counselors and
wisdom keepers. Some tribes allow women to vote in tribal affairs and to own homes and livestock.


fever-inducing Isthmus of Panama or sail for months around South
. America's tip to reach the West. The Pacific Railroad Act of 1862
enables the building of the rail lines, granting the builders 6400
acres per mile along alternating sides of the tracks. The builders

"+ promptly sell this land to settlers to raise additional money.

" Workers begin laying tracks from both East and West, intend-
ing to meet somewhere in the middle. Work continues despite
detours and loss of manpower necessitated by the Civil War. In

1865, more than 12,000 Chinese workers are brought over to lay
the rails. Stargazers and Iron Riders follow their Kinfolk. Explosives
blast trails through the lofty redwoods, into mountain sides,
and sometimes, through them. Tunnels as long as 490 feet
are hollowed through solid granite mountains too high to
cross; where the builders figure the mountain gapsaren't toosteep,
they lay the tracks upward and through. Trestle bridges, some as long as 800
feet and 120 feet high, span gaps or rivers, anchored to either end and to
pylons that snag trees and sometimes change the course of rivers due to
buildup. Miles and miles of hand-hewn wooden tiesstretch across the great flat
deserts and prairies, forming straight wood and iron roadway stretching to the
horizon. In order to cross through the western mountains, an unheard-of
engineering feat is undertaken, and the iron road rises 3800 feet in only 39 miles.

As the rail lines lengthen, they bring along "hell-on-wheels" towns such as

Cheyenne and Reno. These pestilential boom towns feature saloons, opium dens,

brothels, dance hallsand gambling parlors, all designed for the fun-starved railroad
workers and the thieves, cardsharps and prostitutes who trail in the train workers'

wake. Murder, theft and mayhem become the order of the day. Reveling amid
the debauchery and easy pickings, the Bone Gnawers find a home.

In May, 1869, East meets West at Promontory Summit. The final spike
rings out asa man hammers it into the track and the transcontinental railway
becomes a reality. This is the death knell to the Plains Indians' way of
life. Even the Wendigo caerns are compromised and their Kinfolk

are left to starve when the bison are eradicated by professional
buffalo hunters (who take the skins and leave the meat to rot) and
joy-riders who shoot from the windows of the moving trains. The
Wendigo must bow to the inevitable, accompanying their
people into exile on reservations or fleeing to the high mountains
to continue sporadic raids against the interlopers.

The Time Period

It is not by speeches and resolutions that the great questions of the time
are decided. ..but by iron and blood.
¢ —Bismarck, September 1862
. Beyond the romantic myths of the noble Indians, bold cavalry
officers, stalwart pioneers, sinister gunslingers and glamorous
outlaws, the reality of the six decades between 1830 and 1890
"pt, tells the epic tale of opposing forces brought into unrelenting
s+ conflict. As Western civilization carves its way across the
: American continent, an older civilization struggles
= ; — desperately —and unsuccessfully — to survive.
Whether filled with expectations of
a new beginning, possessed of a
desire for the untold riches
that lie west of the Mis-
sissippi, consumed


|

witha lust for adventure or driven to escape the crowded confines
back east, the tide of settlers, homesteaders, entrepreneurs and
fortuneseekers who trek across the continent—dragging "progress"
in their wake—leave nothing they touch unchanged. Werewolves
run with them, either as protectors of Kinfolk and human flocks,
or as Lone Wolves seeking a place for themselves. These Garou
bring their own outlooks and prejudices with them, setting
themselves up for an adversarial relationship with the native
skinchangers. The native human population views the newcom-
ersat first with equanimity, but later, to their growing dismay and
anger, understand the danger these strangers present. The Uktena
and Wendigo greet their European cousins with mixed feelings,
but outright war between werewolves doesn't erupt until the
threat to the Pure Lands becomes all too apparent.

Farmers' plows, builders' tools and hunters' and soldiers'
guns — all transform the landscape. The "taming" of the
wilderness relegates native populations to reservations or con-
demns them to extinction, to drown beneath the inexorable
wave of westward-bound civilization. Even worse, Garou caerns
and protective wards are stolen, abandoned or destroyed. This
frees uncontrollable forces of the Wyrm and the Wyld, that
twist the Penumbra into the Storm, spreading a new brand of
chaos to the Savage West.

=
Changing Breeds

A few other shapeshifting breeds inhabit the American West,
quietly coexisting in a tenuous truce with the Garou. In the early
days of the world, the Garou battled other Changing Breeds in a
bloody conflict over rights to the title of Gaia's chosen people. The
Garou emerged triumphant, but their victory left deep scars in
the minds of the other shapeshifters, making them reluctant to
trust or work with the victors. Many shapeshifters withdrew from
active participation in the affairs of the material world.

When the European Garou first landed on American soil, a
second, shorter War of Rage slew many of the indigenous
shapeshifters. The Uktena, Croatan and Wendigo refused to aid
their foreign Garou cousins in this war, but the damage was done.

Legends of these Wars of Rage, etched into the memories of
the Children of Bear, Coyote, Raven and Cat, continue to
prevent outright alliances between the other Changing Breeds
and the Garou. The native humans see all of these animals as
powerful manifestations of the spirit world and honor them as
totems and messengers from the unseen world. For their part,
these rarely seen skinchangers quietly maintain their own Kin
among the natives and avoid the werewolves. Even the Uktena
and Wendigosee little of the Gurahl, Nuwisha, Corax and Bastet
these days; the wounds are still too painful.

Though natives hunt and utilize animal skins and meat, they
do so sparingly and with reverence for the gifts thus given. The
arrival of the Europeans, who see profit in the abundant wildlife

for its skins (and occasionally, meat) puts the Changing
Breeds under attack again as they see their Kinfolk fall
i in lesele to the nine anal erates Gb the himiter

Garou Society

Death's couriers, Fame and Honour, call

Us to the field again.

— Robert Cunninghame-Graham, "Cavalier's Song"

The social structure, beliefs and customs of the Garou show
the influence of both human and wolf societies as well as their
own unique outlook. Creatures of Rage as well as instinct,
Gnosis as well as intelligence, they truly possess a dual — and
sometimes conflicting — nature, one that places them on the
border between the spirit and material worlds.

Becoming Garou

Since the earliest times, werewolves have cultivated both
human and wolf flocks, selecting the best among them as
breeding stock. During the Impergium, a time in the distant past
when the Garou exercised strict control over the proliferation of
humans, the werewolves culled the human population of its old,
weak and sick, trying to keep humans from spreading unchecked
across the face of Gaia. This era gave rise to the phenomenon
known as the Delirium, a powerful madness that strikes humans
who see the Garou in their half-human/half-wolf form.

Even during the Impergium, most werewolves recognized
that without humans, there would be no Garou. As groups of
Garou developed preferences for certain types of humans and
wolves, this specialized breeding gave rise to the unique char-
acteristics that differentiate the tribes from one another.

Only about one in 10 successful matings gives birth to a
werewolf, although "normal" children with a Garou parent
carry within them the potential to pass on the blood to later
generations. Known as Kinfolk (see below), these cousins tend
to be immune to the Delirium, and serve as allies and links to
the larger communities of humans and wolves.

Among the European tribes, those children who will one day
run under the moon as Garou begin their lives as mostly normal
members of their respective families. They tend to be loners and
misfits, subject to bizarre dreams, great emotional upheavals and
other signs of their.. difference. Often their families or commu-
nity members believe they are "tetched in the head" and consider
them at least marginally dangerous. These yet-to-become Garou
live tenuousliveson the fringes of society, dreaming vague dreams
ofsomething they cannot quite understand, yet long for intensely.

Natives of the Pure Lands who will become Garou walk a
different path. They too are often loners; they too dream and
suffer physical and emotional crises no less severe than those
experienced by their European counterparts. However, their
families attach much more importance to these manifesta-
tions, and interpret them quite differently. Where Europeans
see madness, natives see one touched by the spirits. Before their
First Change, many Uktena and Wendigo are welcomed as
potential shamans, medicine workers or wisdom keepers. Few
tribes ignore or harm those who evince such potency. Most
tribes believe that if these "touched ones" are shamans, they

can only help the tribe; if they are witches, it does not pay to
anger them. Thus, while young, unchanged Garou are not
treated the same as others of their tribes; they are frequently
given great respect and honor.

But throughout the cubs' childhoods, werewolves are watch-
ing from afar. Those marked with the unmistakable signs of their
destiny usually undergo some traumatic experience between the
ages of 10 and 16 (for humans) or one and two years (for wolves).
Whether struck by a sudden outburst of near-Rage or some
partial physical transformation, a new-changed Garou's normal
life is over. In many instances the younglings are driven from
their packs, if they are wolves, or are socially ostracized or even
confined to the cellar or upstairs attic, if they are European
humans. Nativesare often required to undergosome test (usually
a visionquest) to show their fitness for spirit-working.

Atsuch points, the Garou intervene. If necessary, they kidnap a
child and spirit her away to their sept, where they guide her through
herinitial transformation and instruct her in the basics of tribal ways.
Before she has time to fully adjust to the knowledge that she is more
than human or wolf, the fledgling Garou, usually with other new
cubs, undergoes a challenge that tests not only her innate strength
and intelligence but her newfound skills and latent instincts. If she
survives this Rite of Passage — and many new Garou do not —she
emerges with the knowledge that she is one of Gaia's chosen,
unsurpassed as a predator and blessed with the charge of serving as
awarrior on the front lines in the battle against the Wyrm. From this
pointon, she is considered fully Garou— though still of lowly Rank
—and accorded membership in her sept and tribe.

Breeds

Before a werewolf undergoes her First Change, she is shaped by
her human or wolf family, just like her mortal brothers and sisters.
Whether a werewolf grows up in human settlements or in the
wilderness as part of a wolf pack has a profound effect on her
outlook and her life as a Garou.

Homid: Garou born into human society retain the knowl-
edge of civilized ways even after their transformation. Called
"homids" to distinguish them from "humans," they are able to
interact easily (though perhaps not comfortably) with human-
ity. They tend to have a more extensive vocabulary than their
wolf-born brethren and better understand the nuances of
language. The mores and standards of human society often
color their way of dealing with the world.

Many European Garou who settle in the New World pass on
their own expansionist dreams and attitudes of racial or cul-
tural superiority — vestiges of the societies from which they
arose — to succeeding generations. Uktena and Wendigo
Garou born from the native human population also inherit the
outlooks of their tribal families, thus planting the seeds of
inevitable conflict between native and immigrant Garou.

Homids often find themselves torn between their
roles as protectors of Gaia's wild places and their
& human urges to shape and "improve" whatever they

come in contact. Although they despair at the folly of their
human cousins, they cannot condone the wholesale extermi-
nation of the species and thus become forced into the role of
apologist for the sins of humanity. This puts them at odds with
their more forthright lupus cousins.

Lupus: Lupus Garou begin their lives as wolves, thus sharing
in and having an innate knowledge of the life of the pack (and
their place within). Called "lupus" to distinguish them from
normal wolves, these Garou learn wildemessskillssuch as tracking
and survival rather than civilized niceties. Wolf society is brutally
direct, uncolored by human morals or pretensions. Lupus Garou
depend on their instincts and trained senses, and have few qualms
about behavior that is natural, if not always "humane." Garou
born into wolf packs adopt these mores and reflect them through-
out their lives, even after discovering their true natures.

Lupus Garou rarely feel the need to speak rather than act. To
them scent, posture and facial expression ought to provide suffi-
cient information for any Garou. Most do not understand their
homid cousins' need for lengthy discussions, becoming bored by
repetition and barely restraining themselves from simply seizing
the initiative and letting the "gabblers" follow as best they can.
When theyarecalledupon toexpress themselves in words, they are
very direct and simple. Havingnever learned human speechas well
ashomids, they utilize words with few syllables. No flattery or irony
colors their speech, nor do they bother to cushion complaints and
criticism in soft words. They say what they have tosay as briefly and
baldly as possible. Lupus make their wants, needs, likes and dislikes
abundantly clear and detest those who do not do likewise.

Despite their disdain for human language and customs,
lupus possess both great intelligence and cunning. They are
capable of crafting devilishly subtle plans and exploiting their
foes' weaknesses. Having grown up in packs, they are masters
at utilizing pack members skills and at working as a team.

The lupus have long been favored by the humans living in
close proximity. Almost all native tribes acknowledge wolves as
their spirit brothers. The few who do hunt wolves do so out of
necessity, never killing many, never taking breeding-age wolves
and always thanking the wolves' spirits for their gifts. Now the
lupus face threats they aren't prepared for. Indigenous Garou of
red and gray wolfstock find themselves challenged by Europeans,
whoencroach on both their packs' territories and breeding stock.
Furthermore, as farmers and ranchers seize more land, hunting
territories shrink. Homesteaders annoyed by the loss of livestock
offer bounties on wolves, further decimating the supreme posi-
tion that the lupus Garou — and their wolf Kinfolk — once held.

Metis: Garou traditions ban mating between Garou, but
sometimes this prohibition falls by the wayside in the heat of
desire or out of necessity. Those born of the forbidden union
between two werewolves are called metis. Metis are often killed
at birth or spend unhappy lives as scorned outcasts even when.
tolerated by Garou society. Many feel that Gaia's curse for the sin
committed by their parents is upon these wretched creatures.
The evidence for this point of view lies in two inescapable marks
on the metis cub: deformity and sterility.

Chapter Two: Setting 41


Every metis is deformed in some way. This usually takes the
form of some obvious physical aberration, such as a clubfoot or
useless hand. Deformity upsets many Garou. Each Garou is a
soldier in a war against the Wyrm. Deformities weaken Gaia's
warriors, making them less effective in combat andeasiertocatch
and kill. Occasionally, metis deformities assume less-apparent
forms, resulting in mental or psychological aberrations.

Some argue that metis sterility is a good effect, claiming that it
prevents deformity from being passed down toa new generation of
Garou. Most werewolves, however, understand that each Garou
bom adds another soldier to the fight. Sterility in any werewolf
diminishes the potential number of Garou in the next generation.

Though brought up in Garou rather than human society,
metis are rarely accorded equal rights unless they fight fiercely for
them. As they easily assume Crinos form, they can make powerful
warriors, and their successes in battles against Wyrm-tainted foes
often win them grudging respect and places in their tribes.

A few Garou have begun to notice the increase in the number
of metis. Once so rare that many Garou could spend their whole
lives without ever encountering one, metis are beginning to
appear with some regularity. The Stargazers, in particular, see this
upsurge in metis births as a sign that the Garou have forgotten
their duties and laws. Even among the Pure Ones, metis begin to
make their appearance with alarming regularity as the troubles of
the century are reflected in the mating habits of the Garou.

Although they are relegated to a less-than-equal status
among Garou, metis who are allowed to survive rarely leave the
only families they know. Among humans and wolves, the
physically deformed or mentally afflicted receive little or no
compassion. Humans treat the physically aberrant with dis-
dain and repulsion; wolves usually kill imperfect pups. Many
otherwise highly intelligent humans are dismissed as little
better than idiots because they are born with crooked spines,
weak limbs or harelips. Because of this general intolerance,
most metis remain among the Garou, where they at least have
a fighting chance to prove their usefulness to their tribes.

Caerns

Speak to the earth, and it shall teach thee.

— Book of Job, 12:8

Within the Earth'smantle exist wellspringsof mystical energy, places
where Gaia's power shines through from the Umbra. These special
places are sacred to Gaia, and they are where Garou build their shrines.
Known as caerns, these places can take many forms. Strange rock
formations, mountain tops, hot springs and geysers, redwood forests and
underground caves all serve this function in the West. Sometimes, the
power comes through in less-obvious places, such as in the desert or
atopa lonely butte set amid 12 more just like it. It is not the physical
configuration that is important, rather the energy found within.

Werewolves use this power to call potent spirits or to travel vast
distances toothercaerns. Those with thisknowledgecan linkcaems
together, forming a spiderweb pattern of energy that can bind and
constrain powerful, malicious entities, rendering them harmless.

Werewolf: The Wild West

Caerns serve as a central focus for Garou life, taking the place
of the homestead or lair so important to human and wolf Kinfolk.
Werewolves draw their spiritual power (called Gnosis) from the
energy residing in their caerns. Thus, protecting a caern from
harm by the outside world constitutes one of their primary duties.
Some werewolves permanently reside within or in proximity to
the particular caerns they guard. All Garou, whether or not they
choose to live at a caern, pay regular visits to the caern nearest
them to refresh their Gnosis and reestablish their commitment
to Gaia. Caerns serve as meeting places, sites for worship and as
graves for fallen heroes. Many older werewolves prefer to die
within the embrace of a caern, giving their bodies back to the
Earth and anchoring their spirits to Gaia's holy ground. The
physical and mental well-being of all Garou hinges on being able
to tap into the energies of Gaia, and those unfortunates who are
denied access to a caern often suffer illness or insanity.

Werewolves dedicate their caerns to particular spirits, usu-
ally their tribal totems. These spirits help guard caerns from
intrusion in the Umbraas wellas in the material world. Certain
mystical rites performed by knowledgeable Garou elders can
open passages into the domain of caern totem spirits.

Most tribes possess their own caerns, though a few communal
caerns exist. Exclusive caerns are just that; Garou who donot belong.
to the tribe claiming the sacred ground are not welcome within its
bounds. In the Savage West, caern rights and their inviolability are
major issues as the native Garou find their places of power usurped
by the onrushing wave of pioneer Garou. Asalways, there are at least
two sides to the problem of caerns. The Garou who left their caerns
back east for the unknown West need to find places where they can
replenish their fading Gnosis, even if it means impinging on caerns
already claimed by others. The Uktena and Wendigo, however,
have spent centuries weaving delicate connections between their
caerns. The coming of the strange Garou upsets this balance. In
some cases, the results prove disastrous, with wars fought over who
has right to which caem. In addition, the native Garou who
accompany their eastern Kinfolk into exile beyond the Mississippi
also seek caerns of their own, pitting them against their tribemates
over already established caerns.

The Garou are not the only ones who depend on the Earth's
mystical energies for sustenance. Native faeries, the Nunnehi in
particular, are drawn to caern sites. They are attracted by the
potency of the magic (which they call Glamour) that gathers in
those spots. Mages, as well, search for places where power (or
Quintessence) collects. Mages often unknowingly intrude on
caerns, believing the sites available for tapping. This becomes
more common as some caerns are abandoned through their
keepers' exile or inability to hold onto the places in the face of
the westbound population. Sadly, many native Garou becomeso
incensed by the greed of their European cousins that they
deliberately shut down the caerns they are forced to abandon, in
effect sealing them off from use by any other than them-
selves. Such caerns cannot be used by newcomers, but
neither do they uphold their places in the chain of
interwoven caerns that contain dangerous spirits.

Towns are occasionally built on or near existing caerns, and
railroad companies (seeking the straightest path) tunnel through
or cross over caerns that are in their way. This destroys most sites,
but a few retain their energy. Urban caerns are often claimed by
Iron Riders or Bone Gnawers, the two tribes who are most
comfortable around humans. Humans usually give such oases of
powera wide berth, as these places make them feel uneasy (often
arent reason). Such sites are usually dismissed as
"haints." In contrast, some rare urban caerns exist among the
more settled Uktena Kinfolk. These usually take the form of
special kivas or sweat lodges that are found within the human
community, but are set aside for use by "spirit messengers."

The Litany

Although werewolves acknowledge few human
laws as binding, they keep their own code of
strictures, a code that has endured the passage of
mille:

The ancient precepts that govern the

lives of all Garou, regardless of tribe, form the song
of songs that lies at the heart of the relationship
between Gaia and her chosen warriors. More than a
simple recitation of laws, the Litany encompasses
what it means to be Garou.

Garou Shall Not Mate With Garou

When two Garou mate, their offspring suffer from deformi-
ties of either body or spirit. The Litany prohibits such pairings.
Garou represent the intersection of the natural and supernatu-
ral worlds, and only through the union of Garou and human or
wolf can the species perpetuate itself. Any physical or mental
weakness severely lessens the survival and effectiveness of
those afflicted. For the Garou, to be less than the physical
embodiment of Gaia's health constitutes not just a crime, but
a betrayal of the Wyld.
Reality: Although breaking this law constitutes one of the
most serious transgressions among Garou, metis do exist.
Although relatively rare, the instances of forbidden love
between Garou grow more frequent as the turbulence
and upheaval of the 19th century seep into the founda-
tions of Garou society. Many see the growing number of
metis as a clear warning that Gaia's warriors are losing
their strength and conviction. These advocate
shunning the parents of metis children, though
they attach no blame to metis children for their
less-than-acceptable births. More intolerant
Garou believe the parents should be killed
and the metis destroyed before they

corrupt other Garouand cause large-
ie scale disobedience of Gaia's laws.


=

Combat the Wyrm Wherever It Dwells
and Whenever It Breeds

Garou are born to protect Gaia from the ravages of the Wyrm, and this
tenet shapes the essence of their psychology. The stories and songs of the

battles between legendary heroes of the Garou and monsters spawned from
the Wyrm's belly serve to remind Garou of why they are what they are. Few
of Gaia's warriors would deny the importance of this simple credo.
Reality: Even among the native Uktena and Wendigo, territorial
disputes have served to distract them from their appointed task. The
coming of the European and Asian werewolves to the western part of the
American continent only intensifies competition for caerns and protec-
torship rights. Too often, Garou expend their energy in deciding who
is fittest to fight the Wyrm than in actually combating their greatest
foe. Further, not every tribe agrees on how best to combat the
Wyrm. Where the Red Talons might argue for the extermination
of the human corrupters, the Iron Riders attempt to control the
Wyrm by binding it with the Weaver's rails and telegraph lines.

Respect the Territory of Another

Justaswolvesmark territory with their distinctive scent, Garouseptsemploy both scent
markers and clawed symbols to indicate the borders of their protectorates. Those not of
aprotectorate are expected to announce their presence through the How! of Introduc-

tion whenever they desire to enter a territory and interact with the Garou therein.

Reality: Before the coming of European Garou, the Uktenaand Wendigo generally
respected each others' territories — insofar as the native tribes associated with them
acknowledged the hunting grounds of neighboring tribes. With the arrival of the
Fianna, Get of Fenris and other newcomers, problems involving territory quickly
arise. Tribal symbols often go unnoticed or unrecognized. Sometimes the
westward bound newcomers deliberately ignore these markings, insulting
the native Garou whenever their territorial integrity is violated. Even
when European Garou keep this precept of the Litany and announce their
presence, they are notalways understood, despite their use of the common
Garou language. The isolation of the Uktena and Wendigo from
other werewolves for so many centuries has caused a slight but
noticeable language drift. Their version of the Garou tongue
differs somewhat from that spoken or howled by the European
tribes. Although the connotations and emotions in a howl are
easily understood, the snarling and posturing aspect of Garou speech
can be more readily misinterpreted. Some belligerent-minded European

Garou deliberately urinate over other tribal markings asa challenge to rights
of territory. Since many native Garou travel with nomadic tribes and cover
large areas, these land claims often go unnoticed until it's too late, sparking
vicious disputes over "uninhabited" territories.

Accept an Honorable Surrender
Introduced into the Litany by the Children of Gaia and the Fianna to
avoid wasting Garou lives unnecessarily through internal warfare and
duels, this tenet allows an individual to gracefully withdraw froma
losing battle with an honorable foe. By offering her throat, falling
to the ground or adopting some other position of submission,
T Yi ft) thelosing Garou surrenders, thus awarding victory toher
Mani R, opponent. No Renown is lost in this fashion, though

Gus) sit. :
MN the winner often gains Renown both for the
Zant

victory and for sparing the life of the loser.

Werewolf: The Wild West


Reality: More than any other portion of the Litany, this
concept causes consternation and confusion among both im-
migrant and the native Garou. Not all European Garou adhere
to this part of the Litany. Tribes too proud to surrender,
including the Get of Fenris and Silver Fangs, and accorded
little respect by their fellows tribemates if they do, maintain
that duels and intertribal battles are necessary to whittle out the
weak among Gaia's protectors. Others, such as the Shadow
Lords, scoff at the idea of "honorable" battle, claiming that the
Wyrm knows no honor, nor do many of the tribes.

In the clash between European Garou and the Uktena and
Wendigo tribes, cultural differences playa big role in undermining the
idea of "honorable surrender." Both Uktena and Wendigo believe in
adifferentformofnonlethal warfare, thatofcountingcoup. Influenced
by the native tribes from whom they draw their Kinfolk, both native
tribes believe thatscoringanondamaginghiton youropponentproves
individual bravery and also shames the victim into quitting the battle.
(Naturally, no such mercy is granted to servants of the Wyrm.) The
European Garou, not understanding the meaning behind such ges-
tures, see this practice as senseless or crazy.

Further, in many of thenative human cultures, surrenderisasure
prelude to enslavement, maiming, torture or slow death by
excruciating methods. Their Garou brethren therefore prefer to die
in battle. When European Garou attempt to surrender, they might
suffer no more than having a lock of their hair cut off by the victor
—orthey might be marched back toa Wendigo caem where they
are torn apart by the sept. They are also baffled by the natives'
practice of adopting surrendered foes with the intent of having
them replace fallen comrades. It takes many years for cultural
translation to make this tenet understandable to both sides.

Submission to Those
of Higher Station

Both lupine and human societies acknowledge the necessity of
hierarchy and status. As creatures sharing in both natures, Garou—
particularly those of lupus stock — recognize the idea of a "pecking
order" within pack and tribe. In addition, many Silver Fangs and
Shadow Lords bring their aristocratic outlook to bear and support
the idea ofstrictRanksand stratification among Garou. The concept
that noble birth, experience, wisdom and valor deserve respectful
obedience is hardly unique in the 19th century. Garou translate this
into an elaborate system of Renown and Rank, one that requires
obedience to reasonable requests from superiors.

Reality: Although generally adhered to by all the tribes in one
form or another, interpretation of this tenet varies. Among the
Uktena and Wendigo, respect for tribal elders, wisdom keepers
and proven warriors goes without question. The native humans
and wolves alike understand the importance of obeying leaders
and following the counsel of veterans. For many European Garou,
however, the concepts of hierarchical leadership and privilege are
breaking down. The wave of revolutions that swept Europe in the

18th century and the emerging notions of egalitarianism
that gave birth to the new American nation are eroding
= the status quo. The Europeans who journey west, more-

Chapter Two: Setting

over, often do so out of a desire to escape the confining structure
of ranik.nd social status. The Garou who accompany them also
question the necessity of kowtowing to older, more seasoned tribe
members, arguing that new lands require fresh minds rather than
ones weighed down by old traditions and tactics.

The First Share of the Kill
for the Greatest in Station

Whether referring to actual meat brought down to feed the pack
or to war trophies captured from foes, this idea supposes that the
Garou with the highest Renown and Rank deserves the tastiest
portions or first picks of powerful fetishes and loot. Some Garou,
notably elders, Silver Fangsand Shadow Lords, take thiseven further,
advocating their claim to all the best items or portions of the kill.

Reality: Among wolves, alphas are always accorded first feeding
rights, for they are the breeders who will ensure the continuation of
the species. The all-lupus Red Talons adhere strictly to this idea.
While most European Garou grudgingly accept this edict to some
extent, many among them insist that the wealth be shared at least
some of the time. The Get of Fenris preserve the Norse custom in
which a chieftain receives all the treasure and the best food. Often,
however, the chieftain will then manifest his generosity by awarding
these to worthy warriors and advisors. Those chiefs who fail to show
such magnanimity do not long remain in command. Among the
Uktenaand Wendigo, the hunter who bringsdown the game claims
its heart and other choice portions. Likewise the warrior keeps his
own battle trophies. He may choose to give such prizes to his chief
asa token of respect, and doing so brings honor to the giver. Among
the Children of Gaia, elders often hold powerful fetishes in trust for
the tribe, apportioning them out as needed.

Ye Shall Not Eat
the Flesh of Humans

This precept dates from the end of the Impergium and arises
from perceptions of the Stargazers and other Garou philosophers
that those who consume human flesh eventually succumb more
easily to the snares of the Wyrm. Further, pursuing such weak
prey in turn weakens the predator, who may forget how to hunt
more challenging quarry. Unlike animals, humans often poison
their bodies by ingesting toxins such as tobacco, opium and raw
liquor, rendering their flesh unhealthy for consumption.

Reality: Cannibalism is the exception rather than the rule
among Garou...but exceptions do occur. The Impergium never
really ended for the Red Talons; most still consider humans as
barely worthy of being food. Some Wendigo, emulating their
tribal totem, consume the hearts of courageous and honorable
foes — animal or human — to gain such qualities themselves.
They view certain kinds of cannibalismas gestures of respect. The
Uktena, on the other hand, rarely indulge in eating human flesh,
finding the thought repulsive. The Get of Fenris tend to overlook
indiscretions as a byproduct of battlelust, as long as no evidence
remains. In extremes, when survival depends on the consumption of
human flesh, most Garou bow to necessity —and later seek purifica-
tion for their actions.


Respect for Those Beneath Ye—
All Are of Gaia

Although Garou possess a healthy dose of individualism (a
gift of their partial human nature), their wolf blood imbues
them with a strong sense of "pack." They recognize that leaders
must have followers and that protectors must have someone or
something to protect. All creatures have their place in Gaia's
natural order, and many "lesser" species (i-e., non-Garou) act
as caretakers for those beneath them. Just as alpha wolves do
not scorn those under them, werewolves are supposed to
acknowledge each others' contributions and refrain from "lord-
ing" over lesser members.

Reality: Most Garou heartily endorse this precept as the
embodiment of their role as caretakers, though they acknowl-
edge that there will be an occasional slip-up, especially by those
too enraged to refrain from killing lesser beings, whether
animal or human, Garou who assiduously follow the inherent
chivalry in this ideal may gain Renown. Silver Fangs, Children
of Gaia and Stargazers (among the Europeans) are the stron-
gest proponents of this tenet. Red Talons, Wendigo and
Uktenaall adhere to this in varying degrees, though the Talons
easily "forget" it when it comes to encroaching humans. Get of
Fenris and Shadow Lords are more likely to sneer at this idea,
claiming that respect means nothing if one doesn't earn it.
Bone Gnawers, who receive little, if any, respect, laugh out-
right when they hear this part of the Litany recited. On the
whole, European Garou tend to look down on their native
cousins, the Uktena and Wendigo, while at the same time
giving lip service to the concept of the simple, "noble savage."

The Veil Shall Not Be Lifted

This is the most well-respected and forcefully kept portion of the
Litany. It prohibits werewolves from revealing their existence and
prevents their discovery by fearful humans and agents of the Wyrm.
The thought of humans exterminating the Garou with silver
bullets is enough to compel most werewolves to hunt their foes at
night and cover their tracks. Few second chances exist for those
who violate the Delirium, the Garou's most potent shield against
discovery. Those who deliberately break this taboo suffer death at
the claws of their packs — or septmates, as do any mortal witnesses.

Reality: The Delirium usually covers up accidental viola-
tions of this tenet, but where it fails, pandemonium and
large-scale "witch-hunts" ensue. Memories of the Inquisition
remain fresh in the minds of European Garou, so few among
them dare expose themselves to humans. Fianna Galliards have
immortalized a few tragic occurrences in which a Garou has
confided in a sympathetic mortal only to have her outraged
pack or sept slay both of them. These cautionary tales and songs
serve to dissuade the "romantics." Wendigo and Uktena who
have integrated themselves into their Kinfolk sometime per-
form shamanic duties for their human kin. Occasional partings
of the Veil thus gain limited acceptance as powerful spirit
magic. These instances, however, are exceedingly rare and
happen only in extreme circumstances.

Werewolf: The Wild West

SSSESN

OXX

Do Not Suffer Thy People to
Tend Thy Sickness

The good of the tribe comes before the good ofa single Garou.
That principle underlies this portion of the Litany. In ancient
times, old or infirm Garou were torn apart by their peers to make
room for younger, stronger members. This was done to keep the
infirm Garou from endangering the rest through slowness or
inability to perform duties, and to preserve the tribe's resources
in times of want. Whether sacrificed ritually (as some Black
Furies practice) to return the elder to Gaia, or slain in a mock
battle, the intention is to honor the Garou by not allowing her
to slide into senility and helplessness.

Reality: Though there are still some proponents of ritual sacri-
fice, most modern Garou believe in allowing the infirm to take their
own lives. Those who see the need and do so are honored just as if
they had fallen in battle against worthy foes. Red Talons are amazed
that this precepteven needs to be stated; their close association with
their wolfKinfolk leads them toan innate understanding of theneed
for this principle. Among the Wendigo, nomadic as they are, ifany
of the old and infirm cannot keep up on the march, they are left
behind to die. Get of Fenris precipitate battles they cannot win
rather than be shamed by a less-than-glorious death. Uktena,
conversely, believe in caring for their elderly or sickly as payment for
their own nursing as children and to preserve knowledge as long as
possible. Some Europeans are beginning to develop the same idea,
believing that Garou are becoming too scarce to wantonly throw
away wisdom and experience simply because an elder can no longer
shapeshift or walk without a cane.

The Leader May Be Challenged
at Any Time During Peace

Despite their pack mentality, Garou recognize that shifting
circumstances often necessitate changes in leadership. When-
ever no outside threat exists that requires a state of readiness
anda united front, challenges to the leadership of asept or pack
may occur. Should the challenger win, she becomes the new
leader —at least until another rises to lay claim to the position.
Tests of leadership may involve physical, mental or social
contests; the loser must accept the outcome and abide by the
decisions of the winner.

Reality: Most leaders desire to retain their positions as long
as possible. Some tribes, such as the Iron Riders and Children of
Gaia, employ complex methods of determining who is fittest to
lead atany given time. In addition, the recent rise of democracies
and other forms of representative governments is introducing
the concept of elected leadership among some Garou. Native
Garou see things a little differently. Among the Wendigo and
Uktena, leaders are usually appointed by over-chiefs because
they have shown their bravery, resourcefulness and ability to get
the job done. Such leaders are almost immune to challenge
unless they show themselves less than worthy. Red
Talonsusually have clearalphaswhorip the hides from
those so audacious as to challenge their authority.


The Leader May Not Be
Challenged During Wartime

In times of war, working in concert is vitally important.
Wyrm creatures that cannot be defeated by lone Garou
can only be brought down by packs working together. At
these times, no Garou is allowed to disobey or even
question the commands of the leader. Failure to comply
means the difference between victory and defeat, be-
tween the pack's survival and annihilation. If a Garou
disobeys a superior during wartime, he is destroyed as soon
as possible after the battle.

Reality: Most Garou have no argument with this prin-
ciple. Garou who countermand the orders of a leader who
displays gross incompetence, signs of Wyrm-taint or magical
compulsion, may escape punishment for their disobedience,
particularly when their actions save their pack or sept from
destruction. Philodox who pass judgment on these offenders
may overlook their "crimes," but award no Renown for an
action — however heroic or selfless — which violates this
tenet. Some tribes such as the Shadow Lords and Bone
Gnawers take advantage of this tenet, claiming that they are

always in a state of readiness or warfare so their leader-
ship cannot be challenged:

Chapter Two: Setting

Ye Shall Take No Action That
Causes a Caern to be Violated

The survival of the Garou as a species depends on preserv-
ing the inviolability of the caerns that channel Gaia's power
to them. No werewolf in her right mind would think to lead
an enemy to her caern. Even if a shapeshifter accidentally
violates this principle, she still faces harsh punishment for
foolishly endangering one of Gaia's holy places.

Reality: Although this portion of the Litany seems to be inviolate,
many Garou break it consistently — without even thinking about
their actions. As they sweep across the land with their Kinfolk,
European Garou drive their native cousins from the caerns they have
held in trust for centuries. Some invaders, who claim they can better
"protect" the caems, cannot do so, either because they offend the
caems' totem spirits or because they intend to keep moving on, thus
leaving the caerns unguarded. The Iron Riders, with backing by
the transcontinental railroad companies and their dreams of
enlisting the Weaver in the battle against the Wyrm, are among
the most blatant transgressors of lands holding caerns. Whether
this behavior results from concer that caerns be guarded by "those
better suited to it," or comes from some twisted sending of the Urge
Wyrm of Greed, the fact remains that more and more caerns are
being left unguarded as those who dwell there are forced into exile.


Justice

Truthisits handmaid, freedom is its child, peace is its companion,
safety walks in its steps, victory follows in its train...

— Sydney Smith

The Litany provides a basic structure of "laws," and most
Garou adhere to these precepts most of the time. Elders issue
strong warnings to young Garou who break minor rules. More
serious infractions may be subject to the "law of the claw," in
which members of the pack or sept make their displeasure
known or bring offenders before a Philodox for judgment.

Philodox rule on transgressions of every kind, whether
involving the Litany or simple disagreements between two (or
more) Garou. Duels and ritual combat or contests remain the
most popular methods to settle disputes. When serious viola-
tions of the law occur, justice usually follows swiftly.

The administration of punishments for crimes or redress of
grievances differs from tribe to tribe, but bears little resemblance
to the complex legal protocol that humans seem to enjoy. The
warlike Get of Fenris settle most of their disputes through trial by
combat; where crimes against the Litany are involved, these
"combats" pit the accused against most of the warriors of the
tribe, thus insuring that Ga-
rou criminals rarely

survive their trials.

®,%,

Fianna employ combat as well as other challenges to determine
justice. The Bone Gnawersstand less on ceremony and often make
judgments based on expediency. Iron Riders hold court trials that
resemble those of human origin. The Children of Gaia and
Stargazers adhere to the process of mediation. The Silver Fangs'
trials contain many apparently trivial formalities, while the Silent
Striders waste no time in bringing justice to bear. Among the
Uktenaand Wendigo, those accused of wrongdoing are frequently
required to undergo grueling ordeals, either to prove their inno-
cence or to purify themselves. Possibly the most frightening judges
come from the Shadow Lords, who possess some of the most
extreme and fearsome methods of determining guilt and exacting
punishment of any Garou. What's more, a camp of Shadow Lord
Philodox known only as the Judges of Doom wander the Savage
West, bringing law and punishment to offenders of all tribes.
Running away from judgment is seen as proof of guilt, and
Garou elders often send young Garou as bounty hunters
charged with bringing back guilty parties, "dead or alive."
Incentives in the form of fetishes or knowledge (or, in the
case of the Iron Riders, money), encourage some Garou to
take on the roles of bounty hunters as full-time occupations.
These traveling administrators of Gaia
assume mythic proportions, adding to
the growing legends of the

's swift justice often

= "Jone gunslinger."


Organization and
Power Structures

Whoever is foremost, leads the herd.
— Schiller, Wallenstein's Tod

Among the Garou, the importance of hierarchy and social
standing forms an innate part of psychological makeup. Like
their wolf kin, much of their sense of belonging comes with
knowing just where they stand in relation to their packmates,
their septmates and other members of their tribes. Within each
group (whether pack, sept or tribe), a pecking order soon
establishes itself, with leadership going to the Garou who
claims and maintains the position of alpha.

But the tribes often use quite different (and sometimes
savage) methods of choosing their leaders. Some, like the Get
of Fenris, prefer to battle for the position, while others
acknowledge the "divine right" of the pure blood (Silver
Fangs) or honor those of great wisdom and cunning (Uktena).
Most Garou use the system of Renown to judge candidates'
worth, channeling aggression into acceptable directions and
making certain that leaders follow the Litany and possess the
qualities needed to guide groups. Many lesser positions within
a group also exist, allowing underlings more scope for their
talents rather than simply being followers. Because they are
part wolf, most Garou are content to take these lesser roles, for
they are necessary for the continuation of the group and
provide chances for them to win honor for their wisdom,
chivalry, insight and cunning.

Leadership is not static. During wartime, no one is al-
lowed to challenge the leader, but when peace reigns, such
constraints do not exist. Werewolves may challenge and
defeat their leaders in many areas, not just the arena of
battle. When a situation calls for diplomatic acumen, a
Garou who excels in such may successfully challenge the
current Ahroun leader for the right to act as alpha. Later, if
knowledge of the Umbra becomes vital to a sept's survival,
acunning Theurge may step forward and prove herself more
fit to lead than the diplomatic Philodox. In theory, Garou
social structures enable the best individual for any given
task to assume leadership when necessary.

This hierarchy is integral to Garou nature. Few find fault
with it or feel uncomfortable with the idea of Ranks and
dominance. Indeed, the Litany reinforces the idea of respect
toward those of higher station. A few Garou tribes don't
adhere as closely to this stepladder social system. The
Stargazers and Silent Striders care less about hierarchy and
more about individual enlightenment than do most Garou,
while the Iron Riders have adopted the more egalitarian
viewpoints that are gaining popularity in the world at large

and in America in particular. The Bone Gnawers see
hierarchy as a joke; Rank matters little to those at
& the bottom of the heap.

Chapter Two: Setting

Foras longas they have existed, Garou have abided by a simple
tule: Leadership goes to the best in any given situation, and the rest
of the group follows her counsel without question. The arcane
politics practiced by humans, Kindred, mages and other complex
social groups are almost unknown to the Garou (with the excep-
tion of the Shadow Lords, who have always been schemers and
plotters at heart). In general, Garou deal with internal and
external matters in a forthright, instinctual manner.

The clash of cultures between European Garou and their
American cousins, however, forces the Garou head first into the
political arena. As the Fianna, Getof Fenris, Silver Fangsand other
non-native Garou seek to establish their claims to the wild lands
west of the Mississippi, they find themselves in competition not
only with each other, but with the native Uktena and Wendigo.
Astrange phenomenon has also emerged, which pits elders against
their own tribal youth. Curiously, among the European Garou,
while elders push for taking over lands and caerns they need,
claiming their ways are superior and shoving native werewolves
further westand into less productive areas, youngsters question not
only their right to do so, but whether such actions are honorable.

Conversely, among the Pure Ones (especially the Uktena), the
chiefs counsel patience and forbearance, seekingaccord, reconcilia-
tionand treaties with the newcomers while their young hotheads
cause trouble and raid the Europeans as if daring them to do
something about it. Councils among both groups have become
strained affairs in which younglings no longer listen respectfully
to elders, but speak their minds and sometimes disrupt proceed-
ings. When they are reprimanded and punished for their lack of
respect, some acquiesce; others merely become more rebellious,
even if they have learned the value of silence and secrecy.

Whether the Garou can survive this breakdown of their
political affairs remains to be seen. However, in a few places
multitribal packs are forming, sometimes with Pure Ones and
European cubs standing side-by-side. Although some septs
scorn these packs as "impure" or "foolhardy," the fact remains
that the Storm Eater, and indeed the Storm Umbra, cannot
be faced alone by any tribe.

Dominance

Establishing dominance comes as naturally to Garou as to
their wolf cousins. When two Garou of very different Rank or
Renown interact, their respective status is always apparent. Pups
seeking to make a name for themselves might challenge ones of
higher Rank, but the more established Garou is under no
obligation to accept — and is often prohibited from doing so by
custom, since it isa given that the stronger Garou will invariably
win. Such interactions do not call for establishing dominance.
When Garou of roughly equal status both claim dominance,
however, the superior must be decided. Since Garou hierarchy
depends on clear-cut lines between leaders and followers, an elabo-
rate protocol arises whereby disputes over the fitness ofany Garou to
lead are resolved easily. In most cases, one of three major ways of
testing dominance suffices: the facedown, the duel or the game.


The facedown pits the opposing wills of the Garou. More

than a simple staring contest, it involves focusing all of
personal will, self-esteem, charisma and bravado on forcing
an opponent to drop her eyes or look away. So intense are
these contests that some Garou succumb to their inner Rage
and frenzy from the pressure of the moment.

The duel involves combat. This is the preferred method for deciding
whois mostfitfor leadership in times of war, but can alsobe used tosolve
other disputes involvingdominance. The victor assumes the position of
leader. Although duels are not meant to be to the death, accidents do
happen —with more frequency among some tribes than others. Tribes
—and packs — differ in their preference for dueling forms. The Red
Talons prefernatural tooth and claw, while the Fiannaand GetofFenris
oftenemployklaives. The propensity for "showdowns," using pistols, has
recently become popular among some European Garou. These gun.
duels are not as deadly for Garou as for humans, however, since lead
doesn't kill werewolves quite so readily. The Uktena and Wendigo
usually employ physical combar, either in human or Crinos form, while
the Stargazers engage in highly ritualized kailindo matches.

The game servesasatest of intelligence, cunningand subtlety,
and is the preferred form of challenge among the less-aggressive
tribes, such as the Uktena, Iron Riders, Children of Gaia and
Bone Gnawers (although the last enjoy a good scrap as much as
any). In fact, it is not unknown for Iron Riders and Bone Gnawers
to resolve matters of leadership at the poker table, combining
game and facedown in tests of cunning and audacity. The Uktena
enjoy the challenge of a good mental battle and sometimes use
contests of storytelling or dancing to settle matters of leadership.

Once the winner and loser are clear, the loser must show
submission immediately. Exposing the throat or belly, falling to
the ground, urinating and displaying the buttocks are all prime
examples, though the less-wolflike tribes such as the Iron Riders
may content themselves with a verbal "uncle." If this step is not
carried out, the winner is justified in attacking the insolent loser
— usually with several helpers from her outraged pack.

Language

Every language is a temple, in which the soul of those who speak
it is enshrined.

—Oliver Wendell Holmes, The Professor at the Breakfast Table

The Garou speak a unique language, one that evolved before the
emergence of tribal divisions. Although the Fianna claim to have
invented the Garou tongue, the fact that all Garou speak itregardless
of their place of origin indicates otherwise. Young Garou leam their
language soon after their Rites of Passage, thus enabling them to
participate in the social discourse of their tribes and increase their
sense of solidarity with the group. One of the most notable features
of the Garou language is the huge number of metaphors for hearing
and smell, which are every bit as important as the sense of sight.
Whereas all Garou in Hispo or Lupus forms can communicate with
wolves (an innate ability), the Garou language must be learmed.

Likeall languages, however, the werewolf tongue is subject to drift,
and its verbal components usually reflect the accent and usage of the

speaker's ethnic origins. Dialectand local terminology have alsocrept
in, so that two Garou from different tribes or distant locations can
sometimes misunderstand one another. Fortunately, words make up
only one part of the language. Body posture and gesture are as much
a part of the tongue as vocal speech is, bearing witness to the wolf-
nature of the Garou. What's more, in verbal communication, tone
and inflection add nuances to Garou speech, while the single most
importantelement— the one that distinguishes it from other spoken
languages — comes directly from the Garou's wolf-nature: the howl.

Howls

The voice that beautifies the land!

The voice above,

The voice of the thunder,

Among the dark clouds

Again and again it sounds,

The voice that beautifies the land.

— Navaho song

Garou, like wolves, use howls to communicate over great
distances. Whether intended to evoke fear in the hearts of
enemies, summon aid in emergencies, or simply to announce
one's presence in the territory of another, howls condense a large
amount of information into a powerful vocalization. Learning
and mastering the distinctive howls forms the lifelong task of
most Galliards. All Garou learn a few basic howls.

Unlike human music, which generally relies on harmony and
assonance to produce pleasing or awe-inspiring choral symphonies,
the melodies of howls borrow heavily from the dissonance inherent
in the songs of nature: the howl of the wind across the vast prairie,
thecacophony of competing bird calls, the torrential rush of mighty
rivers. Like their wolf cousins, the Garou search for discordant
sounds. Although a single werewolf may initiate a howl, she is often
joined by others. Rather than matching melodies, each individual
alters her pitch to provide a layered texture of controlled noise. In
this way, a single pack can sound like a swarm, intimidating its
enemies into overestimating the size of its opposition.

Some of the better-known howls are listed below.

Call to Hunt — Almost indistinguishable from the howl
of a normal wolf, this prolonged, low-pitched wail tells the
pack what prey is near and where it can be found.

v Chant of Challenge — Part recitation, part insult, this
invention of the Fianna harkens back to the Celtic bardic
tradition. Intended as the opening gambit of a duel, it consists
of a laudatory exposition of the challenger's name, pack, sept,
lineage, tribe and deeds as a prelude to an imaginative slur
against one challenged, impugning everything from her birth
to her personal hygiene to her sexual habits. As their customs
run more to simple statements of their names and positions
within their tribes, some Uktena and Wendigo mistake this
chant for a particularly inept Death Song. When Fianna
challenge one another in this fashion, the chant itself
sometimes becomes the duel, with particularly vi-
cious barbs serving as winning blows.

Werewolf: The Wild West


Cry for Help — This howl, which resembles the mewling
cry of a pup for its mother, summons a werewolf's pack to her
assistance. Many a hunter takes this cry as the despairing final
wail of his prey, only to find, as the pack surrounds him, that it
is something far worse.

‘w Death Song — Most common among the Uktena and
Wendigo, though also occasionally practiced by the Fianna and the
Get of Fenris, this defiant, passionate challenge in the face of certain
death both summarizes the Garou's life and prepares her for her
impending reunion with Gaia. While native werewolves recognize
and respect this howl, many European Garou, upon hearing such for
the first time, are amazed by it. Some respond to the inherentbravery
of thesings
ina few cases, ceasing hostilities and withdrawing in the face of such
nobility. It is said that those who heard the haunting Death Song of
the Croatan were never able to fully dismiss it from their thoughts.

‘¥ Howl of Introduction — This "talkative" howl is used
when entering another's territory to introduce the visitor and
ask permission to enter or to meet with the residents.

‘w Lament for the Fallen
this mournful, low-pitched dirge expresses both sorrow and

either granting her the respect due a superior Garou or,

Most solemn of all the howls,

praise for the honored dead. Like keening, its volume and
duration indicate the status of the deceased.

‘w Shame Song — This harsh, irritating whine is used by a
pack or sept to shame those who have acted dishonorably.

| oe |

W Snarl of Precedence — European Garou use this howl to
initiate one-on-one combat with other Garou of different Rank. A
werewolf with higher Renown does not have to acknowledge this
howl, but few so challenged refuse. Before the present times, such
presumptuouschallenges were almost unknown amongnativeGarou.

‘w Symphony of the Abyss — The Black Spiral Dancers
have perfected this insanely perverse, reverberating whine as
their hunting cry. Its mere sound is often enough to strike fear
into the hearts of their enemies.

w War Cry — Used by both European Garou (under the name
Anthem of War) and native Garou, this inspiring howl not only
serves to gather troops for battle but elevates faltering morale. The
Wendigo are masters of a particularly chilling form of this howl.

te Wyrm Warning — Used only toalert Garou within hearing
distance of the proximity of Wyrm-creatures, this high-pitched,
staccato series of howls is unmistakable. No werewolf, regardless
of upbringing, fails to recognize this as the cry of danger.

Iv is no surprise that Garou, being cousins
to wolves, gather in packs as a matter of
~ instinct. The most basic and closely knit
communal units among Garou, packs usu-
ally have between two and 10 members who
have a common purpose and are acknowl-
edged by atotemspirit. Packscan be transitory,
existing only for the length of time it takes to
accomplish a finite goal, or they can be bind-
ing for the life of the members. A pack may
form for a specific goal such as hunting down a
particular supernatural gang, or for a more
general purpose, such as seeking out and fight-
ingasmany of the Wyrm'screaturesas possible.
A pack is more than a group of like-
minded individuals. Greater than mere
friendship, the bonds of the pack become
almost unbreakable, with individuals as-
suming their places within the group so
seamlessly that they all appear to share a
group mind. While rivalry and even dislike
may occur among individual members
and challenges may be frequent, it is
almost unheard of for a pack member
nottodefend his packmates tothe death.
When the pack first comes together,
sept elders perform a great rite that binds
a special spirit, known as the totem
spirit, to the pack. The totem acts as a
guide, an Umbral messenger and guard-
ian to the pack. If the pack breaks up, the
totem spirit is released from its duties.

4 Lin
Justas humans trace theirancestry through
family trees, werewolves keep track of their
own familial lines. Among some tribes, such as
the Silver Fangs, Fianna and Get of Fenris, lin-
eage is an important issue. Tribe members expect
much from a Garou who is a descendent from a
hero, and indeed, blood seems to run truer in Garou
than in most other species. Tribes like the Iron
Riders and Bone Gnawers care less about ancestry
than about individual merit and cunning, but even those
who downplay the importance of lineage still keep track of
their famous (or, in some cases, infamous) relatives.

Since elder Garou often monitor young members of their
bloodlines, chastising them when they deviate from traditional
expectations, many Garou venture west to escape the burden so
imposed on them — or in hopes of shedding the bad reputation
attached to them asa result of their lineage. In the West, actions

speak louder than words or ancestry. *

The Sept

Garou who elect to reside in the vicinity of a caern and act as
its protectors forma unique group called a sept. Most septs consist
of a single tribe, though occasionally Garou from more than one
tribe will join forces to share the duties of protectingacaern. This
is the case in areas where there are not enough members of any
one tribe to fulfill this purpose. The Black Furies, Silent Striders
and Stargazers form such a small percentage of Garou population
in the American West that these Garou find it easier to affiliate
themselves with already existing septs.

Before the westward expansion, most septs west of the Missis-
sippi consisted of Uktena or Wendigo, with almost no intertribal
mixing. As Americans seeking their "Manifest Destiny" drive
the Native Americans of the East and their Uktena allies west,
forcing them into lands already claimed by other native Garou,
competition for existing caerns triggers skirmishes and occasion-
ally, outright war. Rather than surrender or violate the sanctity
their caerns, many native Garou settle their disputes by accept-
ing native newcomers into their septs.

As European Garou settle in the wide-open spaces of the
West, septs of mixed tribes increase in number as they find that
one or two packsare unable to adequately defend newly acquired
caerns — especially if their former protectors want them back.

The Tribe

The primary divisions among Garou are equivalent to ethnic
background divisions among humans. A tribe consists of Garou
who share a common lineage and the traits that go with them.
Just as a human's nation often determines cultural outlook,
specific customs and physical appearance, a Garou's tribe fre-
quently denotesacertain attitude, philosophical bentand physical
characteristics. Although a Garou's Auspice determines her
general role in her larger society, tribal perceptions tend to affect
the way in which that Auspice manifests itself. An Uktena
Ragabash, for example, might see himself as a follower of Coyote
the Trickster or might join the Society of Sacred Fools (or
contraries), while his Iron Rider counterpart might gain a
reputation as a conniving confidence man or cardsharp.

The 13 Tribes

By 1830, 13 distinct tribes of Garou inhabit the North Ameri-
can continent, and are often at war with each other. A werewolf's
tribe can easily be a point of contention in the Savage West,
particularly when crossing lands that belong to other tribes.

‘w Black Furies — As passionate as its name suggests, this
tribe is predominantly made up of female werewolves who have
dedicated themselves to preserving the Wyld. Originating in
Greece, the Black Furies' fierceness and intensity in the time
of the Impergium gave rise to legends of bloody sacrificial and
punitive rituals among the humans of the Mediterranean.

Although their Kinfolk were relatively late arrivals to
the Pure Lands, a few of these zealous warriors joined
* the early Spanish expeditions to the New World,

Chapter Two: Setting

attaching themselves to the armies of conquest long enough to
set foot on the untarnished ground that would later become
California and Mexico.

Their ideas of independence and equality find fertile soil in the
West, where the general hardships of day-to-day life test the grip of
male dominance among white settlers. Many of them claim places
along side the Uktena and Wendigo, where their warrior spirits and
hatred of anything that hints of the Wyrm orthe Weaver gains them
respect and glory. Others attach themselves to small settlements of
American pioneers, where their inner strength helps bolster the
fading courage of women who accompany their husbands into
strange new lands. Very few reside in the growing cities and boom
towns of California and Texas, where women are too often consid-
ered expendable commodities by boorish trappers, prospectors and
expeditionary forces. The Black Furies become dual champions of
Gaia and womankind wherever they plant themselves.

‘w Bone Gnawers — The lowest of the low, the Bone
Gnawers exemplify the adaptability of nature and the need to
survive whatever the cost. For years, the American continent
serves as a dumping ground for Europe's poor, including con-
victs and debtors. Among these down-and-out individuals are
the first Bone Gnawers to set foot in the Pure Lands,

Their hardiness makes them ideal wilderness survivors as
well as scrappers who claim rough new settlements as their
homes. Nominally serving Gaia, the Bone Gnawers thrive in
the harsh environs of the Savage West. Here they claw out
niches for themselves among solitary prospectors, digging for
gold (or searching for signs of the Wyrm) or living apart like
rag-tag mountain men and trappers who come into town once
a year for supplies. The Bone Gnawers represent tough but
disreputable "low heroes," able to survive anywhere. In the
growing cities, they dwell among the dissolute and fallen, eking
out their existence on the fringes of human and Garou society.

Children of Gaia — These Garou embody the hope that
all of Gaia's children can learn to live peacefully. When they
first encountered the Croatan and Uktena along the eastern
seaboard, the Children were heartened to find that coopera-
tion rather than conquest was possible.

The Children of Gaia encourage the unity of all Garou tribes.
They foster education of the European people under their protection
and seek to reach out to the Uktena and Wendigo. Seeing a chance
to promote Gaia's cause, they help establish many alternative
religious communities, some of which are deist rather than Christian.
Hoping toeradicate the wounds inflicted on the Pure Ones, theysend
missionaries, teachers, chroniclers and artists to the tribes and the
growing urban areas of the West. Despite their good intentions, most
believe thatthe Garou of the Pure Lands have grown weak from their
longseparation andare no longer fit protectors of Gaia'ssacred places.
These well-meaning emissaries thereby come to occupy a singular
place in the European versus Pure Lands Garou squabbles — their
obvious attempts to help the native Garou make them the most
trusted of any Europeans, while their assumption that they know best
and can teach their backward brethren how to behave properly often
ears them great hatred and resentment from the proud natives.

‘w Fianna— One of the worst offenders of the "invading Europeans," this aggressive and
fearless tribe forces its way into the heart of the West, riding alongside farmers desperate
for land, railroad navvies, gold and silver miners, cavalry officers, Indian fighters and
cowboys. Pioneering the move west with their Scotch-Irish Kinfolk, the
Fianna help blaze the trail to the Pacifi
the lands in between. Just as they are among the first to help
extend European influence beyond the Appalachians,
they spearhead the mandate of "Manifest Destiny."
Staunch defenders of Gaia and bold warriors
against the Wyrm, the Fiannaconsider them-
selves superior to the native Garou and
soon make enemies of the local Uktena
and Wendigo tribes. On the other hand, some Fianna "go native,"
realizing that they ha
the Pure Lands alone for centuries.

te Get of Fenris — Savage fighters with proud traditions,
children of the harsh northern lands near the Arctic, veterans of
centuries of fierce battles against the Wyrm, the Get of Fenris
tolerate no challenges to their right to fertile western lands from
neither human nor Garou. Their unpolished manners, assumption
of their own superiority and rough-and-tumble attitudes win them
few friends among the Uktena or Wendigo, but their undeni-
ablesense ofhonor, consummate bravery and ferventdevotion
toprotecting whatever they claimas their own assures them
grudging respect. Staunchly loyal to and protective of
their Kinfolk, who are mainly homesteaders, farmers
and artisans who claim plains and prairies, the Get of
Fenris exemplify the grim determination of Gaia's
proudest warriors. The Wendigo see something of
themselves in these great wolves of the North, but
would rather die than admit it.
*# Iron Riders — Believing that the powers of the
Weaver should be enlisted in the battle against the
Wyrm, these Garou maintain a constant presence in
human cities, learning to use available technology to
further Gaiz
with their Kinfolk to the growing cities of the West,
insinuating themselves intonewly established banks,
gamblinghallsandcommerce. The Ridersare staunch
supporters of the railroad and telegraph networks.
They believe that these tools can eventually con-

ause. Naturally, they gravitate along

nect caerns all over the continent. Iron Riders are
found among city boys and schoolmarms, dance-
hall owners and railroad tycoons, shady
businessmen and snake oil salesmen. The
least trusted of all the Garou, they neverthe-
less take root in the new frontier.
‘w Red Talons — The exclusively lupus Red Talons are a sizable force in
the Savage West. Fleeing the gradual diminishment of European
= __ wolfstock, who had been hunted almost to extinction insome areas,
the Red Talons endure the change to homid form long enough to
arrive in America and begin breeding with the native
wolf population. Lack of enough breeding stock
along the eastern seaboard forces them west.


Native Americans consider wolves brethren and treat
them with respect for their place in the natural order. The Red
Talons therefore temper — a little — their naturally tendencies
with respect to the indigenous people of the western lands. Ironi-
cally, they begin taking over the lupus strains of the Uktena and
Wendigo, weakening those influences in the native Garou tribes. Ameri-
can Red Talons thus take on more of the traits of the red and gray wolves of
the Pure Lands, becoming virtual separate strain from their European cousins.
With the coming of more Europeans, white settlers hunt wolves, decimate the
buffalo and other wild game, and set themselves up as exclusive predators.
The Red Talons' anger escalates, driving them to attempt overtures of
alliance and peace with the Uktena and Wendigo in order to fight the
encroaching claims of foreign Garou. Still plentiful in the early part of
the 1800s, their numbers steadily decrease as their Kinfolk succumb to
the guns and snares of bounty hunters and trappers.

tw Shadow Lords — Traveling to the New World with their
Eastern European Kinfolk, the Shadow Lords quickly move to take
over key political positions and insinuate themselves into estab-
lished septs in the East. Among the first to recognize what the
opening of the West truly means, these cunning Garou manipu-
late their Kinfolk into making the westward exodus.
Fighting and tricking their way across the breadth of
the continent, they maneuver to take their "right-
ful" place as movers and shakers of both human
and Garou society. Many Shadow Lords become
virtual monarchs of the towns their money helps
to finance and build, controlling the general
stores, banks (wherever possible), assayer's offices,
grange and cattlemen's associations and stables.
Often a single Shadow Lord serves as mayor,
sheriff, circuit judge and postmaster. Less-
settled Shadow Lords move into higher
country where Grandfather Thunder holds
court, displacing the native Garou — killing
them when necessary. Others become gun-
slingers, moving along the fringes of
society, feared for theirnarrow-eyedmen-
ace and fast hands. No one crosses a
Shadow Lord more than once; not even
those who annoy them live to tell the tale.

Silent Striders — The enigmatic Silent
Striders first arrive in the West alongside travel-
ing medicine shows or as solitary travelers.
Although the panoramic vistas of Monument
Valley, the Painted Desert and the Rocky
Mountains win their admiration, their real
attention is reserved for the unexplored
Umbral landscape of the Pure Lands. Search- >
ing for the most isolated places, these exotic seekers
of undiscovered knowledge explore the chaotic
depths of the Storm Umbra, a place even "wilder"

than its counterpart in the physical world. <ao

They are among the first non-native Garou to discover the
= growing menace arising from the breaking of Uktena and Wendligo
