wards against the Wyrm. Unlike the Children of Gaia, when these
solitary Garou make contact with the natives, they come to learn,
not to teach. They often act as interpreters between Europeans
and various native tribes, or make laconic attempts to reconcile
native Garou with their European cousins, but their overtures are
rarely effective. Silent Striders occasionally search for abandoned
fetishes. A few become circuit riders, either to bring "the word" to
the benighted folk of small towns along their paths or to carry
justice to places that have no law.

‘ Silver Fangs — Revered as legendary heroes, the Silver
Fangs form an elite aristocracy among the Garou, yet their
nobility and fitness to bear the standard in the battle against the
Wyrm face challenges from within and without. Other tribes,
notably the Shadow Lords and the Get of Fenris, feel that the
Silver Fangs should share the glory of being Gaia's "chosen ones."
Extreme selectivity in breeding hasalso leftits mark, asthe highly
inbred Silver Fangs sometime manifest peculiarities of personal-
ity. Along with the Fianna and Get of Fenris, the Silver Fangs
(though in smaller numbers) are among the early trailblazers in
the West. After the Civil War, however, an even greater influx
of these aristocrats makes its way into the new wilderness,
bringing Southern families, uprooted from their privileged plan-
tations. Although Silver Fangs swell the ranks of Union and
Confederate soldiers looking for new wars to fight, others come
as bearers of culture, attempting such unlikely feats as educating
the roughshod residents of western cities to the delights of the
opera and the ballet. Many come west with noble intentions,
attempting to bring their isolated Uktena and Wendigo brethren
into "civilized" Garou society. Needless to say, their presence
only exacerbates tensions between native and newcomer Garou.

‘w Stargazers — These mystic wanderers hail from the
Orient. Having watched the philosophies of the Far East de-
velop, they possess unique martial skills that make them at once
fierce warriors and gentle peacemakers. A few arrive with their
Asian Kinfolk brought to work on the railroads. These Stargazers
attune themselves to the awesome splendor of the western
landscape. Mysterious and intriguing because of their rarity,
some few gain acceptance among the Uktena and Wendigo —
chiefly because they lack the arrogant attitudes so prevalent
among European werewolves. Of the few Stargazers to come to
the Savage West, most become explorers of desert wastelands,
high mountains and deep evergreen forests, reveling in both the
open barrenness and enclosed wilderness. They are almost never
seen or encountered. Some others, who remain in cities such as
San Francisco, bring the seeds of alternative, herbal medicine,
astrological forecasting and mystic spiritualism that shape future
perceptions of the "strange and innovative" West Coast cities.
Rarely seen by any but fellow Asians, these mysterious wonder-
workers aren't even known as Garou by their patients.

w Uktena— One of the two remaining Garou tribes native
to the Pure Lands, the Uktena embody the fires that burn within
Gaia's heart, concealing her deepest mysteries. Great seekers of
knowledge, the Uktena concern themselves with uncovering
the secrets of the Umbra, particularly those belonging to the

Werewolf: The Wild West

Wyrm. They labor in the Pure Lands to identify and destroy or
bind creatures of the Wyrm, setting up an elaborate network of
protective wards throughout the Umbral West.

These mystics and seers make their homes among the pueblo
tribes such as the Zuni and Hopi, as well as the Utes, Navaho and
other peoples of the Southwest and California. Exposed to
Spanish conquistadors and missionaries who established out-
posts in California and Mexico, Uktena regard the increasing
number of European werewolves and settlers with uneasy suspi-
cion. The ranks of the Uktena grow with the arrival of tribemates
from the East and Southeast in the 1830s, driven westward by a
government determined to claim their lands. Many displaced
natives are forced into the territories of the fierce Plains tribes
and their Wendigo protectors, sparking violent confrontations
over new territories, hunting rights and protectorates. Initially
unwilling to resort to warfare against the Europeans, the Uktena
soon realize the futility of treaties and truces. They also recognize
the coming of the Storm Eater as the release of a great Bane their
ancestors bound long ago, and they heartily resent those who
have unwittingly loosed its chains. Their hidden fires are being
fueled, and are ready to explode into a great conflagration.

te Wendigo — The Wendigo reflect both the icy fury of their
totem spirit and the hot passion of the tortuous and exhilarating
Sun Dance practiced by their native Kinfolk. Making their
homes among the Native Americans of the Northwest and the
wandering tribes of the Great Plains, these warriors resist Euro-
pean encroachment almost from the first. Although their initial
encounters with French and Russian trappers and explorers
weren't always hostile, the Wendigo were quick to perceive the
threat that the white-skinned invaders pose to their lands and
their Kinfolk. Some of the fiercest battles against white expan-
sion take place in the lands of Geronimo and Crazy Horse. At
once noble and furious, bloody-minded to their enemies and
surprisingly compassionate to those Europeans willing to forsake
their corrupt ways and adopt native ideals, the Wendigo, perhaps
more than any other Garou, represent the doomed struggle to
preserve the pristine glory of the Pure Lands.

The Lost Tribes

Long ago the Garou had more than 13 tribes, but battles with
the Wyrm caused the disappearance of entire bloodlines. Three
of the greatest tribes either traveled the road to extinction or
stand on the verge of annihilation.

White Howlers

The proud tribe of White Howlers that once served as Gaia's
fiercest guardian in the highlands of Scotland and northern
England now exists only in the form of its twisted descendants,
the Black Spiral Dancers. Legends tell how the White Howlers
faced the Wyrm in its own territory, and emerged from the foul
pit that was their last battleground as warped versions of what
they once were. Although all Garou know of the
White Howlers, few speak of their lost cousins lest
they bring their curse upon themselves. +

SSSSSSS3

E

Croatan

One of the three original Garou tribes to settle in the Pure
Lands, the Croatan flourished along the eastern seaboard.
When the Wyrm-spirit known as Eater-of-Souls arrived in
North America in the company of the Roanoke colonists, the
Croatan sacrificed themselves and all their Kinfolk in a great
tite that banished the creature from the physical world. Their
sacrifice serves as both inspiration and warning to all Garou.
Their Uktena and Wendigo sisters and brothers continue to
mourn their passing, and fear that the ongoing encroachment
of Wyrm-tainted humans into the once-Pure Lands bodes
similar disaster for them.

Bunyip

The Bunyip are neverseen outside their native Australia, tied
as they are to their Kinfolk, the Tasmanian wolves. As more
Europeans (and their Garou protectors) establish their hold in
the Land Down Under, the Bunyip numbers diminish as their
lands are taken from them and their Kinfolk are hunted to
extinction. Before the end of the 19th century, they will vanish
forever, as was the fate of the Croatan and White Howlers. Their
tragedy, however, is that unlike the Howlers, who fell to the
Wyrm, or the Croatans, who willingly sacrificed themselves, the
Bunyip's extermination comes at the hands of other Garou.

Lone Wolves

Although they are social creatures by nature, werewolves
have their misfits as well — individuals who scorn or are
scorned by their tribes. Variously called Lone Wolves, Outlaws,
Renegades, Ronin or Outcasts, these solitary Garou walk their
own path on the fringes of Garou society or else turn their backs
on it altogether. Lone Wolves possess no official Rank among
Garou, although they may acquire Gifts from mentors (if they
can find any and convince them to share their knowledge).
Most Garou sneer at Lone Wolves, considering them useless
deviants who serve no real purpose in the war against the
Wyrm. In fact, some Lone Wolves do fall under the influence
of the Wyrm or else succumb to madness.

Few Lone Wolves originate from the Uktena or Wendigo
prior to the Europeans' arrival, but as the century progresses,
Lone Wolves become more common. Many whose views are too
extreme (either advocating unstinting violence or appearing as
toadying weaklings addicted to the Europeans' fancy goods and
liquor) are cast out. Further, a number of otherwise blameless
Garou find themselves alone when their septmates fall to the
claws of the invaders who steal their caerns, or to supernatural
sickness, starvation or other ills brought by the European settlers.

On the other hand, a significant number of European Garou
who travel west fit into this category. Like many human pioneers,
these Lone Wolves deliberately set out to get away from societal

constraints — either because they have been banished from
their tribes or because they simply desire to go it alone.
Many of them ride the range as gunfighters, bounty

& hunters, scouts or solitary prospectors.

Chapter Two: Setting

Kinfolk

Garou need to mate with humans or wolves in order to
perpetuate their species. Only one in 10such unionsresults in the
birth of a true Garou. The majority of children born with one
werewolf parent are indistinguishable from normal humans or
wolves except for the fact that the Delirium does not affect them.
This immunity gives them the ability to interact with their
Garou relatives, serving them as allies, companions, friends and
liaisons. These are the Kinfolk of the Garou.

Kinfolk form the vital connections between werewolf society and
the outside world. Some tribes rely on their kin more heavily than
others do. The Iron Riders, Shadow Lords and Bone Gnawers, in
particular, make use of their Kinfolk as go-betweens in their political,
business and underworld dealings. Attitudes toward Kinfolk differ
from tribe to tribe. While Shadow Lords may hold themselves distant
from theirhuman orwolfkin, seeing themasnecessary forbreedingbut
too frail for more important tasks, the Fianna and Children of Gaia
enjoy close relationships with their extended families, includingthem
in many aspects of their lives. The Silver Fangs point with pride to the
aristocratic blood from which they come, while the Bone Gnawers
take a similar (though reversed) satisfaction in mating with those who
are otherwise considered outcast, unwanted or outlawed. Among the
Uktena, Kinfolk are valued and respected for their wise counsel and
cherished as little brothers and sisters. The Wendigo realize that both
humans and wolves are predators by nature and encourage their
Kinfolk to develop as hunters and warriors.

Although they hail from the same stock as Garou, Kinfolk
do not share in the abilities unique to Garou. Tales exist of rare
exceptions in which extraordinary Kinfolk display evidence of
Rage or Gnosis. Indeed, some Kinfolk are capable of becoming
mages, shamans or psychics, although the Garou know little
about these prodigies.

Moots

A blend of socializing, politicking and religious observances,
moots are gatherings in which Garou bond, discuss business,
honor heroes, revile breakers of the Litany and recharge the caern
with their passions and Gnosis. Used to reaffirm their loyalty and
reverence for Gaia, moots are always held at caerns, most often
during the full moon. Werewolves summon spirits and open
Moon Bridges during some moots, inviting visitors from other
caerns. Songs, dances, tales and feasts play large roles in moots.

Types of Moots

Moots may vary by size, purpose and attendance. Each tribe
has its own customs, making each tribal moot somewhat
different than any other tribe's. The following are the most
common sorts of moots.

4@ Sept Moot: Most common of the different moots, sept
mootsare usually held oncea month at the caern of a particular
sept. Though outsiders may attend, they are sometimes viewed
with suspicion. Sept moots recharge the caern.


‘@ Grand Moot: Grand moots are reserved for discussing
weighty issues that affect a whole tribe. All tribe members near
enough to attend are expected to do so.

‘@ Concolation: Concolations are the largest and rarest type
of moot. Called by five elder Garou from five different tribes
during regular moots, concolations are scheduled to occur three
months after being called. Word is sent out and all Garou,
regardless of pack, sept or tribe, are expected to attend.
Concolations are only called to address the most critical matters,
ones that may affect all Garou — such as the building threat of
the Storm Eater.

Revel

When the business of the moot is concluded, Garou cel-
ebrate by changing to Crinos form and rampaging out into the
night to clear away any threats in the area. The activities are so
frenetic that some elderly Garou have died while attempting to
keep pace with youngsters. Revels sometimes send Garou into
frenzy, making them a danger to everything in their path. Once
the revel begins, it continues until dawn or until all are too
exhausted to go on.

Garou Cosmology

Let your soul stand cool and composed before a million universes.

— Walt Whitman, "Song of Myself"

Ac the heart of Garou beliefs is the concept of the Triat, or
the three-fold being of Wyld, Weaver and Wyrm from which
all things come and to which all things go. The Wyld repre-
sents unbridled creation — the formless, raw, ebullient
manifestation of chaos. The Weaver stands for order and
symmetry — the principle that gives substance to shadow and
imposes form and shape on that which has none. The Wyrm
embodies inevitable decay — the destruction of existing forms
to make way for the coming of new ones.

Once, these three elements existed in a harmonious dynamic,
forming a continuous cycle in which chaos gave way to pattern,
which in turn decayed into chaos once again. Noone knows what
brought about a change in this relationship, but change it did,
and disharmony, evil and corruption entered the mix, transform-
ing the world forever. Some lorekeepers among the Garou claim
that madness seized the Weaver and that, in succumbing to the
compulsion to spin a pattern around all of creation, she trapped
the Wyrm in one of her webs. Injured in its escape, the Wyrm, in
turn, fell prey to an insane desire to destroy everything and to
corrupt the stuff of the Wyld so that nothing could ever be made
again. Others maintain that the seeds of perversity lay dormant
within the Wyrm from the beginning, only awaiting a chance to
grow, thus infecting the Weaver through her creations.

The Wyrm stands as the foremost opponent of everything
the Garou have swom to protect. Humanity, as perfect tools for
the Weaver, has given it unfair advantage over the Wyld, and
so the Garou dedicate themselves to limiting the Weaver's

58 Werewolf: The Wild West

manifestations. As protectors of the Wyld, the Garou are
poised as buffers against two fearsome and powerful antago-
nists, destined to fight what seems to be a losing battle.

Beneath the Triat, there exist beings known as Celestines,
potent godlike spirits such as Luna, Helios and Gaia. Certain
septs devote themselves to one of these powerful beings,
though all Garou, as Earthly creatures, are the children of Gaia.

Under these are the Incarna or demigods, who are given
reverence, but are rarely worshipped. Tribal totems such as
Grandfather Thunder and Unicorn are Incarna. Lesser spirits
also exist, known as Jagglings or Gafflings. Elementals belong
to this category as well. Wyrm-corrupted spirits are known as
Banes (see the section on the Wyrm).

Umbral War

The Umbra that surrounds the Pure Lands resonates with
the chaotic confusion of a spirit battleground, echoing (or
perhaps causing) the violent clashes that arise as the Weaver
takes that part of the spirit world by storm. Where the Pure
Lands once found their most pristine reflection in the sur-
rounding Umbra, now (unlike other parts of the Umbra)
Luna's light shines only dimly, obscured by oddly-tinted
clouds in the overcast sky. Wyld spirits range freely in this
turbulent realm, but where once the Wyld held complete
sway, it now finds itself pressed by the newly arrived forces of
Weaver and Wyrm.

Although the native human populations of the West em-
ploy the tools of the Weaver in crafting their societies, they
have not completely given themselves over to the idea of
constant "making." Thus, Weaver-spirits exist in relatively
small numbers, but pose little challenge to the Wyld-spirits,
which consider this part of the Umbra their playground. The
vast network of Uktena and, to some extent, Wendigo caerns
holds in check the destructive spirits spawned by the Wyrm. In
particular, one great, bloated Wyrm-spirit, a gluttonous Bane
who sated his hunger on the spirit energies of the Wyld, was
bound beneath the earth by achain of potent caerns. Prevented
from loosing their powers of corruption upon either the physi-
cal or Umbral realms, Wyrm-spirits managed only occasional
forays — until the arrival of Europeans.

With the coming of Europeans and their Garou allies, both
Wyrm and Weaver receive sudden and overpowering rein-
forcements. Wyld spirits everywhere react with outraged panic,
and their emergency is reflected across the Gauntlet. In addi-
tion, as European Garou claim Uktena and Wendigo caerns,
they unknowingly disrupt the bindings that hold the Wyrm's
minions in check, allowing the foul beings to assume an active
role in both the Umbra and the physical world. Eventually,
they release the great Bane — the Storm Eater. This horrid
manifestation of the Wyrm exudes its poisonous presence
throughout the remainder of the century, combining its

essence with the Weaver's newly gained powerful

energies to befoul the lands once called pure.


Brother, I would tell you, in a most soft, loving and friendly
manner, to go back over the mountain and stay there.

— Chief Beaver (Delaware)

Often thought of as one large area of land, the West
actually consists of several distinct topographical re-
gions. Between 1830 and 1890, most of this vast expanse
of mountains, deserts, plains and prairies becomes in-
corporated into the United States
eventually, as states. The Garou influence in these areas
changes throughout the 19th century as the influx of
immigrant Garou tribes displaces the native Uktena and
Wendigo, mimicking the patterns of the human settlers
and native humans.

The Great Plains 4 | |

territories and,

The vast, open prairies that stretch west from the
Mississippi to the Rockies hold little initial attrac- A
tion for settlers drawn by the promise of fertile land (4
or instant wealth in California and Oregon. Com- 9, 4
prising most of the land included in the Louisiana
Purchase, this region includes Missouri and what will
eventually become the states of Arkansas, lowa, Minne-
sota, North and South Dakota, Nebraska and Kansas. The
Wendigo, in the northern plains, and the Uktena, farther
south, gradually find their lands "claimed," first by the
Fianna and Get of Fenris, who accompany their homestead-
ing Irish and German Kinfolk, and later by other European
tribes such as the Silver Fangs and Iron Riders, who move
west in greater numbers after the Civil War.

Rockies

The towering peaks of the Rocky Mountains form a formi-
dable barrier to western expansion. Until the coming of the
transcontinental railroad, they limit travel through passes to
certain times of the year and discourage many hopeful settlers.
Wendigo comprise the bulk of native Garou in the land that will
become the states of Idaho, Montana, Wyoming and Colorado,
although a few Uktena claim lands in the southern Rockies.

European Garou arrived in the region as early as the 18th
century, when French and English trappers and soldiers
established trading posts and forts to secure the valuable

furs in the region. Later, th
these same outposts be-
come stopping places
(and, occasionally,
settlements)
for westward-
bound settlers.

ZLELG

Great Salt Desert
and Surroundings

The region that will become Utah and Nevada consists of
mountains, desert, sagebrush and the Great Salt Lake Valley. This
land is stark and beautiful, but forbidding to all but the hardiest of
people — both native and newcomer. This region is the crossroads
for both Wendigo and Uktena; a few European Garou settle here
until the middle of the century. The discovery of silver in the
Comstock Lode and the coming of the railroad bring the Bone
Gnawers, Iron Riders, Silent Striders and Shadow Lords, along
with some Children of Gaia who accompany the Mormons in their
search for a place to call their own.

Southwest

The home of the Grand Canyon, the Painted Desert and
other spectacular geological formations, the hot and dry lands
that will become Arizona and New Mexico are among the first
regions that European newcomers encounter. As early as the
1600s, the Uktena, along with their human Kinfolk among the
pueblo tribes, struggled against Spanish attempts to establish

connections between their settlements in Mexicoandalong

the California coast. Although only a few European
Garou accompanied the Spanish, other tribes soon
follow as American claims to those lands

intensify. By
the

time of the Mexican-American War in 1846, the Fianna and
Silver Fangs, along with most of the other European tribes,
constitute a significant presence in this hotly contested region.

Indian Territory

The panhandle-shaped region north of Texas stands as a
holding-ground for
their homes in the eastern United States. Here, uprooted Uktena
compete for land against their native cousins. Throughout the
19th century, the eastern part of what will eventually become the
state of Oklahoma retains the status of "Indian Territory."

Texas

Part of Mexico until its rocky road to independence and
eventual assimilation into the United States, Texas and its
sprawling, varied landscape represents one of the earliest
meeting places for native and European Garou. Here the
Uktena, already beleaguered by their dealings with the Span-
ish, find their territory claimed by incoming Fianna and Get of
Fenris, who arrive with their Kinfolk.
California

By 1830, most of the Uktena have already seen the decima-
tion of their Kinfolk due to the depredations of the Spanish. The
fertile valleys and redwood forests of California,

the objects of early westward expansion,
attract European Garou. Nearly all the
tribes are represented along the Pa-
cific Coast, although the cities of
San Francisco and Los Ange-
les are the special province

of the Iron Riders,

Shadow Lords and

Bone Gnawers.

ative Americans driven westward from


Pacific Northwest

The short-lived but lucrative fur trade brings French, English
and Russian trappers to the dense forests of the Oregon Territory,
and early settlers traverse the Oregon Trail in search of new homes
far from the crowded eastern seaboard. Along with themcome the
Garou— Fianna, Silver Fangs, Bone Gnawers and Red Talons —
all eager to claim part of this wilderness for themselves. The native
Wendigo, jealous of both their human and wolf Kinfolk, react to
the presence of strangers with wariness and, often, hostility.

The Wyrm

Lam the pebble in your very own eye

Iam the sword and your enemy dies.

Tam the storm and the hurricane wind

I am the thom of unkind friend

Tam desire what color my eyes?

1 am Loki wizard of lies

Catch me, find me, see me if you can

Tam the guilt of an honest man

— Incredible String Band, "Creation"

Personification of corruption and decay, god-demon and
spawner of monstrosities, the Wyrm haunts the Garou. They hate
and fear it like no other enemy. Though they have battled its
minions since the dawn of time, no werewolf (except some of the
Black Spiral Dancers) has directly interacted with it. Its destruc-
tive manifestations are caused by Banes, fomori and other Wyrm
servants, though all are apparently guided by the malevolent
entity's will. Eater-of-Souls, who devoured all in its path and who
was driven from the shores of the Pure Lands by the Croatan's
sacrifice, is rumored to be one of several aspects of the Wyrm itself.
Regardless of what aspect it assumes, the Wyrm spews forth
violence, destruction, corruption and horror wherever it goes.

Banes

Though the Wyrm has mortal agents, corrupt and evil
spirits called Banes also act as its servants. Some Garou believe
Banes are smaller parts of the Wyrm itself, spun off as Avatars
of different aspects of that dread creature. Most think these
horrid spirits possess their own intelligence and therefore work
for the Wyrm rather than as an integral part of it.

Almost unlimited in their variety, Banes personify negative
and malevolent forces. Banes of hatred, terror, corruption, pollu-
tion and disease are all known, as are myriad other types. Banes
are most often encountered in the Umbra, which is their home,
and these foul spirits have knowledge of spirit Charms that they
use to corrupt those they meet. Some possess Garou and humans,
corrupting them from within as they urge their victims to commit
horrible acts of destruction and evil. When they manifest in the

physical world, they assume horrific forms to disgust and
sicken their opponents.

Chapter Two: Setting

But see, amid the mimic rout

A crawling shape intrude!

A blood-red thing that writhes from out

The scenic solitude!

It writhes! — it writhes! — with mortal pangs

The mimes become its food,

And seraphs sob at vermin fangs

In human gore imbued.

— Edgar Allan Poe, "The Conqueror Worm"

Places similar to caerns exist — tunneled deep beneath the
earth, like poison mines — that generate power and exalt the
fetid corruption of the Wyrm. In these festering cesspools,
foul toxins and destructive energies accumulate and spread
corrosive poisons, even into the corresponding Umbra. Min-
ions of the Wyrm congregate here, like vultures drawn to an
eternally decaying corpse, feeding on the luminescence of the
unearthly fires that burn with a sickly, green glow. These are
the unholy temples where the Wyrm's agents, human and
otherwise, conduct unspeakable rites and garner power for
their vile schemes. The original Black Spiral Dancers arose
from one of these ancient sites of evil and their descendants
still seek out such places.

In those parts of the world where the Wyrm has been able
to gain a secure foothold through the pollution caused by
unchecked industrialization and by the wanton destruction of
nature, such as in Europe and the American Northeast, Wyrm
caerns sometimes encroach upon the surface of the Earth.
These exposed places did not exist in the Pure Lands until the
19th century. The arrival of settlers in the West, however,
provides the Wyrm with just the opportunity it has been
seeking. Where merciless wars between indigenous and new-
comer Garou result in the abandonment or destruction of
caerns, the minions of the Wyrm seek to befoul these once-
sacred spots, warping them to their master's service. Formerly
pristine glades, waterfalls, canyons and forests soon become
festering morasses where normal people fear to go and where
Garou sense the Wyrm-taint that saturates the environs.
Where the armies of progress carve away great chunks of
mountain to make way for their roadway of iron rails or dig
mines deep into the bosom of the Earth in search of mineral
wealth, they unlock some of the heretofore hidden places of
the Wyrm, freeing the trapped poisons within and hastening
the forces of destruction and spiritual decay.

People who dwell near these infested areas either fall
prey to the unseen horrors spawned in them or else suc-
cumb to an inner sickness that twists them into despicable
parodies of human beings. The Wyrm wallows in its ability
to warp that which is pure and clean into something
utterly filthy and depraved, almost as much as it craves
sheer destruction.

wee AN

=
Wyrm Corruption

It begins with something small — assistance in a time of
need, a snippet of vital information or secret knowledge, a
sudden chance to turn certain defeat into victory. These and
similar temptations offered by the Wyrm through its minions
result in the slow and inexorable corruption of individuals —
both human and Garou — into servants of the Wyrm.

The Wild West provides ample latitude for the Wyrm to do
its work. Lone Wolves are particular targets for the Wyrm's
attacks since they are cut off from most channels of power and
often accept offers to increase their knowledge of Gifts or
replenish their diminishing Gnosis without looking too closely
at the sources of these boons. In addition, some Uktena and
Wendigo, desperate in their losing battle against European
Garou, seek aid wherever they can get it, even if such assistance
involves compromising themselves by trafficking with minions
of the Tunneler (one of their names for the Wyrm). European
Garou are not exempt either, since Urge-Wyrms of Greed,
Envy and Anger find fertile ground in the hearts of
Gnawers and Silver Fangs alike, preying on their desi
claim the Pure Lands and blinding them to the methodsib
which they assert those claims.

Once a werewolf takes an initial pact, his d
into corruption is inevitable. The shadowy fore
deals offer him more power, or provide sanctuary ft
discover the Garou's traitorous dealings with the Wyrm.
Wyrm-seduced Garou are some of the great
race, for their knowledge of caern locations and fril
soon offered to the Wyrm. Former companions, feat
and suspicious of strangers who might be agents
become ever more insularand xenophobic, refusi
Garou who might be allies. Distrust becomes,
breeds violence, and the Wyrm has snared far m
victim in its manipulative coils.

Black Spiral Dancers

Mar it the more, and make it run to wildness.

— Dante, Purgatorio

Long ago a noble Garou tribe known as the White Howlers
fell to the Wyrm. Rather than being destroyed, the Howlers
were horribly changed, driven deep into malodorous caverns,
their bodies twisted and minds unhinged. When they emerged,
they had become the Black Spiral Dancers, maddened
werewolves who have been embraced by the coils of the Wyrm
itself and who had looked upon that dread god's face. Corrupt
and depraved, they delight in inflicting torture and engage in
cannibalism, gorging themselves on Garou flesh.

In many ways, this corrupt Garou tribe functions as the
Wyrm's most valuable tool, since its members mirror both the
inner and outer madness of their creator. Born to suitably
debauched Kinfolk, Dancers-to-be undergo abduction and
initiation like other Garou, although the nature of their

experiences is tailored to produce warped creatures dedicated
wholly to the Wyrm. Within each Black Spiral hive (their
word for caern) lie foul pits of darkness and a labyrinthine
spiral whose pattern replicates the Wyrm's tormented nature,
ora pool in which the Wyrm's reflection can be seen by those
who gaze into its depths. Confined in these places of torment,
forced to walk the spiral or stare into the pool, newly made
Black Spiral Dancers suffer the breakdown of all sanity,
discovering within themselves their own particular demons.
Those who don't die from terror emerge as full members of the
tribe, twisted in both spirit and body.

Granted both inherent strength and weakness by their
madness, Black Spiral Dancers hurl themselves recklessly
into battles, real and imaginary, howling their nerve-shatter-
ing war cries, heedless of the dangers they face. As agents of
destruction, they have little to lose.

Like other Garou, Black Spiral Dancers employ Rage and
Gnosis, gaining power and learning their own unique Gifts
that allow them to travel the Umbra, summon Wyrm-spirits or
Banes, and channel the energies of their destructive maker into
against their enemies and toxins to pollute the land.

The Black Spiral Dancers arrived in the West as early as the
16th century, traveling in the company of conquistadors and
egging on the enslavement and decimation of the native peoples,
weakening their Garou protectors. Now the Dancers journey west
with their Kinfolk, indulging in acts of violence and terror along
the way. The harsh conditions of life in the early boom towns and
primitive outposts and the general lack of law and order enable the
Dancers to exert their insidious influence with little impediment.
Their murderous rampages are all too easily mistaken for Indian
massacres or cavalry "actions." Thus do the Dancers escalate the
general trend toward brutality already evident in the
struggle for dominance of the West.

Werewolf: The Wild West


Tey
The Storm Eater

‘Mid the lightnings,

‘Mid the lightning zigzag,

‘Mid the lightning flashing,

Comes the rain,

Comes the rain with me

— Navaho, "Song of the Rain Chant"

Precious few Garou are aware of the existence of this
potent Wyrm-spirit. It is so powerful that the greatest forces
of nature bow in the face of its rampant might and awesome
capabilities. Fewer still remember precisely when the Storm
Eater was first bound and its terror leashed by the spirit chains
and powerful wards of its Uktena binders. Once a great Bane
that fed upon the Wyld's spirit energies until it assumed
massive size and enormous power, the Storm Eater lay dor-
mant for countless centuries, locked away in its Umbral
prison unable to summon any of its minions.

In the 1830s, a great tremor rocks the Umbra surrounding the
Pure Lands as the European takeover of one too many Uktena and
Wendigo caerns weakens the seals that secure the great Bane's
prison. The creature makes good its escape and quietly begins
feeding on Wyld energies once again, building back its strength.

Its half-sated rumbles resonate into the province of the
Weaver, arousing one of her mighty spirits and attract-

& ing it by the sheer force of the Bane's magnitude.

Chapter Two: Setting

Weaver and Wyrm combine into a cataclysmic new entity —the
Storm Eater—and a new horror erupts into the world. Exploding
into the High Umbra, it exults in its newfound freedom and
thunders its triumph across all the realms. Minions of the trans-
formed Storm Eater, bearing aspects of both spiritual parents,
range throughout the Near Umbra and traverse the Gauntlet into
the physical world, wreaking havoc upon creatures of Gaia —

Garou in particular — and causing chaos and disruption.

Because so little is known about this entity, the Garou
who encounter the Storm Eater's spawn are hard pressed to
deal with them successfully. European Garou have alien-
ated those among the Uktena and Wendigo who might
retain some arcane knowledge of its precursor, the great
Bane (including the secrets of its binding). Native Garou
are too beleaguered by their battles to retain their home-
lands to turn their attention fully to its defeat. The shadow
of this monstrous entity casts its pall over the whole of the
Savage West, heightening its excesses and adding to the
tribulations that face all Garou.

Mockeries

Humans and animals exposed to the corrupt influences of
the Wyrm become transformed into horrible, frightening crea-
tures known as mockeries. Also called "fomori" by the Fianna,
these infested servants of the Wyrm act as agents of corruption
and front-line soldiers in the battle against Gaia and the Wyld.


Mockeries are born from exposure to poisonous sites such as
Wyrm caerns, from ingestion of substances corrupted by the
Wyrm, and from possession by Banes. The result isa vile, though
formidable creation capable of facing off against Garou. The
Wyrm grants certain powei
making them almost invincible against mere humans or normal
animals. Even Garou fear these foul creatures, for their powers
enable them toseverely hurt, cripple oreven kill Garou. Defenses
such as armored skin may enable mockeries to withstand the
fangs and claws of Garou, while offensive powers like sharpened
silver teeth, poisonousspittle or acidic blood allow them to inflict
tremendous damage. Occasionally, entire families fall under the
control of powerful Banes, forming twisted dynasties as succeed-

s — similar to Gifts — to mockeries,

ing generations are initiated into the service of the Wyrm.

Since the Wyrm embodies decay and destruction, mock-
eries, in return for their powers, show the inner rot that
infects their bodies and souls. Bleeding pustules, snaggled
fangs set into oozing gums, weird gangrenous lumps and the
reek of a five-day-old corpse are just a few of the ways their
taint may outwardly manifest.

Many mockeries, especially those in the final stages of corrup-
tion (just before their deaths), are little more than mindless
automatons bent on savage displays of aggression. Many more,
however, retain some semblance of their former selves
and insinuate themselves into human society,
where they continue to work for the
Wyrm as corruptors of the in-
nocent and weak.

ZIELs

Mockeries often appear in the guises of bestial, barely civi-
lized bounty hunters, trappers, bandits, prospectors, and various
low-lives in the early boom-towns and whistle-stops along the
western trails. S

Sometimes entire towns house fomori families,
forming communities of terror that engulf strangers unfortunate
enough to enter them. The Uktena and Wendigo tell stories of
evil spirits that wander the land in the bodies of humans, driven
by murderous desires and an insatiable hunger for human flesh.
By far the most frightening fomori, however, are the ones who
seem normal to the naked eye, for they can often hide their foul
natures long enough to perform the most heinous acts of
destruction and perversion, all while remaining in the guise of
a local banker or sheriff.

warriors.

Other Supernaturals

2
Supernatural antagonists pose an even greater threat_to

Garou. The powers and natural weaponry of vampires, the.
reality bending power of mages, the eerie abilities of wraiths »y
and the unpredictable talents of changelings often make them
serious contestants in battles of wits, weapons and wills.

Vampires
Much as folks would like to believe otherwise, the dead walk the
Savage West. Centuries ofhatred run between werewolfand vampire;
the two are swom to kill one another on sight. Corrupt and foul, the
Wyrm-tainted undead unnaturally prolong their lives long past the
time when Gaia intended them toretum todust. Werewolvessee
vampires as the embodiment of the principle of undying
corruption. Their very existence constitutes a slap in the
face of Gaia by denying the natural cycle of life and death.

The dependence of vampires on large populations of hu-
mans toslake their twofold thirst for blood and temporal power
makes these walking cadavers stealthy supporters of the rise of
cities and the development of urban centers of population.
This angers the werewolves, who view the proliferation of
towns as blights on the landscape.

The sparsely settled West, however, provides little in the way of
hospitality for most vampires. Only in growing cities such as San
Francisco, Houston, Santa Fe and other sizable human enclaves can
more than a handful of these leeches find sufficient population to
support them. In those places where vampires have managed to
insinuate themselves, they bring human institutions under their
cntfol. Many of the fiercest battles between Iron Riders, Shadow
‘Lords!and Borie Gnaweis against vampires take the form of power
struggles ‘ver which group will exercise the greatest influence over
Unaispectinigmortals. Still those cadavers who do walk the wild lands

are uauallyctey ad powerful enough to give pause to most Garou.

Wraiths

G Most Garou, European and native, have a healthy respect for the

4 spicits of the dead known as ghosts, wraiths or ancestors. Seen as a

fatural part of Gaia's cycle of life and death, wraiths inhabit their
‘own part of the spirit realm, the Dark Umbra, and rarely manifest in
the physical world. When they do, they usually haunt the sites of
their deaths or places or people who meant a lot to them. While the
native tribes honor their dead ancestors, they fear the dark, twisted
wraiths knownas hungry ghosts. Uktenaand Wendigo whoencoun-
ter these angry spirits recognize the shadow of the Wyrm in them.
Massacres, famine, natural and man-made disasters, gun-
fights, lynchings and range wars result in the untimely
deaths of many mortals, whose restless spirits linger just
beyond the world of the living, tied to it by a sense of

Chapter Two: Setting 65

1


unfinished business. Boom towns where gold and silver lines
have played out are called "ghost towns" with good reason;
wraiths find solace in these abandoned and desolate places.
Garou rarely have dealings with wraiths, with a few notable
exceptions. Occasionally, Uktena or Wendigo Theurges seek
to lay an unhappy spirit to rest or banish a hungry ghost to
protect their Kinfolk. Among European Garou, only the Silent
Striders show an active and sometimes compulsive desire to
communicate with the spirits of those who have passed on.

Fae

AllGarouare wary of the tricks that the fae can pull whenannoyed.
European fae of any type are extremely rare, however. Finding more
than one or two together is a task almost as difficult as locating the
fabled city of El Dorado. Native Garou, especially the Uktena,
occasionally involve themselves in the war between their allies, the
native changelings known as Nunnehi, and these newcomers.

Native changelings, exist in many forms and are not usually
hostile to Garou. The remnants of the once-proud faerie races,
now reduced to housing themselves in mortal flesh, share with
the Garou a profound hatred and distaste for the harsh reality of
iron rails and spiritual barrenness. Unfortunately, the Nunnehi
are also forced to compete with the Garou for the diminishing
sites of available power that they, too, need to survive in their
present forms. Among the Uktena and Wendigo, the Nunnehi
more often find common ground and form alliances. In particu-
lar, the Uktena seek out contact with the Nunnehi, sharing lore
and attempting to preserv itions. Nunnehi atti-
tudes toward European Garou, however, reflect their general
distrust ofanyone who might endanger their tenuous holdon the

few enchanted jp places left to them.

Suggested Books
and Films

Books

The following selection of books may provide more detailed
information on the West and its people, both native and
newcomer. It is by no means an exhaustive list and more
sources can be obtained at libraries and bookstores. Good
picture books of the West are highly recommended, particu-
larly to get a visual feel or even photo references for the game.

W The Life and Death of Crazy Horse, by Russell Freedman,
drawings by Amos Bad Heart Bull. A wonderful portrait of the
influential war leader.

W The Disputed Lands: A History of the American West, by
Alexander B. Adams. This provides a thorough, though
Eurocentric, overview.

Ye The Native Americans: An Illustrated History, by Turner
Publishing. This coffee-table book contains a wealth of infor-
mation and illustrations.

w The American West, by Dee Brown. Recounts the power
struggles between Native Americans, ranchers and settlers.
4@ The American Heritage History of the Great West, by David
Lavender. Somewhat dated, but with good maps and photos:
%W The Oxford History of the American West, edited by Clyde
A. Milner II, Carol A. O'Connor and Martha A. Sandweiss.
Comprehensi cademic sourcebook.
w The Wild West, by the editors of Time-Life Books. Com-
1) panion book to the PBS miniseries, itis lavishly
fl
ay) W

illustrated, informative and inexpensive.


‘W In Their Own Words: Warriors and Pioneers, collected and
edited by T. J. Stiles. Collection of eyewitness accounts from
and journals by the people who lived the stories.

w Seven Trails West, by Arthur King Peters. A look at
westward expansion through the pioneering routes that linked
the East to the West.

@ Insight Guides: Wild West, ed. by John Gattuso. Though
designed for modern tourists to the West, this contains historic
essays, bits of trivia and lots of color photos.

Films

You can't spit in a video store without hitting a Western.
Some of the better known or "classic" treatments are given
below. All of these portray either the realities or bigger-than-life
myths that have shaped modern perceptions of the Wild West.

W The Magnificent Seven. Hired good guys fight off banditos
terrorizing a town.

w Dances with Wolves. Kevin Costner's paean to the wide-
open spaces and the glories of the Lakota Sioux.

W The West. Excellent Time-Life video series focusing on
all the factors that formed the drama of westward expansion.

‘ Little Big Man. Dustin Hoffman as the last "white"
survivor of the Battle of the Little
Big Horn, though basedon _ (A
a composite of several real
characters. Less
realisticthan " es
ironic. xt Cs

‘e True Grit. The Duke himself as ornery-cuss-good-guy.
This is especially interesting for the period dialogue, more true
to the actual speech of the times than most Westerns portray.

v Unforgiven. Retired bounty hunters take on one more job.
Gritty, dirty and featuring a black character; far more represen-
tative than '50s squeaky-clean depiction of the Old West.

v Silverado and Lonesome Dove. Modern classics.

‘w Wyatt Earp and Tombstone. Different treatments of the
story of Wyatt Earp and his brothers, with depictions of the
famous gunfight at the O.K. Corral.

*W El Dorado. Classic.

W The Good, the Bad and the Ugly and Pale Rider. Two
Spaghetti Westerns; vehicles for Clint Eastwood's portrayals of
resolute loners fighting against overwhelming odds.

W Stagecoach. Classic.

‘¥ Geronimo. Recent film supposedly about Geronimo, the
Chiricahua Apache leaderwhom the whitescouldnot defeat. Inreality,
itdealsmore with the whitesinvolvedin thestory, relegatingGeronimo
to some half-mythical status that fails to explain much about him.

‘w | Will Fight No More Forever. Well-told story of Chief
Joseph and the Nez Perce's valiant fight to remain independent

and free in their own lands.

WA Man Called Horse. Probably most interesting for its
depiction of the Sun Dance and other Plains Indian rituals,

w Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid. The quintes-
sential "buddy film," interesting forthe legendary outlaw
personalities, trainand bank robbery scenesand change
of setting to South America.

le:



Are you gonna do something or just stand there and bleed?

— Wyatt Earp, Tombstone

Whether you and your troupe are gathering for a one-shot
game of Werewolf: The Wild West or playing the latest
session in an epic, ongoing chronicle, the story is what brings
the characters together. The characters' common goal is the
glue that binds them, the reason why a motley crew of bellig-
erent and disparate werewolves is riding together.

Unlike attending a movie, reading a book or watching televi-
sion, the characters in this story are the audience, and their
enjoyment is a direct reflection of their input. Every participant
playsa vital role. Sure, you're not entertaining a playhouse full of
guests or millions of television viewers, but you're not dependent
on ascript, either. The tale you and your troupe produce is part
of the game. Ultimately, youand your troupe will find yourstories
are part of a living thing. The players are the breath of life. Once
brought to life, the tale grows, sometimes unpredictably. The

quality of the tale is dependent on what goes into it. A game

is simply a pleasant diversion, while a well-told story,
like a work of literature, is high art.

Chapter Three: Storytelling

The Storyteller's Role

Ic's simple to be a player. Just show up with your character
sheet and a fistful of dice and you're set. But your character's
motivation, the people she meets and the resistance she
encounters are all part of the responsibility of the Storyteller.
No small job, that.

The first aspect of this responsibility is the story. It is part of
the Storyteller's duty to create the tale in which the players'
characters take part. This can be as simple as a five-word
mental note or as complex as a vast storyboard flowchart. The
Storyteller must know, however roughly, where she wishes the
story to go and what the characters can expect to encounter
along the way. This is the most important of the Storyteller's
responsibilities, for without a story you only have a tableau.

The Storyteller is the sensory connection to the players,
describing what they see, hear, feel, taste and smell. Is the
street thin or wide? Does it have wooden sidewalks? What


SSSs

ZLLL,

do the tethered horses smell like? Is that the sound of a pistol being drawn? How gritty is the trail dust?
This is the Storyteller's second most important responsibility; without a vivid scene, the players may as well
be grubs digging blindly though dirt.
The Storyteller additionally plays the roles of the nonplayer characters that her players encounter. The world is
full of individuals, and the Wild West is no exception, especially when there are werewolves about. These nonplayer
characters should be as dynamic and vivid as the sensory detail.
The last responsibility of the Storyteller is to interpret the rules. She decides how hard it is for the characters
to carry out their actions, based on the mechanical systems detailed elsewhere in this book. The most important
thing to remember is what the rules are designed to do: help tell a story. If the rules become cumbersome or
anticlimactic, it's the Storyteller's duty to scale them back or forget them entirely. After all, it is more fun and
dramatic to have a character leap from a train into some brambles as it flies from its rails over a bridge than
to have her die a stupid and ignominious death because she didn't have enough dots in Dexterity.

The Players
Soas the Storyteller, you have a vast job before you, but luckily you don't go it alone. One thing you should

do is look at the characters your players create and adapt the story to them. Do the characters have extensive
? Are they stealthy? Do they seem inclined toward politics? Moderation is important here,
because all characters are rarely geared toward the same thing. Most stories are complex combinations
of different types of conflict, giving every player an opportunity to contribute to the tale.
Keep in mind that the players want to enjoy the story as well. Don't let them become
ercising your godlike powers of storytelling. Don't be a jerk; a
is soon without them.

combat skil

mere vehicles for e
Storyteller who fails to consider the needs of her players

On the other side of the coin, don't feel compelled to buckle under every
player's whim. Struggle and conflict in the face of unfavorable odds are
what Werewolf: The Wild West is all about. The best Storytellers
subtly guide the story where the players wish to go, while giving
them enough resistance within the context of the story to give
them a sense of accomplishment. If the characters feel that
they easily overcome what is thrown at them, the inher-
ent tension and excitement of the
game is lost, at which point it turns
into a soulless hack-and-slash.
Storytelling is a tough job, but
someone has to do it. The Story-
teller is the focus and inspiration
for the excitement of the players.
also the most potentially re-
warding of the game
responsibilities. Make sure
the players have fun, and
their excitement pays off
for you as well. You're
the one who creates that
situations that give them
such enjoyment, and
you deserve the satis-
faction of a job
well-done.


LIOR EL:

The Chronicl

Keeping the relationship between players and Storyteller in mind, it's time to get to work
on creating your story. The chronicle is the whole enchilada, the big picture, and it can be as
long or as short as you and your players wish. Each individual session is a small piece of the
greater tale, and can last as long as the Storyteller and players want to keep the story rolling.

What in Tarnation's Going On?

The first step in beginning a story is deciding on a theme and mood. More
than just English-class buzzwords, these two ideas are the core concepts that
give astory meaning and depth. If the Storyteller gives no forethought to
the type of story he wishes to tell, the plotand the characters suffer; they
have no common thread to unite them in their travails.

Theme

Theme is a difficult to grasp as a concept but easy to imple- }
ment ina game. It is the greater meaning, the moral tothe story
and the big picture all in one. It doesn't have to be heavy-
handed or preachy, but it should remark upon or reinforce
some simple truth. Use subtlety; the players will more often
than not illustrate your theme without knowing it.

Some common themes in Werewolf: The Wild
West include:

‘v Strangers in a Strange Land

The vast majority of Garou tribes aren't indigenous to America. This country
isavery newand different place from continental Europe, from which most of the tribes
originally hail. The Wild West lacks the refinement and culture of the Old World,
butitdoesn't have any shortage of its own customs. Inaddition, werewolves, even
Theurges, unfamiliar with the Storm Umbra have difficulty acclimating to the
strange workings of this new land. They are also probably unaware of the
subtleties of the Storm Eater, and may likely do more harm than good.

The other side of the coin is the perspective of the Na
people and Garou. Recent years have seen a prodigious influx of cast-
offs from other nations, and they come in sufficient numbers to
irreparably damage these once-pure lands. The Europeans force the
Native American onto reservations and police their activities.
European settlers, termed Wyrmcomers by the indigenous Garou,
bring with thema host of unpleasantries, including disease, Weaver-
spawned technology, and demon alcohol. Even the natives'
European brethren are problematic and bellicose, as well as
responsible for awakening the Storm Eater itself.

v Spirituality

At its heart, Werewolf is as much a game about spirituality as
about horror, violence or anything else. One of the most common
conflicts between the Pure Ones and the Wyrmcomers is the
disparity of traditions dealing with the Storm Umbra and its native
spirits. Native American tribes of Garou live in symbiosis with the
Velvet Shadow, respecting its spirits and often giving in return for what
they ask. The European Garou have a less harmonious relationship with
the spirit world; most of them usually just stomp into the unfamiliar

Umbra of the West and demand service from its residents. The
spirits become angry and indignant. It is precisely this European
defensiveness and arrogance that awakens the Storm Eater.

Chapter Three: Storytelling


STEN

More so than in any other time in history, people now have the
opportunity to make their own fortunes and stake claims to their
legacies. Manifest Destiny is a powerful motivating force, urging
people westward in the face of adversity. The will of the westem
pioneer is indomitable, and this reflects in his urge to survive and
his determination to make a better life for himself. The Garou are
similar, as they see the Westas eitheran opportunity torenew their
power bases (in the case of exiled Shadow Lords and disenfran-
chised Silver Fangs) ortofinally establish sovereignty forthemselves
(in the case of the Bone Gnawers and the growing Iron Riders).

Mood

Mood is the "feel" or tone of the story, and should be
consistent throughout or players may become confused. Mood
is accentuated by carefully selected settings and nonplayer
characters. Mood doesn't concern the greater meaning of the
story, as theme does, but details the emotional aspect of the
story and how players should react when confronted with a
situation. To create an enjoyable experience, the Storyteller is
free to adjust the mood to her and her players' satisfaction.

Some moods common to Werewolf: The Wild West are:

w Horror

At its core, Wild West is a game about the horror of being a
werewolf. Neither fully human nor fully wolf, the Garou walks
between two worlds. Numerous terrible and unnatural things walk
between these worlds as well, and it is the werewolfs fate to
confront them. The unknown lurks in every shadow and death is
inevitable. Dreadful Wyrm-creatures, the vast infinity of the
Umbra, and the inability to reason with other fanatical Garou are
all good bases for horrific mood. More than that, the possibility of
running human characters in the Savage West can really get the
goose-pimples rising. Even if they're lucky enough to have a
handful of silver bullets, death can still come quickly —and who's
to say they even know that werewolves are real? Horror is arguably
the single most important mood of Werewolf: The Wild West.

¥v Tragedy

The European immigrants press inexorably westward, dis-
placing and murdering the Native Americans as they go. Indian
counterattacks massacre villages of men, women and children.
The Storm Eater destroys without bias. The Garou are slaves to
their shapeshifting nature, and their alienation from both the
human world and the animal often proves their damnation as
they slip into Harano.

wv Violence

Garouare inherently creatures of rage, and they attract violence
like a saloon draws drifters. They are also passionate, and often
resort to physical means when their ire is aroused. Their oppo-
nents, both mortal and supernatural, are also quite adept at
violence because they have to be. More often than not, conflicts
explode into catastrophic levels of violence, sometimes catching
people who have nothing todo withasituation. It's tough outthere
on the frontier, and you'd better be prepared to take your licks.

Considering a location for the story is one of the most
important decisions a Storyteller makes. The characters can
only interact with their environment and with nonplayer
characters, so they better be both convincingand entertaining.
As Werewolf: The Wild West is based in a certain historical
time period, a large part of this decision is made for you. The
second aspect of setting upon which you must decide is history.

If you want to, goahead and cast history to the four winds. The
Savage West is a huge place, and who's going to notice if a
mythical city pops up overnight? Creating completely fictitious
location accomplishes multiple ends. Firstand most importantly,
everyone playing the game can shape their environment, as it
will likely enter the game tabula rasa, You can make up a Savage
West "dream town" and stock it with the people, background and
political structure of your choice, as well as sow a few plot seeds
among the citizens. The disadvantage to creating a completely
new location is that players won't know a great deal about it (and
can't even go to the library to find out more), which may cause
some frustration, especially early in the game.

Remember that if you wing it altogether, you must cover all
aspects of the new location in as much detail as possible to
avoid later contradiction. You must also work out where it fits
in with the rest of the country and its populace.

Another option is to select a real historical location, and cast
its history to the four winds. Who cares who the mayor of Dodge
was in 1853? If you choose todo this, make sure the players know
about it, or they will make assumptions that may or may not be
true in the story. The main benefit of setting the story in a
"historical fiction" milieu is that most players will at least be
somewhat knowledgeable of the location (unless you pick some-
where strange, in which case you have only yourself to blame).

The "historical fiction" route is arguably the best type of setting
for Werewolf: The Wild West due to the design of the game. This
book is essentially designed from a historical standpoint with
werewolves and spirits included; Custer still dies at Little Big
Hom, and Geronimo still surrenders in 1886. The true test is how
the Storyteller and players use these assembled guidelines; if your
troupe finds it more fun to play it another way, more power to you.

Lastly, a purely historical campaign is an option. If you choose
to go this route, a great deal of your work is done for you, and you
must only integrate the characters into the story. Players are
familiar with the area, and may know afew of the local figureheads,
while the Storyteller needs only doa bit of research to get her facts
in order. The disadvantage to playing in a truly historical setting
is that the results of the characters' actions are fairly closed-ended,
or you end up changing history. Not that this is a bad thing...

Ideally, some combination of these settings is probably best.
Players will appreciate the flexibility offered by an open-ended
plot, while the Storyteller can make use of established historical
locations to add a sense of realism to the game. As always,
erron the side of enjoyment. We are not going to send
the setting brigade to whip you into compliance.

7 Werewolf: The Wild West


Keeping the type of setting in mind, it is best to begin
detailing the area in which the characters spend most of their
time. This may be a city or town; it may be a farm well off the
beaten path, ora glade in a dwindling forest. Heck, it may even
be in the middle of the Storm Umbra! Wherever the action
takes place is the logical starting point for fleshing out details.
Either research or invent the pertinent information.

As yououtline your locations, think about which aspects prove
most useful. Where will the characters visit frequently? Where's
the best place to get gossip or other information? Where's a safe
place to make camp? Are the hotels reputable — and is that a plus
or minus? Coming up with specifics will help establish your setting
in two ways. First, if the players go somewhere for which you have
already created details, you don't have to make anything up as you
go. Second, if you do find yourself making things up, you can use
what you have already established as a frame of reference so that
you can at least improvise with consistency.

A Hardy Environment

One thing thatall characters in Werewolf: The Wild West
have in common is harsh surroundings. This makes a good
theme, mood modifier, and applies to any setting; the fact that
it's a tough world is a universal truth.

Medical science in a Savage West setting may be as primi-
tive as "aspirin or opium." Unless you're playing very late in the
period, there's no electricity. People bathe rarely — if ever —
and sometimes you have to eat hardtack for a week straight
(though a werewolf can probably bring down some game). You
have to go outside to a little shed to go to the bathroom, and
there are times when the seat is really cold.

Law also gets a bit sketchy the further west you travel. In some
towns, the sheriff is actually the most recent thug to wander in and
proclaim himself deputized. News travels as fast as a horse or
telegraph signal, and you can bet Black Bart keeps his eye on the
latter. Shootings in the streets are common, as are lynchings after-
ward. Those with enough money or connections are untouchable.

Highlighting the outstanding differences between what we
take for granted in the modern day helps make a Wild West story
all the more realistic. Make subtle references to the differences
between our world and the world of the Wild West, and the story
will benefit greatly. At the same time, sharpen those differences
by grounding them in reality: Life can be quite brutal (especially
when there are werewolves waiting just beyond the door), and it
is frequently so in these wild and frightening times.

s = .

Building the Chronicle
After hammering out a theme, setting and mood that the
Storyteller and the players all enjoy, other specifics of the story
need attention. At this stage, the Storyteller decides how long
the chronicle will last, as well as its foundational events. Player
input is important during this stage of the game's

= formation; if the players don't enjoy themselves, they

won't come back.

¥ Scope

Whatis the focus of the chronicle and how grandare its schemes?
Will the characters protect a band of Hopi from forced residence on
areservation, or aim to prevent California from falling into the sea?
Does the fate of the entire continent hang in the balance or are they
just trying to save their own hides? Define how greata scale you want
to work on and some of the other blanks will fill themselves in.

‘¥ Establish Intent

Hand in hand with theme and mood, intent defines a great deal
about the nature of the conflicts in the chronicle. How will the
players go about resolving their issues? Player input is vital at this
point; if you envision asecretive plot involving tribal schemes and
politicking, and the pack just wants to go out and whup some
paleface ass, there are going to be problems. Like mood, you can
mix and match your conflicts for a captivating combination of
wild action and varied intercharacter relations.

‘ Work in the Characters

Werewolves, being the socially disparate folk they are, need
some kind of common motivating force to keep them working
together. Perhaps they all know the marshal who mysteriously
turns up dead, or they grew up around the city that the railroad
commission plans to destroy. Give the characters a bond. It
keeps the players interested in the story's resolution, and holds
characters and players together as a group. If the only thing the
characters have in common is the fact that their players want
to play Werewolf, the pack will soon degenerate into petty
squabbling and a mess of pointless combat. That's no good.

¥v Plan the Specifics

This is the really cool part. At this stage, the Storyteller takes all
ofher half-baked ideas and puts them back in the oven. Planning the
specifics involves actually creating the plot events of a story and
chronicle, including who the characters are after (or who's after
them), what they're trying to accomplish and where they're going,
alongwith myriad other things. Suffice it to say that here is where the
Storyteller makes up everything that the characters will encounter.

A key part of planning the specifics is creating a powerful
opening scene, as the first scene helps to set the tone and mood for
the whole chronicle. Ifthe opening scene isdull or uninspired ("An
oldman walks up to you in the saloon and gives youa map"), players
will be unimpressed and unlikely to give the game their all. Players
react more favorably and with greater excitement if the opening
scene is riveting and eventful. You may even wish to throw the
players headfirst into the action withoutany back story: "The moon
rides high in the dark Montana sky as the train approaches the
bridge just outside Evansville. The rhythmic clacking of the wheels
on the track lulls you all into half-awareness as the cups of water on.
the table in front of you ripple with the motion, dripping conden-
sation from their sides. A lone trickle of sweat rolls down your back
and is strangely cold. Suddenly, the smell of black powder fills the
air. A tremendous flash of light blinds all of you and you are
deafened by a thunderous explosion. The bridge has been blown!
This train is going to hurtle into the Evansville chasm. Unless you
do something, it's going to take you with it!"

Chapter Three: Storytelling "

9

» \


=

Tools for Storytelling

Once the story is established and the game begins, the
players look to the Storyteller to bring them fully into the
experience of the game. As the Storyteller arbitrates every-
thing the characters do, this is a reasonable expectation and
the Storyteller has her hands full making the game memorable.

There are numerous methods and tools that the Storyteller
may use to increase the excitement ofherstory. One of these tools
is an integral part of the tale itself, the mood. Maintaining mood
helps players decide their characters' courses of action and fleshes
out the world in the dark corners that the Storyteller does not
illuminate. Imaginative players fully visualize their environ-
ments with their mind's eyes, including items and people that
have not even been mentioned. For instance, in a game with a
mood of terror, the shadows on the main street loom long and
black as the town clock chimes midnight. Shutters and curtains
hastily close, drawn by dwellers who have unknown business in
the late hour. By comparison, in a chronicle with a mood of loss
and yearning, citizens languish in the streets, without hope to
motivate them. The clock in the center of town doesn'teven tell
the real time; it has been broken so long that it only occasionally
gives out a pathetic metal creak instead of chiming the hour.

Skilled Storytellers also consider the following tools:

w Pacing

The pace of the game helps build a sense of tension for the
players, especially if the pace changes according to the envi-
ronment. Days in Dry Gulch may pass slowly, but when a
beautiful traveling singer comes to town, there doesn't seem to
be enough hours in the day to appreciate her voice, or the
characters may be saving the schoolmarm from the old mine
shaft that could collapse at any minute.

Pacing is also particularly useful when dealing with a chase or
fight scene. The trees of the forest blur by as the lupus Garou charge
after the scent of Wyrm-tainted blood on the wind. The mon-
strous opponents move so quickly that the characters are struck
numerous times before they can deliver a single strike of their own.

But be careful of too many pace changes; what you believe
is a good series of transitions might feel like a jerky roller-
coaster ride to the players.

¥ Voice

The manner in which the Storyteller varies her voice is
another opportunity to bring diversity and quality to a game.
Moments of awe may find the Storyteller speaking in loud and
ominous bass tones, while times of secrecy may require barely
audible whispers. A shrill, high tone is appropriate for times of
frenzied and fast-paced action. Monotonous travel and endless
labor can be portrayed in a dull and droning voice.

The numerous characters that the pack encounters also
have their own distinct voices if the Storyteller has an ad-
equately wide range. Too much range can make characters
seem ridiculous and comical, though, and this technique
should be used without resorting to extremes.

Werewolf: The Wild West

¥ Detailed Supporting Cast

As the world is full of individuals, no two characters that the
pack encounters should be alike. Each of these people is a
thinking, breathing entity with her own likes and dislikes,
enemies, contacts and motivations. Storytellers may select at
least one attribute of any given nonplayer character and
accentuate it, adopting it as the character's master gesture. The
mayor of Rock Canyon might constantly fidget with a pocket
watch while the Uktena shaman speaks condescendingly and
contemptuously to non-native characters. The town drunk
may hum in his stupors, while the old blind man moves with
surprising speed. These mannerisms aren't a perfect substitute
for a fully developed personality, but they make a character
recognizable. The more the Storyteller plays up these "keys"
and the more fleshed out the characters' backgrounds are, the
more vivid and realistic these individuals become.

v Use All Five Senses

Garou are both human and wolf, and one of the inherent
boons of the latter is acutely heightened senses. When detail-
ing any event or circumstance during the course ofa game, give
outstanding reference to these sensory perceptions. Feel the
fragile lightness of the ancient Wendigo fetish and its soft
feathers in your hand. See the astounding array of shimmering
colors of the Lunes as they flicker around you and warn you of
the Storm Eater's passing. Hear the high whine of the steam
brakes on the locomotive and the heavy rumbling as it lurches
into the train depot. Smell the sweat and vile stench of the
Kinfolk turned fomori as he flees into the fresh pine forest.
Taste the subtle hint of salty metallic blood in the otherwise
refreshing cool stream.

Storytellers may wish to emphasize taste, smell and sound in
their stories to increase the alien experience of the Garou; their
senses exist on a level most humans never know. Storytellers
may also wish to focus on these senses with lupus characters,
giving them points of view that are distinctive and different
from those of their homid counterparts.

‘wv Use Music

This one's optional. A few CDs of western trailsongs, Native
American chants or movie scores goa long way toward enriching
the experience. In asense, the game itself is likea movie, and you
may wish to key certain music to certain scenes for the purposes
of making them more dramatic. For example, you can have a
particular piece of music in mind for the big fight between the
characters and the Black Spiral Dancer marauders, or you can
have a Storyteller character sing "Red River Valley" when the
characters are just bedding down after a hard day's ride.

Youmayalsowish touseambient music instead ofa preprogrammed
play list. Put a few soundtracks in the stereo, hit "random" and
go! Ifyoudo this, though, keep the volume low as everyone tends
to get distracted if any vocals come up, and an altogether
inappropriate piece of music may surface at an inoppor-

tune time. Music should be used subtly in this regard, ap

soas not to divert any of the focus away from the game.


Conflict

Does this mean we're not friends anymore?

—Doc Holliday, Tombstone

Conflict creates intensity in a story. It is what keeps the
game interesting, gives the characters motivation and an
agenda to pursue. It should be remembered that although
Werewolf: The Wild West is a proactive game, if the
characters don't take any initiative,
looking for them.

Conflict takes many forms; it doesn'talways have to be combat.
Tribal politics often cause Garou to butt heads over numerous
issues, and few characters are willing to fight an elder. Property-
development corporations acquire deeds to entire towns and aim
to demolish them in pursuit of oil or precious metals; it is foolish
and counterproductive to actually assault a company or its em-
ployees, and characters have to find a more peaceable alternative.
Perhapsa character aims to create a fetish that requires thata spirit
be bound into the item. What if the spirit doesn't want to become
part of the fetish? There are infinite varieties of conflict that occur
outside the realm of physical confrontation; indeed, there are
more situations that call for a thoughtful and civilized approach
than can be remedied by brute for

omething will come

This is not to say that combat does not have its place. This
is, after all, a game about werewolves, savage and atavistic
creatures designed to be sentient fighting machines by their
mother, Gaia. These creatures have every right to want to solve
problems with violence, because it's the way they are best suited
to operate. Combat is one of the most dynamic experiences in
the game, and it's one of the most effective tools — as long as

SSF

it's used with skill. Anyone can come up with a bunch of
statistics for monstrous opponents and throw wave after wave
at the playe
roleplaying. The story and its value become infinitely richer,
however, when there is meaning to the combat or there are
twists that make the players think about why their characters
are fighting. The simplest of these is, of course, the archenemy.
The moment of conflict has greater impact if said enemy has
eluded the characters for a long time, and they have finally
tracked him to his lair with a great deal of effort. The resulting
showdown can be the climax of the chronicle, or the close of
one "act" that sets the scene for another.

his hearkens back to some of the earliest days of

Storytellers find as they progress that complications with
combat add layers of depth to their stories. What if the
characters come face to face with a gang of fomori, the leader
of which is the brother of one of the characters? What if they
chase a Black Spiral Dancer from one end of town to the other
and she holes up ina schoolhouse, which just happens to have
class in se:
control of the enemy and is forced to fight her own packmates?
These and numerous other plot-based devices help make the

combat experience more outstanding — heap them
on! Players appreciate everything the Storyteller
does to alleviate just another fight scene.

Chapter Three: Storytelling
