OX

Establishing Conflict
What follows isa series of suggestions intended to illustrate
the myriad options at a Storyteller's disposal for creating
conflict. Many of these conflicts combine and intermingle
with each other, providing greater depth, detail and meaning
Ae to a story. Naturally, this list is not all-encompassing; it is
I} merely a collection of stock conflicts. No list can ever compare
to the creativity of a gifted Storyteller.
‘¥ Garou vs. the Wyrm
This is the classic struggle of good versus evil and right
versus wrong. The Wyrm is the embodiment of all evil,
corruption and decay, destroying everything it touches. The
Garou are bound into service by Gaia to oppose the its
depredations, and the vast majority of their conflicts are
against the servants and influence of the Wyrm.
Atitssimplest, the Wyrm isacatch-all forevery bad guy the
Storyteller wants to throw at the players, but the Wyrm can be
a great deal more. In addition to wanton destruction and
mindless rampage, the Wyrm works very subtly, corrupt-
ing those who otherwise have good intentions or who
have already begun to embrace its tenets unknowingly.
In fact, the Wyrm often corrupts ou themselves,
offering them power or other benefits in return for
service or other gratitude. The Wyrm commonly comes
in the guise of a sheep among wolves in this regard, in
order to hide its true malevolence and vile ulterior
motives. A Garou who succumbs to the wiles of the
Wyrmisa wretched individual, one who hasultimately
turned her back on everything she once held dear.
The Wyrm is taking anew shape in the form of
the Society,of the Weeping Moon. This pseudo
fraternal mystical order subtly spreads corrup-
<}| -})) tion and quietly taints everything it touches.
HF ‘The society's gradual growth and spread and

increase from the 1830s through the 1890s provides a worri-
some threat to Garou in these nights of impending apocalypse.

v Garou vs. Garou
SY

The Garou Nation is fractured at best and suicidal at
SSN worst. The agendas and goals of the numerous tribes
and their members come into conflict with fright-
ening frequency. This type of conflict covers
<\ everything from intertribal warfare to interper-
sonal rivalries between individual Garou. Thisis
one of the broadest areas of conflict — there are
just so many possibilities.

This type of conflict is particularly powerful when
it affects members of the same pack. Perhaps one
member of the group has a grim reputation or is the
Fes' suspicious sole survivor of another fallen pack; can she
be trusted? It is quite possible that members of mutu-
ally hostile tribes end up in the same pack, as

well, and the antagonisms that occur are
the stuff tragic heroes and antiheroes are made of.

Werewolf: The Wild West


‘ Garou vs. Human

Werewolves often find themselves at odds with the whims of a
short-sighted and self-serving humanity. The Wild West is now
seeing the formation of large cities, which some Garou perceive to
be blights on the face of Gaia. Many Garou rabidly oppose
humanity, wishing to reinstate the Impergium and cull the ranks
ofmen. OtherGarouact onasmallerscale, working to foil the plots
of those who would rape the land for their own benefit.

The tribes may also run into conflict with members of
certain nationalities or ethnic groups, especially during this
time of mass exodus and immigration. It is quite conceivable to
have a Nordic Get of Fenris vehemently opposed to the influx
of Irish and Italians regularly arriving on the eastern shores —
ora Fianna who's bigoted in just the other direction. Neither
is likely to call a Pure One "brother." This kind of prejudice
frequently raises its ugly head and contributes to the ethnic
ghettoes that arise in cities.

This conflict is one of the strongest underlying themes
pervading Werewolf: The Wild West. The Garou, though
they share some ties with humanity, are not humans them-
selves and never see eye-to-eye with mankind on all things.
In the end, the Garou are monsters: horrific shapechanging
stalkers of the midnight hour. Only their allegiance to Gaia
prevents them from becoming mindless beasts. Yet it is this
same allegiance to Gaia that puts them at such great odds
with their human cousins.

v Garou vs. Storm Eater

The Storm Eater's recent release is a point of concern for all
Garou, whether native or immigrant. Its sheer destructive
capacity worries elders and cubs alike, and the wreckage it
leaves in the Umbra is seeping into the physical world.

The problem arises of how to confront the Storm Eater. A
direct physical assault would be sheer folly and doubtless
suicide. Stealthy minor strikes are futile as the Storm Eater is
nota thing that can be slowly whittled down to death. Is there
a way to contain or banish the thing?

This particular conflict is also a direct link to other conflict:
Garou vs. Garou. As the coming of the European tribes
contribute to the release of the Storm Eater, the indigenous
Garou of the Uktena and Wendigo are loath to cooperate with
any of the European Garou, even with regard to uniting against
acommon enemy. This clash of cultures is one of the central
themes of Werewolf: The Wild West, and the groundwork for
many stories can be laid here.

‘ Garou vs. Vampires

The warriors of Gaia eternally clash with the walking dead,
believing them to be servants of the Wyrm as gruesome as any
fomori. The vampires prolong their existence in defiance of Gaia's
will, rising from the grave in gross mockery of all that is natural. In
addition, vampires who often pull the strings of human society
take cover in the cancerous cities that dot the landscape;

the fact that any of these bloodsucking freaks exist at all
& is an insult to the Garou and Gaia herself.

Chapter Three: Storytelling

Some vampires, most notably Gangrel and Ravnos, possess
qualities shared by the Garou, and very few werewolves have
the exact same opinion of these clans. Some Garou welcome
them, seeing the desire for redemption in their motives while
others perceive them to be up to the usual vampire trickery and
deception. Either way, a vampire consorting with a Garou is
not safe from antagonism. What's more, the dead possess their
own array of supernatural powers that rivals the Gifts and rites
of the Garou, making them foes as fierce as any.

‘ Garou vs. Mages

Mages are a peculiar and diverse lot, and very few generali-
ties can be made about them. Sometimes, as with the workers
of true medicine among the native tribes, mages can exist
harmoniously with the Garou. Other times, mages are just as
bad as humans or worse, as they plunder Gaia's resources to
further their own arcane ends.

Many Garou hesitate to have any truck with these bizarre
mortals, as the magick they wield is powerful and incompre-
hensible. Some Garou actively oppose mages, forming raiding
parties to overwhelm their targets with sheer numbers. All
Garou know that mages are dangerous quarry, and anyone who
finds himself at odds with one had better devise a sober plan of
action and know what he's getting into.

¥ Garou vs. Wraiths

Most werewolves have little interaction with the Restless
Dead, as ghosts seem to be trapped in an inaccessible part of the
Umbra. Some wraiths do manage to cross the line between the
realms of the living and the dead, though, and manage to
encounter the Garou. What they seek to accomplish is un-
knownand often ofa very personal nature. Sometimes, however,
deranged and dangerous ghosts find passage across the Gaunt-
let; these phantoms wreak as much havoc as any Bane.

Some Garou find themselves haunted by the lingering
ghosts of people they have bested in combat. Many Theurges
and Silent Striders come in contact with wraiths who are for
some reason fettered to particular places or objects, and who
enlist the aid of the Garou in resolving business to which they
could not attend before they died.

‘¥ Garou vs. the Fae

Werewolves regard faeries as an inscrutable lor, rife with inherent
insanity, and who live out bizarre recreations of the "days of yore."
Though the fae are not frequently involved in the affairs of the Garou,
sometimes the interests of the two cross. During lucid moments,
faeriesseem to believe thatthe Garouare apart of themselves, perhaps
lost to the ravages of time through miscommunication.

Much more frequent, however, are the Native American
changelings, or Nunnehi. While not as capricious as the
increasing quantities of European fae, these dangerous "small
folk" have relations with the Garou that are spotty at best.

Most werewolves feel that the best policy when dealing with
these living embodiments of the Wyld is to let the fae be, lest they
be dragged into some newfangled rendition of A Midsummer
Night's Dreamandbesurreptitiously turned intobunnies. Forsooth.


¥ Native vs. Wyrmcomer

Hand in hand with one of the main themes of Werewolf: The
Wild Westisthe struggle between the indigenous people of the land
and those who arrive from foreign countries and lay claim to the
West. The sheer, overwhelming numbers of immigrants, combined
with their advanced technologies, makes the resistance of the
Native Americans ultimately futile.

Garou relations proceed along the same lines, and the native Uktena
and Wendigo bear deep and justified gradges against European Garou,
whose very presence unleashes one of the most monumental Wyrm
entities ever known. Profound relationships can and must be explored
between these Garou of different origin; there will certainly be conflicts
between charactersifbothnativeand WyrmeomerGarouareinthesamepack.

Shades of Gray

Ultimately, the Garou are only human. Okay, they're not really, but
they are just as prone to personal foibles and flawed vision as anyone
uring these wild times. As such, not every conflict needs to be an issue
of black and white. What ifthe Storm Eater's passing has opened a vein
ofgold, bringing newfound prosperity to an otherwise destitute town?Or
ifa cabal of mages is trying to solve the puzzle of the riotous happenings
inthe Umbra? Twistingan existingconflict by makingnone ofthe parties
in question fully "in the right" is an excellent way to add drama to a story,
and givestheplayersan opportunity toexploretheircharacters' conscience.

Storyteller Hints

Always leave ‘em wantin' more. Then shoot ‘em.

— "Texas" Pete Barleycorn, Bone Gnawer Galliard

The more involved youget with storytelling, the more youfind
yourself developing your own style. During the course of a game,
every Storyteller makes decisions based on her own personal
preferences for how the game should run. Key principles to keep
in mind include the needs of the story, the desires of the players
and how smoothly you can integrate snap decisions.

Listed below are a few gentle reminders and helpful tips to
guide your judgment when running agame. Aftera few sessions
in the Storyteller's seat, you will doubtlessly come up with
tenets of your own, but for now, these are a start.

‘¥ Wing It

Given an average group of players, things will typically deviate
from their original course. When this happens, don't panic; it
shows that the players are thinking things through and are
examining new ways of accomplishing their goals. You should
encourage this, as it reinforces the active role of the playerand also
keeps them from feeling constrained by the story, which can easily
become ascript in the hands ofan unskilled Storyteller. When the
players come up with something off the wall but viable, run with
it, and improvise the story on the fly. With a little practice and a
good poker face, they will never know you are making it up.

¥ Don't Take Any Guff

Don't kowtow to the players. It's fine and good for them to come
up with creative ideas, butsometimes those ideas simply don't work.
As Storyteller, they must accept you as the world-maker, and what
you say is law. Some boulders just can't be lifted, nor should they be.

78

Werewolf: The Wild West

SSSSSN

OXX

Keep an Ace up Your Sleeve

Go ahead and cheat. As the Storyteller, part of your job is
interpreting the rules. You must know when to break them in the
players' favor, when to break them in the antagonists' favor, and
when to throw them out altogether. Temper your use of the rules
with the drama required by the story; that way the players enjoy
the experience more. For example, if your players spend several
sessions foiling the plots of the powerful and corrupt Bane Bonetaker
and they finally have him in their grasp, it's ludicrous to have one
player roll well on a single attack and utterly obliterate the spirit.
This lets down both the players and the Storyteller, while being
anticlimactic. A little tampering can fix that situation; nothing
makes players blanch like saying, "He looks faintly confused, rubs
at the wound a little bit, then snickers in contempt."

¥ Make It Real

When delivering narrative, select your words carefully. Use
language that evokes a vivid sense of reality rather than bland and
lifeless verbiage. See? There'sa contrastright there. One of the easiest
methods for this technique isavoiding game termsunless they're truly
necessary. Players and Storytellers alike rather hear, "Ina remarkable
display of dexterousness, the Fianna leaps from the stagecoach,
grabbing the luggage rack on the way out and somersaulting onto the
roof," than, "Yousucceed with your Dexterity roll andare on the roof."

Camp

Some people deserve to die.

— Ellen, The Quick and the Dead

Though Werewolf: The Wild West is designed with mature
roleplaying, dynamicrelationshipsand problem-solving in mind,
it is quite possible to play strictly for laughs. Known by the same
epithetas the film genre, camp is a combination of mood, theme
and tone that emphasizes comedy and a tongue-in-cheek style.
Just think of any B-grade horror movie spliced with Zane Grey
Theater. Stir in the World of Darkness and you're ready to go.

Addinga little melodrama toany of the elements ofa Werewolf:
The Wild West story results in camp. So can gratuitous use of
western stereotypes and inflated "pioneer gibberish." Any group of
players looking for a light one-shot game or a change of pace from
"serious" roleplaying can always look to camp for an opportunity.

Style

Camp need not be slapstick, though it certainly can be.
Campiness is easily achieved by assuming a schlock Hollywood
attitude and by making every character a caricature. Pick a
character trait and blow it way out of proportion; the squint-
eyed town sheriff's voice becomes a perpetual gravely grate, the
preacher lectures against "evil" (really a scapegoat term for
anything he doesn't like) and the crazy miner carries around a
dust cloud like Pig Pen from Peanuts.

Likewise, the good guys are ultimately good and the bad guys
are ultimately bad (and they only wear black). Play
revolves heavily around the characters' diatribe, er,
dialogue, and the ridiculously pulpy situations in
which they find themselves. Lay iton thick, pardner. *


Them's Fightin' Words

Camp isa great venue forextremely cinematic combat. Playersand
Storytellers alike may wish to consult World of Darkness: Combat
for some particularly extraordinary fighting maneuvers. Camp com-
bat isa clearinghouse for every western bar brawl you have ever seen
and then some. Imagine nine-foot-tall, seven-hundred-pound
werewolves in serapes sliding each other down a bar while adeadeye
Get of Fenris shoots the chandelier cord, bringing the fixture down
and pinning the Black Spiral Dancer's arms to his sides. Two fomori
graba Child of Gaia and pitch him through the plate-glass windows
atthe front. The Kinfolk bartender goes under the liquor cabinet for
his shotgun and the Silent Strider showgirl at the top of the stairs
cracks the heads of two omery cusses together. Suddenly everything
grows quiet as the saloon doors swing open and the Silver Fang
lawman strides in, white hat and silver badge (how does he do that?)
gleaming in the moonlight. A tumbleweed follows him.

Camp as a Tool

Camp may also be used to break up periods of ongoing
chronicle. If combined with a "straight" chronicle, camp
should only appear in moderation, and certainly not when the
dramatic or climactic needs of a scene make it inappropriate.

The old adage states that truth is stranger than fiction, and that
is precisely what injecting a bit of camp into an otherwise

dramatic campaign represents. It bears mentioning that

this is, after all, a game, and sometimes a brief bit of wit
gets a languishing chronicle back on its feet.

Chapter Three: Storytelling

Conclusion

Asevery movie, book, television show, play and song ends, so must
every story. Though there may be loose ends that must still be tied,
villains and Wyrm-spawn to fight, and secrets to uncover, the
governing plot must be resolved at some point.

Thisisnottosay that every chronicle must end; the series of games
you play is as infinite as the enjoyment you glean from them and the
creativity of the players and Storyteller.

Concluding a particular story, however, gives everyone a sense of
completion and ofa job well-done. Granted, some new friendships and
enmities are made along the way, and some secrets revealed, but these
are best left resolved in other stories. The best stories unfold slowly,
leaving clues to further mysteries and opportunities for future adven-
tures. Characters met in one story may be key villains or surprise allies
in others. In this sense, the story is but a small part of the ongoing
chronicle, whether the chronicle itself s finite or epic and boundless.

Ttalsobearsmentioningthatachronicleneed notbe infinite. Thechronicle
and story may be one and the same, a one-shot created and played for simple
enjoyment without any long-term obligations. In the end, it's all up to the
Storyteller and the players, for only they can give cach other exactly what they
want, and each participant will get exactly as much as she puts into the process.

Remember that characters are vital to the success of the story. Without
characters, thestory goesnowhere because there isnoone tosolveitspuzzles.

In closing, the whole process of storytelling can be described with
a few simple phrases that should always be kept in mind: Have fun.
Live vicariously. Participate with reckless abandon, and rotate the
responsibility of bringing whisky and cigars.





You gotta know when to hold ‘em,

Know when to fold ‘em;

Know when to walk away

And know when to run.

You never count your money
While your sitting at the table;

There'll be time enough for counting
When the deal is done.
— Kenny Rogers, "The Gambler"

All games have rules. Knowing and bending the rules are the
mark of a winner; riding roughshod over them — breaking them
— that's the mark of a loser. Some games are easy, like a duel
Some games, like poker, are tough because there's more to know
and strategy is required (what with outsmarting the toothy dude
at the table and his greasy, pale, drooling accomplice sitting
behind you trying to spy on your hand), Werewolf: The Wild
West is no different — there are simple rules, but the more you

think about them, the more shades of gray yousee. You only
need the basics, though; your imagination will fill in
the gaps and color the story, making things seem real.

Chapter Four: Rules

This chapter lays down the basics. This chapter takes you
out West like a rail line, to plains thick with cattle and
buffalo, towns full of frightened and untrusting folk, and dark
woods smelling of musty, rotten timber and home to chatter-
ing, rustling things that no one has seen and lived to speak
of. These rules are like signposts telling you what you need to
know — like how far to water or a town. Learn them and you
might just live to see daylight. The facts that are laid out here
might seem odd at first, but they're simple and will become
second nature in time. Learn them and you'll know how to
play the only game in town.

——al


SSeS

Playing a game and staging action scenes requires that you
measure time in certain increments. There are five ways to
measure time in Werewolf: The Wild West.

w Turn — This is a portion of time ina scene, about three
seconds to three minutes. A turn is enough time to do some-
thing (like taking a shot at a jackrabbit).

*w Scene —Thisisa period of roleplaying and action that takes
pla particular locale. A scene has a bunch of turns in it (as
many as you need to finish the scene), but you can just roleplay a
scene as wel

you don't have to use turns to measure it.

‘w Chapter — A big chunk of story, played out in one game
session. It results from connecting scenes with periods of
downtime and rest.

‘w Story — A whole tale with a beginning, a middle anda
lot of hoopla and shouting at the
end, Naturally enough,

the story is the basic
principle of the
game.

‘Chronicle — A whole bunch of stories and adventures about
the lives of your characters and usually centered on an overriding
plotor theme. A chronicle is the ongoing story you all tell together.

Hooting and
Hollering

"You wanna fight?"

"Hey! Them's fightin' words!!"

— Two hicks from Spittleville, The Simpsons

ter's actions, but
roleplaying can only go so far. Sooner or later you'll want your
character to take actions, do things — or try to do things —
that you can't act out, but must describe to the Storyteller. An
action can be anything from jumping a fire-belching crack in

You can roleplay many of your char:

company. You tell the Storyteller what your character is

or fails.


Rolling Dice

Life is about taking risks, from staking a claim to pan for
gold ‘to facing a blister-oozing cougar mad with Wyrm-fever.
It's only natural that Lady Luck plays her hand in Werewolf:
The Wild West. Players roll dice (or throw bones) to represent
chance; events aren't guaranteed in this type of storytelling.
This game calls for 10-sided dice, which you can buy at any
game store worth a damn. If you're the Storyteller, get a lot of
dice, like 10. Players can share.

Whenever your character is performing an action and there
is the possiblility of failure, or if events are complicated, the
Storyteller will tell you to roll dice to determine success. Such
rolls are based on your character's strengths and weaknesses,
and display what he's good or bad at.

Ratings

Your character is described by her Traits — the things she
knows or has learned. Traits are defined with numbers
ranging from | to 5. They representacharacter's
competence and ability. For regular folks,
Traits are around | to 3; the average is 2.
Exceptional folks can have Traits of
4 (Honorable) or 5 (Biblical),
or even 0 (Tetched; mighty
rare for Attributes, but
not unheard of).

Chapter Four: Rules

x Tetched; it's as if you wanted to shoot yourself
. Sissy; you shoot like a little girl

ee Fine; you shoot like my kid brother

£ Respectable; you shoot like Ma when she's madat Pa

jase, | Honccblesyoudhoor ibe you were
peekiatiet hieiareeeden
eeeee Biblical; only the Lord himself could beat that shot

You throw one bone for each dot your character has in a
Trait. That's four dice for four dots in Strength, or one die for
one dot in Perception. Yet you almost never roll based on the
dots in an Attribute alone. Attributes represent intrinsic
ability. You usually add a number of dice equal to the number
of dots your character has in an Ability — the stuff that he has
learned — to your Attribute total. $
wants you to roll to see if your character
notices the spider-legged

rattlesn se,
crawling

if the Storyteller

=z


up behind her, you roll her Perception + Alertness (an Attribute
plus an Ability) as a total number of dice. You roll as many dice
as you have dots in Perception and Alertness combined.

Acharacter's Dice Pool (or "Fist"; "throwing Fist" sounds more
authentic than "rollinga Dice Pool") is the total number of dice that
you can roll in a single turn — usually fora single action. You can
splita Dice Pool if your character attempts more than one action in
a single turn (or your character can get extra actions and Dice
Pools if you spend a Rage point). You almost always roll a number
of dice equal to your character's permanent Trait ratings (the circles
on your character sheet), not his current scores (the squares).

Some actions don't require Ability modifiers, such as when a
character soaks damage from an attack. You use only an At-
tribute in such circumstances, rolling dice equal to the Trait's
rating. Stamina is rolled in the-case of soaking damage.

Never get caught using more than two Traits to make upa Dice
Pool. Furthermore, you can only use one Trait ina single roll ifthe
Trait can be rated as high as 10 (such as Willpower). That Trait
canneverbe combined with another one. It'sgenerally impossible
for a normal soul to have more than 10 bones in a Dice Pool.

Life's Tough All Over

Now that you know how to roll dice, you need to know what
you're shooting for. The Storytellersets each task a difficulty rating
that the player rolls against — like a target to shoot for. Difficulty
is between 2 and 10 (see below for the meaning of the numbers).

Example: Your character wants to shoot a no-good, bloated
critter covered in festering boils. The Storyteller rates that
action difficulty 6. You roll your Dice Pool and get 2,3, 5, 6and
9. Each number that is equal to or higher than the difficulty
number is called a "success." In the example, you get two
successes, though you need only one to get the job done. Still,
the more successes rolled, the better the result.

Difficulties

3 Easy; you can do it sleepwalking

4 Routine; you can do it blindfolded

5 Straightforward; you can do it with one eye closed
6 Standard; a light wind's blowing dust in your eyes,

but you can see well enough

7 Challenging; your fever's making you feel a little
dizzy and is blurring your vision
8 Difficult; you're facing the sun, have blood in your

eyes, and your hog's hot in your hand

9 Extremely Difficult; like shooting black ducks at
night during a lunar eclipse

Degrees of Success

One Success Marginal; you made it to the right town

Two Successes Moderate; you're in the right neighborhood
Three Successes Complete; you have the right address
Exceptional; you know the innkeeper
Phenomenal; the innkeeper sends his full-
grown firstborn to keep you company
during that long, cold winter night

Four Successes
Five Successes

The lower the difficulty, the easier it isto succeed. The higher
the difficulty, the harder it is to succeed. But you know that. The
Storyteller sets high difficulties when your character's action is
complicated, but either sets a low difficulty if the task is easy, or
lets your character succeed automatically. The standard diffi-
culty is 6 and can be assumed when no other difficulty is stated.

So what about difficulties of 2 and 10? Why bother? A 2
means something's so easy that an infant could do it on an empty
stomach. And a 10! That's so tough that you have an equal
chance of succeeding or botching (see below) — no matter how
many dice are in your pool! If you're a Storyteller and you set a
difficulty 10, understand how tough you're making the task. But
if the player gets a 10 on a roll, it's an automatic success, no
matter what. Of course, sometimes even that isn't enough...

The Snake's Eye

Ifyou have luck like the Donner Party androlla 1 (orsnake
eye) on one of your dice, it takes away a success. Take a
success die and the snake-eye die right off the table. Worse,
you can roll enough Is to eliminate all of your successes,
meaning that your character fails at his action. Worse still, if
you roll more Is than successes, something awful happens to
your character — you botch (or misfire). The number of Is
rolled in excess of your successes doesn't matter — one or five
means the same thing. It's up to the Storyteller to decided if
the circumstances call for a minor mishap (your character's
last good match falls into a puddle) or a total disaster (your
character should have set a longer fuse).

Bullseyes

Throwing bones all the time can become dull and can gum
up the works of a story. If there is an action that your character
constantly repeats or if it's simple, assume an automatic success
to keep things moving.

There's a slick way of measuring automatic successes. If the
number of dice in your pool is the same as or higher than the
difficulty of the action, your character succeeds, no ifs, ands or
buts. This kind of success is marginal though, like achieving
only one success. If you want a more spectacular effect (more
than one success), you have to roll normally.

Telling your story or putting on a show is important. Hang
the rules. But keep something in mind: Don't allow automatic
successes for certain kinds of events, like fighting. Some events
are problematic and characters must be cautious.

Sometimes it's possible to achieve an automatic success
even when a roll is required. You can spend a Willpower point
(go read the chapter on Traits if you don't know what Will-
power is all about) for your character to get an automatic
success. Asa player, you don't want to do this all the time; your
character will run out of Willpower points before long and you
won't have any left when she really needs them. Also
remember that a Willpower point only counts for one
success; buying an automatic success is worthless if
you need more than one success.


Dice rolls and automatic successes are used best when

they're combined. In intense or powerful scenes, you might not
want to roll dice because you want to roleplay without inter-
tuptions. When you want to play a game or determine how luck
affects an action, grab a fistful of bones and have at it.

Complications

You already know that by rolling only one or two dice you get
a three-out-of-four shot at scoring a marginal success when the
difficulty is 6. Sounds easy, huh? There are ways to complicate
things, though. Strict roleplaying, simple dice rolls, and automatic
successes are invaluable for telling a story. But complications are
useful when you want to leave an outcome more to chance than
roleplaying, or when you want to create a realistic simulation.

Extended Actions

Actions that need only one success are called simple ac-
tions, and they're resolved quickly. There are some things that
your character will want to do that take time, though, like
climbing out of a gorge. Those are called extended actions. To
succeed at an extended action, you need to roll more than one
success — you need four or eight or even as many as 20! Youroll
repeatedly over subsequent turns until you've accumulated
enough successes.

Forexample, your Storyteller might say, "Well, Runs-Swiftly,
climbing out of that deep gorge is going to take you nine
successes." You start rolling. You know that your character can
climb out if your rolls are successes, but the more rolls you make,
the more chances you have of rolling failures or botches. And if
there's an emergency —say, if a poison-spitting Wyrm-creature
is climbing after your character — your character can't dally.

During an extended action, you roll to accumulate successes
every turn for as longs you want or until you fail to get even one
success. If you don't get any successes, your character might fail
at her action or might not get any headway for a turn. If you
botch, yourcharacter has to go back to herstarting point; all your
accumulated successes are eliminated, you have to start rolling
again from scratch, and your character might get hurt to boot!

Extended actions are more complicated than simple ac-
tions, so watch out for them during intense roleplaying. They
tend to slow things down a touch.

Resisted Actions

Sometimes your character does something that conflicts
directly with what someone else is doing, like two robbers
struggling for a payroll sack. Both players roll against a diffi-
culty number that is usually based on a Trait of the opponent,
and the character who gets the most successes wins. After both
players roll their appropriate Dice Pools and account for
botches, the players' successes cancel each other out on a one-
by-one basis. The player with successes remaining is victorious.

If you roll four successes and another player rolls
three, you win the resisted roll because you have
one more success. If both players roll the same

Chapter Four: Rules

number of successes in the same turn, neither character wins
and the struggle continues into the next turn unless one
character breaks it off.

Obviously it's difficult to achieve an outstanding number of
successes on a resisted roll. Even if your opponent can't whip
you, he can at least take the logs out of your fire.

Actions can also call for resisted and extended rolls. One of
the opponents must accumulate a certain number of successes
in order to succeed completely. Each success above the
opponent's total number of successes rolled in a single turn is
added to a success total. The first opponent to collect the
designated number of successes wins the contest.

Teamwork

Some or all of the characters in a pack can work together to
collectsuccesses. Teamwork is commonly used forextendedactions,
but the Storyteller has final say about when characters can effec-
tively work together. The players roll separately and the group
accumulates all its successes. (Characters' separate Traits can't be
combined for one roll.) Teamwork is useful when there's a ruckus at
asaloon or when fixinga stagecoach wheel before the Indians arrive.

Atother times, acharacteris better off on his own, like when he's
making eyes at that high-stepping, sweet young thing from The
Review, or when visiting the outhouse. No need for friends there.

Second Chances

Failure is worse than flea bites and saddle sores. Sometimes
failure is the only hand Lady Luck deals you. Of course, a
character can always try an action again, but each attempt can
make the action more difficult. Whenever your character tries
to do something he's already failed at or botched on, the
Storyteller has the choice of increasing the difficulty by one.

For example, say your character tries to put the fear of God
in adrunk. If she fails, the drunk isn't going to be too impressed
by her the next time, so the difficulty is one greater. If you're so
pigheaded-stubborn that you just have to try a third time, the
Storyteller again adds another one to the difficulty rating.

The Storyteller always has the option to put a limit on the
number of attempts that are allowed; two attempts are common.
Howcan yourcharacter frightenaman who's busy laughingather?

Other examples of when to impose a limit on the retry rule
are when a character attempts to spring himself from the
hoosegow by picking the lock with a hat pin (Repair), when a
character tries climbing a tree (Athletics) or when he tries to
remember the Apache phrase for "I'd like just a little off the
top, thank-you-kindly" (Linguistics).

Thereare times, however, when the Storytellershouldn't impose
limits on successive attempts, such as in combat. It isn't too
frustrating toshoot atand misssomeone —happensall the time. But
if a character is running out of bullets or keeps missing when she's
standing only a few yards from the target, well... More examples of
when to suspend the limit on retries might be when a character sees
something out of the comer of his eye and he has some advance
warning of trouble (Alertness) or when he dodges (Dodge).


OX]

BAAN |

Well, that's done. Those are the rules. This system for rolling
dice is all you really need to know to venture into the Savage
West. All the other rules in this book are the exceptions or they
help you complicate actions as you like. As long as you under-
stand the mechanics presented here, you won't have any trouble
understanding any of the complications that follow.

You think you need some practice? Use the sample charac-
ter in Chapter 5. Megan Forrest is looking to put down a
freakish Bane she thinks has slinked into the physical world
from the Umbra. The Storyteller decides that finding the
creature has a difficulty of 6. Grab two dice because Megan's
Perception is 2, and two more dice because her Alertness is 2.

You now have four dice — not bad, but not stellar, either.
There's not too much danger of failing; if you had two more
dice, you could even settle for an automatic success because a
Dice Pool of six would match your difficulty number. Go ahead
and throw the bones. Tally up your number of successes,
making sure to take away a success for each 1 you roll. Did you
make it or botch? The more successes you get, the sooner
Megan spots the Bane.

However, one success might mean that while Megan senses the
Bane close by, she isn't in position to get a good slash with her klaive.
Three or four could give her a real edge, maybe even let her look it
straight into its one watery good eye and know it for what it really is.

Fun, huh? Try it again, this time doing an extended and
resisted action. An example would be a stare down, where two
werewolves (or hell, even gunfighters) glare into each other's
eyes until one breaks. Victory requires an indefinite number of
rolls, each requiring different difficulties. You need to collect
five successes more than your opponent to win. Don't forget: A
botch cancels all of your accumulated successes, assuming you
don't lose the match automatically.

Life is easier than you think in Werewolf: The
Wild West because there is only one real rule:
There ain't no rules! Like a blacksmith hammers an
ingot into a shoe, so should you make this game
into whatever you need it to be. If the rules get in
your way, toss them out or bang them into shape.
There's no way a set of rules can capture the
grandness and complications of the real world;
that's what yourstorytellingand imagination is for.
The fact is these rules are more like guidelines or
suggestions and you're free to use, abuse, ignore,
shoot or spit on them as you see fit.

Each player rolls Charisma + Intimidation; the difficulty is the
opponent's Willpower. With three dots in Charisma and none in
Intimidation, the stare down is hardly Megan's forte; thankfully,
her Willpower of six doesn't make it easy on her opponent. And
if these numbers seem a little stingy, don't worry; that's what time
and experience are for. Megan'll get betterasshe goesalong, which
is one of the best reasons for continuing to play the game —
watching your fledgling cub evolve into a truly heroic individual.

Examples of Rolls

Here are some ideas and examples on how to use the rules
presented in this chapter. Each Attribute works with each
Ability described in the Traits chapter. That makes over 200
different kinds of simple rolls that can be made. Some combi-
nations won't come up often (Appearance + Dodge springs to
mind as one example), but you never know.

‘w Rattlers offer a gentlemanly warning, but scorpions are a
lot less noisy. Roll Perception + Alertness (difficulty 9) to see
if you notice the bug crawling into your packmate's bedroll.

w You've been riding for hours on end, but you have to keep
going if you're to reach the caern before the cavalrymen come
through. Roll Stamina + Ride (difficulty 6) to keep going.

4 The lynch mob's one of the ugliest collections of humanity
you've ever seen, and you've got to calm them down. Roll
Charisma + Leadership (difficulty 7) as you pontificate on the
value of law and order. Youneed four successes to convince them.

‘ Youtry toshame the ugly drunk for the temerity of standing
close to you. Roll Appearance + Intimidation (difficulty 8).

4 You're keeping watch while your packmates catch some
shut-eye. Make a Stamina + Alertness roll (difficulty 7) to see
how awake and alert you are when bushwhackers approach your
camp in the predawn gloom.

@ After you've been interrogated for hours, roll Stamina +
Acting (difficulty 8) to see if you can keep your story straight. Five
successes and you finally convince them.

te What the hell kinda lingo is that?! Roll Intelligence +
Linguistics (difficulty 6) to figure it out.

tv That traveling salesman talks a pretty smooth pitch. Roll
Perception + Subterfuge (difficulty 8) to see just how much of
it's bunk. If you get five successes, you can call him on his lies
in broad daylight.

@ It's going to take little muscle to get that wagon wheel back
on the axle. Roll Strength + Crafts (difficulty 6).

@ You have to get the children to stop crying before the
mockeries hear you. Roll Wits + Empathy (difficulty 6) to
gently quiet them in time.

Was that a veiled threat or just a slip of the tongue? Roll
Perception + Intimidation (difficulty 6) to see if the fool's just
tongue-tied or truly asking to be killed.

‘w Reining in the runaway horse team takes a lot of
force and expertise. Roll Strength + Animal Ken

(difficulty 9).


‘w A man throws a crate off the coach that you're chasing.
Roll Wits + Animal Ride to see if you can avoid the obstacle
while keeping your horse under control (difficulty 6).

w You need to cut exactly the right amount of sulfur into
your gunpowder. Roll Dexterity + Science (difficulty 5).

tw How long can you sit still in the bushes outside the
stockade as the two guards chat hour after hour? Roll Stamina
+ Stealth (difficulty 7). Each success buys 30 minutes.

w You need to treat the snakebite, and you need to treat it
now. Roll Wits + Medicine (difficulty 7).

W You foolishly try to break into a Pinkerton-protected
vault. Roll Intelligence + Larceny (difficulty 3) to realize that
you can't possibly open the thing.

W You try to convince the Pinkertons that you're not part
of the band of outlaws that broke into the vault. Roll Manipu-
lation + Subterfuge (difficulty 10). (Damned Pinkertons will
probably confiscate all your gear anyway.)

‘w Istheclerk in the general store lying about what happened?
Roll Perception + Investigation (difficulty 7).

‘w The best way to impress these Fianna roughnecks is to
prove you're as talented as you say you are. Roll Charisma +
Melee (difficulty 8) togive thema fancy display ofbladesmanship.

4 The Sun Dance is grueling, to put it mildly, but you've got
to keep going to attract the spirits' attention. Roll
Stamina + Rituals (difficulty 7).

‘w Incarcerated, you try to squeeze out of your leg-irons. Roll
Dexterity + Athletics (difficulty 10). Youneed three successes.
4v Can you sway the hanging judge toward being lenient? Not
likely, but you have to try. Roll Charisma + Law (difficulty 9).
‘w Which berries are edible and which ones are poisonous?
Roll Intelligence + Survival (difficulty 5).

‘w Better hope you're as fast on the draw as you say you are.

Roll Wits + Firearms (difficulty 4).

Downtime: The time spent between scenes when no

ra



Eastward I go only by force; but Westward I go free.
— Henry David Thoreau, Walking

In Werewolf: The Wild West, you have the opportunity to
create the hero of a chronicle, infuse him with personality and
decide his actions. Your character can fight ancient evils, work
to cleanse the land of corruption, or hurl himself against rival
tribes and do his best to strike it rich. He can struggle to save the
dignity of his people, or help settlers tame the American frontier.
Your character's motives shape whatever goal you strive for.
Along with the characters of your fellow players, your character
is the main focus for the Storyteller's chronicle, and has an
accordingly suitable amount of control over a game's direction.

Unlike the usually one-dimensional personae of childhood
games of make-believe, a player character should be a fleshed-
outcreation, like the main character ina novel or movie. Good
characters create their own stories, making the game more
enjoyable for everyone. The more you put into a character,
generally, the more fun you get out of this game.

This chapter details how to design your own individual
character, from choosing a broad concept to calculating num-
bers for the character sheet and on to developing a picture of

the character's personality and past. By the time you're
done, your character will be more than a simple jury-
rigged pile of statistics — she'll be a person.

Getting Started

The character creation system for Werewolf: The Wild West
has five main concepts that players should understand before they
start designing characters. They're pretty simple, really.

‘w Characters can be of any age from any culture or region,
depending on the type of chronicle your Storyteller creates;
however, all beginning characters start as inexperienced and
unsophisticated werewolves, fresh from experiencing their First
Change. Most chronicles take place in the Savage West, and
players should give their characters a reason to be in the
American frontier. Apart from that, you can play a Chinese
character who came over to help work the rails, a touring French
actress, a Tlingit who's come south for a spell — whatever you
and your Storyteller agree is interesting and appropriate.

‘¥ This creation process should help you define your
character's capabilities as well as produce a persona to interact
with others during the game. The numbers ona character sheet
can't fully capture a character's essence, but they should
provide a framework for the player to infuse personality. By the
time you're finished, the integration of Traits and personality
should be pretty unified.

Chapter Five: Character Creation 99

OD OOO %

Peet


w Werewolf: The Wild West uses
apurchase system to generate characters. There >.
are no random elements or dice rolling involved _
in the initial character creation. A player makes a
variety of choices to decide who his character is and
‘what strengths and weaknesses that character has.
This system leaves a character with room to
grow, so don't be surprised if a character can't do
everything you want. As game play proceeds,
characters receive experience points that players
can spend to develop the characters more fully.
‘w Werewolf: The Wild West defines most Traits
with one to five points or "dots" on the character sheet.
If you only have one dot in a Trait, you're a novice or below
average. Three dots in a Trait is slightly above average, and
five dots in a Trait puts an individual into the ranks of the
elite. When purchasing physical Traits, keep in mind
that the physical Traits of werewolves improve drasti-
cally as they change forms. Even a bookish scholar can
hold his own when taking one of the war-forms of the Garou.

>

@ Players have a responsibility to create characters who can cooperate
with the other members of the gaming group. If you consistently disrupt the
stories because your character cannot cooperate with others, you'll probably be
required to create a new character. Life in the Savage West is tough enough,
especially for werewolves. A pack that can't work together is vulture food.

. The Storyteller's Role

As the Storyteller, you should guide players during character creation,

Bi describing the basic premise of the game and introducingplayers to the rules.
See Try to make the players' lives easy; keep things simple for beginners and
don't overwhelm them. Make sure that you tell the players your initial
ideas about the setting and the chronicle so that they can create
characters who have an easy time fitting into your stories. (For
/ example, if you want to run a game focusing on taking caerns back
from the European Garou, and don't tell anybody, you'll more than
likely wind up with a pack of European Garou, and your plans will be
spoiled.) This is especially important to players who are not familiar with
the Wild West or who have preconceived notions that your chronicle will use
theirfavorite spaghetti Westernas the backdrop. The environmentof Werewolf:
The Wild West lendsa unique flavor to chronicles, and it's important to make sure
the players understand your interpretation of the setting. Remember,
their characters are going to be the protagonists of your chronicle.

from the players, and then go through
the process of filling out

offer recommendations. It's important to make sure that three dots in
a Trait mean the same thing to you as to your player. By the same
token, offer them as much leeway as you can. E
guess at first why the burlesque dancer has Survival, her player may
offer an explanation that kicks your imagination into overdrive.

Most Storytellers find it's a good idea to spend an entire game
session on creating characters. Youcan spend thisfirst session running
your players through preludes, a type of abbreviated storytelling to
flesh out their backstories and help develop stronger character
conceptions. Preludes are covered at the end of this chapter.

anything else that helps a player define his choice of

incorporate changes. Storytellersshould approve

making peace with the Pure Ones. (It may make

a little too interesting for novice groups!)

There are any number of possible concepts, even as seem

include: avenger of the wolves, gunfighter, rancher, shaman,
railroad worker, doctor, prospector, missionary, stage-

Chapter Five: Character Creation

Among the werewolves, particularly the lupus, skin color and
gender can often mean much less than they do to humans. Sexism
is rare in sept politics, and typically only homid Garou fall prey to
the racism prevalent throughout the Savage West. Of course,
intolerance among werewolves has led to the current wars over
caerns and the like, but the Storyteller should be wary of overly
punishing a player for the crime of roleplaying an outsider.

Once youhaveageneralconceptin mind, choose yourcharacter's
breed (hi ecies), auspice (role in Garousociety), and tribe (the
group that claims him as a member). These choices help further
define the character in terms of Traits and available options.
f, Carefully consider how your
basic concept works with the
choice of breed, auspice, and
. tribe. Remember that the
purpose of this decision pro-
cessistohelpyoufully
develop a


ZOE,

OXX

character, not to limit your options. You may wish to choose an
auspice that does not fit with the basic concept for the character.
As longas you can rationalize any conflicts and enjoy playing the
character, such decisions tend to make the character more
interesting. Storytellers and fellow players may wish to steer
beginning roleplayers away from characters that are difficult to
play, such as an Ahroun with no fighting skills. Again, this can
be interesting (see the reluctant Ahroun in the Prologue), but
you might want to try out the stereotypes before you break them.

Everyone also needs to bear in mind what sort of characters
their fellow players intend to create. Generally a player should
avoid making a character with abilities or concepts that
overshadow or duplicate those of another player character
without discussing with both the other player and the Story-
teller how the characters will interact and their differences.

‘w Breed: Werewolves are torn between wolves and men,
sharing aspects of both, but truly belonging to neither. When
werewolves reproduce, they breed with either wolves or hu-
mans. Garou born of humans are called homids; those with
wolf parents are lupus. Although it is possible for two Garou
to mate, such liaisons are taboo; the offspring, the metis, are
sterile and deformed.

The children of werewolves aren't usually Garou. For some
unknown reason, the blood often remains latent among
Kinfolk for generations. Therefore, werewolves try to keep
careful track of their bloodlines so that any werewolves born
into these families may be found and raised among fellow
Garou. In the course of westward expansion, many European
tribes have lost track of Kinfolk who were pioneers.
Werewolves who are not watched by their tribes are called
lost cubs. When a lost cub discovers his true nature, he is
alone. He does not have others to guide and comfort him.
Many lost cubs go insane or become outcasts.

Decide whether your character is homid, lupus, or metis.
The choice of a breed colors a character's entire point of view.
Metis characters must select a deformity (see pg. xx for sugges-
tions). There are very few metis in the Savage West, and the
breed is looked down on by almost all werewolves.

‘w Auspice: The phase of the moon a character was born
under determines his auspice. The auspice indicates his
strengths, weaknesses and profession among the Garou. There
are five different auspices, each one with its own unique role
from trickster to warrior. For a complete description of each
auspice, see the Traits chapter.

‘ Tribe: In ancient days, packs of Garou were all composed
of members from one tribe. In the Savage West, packs form
with members from many different tribes. Some enlightened
packs try to maintain a mix of native and European members
in an attempt to avoid conflicts; others only allow members
from one group or the other. Young Garou have found that a
mix of different tribes makes a stronger pack. Garou from
different tribes unite for a variety of reasons. Some share
spiritual beliefs; others have acommon goal, suchas vengeance

against a corrupt cavalry officer, or driving settlers away from
acaern. The Savage West is an unforgiving, mysterious place
where death can hit with the lightning speed of an outlaw's
bullet. Those who travel alone only get to make one mistake.

Each tribe has its own traditions, bloodlines, and markings.
These tribes guard their own Kinfolk and have their own
protective totem-spirits and secret Gifts. A full description of
each tribe is in the Traits chapter.

Step Two:
Choosing Attributes

Now you determine your character's basic Attributes. At-
tributes measure a character's natural prowess. They provide a
measure of his raw potential, although with time and effort, a
character can increase his potential. Attributes answer ques-
tions such as: How accurate a memory does my character have?
How much can he lift? How attractive is he?

You start by prioritizing the three categories of your
character's Attributes: Physical, Mental and Social. Think
about which category is most important for your character
and which is least important. There isn't any particular
hard-and-fast rule to assigning Attributes; just try to stay
true to your original concept.

Physical Attributes cover your character's capacity for
physical feats; they include her speed, toughness, and pure
might. For action-oriented characters, physical Attributes
are often primary. They concern all the strengths and weak-
nesses of the body, both trained and instinctive.

Social Attributes involve the character's natural ability
to interact with others. They determine making first im-
pressions, speaking well, inspiring others and charming
members of the opposite sex.

Mental Attributes involve your character's intellectual prow-
ess. They include her awareness of her world, how well she thinks
on her feet, her talent for logic and reason, and her memory.

All characters start with one dot in each Attribute. Your
priority selection determines how many dots you get to spend in
each category. You may divide seven dots among your character's
primary Attributes, five dots among her secondary Attributes,
and three among her tertiary Attributes. Thus, you may choose
to add seven dots to your character's Physical Attributes, five to
her Mental Attributes, and only three dots to her Social At-
tributes. The dots may be divided among the Attributes of a
category as you see fit; you can assign all three of your Social
Attribute dots to Charisma, one dot to each of the three Social
Attributes, or two dots to one and one dot to another.

Don't spend too much time agonizing over how to assign the
last few dots in any category. You'll get freebie points to spend
later in the character creation process, and with time,
you'll gain experience points. Remember, your char-
acter needs room to grow.

West, and always going by an outland- Step Four: Advantages

Many players spend as much time

You prioritize Abilities in the same way as Attributes.
However, unlike Attributes, characters do not start with a
free dot in any of their Abilities. Also, a player may only raise
an Ability up toa maximum of three (although freebie points
may be used later to raise Abilities up to 5). The primary
choice has 13 dots, the secondary category receives 9, and the
final category gains five.

It's strongly advised thatall homid characters in Werewolf:
The Wild West have at least a single point in Ride. It's just
the most reliable way to get around.

ish name tends to draw some notice. — : ;
You do not prioritize or rank the different categories of

Advantages. You instead have a designated number of dots to
assign within each one. Though this number is fixed, you can
purchase additional dots later with freebie points.


Backgrounds

Every character gets five points to allocate among the 10 Back-
ground Traits. You have only five points to allot, and some tribes
restrict access to certain Backgrounds. Your Background Traits
should fit into the general scheme of the original concept. Back-
grounds are Advantages based on the character's past; essentially,
they reflect where you come from and what you bring with you.

Gifts

Beginning Werewolf characters may also choose three
Gifts: the special secrets and abilities of the Garou. You begin
with one Gift each from your breed, auspice, and tribe (three
total). You may purchase more Gifts with freebie points, but
they must be Level One Gifts.

Renown

Renown is fundamental to werewolves, for it describes how
famous they are among their own society. You begin with a certain
amount (determined by auspice) of permanent Renown in three
different categories: Glory, Honor and Wisdom. Characters begin
with no temporary points; they must earn these during play.

Rank

A character's Rank is determined by the amount of perma-
nent Renown possessed. Rank determines the character's station
and trustworthiness in Garou society, as well as her adherence to
the virtues thereof. All characters begin at Rank One.

Step Five:
Finishing Touches

Often the most important stage of character creation is apply-
ingthe final touches, the little details and flourishes that complete
the character. In this step, you gain your 15 "freebie" points
enabling you toadd more dots toany of the Traits on the character
sheet. Before you spend these points, however, you must record
the base scores for your character's Rage, Gnosis and Willpower.

Rage

Rage quantifies the inner fury of a werewolf. It is the fuel of the
warrior. Rage lets Garou react more often in combat, shift forms
at will, and heal crippling or even lethal wounds. However, a
high Rage makes a werewolf more likely to frenzy and lose control
of her bestial side. Your auspice determines your initial Rage.

Gnosis

Gnosis measures how attuned a character is to the spirit world
and her own spiritual self. Gnosis provides the key to using many
Gifts and conducting Rites. Breed determines starting Gnosis.

Willpower

Willpower measures how much control a character has over
herself and her actions. It also provides a measure of inner strength
andself-confidence, allowinga character tosucceed in situations that
she otherwise may have failed. Willpower is determined by tribe.

Werewolf: The Wild West

ZLELs

Freebie Points

Finally, you have 15 freebie points that you can spend to
improve your Traits. The Character Creation Chart shows the
freebie costs involved to improve a Trait. You can also raise
Abilities over three dots with freebie points.

Spark of Life

So much for rules; now for the fun part. Once all the dotsare filled
inon the character sheet, it's time to polish up the concept. By adding
more details, you create a more complete picture of your character.

Appearance

So, what sort of first impressions will your character make? A
strong description makes it easier for other players to envision the
character. When creating your character's appearance, keep her
Traits in mind. A Garou with a high Dexterity may have a graceful
walk. A character with a high Intelligence may have strong gaze, a
bored look, or a strange self-confidence. A werewolf with a strong
Perception may constantly seem edgy, as if listening to things
ordinary folks can't hear. By evoking Traits to guide a description,
a player creates a stronger image and a more interesting character.

Also consider how the character dresses and grooms herself. In
the 19th century, most people wear hats. Whatsortofhatdoes your
character wear? Does she prefer wearing dresses or is she the type
of woman who puts on a pair of pants (sure to draw attention and
scandal)? If she's a Wendigo or Uktena, does she wear traditional
clothing, European styles or a mixture of both? Is there a particular
color, style or item that the character always likes to wear?

Specialties

You can also assign specialties to any extraordinary Attributes or
Abilities (scores of 4 or higher). These specialties help describe and
define the character, adding qualities to bare numbers. In some
circumstances, the Storyteller may give you a lower difficulty, rerolls
or an extra die if a specialty seems particularly appropriate. For
example, a player may choose to give her Firearms Ability the
specialty: Quick Draw. Another player may have the specialty: Rifles
orTrick Shooting. Ifyouwant, youshouldn'thesitate toadd specialties
to lowerscores; ifa specialty adds toa story, the Storyteller may reward
youwithabonusofsomenature. But whether yougetarulesadvantage
or not, a specialty allows you and your fellow group members to better
envision the character. That's justification enough.

Equipment

If your character has any special equipment or possessions, you
should take some time to work out the details of these items. Fetishes
should certainly be defined, but even mundane equipment deserves
mention. A favorite rifle or shotgun could have a name, a piece of
clothing could hold special significance, or a locket worn by your
character could be herlast tie toa family left behind in the East. Items
are very precious in the Wild West, and not easily replaced.
Werewolves rely less on material objects to get by, buteven
they can't afford to keep buying equipment to replace the
things they leave behind them. »


Horses

In Werewolf: The Wild West, almost every man, woman,
and child know how toridea horse, regardless of whether they
own one or not. If your character owns a horse, you should
name the animal, describe it, and give it a few special personality
traits. A good horse is worth his weight in gold out on the frontier.
Quirks

Quirks make a character unique. Think about any personal stories a
character may have, particular sayings she's overly fond of or habits that she can't
give up. Don't go overboard with quirks and make her silly or unplayable, but
give heras much personality as possible. Maybe she always sits at the bar in
asaloon. Doesshe have a favorite color that she wears? Doesshe always
swear by the ghost of her grandmother? Does she like to watch the

stagecoaches come into town? Does she refuse to let anyone else touch
her horse? Does she always grunt when she means yes? You get the idea.

Motivations

Everyone has their own goals and beli aracters are nO
different. In Werewolf: The Wild West chronicles, each
characterhas his ownstory totell. Sure, there's the war forthe
West, but does your character have any more-personal
motivations for what she does? Each character's personal story
can be woven into a subplot connected with the main plotline;
everyone has their own conflicts to resolve. Perhaps one character
is unable to cope with the conflict between aiding his packmates of
different European tribes and the suffering that pioneers blindly inflict
on his native tribe. Another character may have a romantic
subplot, such as a Kinfolk lover or (Litany forbid) feelings
for another Garou. And a common theme of Westems is
the character who's looking for revenge; this hook works
just as well, if not better, for an easily angered werewolf.

Double Life

One of the most frightening things about werewolves is that
they look just like ordinary people. Who knows, even the town drunk
could be a supernatural killer after sundown. Does your character main-
tain a normal identity, separate from his life as a Garou? How does he
maintain his masquerade? What measures does he take to protect his loved
ones? How does he resolve conflicts between the obligations to his people
and his duties as a Garou? Does anyone know about his dual nature?

ISB Ver al
SNK)

{

As everyone knows, handling a normal life can be quite a chore by itself.
Adding the trials of life on the western frontier to the conflicts of the Garou has
advantages and disadvantages. On one hand, if a mob of railroad men
decides to move you off your property, the power of the nine-foot war-form >.
can be very useful. It's the same if you get trapped ina collapsed mine; the
strength of a mad bull and claws to dig out the rubble are a definite
bonus. On the other hand, if you are trying to hunt buffalo and Banes
keep driving them away, you may cause your people to starve.

The worst part of being Garou is not being able to control
yourself. In a single moment of unchecked anger, a werewolf

can savage her lover, ripping him into blood-flecked
chunks of meat and bone. Werewolves are a constant
threat to those around them. Wolves are very edgy around their

killing 10 to 20 of his old friends in a single frenzied moment.
However, most Garou don't want to give up friendship or love for
loneliness or isolation. Some cling desperately to their old lives, but
most attempts to lead normal lives ultimately result in tragedy.

Example of
Character Creation

The game's afoot! More specifically, Tashia has been in-
vited to join in a Werewolf: The Wild West game, and it's
time for her to put a character together. With a copy of the
character sheet and the assistance of her Storyteller, Mark, she
starts designing.

Step One: Concept

Mark explains his ideas for the setting to Tashia and reviews
the character sheet with her. She starts thinking immediately
of possible concepts. Tashia decides that she wants to play a
woman who has lost her family and who is trying to hold on to
her family ranch by herself, maybe with the help of a small
number of ranch hands. For starters, Tashia comes up with a
name for her character, Megan Forrest.

Tashia's pretty much already decided on a homid character.
She figures it will be difficult enough getting used to the Savage
West, and she can't figure out what a lupus would do with a
ranch. She then chooses Ahroun as Megan's auspice, because
she likes the idea of playing a character who's a fighter, tough
enough to protect her ranch from all sorts of enemies.

Finally, Tashia chooses Fianna as her tribe, although she almost
chooses the Black Furies. She decides that being Fianna gives her
charactera bit of history, and she makesanote thatherfamily had lost
their traditional home overseas and moved to the American West.

Step Two: Attributes

Tashia decides that Megan is primarily a physical person.
She works her ranch, loves getting into fights, and can out-
draw most of the men in the nearby town of Dry Rock. Tashia
wants Megan to be able to ride well in addition to being a good
shot, so Dexterity is very important to her. She places three
dots in Dexterity, giving Megan a starting score of 4. She then
spends two points in Stamina and two in Strength, giving her
a total of three dots in each: above average.

Tashia then makes Social Attributes her second priority.
Tashia wants Megan to be able to hold her own against the
prejudice and ridicule thata single woman runninga ranch on the
frontier would suffer. Tashia imagines Megan having an almost
intuitive understanding of theanimalson herranch, especially her
horse. She spends two points in Charisma, two points in Appear-

100

Werewolf: The Wild West

3, and 2 respectively. Megan does okay until she runs her mouth.
She has a tendency to say a bit more than she ought. Megan also
has a bit of an honest streak, the sort that compels her to give her
true opinions on a matter even when it gets her into trouble.

Finally, Tashia places her three points in Mental At-
tributes. She wants Megan to have a decent amount of Wits
and Perception, but she doesn't have the points to raise her
dots. She puts one dot in each. Mark advises Tashia that she
will have some freebie points later on, and Megan will earn
experience and can always improve.

Step Three: Abilities

Tashia spends her time looking over the list of Abilities.
Although several are self-explanatory, she has a few questions
about how Cultures, Enigmas, Primal-Urge, and Rituals come
into play. Mark gives examples of how the different Abilities
works in his chronicle. He promises to let Tashia move some
points around at the end of the first game session if she doesn't
like her Abilities. (A note for Storytellers: This is an excellent
idea. In many cases, any changes a player makes after getting a
feel for the character are almost always for the better.) In the
end, Tashia decides to make Skills Megan's primary choice,
Talents secondary, Knowledges tertiary.

She has 13 points to fill in her Skills; however, she's limited
toamaximum score of three dots in any single Skill. She spends
three dots in Firearms, two in Melee, three in Ride, two in
Animal Ken, one in Leadership, one in Crafts and one dot in
Survival. Tashia feels that the combat skills fit in with her idea
of Megan as a tough lady holding her own ranch and with her
Ahroun auspice. Animal Ken, Crafts and Survival are simply
appropriate for anyone trying to get by on the frontier.

Tashia has nine points to use in purchasing Talents. After
groaning about all the Abilities she wants and briefly consid-
ering changing Talents to primary, she spends her points: two
points each in Alertness, Athletics, and Dodge; a single point
each in Brawl and Empathy.

For her Knowledges, she spends a single point in Enigmas,
because she wants Megan to be able to look at problems from
different sides. She places a point each in Linguistics and
Culture so she can start developing a friendly relationship with
local Apaches (who happen to be the Kinfolk of a fellow player
character). She places a point in Medicine, because Tashia
decides Megan doesn't trust doctors. She puts a final point in
Law so Megan can defend her claim to her land.

Step Four: Advantages

Tashia then moves on to Megan's Advantages and what she
can do as a werewolf. First, Megan has five points for her
Backgrounds. She buys a Kinfolk (the one ranch hand who
hasn'trun out on her) forasingle point. Tashia wants someone
who isn't going to abandon Megan no matter what
may happen. She spends two points on Resources for
her ranch, and two points on Fetish, a heirloom r


passed down from her Celtic ancestors. Tashia decides not to
define the heirloom completely (she wants to write a good
history for it), but she and Mark agree that it is a knife of some
nature, something like a Fang Dagger or klaive. Mark reminds
her that she'll have freebie points to raise her Background to a
point where she can buy either of those.

For Gifts, Tashia looks over the ones open to Megan.
Comparing her choices for breed Gifts with her choices for
tribal Gifts, she notices that Persuasion is listed under both
categories. Tashia decides Megan needs Persuasion (to make
up for her lousy speaking ability in important situations) for her
breed Gift and decides on Resist Toxin as her tribal Gift. Her
other choice as a Breed Gift, Jam Gun, might prove useful, but

Tashia decides Megan would rather use Resist Toxin, her tribal
choice, to drink people under the table. She briefly considers
dropping Persuasion and taking Jam Gun instead, but Tashia
likes talking and is sure Megan will need Persuasion to get out
of trouble at some point in the chronicle. If there's physical
trouble, an Ahroun like Megan ought to be able to handle it
without Gifts. For her auspice Gift, she chooses Inspiration.
Tashia feels Megan will be capable enough in combat with the
klaive she wants as a fetish, so she thinks Inspiration will give
her future packmates an extra reason to like her character.

The next step is to assign beginning Renown. She checks
the list for an Ahroun and marks down 3 Glory. She is now a
Rank One Garou.

Morn, Cearacrzas

So, what if you don't want to play a werewolf? What if you want to play a Kinfolk in the Savage
West, or if you want to just be a normal person caught up in a supernatural hortor story he didn't ask
for? What if you don't want to run a supernatural game at all — just a classic Western?

Well, you certainly don't have to play a werewolf if you don't want to. The supernatural threats
are all the more horrific to characters who don't have Gifts, regeneration ora Crinos form to get them
out of trouble, and even the most mundane threats become 10 times as dangerous. The following
guidelines are recommended for creating mortal characters in Werewolf: The Wild West chronicles,
or even for generic Westerns without even a hint of the supernatural.

Mortal characters prioritize their Attributes and Abilities as do werewolves, but they don't getas many
dots (after all, their potential is somewhat less). Mortals get six dots to divide among their primary
Attributes, four for secondary Attributes and three for tertiary Attributes. By the same token, they get only
11, seven and four dots for their primary, secondary and tertiary Abilities, respectively. Naturally, mortals
receive no Gnosis, Rage, Gifts or other supernatural powers; their starting Willpower rating is 3. By way
of compensation, mortal characters receive 21 freebie points rather than the initial 15.

The real trouble comes when you get to Backgrounds. For obvious reasons, mortals cannot
purchase Fetish, Kinfolk, Past Life, Pure Breed, Rites or Totem. This leaves only Allies, Contacts,
Mentor and Resources. The Storyteller may want to invent some additional Backgrounds for you to
purchase — for example, Backing (meaning you have some authority vested in you by the state,
federal government, or some other powerful institution), or Territory (meaning you have a valid
claim to a lot of land even if you aren't rich in your own right).

Certain Abilities
soul, can't be learned by ordinary people. Likewise, ordinary humans can't learn Garou rites; although
aKinfolk might learn something of the Rituals Knowledge, it's likely to be all but useless to a normal
person. Also remember that unless you're playing a Kinfolk, your character is going to be subject to
the Delirium's effects to a degree dependent on his Willpower.

are also off-limits to mortals. Primal-Urge, as a connection to the shapeshifter

(It might even be interesting to create mortal characters with the intention of eventually going through
the First Change, and then updating their character sheets accordingly. This gives players the chance toreally
get used to a character before he changes, making the shift to a werewolf perspective all the more dramatic.)
