Step Five: Finishing Touches

Tashia records Megan's initial Rage of 5 (becauseshe'san * - . : Feats
Ahroun), her initial Gnosis of 1 (because she's a homid), :
and her starting Willpower of 3 (because she's a Fianna).

Tashia finally gets to spend Megan's 15 freebie \?
points. She spends two points on her Fetish Back- ;
ground (raising it to 4) so she can have her klaive. She
spendsa pointand raises her Resources to three, giving
Megan a small stash of money to keep her little ranch
going fora while longer. Tashia also spends a point on
the Totem Background as a way of showing other
players that she cares about their pack. Now she has 11
points left to spend.

She wants to have a higher score in Wits and Perception,
which are Attributes and cost five points per dot. Shealso wants
a higher Gnosis score (two points per dot) and a higher Will-
power (only one point per dot). Tashia knows that she wants
more Abilities as well, but she decides not to look at her Abilities
because she already isn't sure what to buy. Tashia finally purchases
four points of Gnosis for eight points, giving her a score of five so
she can feel fairly certain of activating her klaive. She spends her
final three points on Willpower, giving her a headstrong score of six.

Now for the finishing touches. Tashia decides that Megan likes
to wear blue. She gives Megan dark auburn hair and gray eyes.
Tashia considers trying to speak with a bit of an Irish brogue during
game sessions, but after practicing gives up the voice for a future
character. Tashia thinks about a Garou name for Megan, but she
decides to wait for a Rite of Passage or a significant event to create
aname. Finally, Tashiacalls Megan's favorite horse Wildfire, after
an old song, and makes hima roan. The other details will wait for
Megan's prelude and further development during the chronicle.
Tashia is pleased, because she feels that she has a good charac-
ter and Megan definitely has room to grow. E

The Long Run

Asyouplayacharacter, you will add to the character's
personality. A good character takes on a life of her own;
she develops habits, nuances and a distinct voice. A
memorable character crafts her own stories, inspiring
the Storyteller and fellow characters.

Expanding your character's capabilities with experi-
ence points is only one of the ways in which she'll grow.
Spend her experience wisely; don't give her Abilities she
shouldn't logically have, but rather expand on what she
should be logically learning. If you want to increase her
Stamina, have her undergo some sort of hardships or heavy work
that would properly increase her endurance. If you want to purchase more 7
Gnosis, perhaps a series of visionquests is in order, all the better to put your
character in touch with her spiritual side. Sometimes you'll even be
struck by the fact that your character "won't let" you spend her
experience points any other way than on whatshe's been working on. [

This just means she's becoming a more vivid personality, and will be »

all the more fun to play. N49]

102 Werewolf: The Wild West


As your character develops, try to look at the world through
her eyes. Character creation is an ongoing process. As time
goes by, characters change, mature and become more capable.
As they become more developed, they become more complex.
A character is a work of art. The more work you put into your
character, the better the finished product will be.

The Prelude

The purpose of a prelude is to give each character a past. By
having a history to draw on, you give your character a sense of
reality that you can draw on during game sessions. You don't
have to worry about figuring out who your character is during the
first game session. Instead, you can concentrate on the story.

Inaddition, a prelude providesan opportunity fora Storytellerand
a player to grow accustomed to each other's interpretations of the
tules. Asaplayer, youcan ask questions that would normally interrupt
a game session. The Storyteller can learn about your character and
find out what sort of motivations and strengths she has.

Questions and Answers

Here are some question and answers that players and Story-
tellers should at least consider during a prelude.

How old are you?

Age is the cornerstone of a character's experience and poten-
tial. Age changes a character's outlook. Are you young,
inexperienced and naive? Are you a grizzled gunslinger tired of
the challenges of the frontier, just looking for a way to settle
down? The only real limitations imposed on age in Werewolf:
The Wild West are those brought on by the First Change. You
probably experienced your First Change just after puberty or
early in your adult life. Few characters Change in middle age, and
none should transform as children. Most lost cubs undergo the
First Change alone and live in ignorance of their true nature for
many years until encountering a fellow Garou — if ever.

‘wv What was your family life like?

The first relationships you form are with the members of your
family. The family teaches a character how to interact with others.
Did you have a difficult family life? (You probably did — the stresses
of the frontier test a family's endurance, and when your relatives
started noticing that you're somehow different, you may have been
treated rather harshly.) Who were the members of your family?
Rememberthata family doesn't have tobe made upof blood relations.
Are any members of your family still alive? If so, how do they see you.
Areyou the favorite son or the family embarrassment? Areany of your
family kin, and if so, do they know what's really going on?

‘ When did you have your First Change?

The First Change is one of the defining moments of a young
Garou's life. With it comes the absolute certainty that you're
someone different and special. Once you've experienced the First

Change, your life is forever changed. What were the circum-
stances of the First Change — what stresses drove you to

€ shift forms? What were your first actions as a full-fledged

Chapter Five: Character Creation

werewolf? Did you kill someone? How did you come to terms with
the knowledge that you wereashapechanger? Was your first reaction
driven by superstition, logic, religion or something else?

‘When did you meet the others in the pack?

Players may want to roleplay through first meetings together
in their preludes. A Storyteller may want to have other group
members very briefly make cameo appearances in a player's
prelude. The pack members should work out the details of the
meeting with the help of the Storyteller. In many cases, it's best
if you've met and formed relationships before the start of the
chronicle. Have you had your Rite of Passage yet?

wv Where do you live now?

What sort of culture do you come from? Although the
chronicle determines geographically where you live, you need
to decide what kind of home or den you keep. Do you live as a
human, a wolf, or do you stay separate from other beings? Do
you dwell in an abandoned mine, an old cave, a house on the
prairie or behind a waterfall? What sort of home do you keep?
Is it dirty or neat? What sort of features make your lair special?

Do you try to lead a normal life?

Do you still interact with people (or wolves) you knew
before your Rite of Passage? Do any non-Garou or Kinfolk
friends know that you're a werewolf? Do other Garou know
about your moonlighting in a normal life?

Details of your normal life can be very important to the
chronicle. Countless stories can be based on events in your
mundane life, like the problems created by juggling more than one
identity. Remember, ahigh Rage score makes sustaining a normal
lifealmost impossible. Relationships become increasingly difficult
as you have to protect your loved ones from yourself.

What motivates you?

Thisisabig one. Every character should have several personal
motivations. Once a prelude is complete, you may gain some
new goals and desires. These may range from being recognized as
a better man than your father or preserving a dying way of life.

Your primary motivations are central to understanding who and
what youare. Once you've Changed, your priorities probably change
drastically. Things that were once important no longer are, and new
values dominate your life. If you want to have a real and complete
character, you must have an understanding of what keeps you from
depression and total aimlessness (which can also be interesting to
play; just decide that your character has no motivations at all).

The Pack

You have come so far, changed skins for furs, and still you do not
understand. You are no longer Shoshoni, Ute, red, white, black.
You are not even men and women. Youare now Garou, and should
not squabble like noisy birds. You must no longer be many, but now
must be one. There is no time for such fighting when the sky is on fire!
Come look at your brothers and sisters. You are now a family. You
must now become a pack. Here, I will show you the way.

— Torn-Two-Hands, Uktena Philodox


Werewolf: The Wild West is not a game with any
winners or hopefully, any losers. The object of Werewolf:
The Wild West is to tell a good story and have fun. A well-
played character rewards his player far more than any
token of victory. Players who spend their time competing
with other group members miss out on the real excitement
of Werewolf stories.

The most important thing to a werewolf is her pack. The
pack is the core unit of Garou society. Packmates share a bond
stronger than blood; a strong, unified pack seems to think and
workas one. The members have complete trust in one another,

"ef

ff
4)

that player and her character.

tuna prelude,

even if they are at odds. Packmates are tied together with a
single spirit. For werewolves (beings who live their lives
separated from man and wolf), the pack offers a point of
stability. In many ways, the pack is the only true home a
werewolf has. The pack is a family that chooses its members.

A pack is more than just a group of close-knit Garou. Packs
have their own identity. A pack develops its own personality
and builds its own legends. The spirit of the pack gains power
and prestige as the pack performs greater and greater deeds. A
powerful pack can outlive its founders and weave itself into
the songs of the Galliards, inspiring future generations.

RUNNING A PREWDE

Each player should undergo a prelude alone. This allows you, as the Storyteller, to fully focus on )

Wo
in

)

<5

You should exert more control over the prelude than over the actual game sessions. When you

ou set scenes for the player from her character's life, and then start them in motion. 4
These scenes can represent turning points in the character's life, or they can just be normal day to
day events — anything that helps a player develop a better character.

During the prelude, you shouldn't be too concerned with Traits or game mechanics. At some
point, you should allow the player a chance to roll dice and learn how to use the game system in a
relatively pressure free situation. Let the player interact with the environment of the chronicle, and
give the player chances to ask questions. A player who isn't sure of the setting will spend game
sessions wondering about Texas Longhorns and cattle drives instead of paying attention to the story
unfolding. If a player has a game mechanics question or a question about the setting that you can't
answer, make sure you get the answer to the player before the first game session.

By the same token, don't let the dice get in the prelude's way —feel free to ignore them whenever
necessary. If part of a character's backstory is the untimely death of his mother, you don't want the player
to make a miraculously lucky Medicine roll and save her, leaving you both at a loss for what to do next.

Remember that you and the player are creating a story together. Stop and ask the player questions
about her character without breaking a scene. Make certain that the player is comfortable with the
game, and that you are comfortable with her character before you end the prelude.

Preludes give a Storyteller a chance to practice before running an entire group. You can bounce i\)
ideas off players one at a time and see their reactions. You have an opportunity to develop a
character's allies and Kinfolk without distracting players from the chronicle.

Appreludecan begin innocuously enough. Itmightbegin something like: "It's been weekssince you'veso much
asseena buffalo, much less tasted its meat. The hunters have brought back some small game, but there hasn'tbeen
enough to go around. You're hungry, and have wandered away from the camp to try and scrounge some food.
Suddenly, you hear a shriek — one of the younger women? — and a sound like thunder. ..a gunshot..." \)

Just as character development never ends, you shouldn't be afraid to run a flashback session to )
add some history to a character's life. If a long period of game time passes in a chronicle without
actions from the characters, you may want to run mini-preludes to cover the lost time. \

SPR

2


Creating a Pack

One of the most important tasks when creating a chronicle
is to define the pack to which the characters belong. This isn't
an idle task, for you must think ahead and prepare for the type
of stories you want to tell.

It's very important for the players and the Storyteller to
work together on the pack. The pack can become the central
character of the chronicle, the thing that unites the players
over the long haul. The players should all agree on what kind
of pack they would like to join, and thus what sorts of stories
they would enjoy telling. Anything the Storyteller can do to
empower the players and involve them in the story is good. For
example, even the basic concept of "misfits even among
Garou" can spark a wild and boisterous motley of Bone Gnaw-
ers, Ragabash and exiles.

When cubs undergo their first Rite of Passage, they often
form packs. The Rite of Passage provides a good opportunity for
characters to join together and pledge one another support for
the first time. Naturally, most packs are formed from a single
tribe, but the growing menace of the Storm Eater's scions
inspire cooperation from tribes that would normally be rivals.
Besides, the players' characters are expected to be the excep-
tion to the norm — that's why the story is about them, isn't it?

The following are a few questions that ought to be answered
byall players afterall characters have completed their preludes.

wv Where is the pack based?

The pack should have a home base or territory, whether it
be an abandoned mining camp, a bustling prairie town or even
a mesa miles from human territory. Usually the territory
revolves around a caern, but it doesn't have to. Many packs set
up a secret meeting or gathering site to base themselves, and
sometimes to store supplies. A pack may even base itself in the
Storm Umbra instead of the physical world — but this is so
dangerous it's hardly worth mentioning.

‘wv How do the pack members interact?

The internal politics of a pack add excitement to the game.
Do some members of the pack have a friendly rivalry? Maybe
the Ahrouns compare kills. Are there close friendships? Are
any of the characters related? Do two of the pack's members
secretly wish they could be lovers? Do the pack members dislike
each other? Dosome pack members have secrets that they hold
back from the others? Which members do packmates trust with
their problems?

‘ What are the pack's missions?

Every pack has its own unique selection of skills and abilities.
Some packs are better at scouting enemies and guerrilla warfare,
while others prefer intimidation and direct assaults. A pack may
choose an area to protect, or it may range across the western
territories, doing its best to fight the Wyrm wherever it hides.

Some packs interact with humans and struggle against the

encroaching tides of humanity, while others spend

their times staving off the corruption of spirit realms.

‘ Is there a pack leader?

Every pack has a way of making decisions. Do you choose
the strongest or wisest as alpha in the manner of wolves? This
choice could be made by voting or by ritual combat. Have you
settled on a permanent leader, or are you quick to battle
among yourselves for the position whenever you hit a lull? A
few packs never settle on a leader. They just let the most
experienced or knowledgeable Garou take command when
the situation warrants.

‘¥ Who is the pack's totem?

A vital part of any pack is its totem. The spirit represents the
pack and binds all members of the pack together. All players
should work together in choosing a totem-spirit that embodies
the pack. They should also work out why the spirit chose them.
Although there are many totems suggested in this book, groups
should not be afraid to design their own.

‘ What is their sept like?

How does sept affiliation affect the pack? Is the sept at war,
or does it enjoy peace with its neighbors? Is it multitribal,
xenophobic, or somewhere between the two? Are the sept
Garou demanding? Do they order or lord over the pack? Does
the pack belong to any sept? Is the sept involved in its own
crises? Does the sept have an active interest in the pack or
could it care less?

‘¥ Who are the pack's friends and allies?

This includes any Kinfolk or Allies listed in a character's
backgrounds. Who are these individuals? What are their
different relationships with the members of the pack? How
much do they know about the pack's activities? How actively
involved are they in the pack's activities? What are their
motivations and goals? Why are they friends of the pack?
How far are they willing to go to help their allies? What do
they get out of the relationship?

Who are the enemies of the pack?

Do the Garou know who these enemies are? Is the enmity a
strong rivalry or a full-blown hatred? Is the hostility based on
fact ora misunderstanding? Perhaps the enemies aren't directly
opposed to the pack, but serving cross purposes. Do any
recurring villains pop up to harass the heroes?

‘ What is the pack's purpose?

Why was the pack formed? The pack is a concept lodged
deeply in Garou thought. Garou society understands its mem-
bers only in terms of the pack; those without packs are either
pitied or cursed. Only the tribe elders live without packs, and
they dissolve their sacred groups only after they have discov-
ered, and hopefully fulfilled, their true purpose.

According to Garou tradition, each and every pack has a
purpose given to it by Gaia, a purpose for which its members are
brought together, a purpose they are meant to serve. Indeed,
packs sometimes share the same dreams when they sleep, and
are guided by the same spirits; only when the purpose is fulfilled
can they peacefully retire from active life.

Character Creation Process

‘v Step One: Character Concept

Choose concept, breed, auspice, and tribe

‘¥ Step Two: Select Attributes

Prioritize the three categories: Physical, Social, Mental (7/5/3)

Choose Physical Traits; Strength, Stamina, Dexterity

Choose Social Traits: Charisma, Manipulation, Appearance

Choose Mental Traits: Perception, Intelligence, Wits

‘¥ Step Three: Select Abilities

Prioritize the three categories: Talents, Skills, Knowledges

Choose Talents, Skills, Knowledges (13/9/5)

‘v Step Four: Select Advantages

Choose Backgrounds (5; restricted by tribe), Gifts (1 each from
breed, auspice, and tribe), Renown (by auspice)

Step Five: Finishing Touches

Record Rage (by auspice), Gnosis (by breed), Willpower (by
tribe), Rank (1)

Spend "Freebie Points" (15)

See also Tribes, pgs. 114-125.

‘w Black Furies: An almost all-female tribe, the Furies are
the servants of the Wyld and defenders of women.

Initial Willpower: 3

Backgrounds: No restrictions.

Beginning Gifts: Sense Wyrm, Song of the Seasons

‘w Bone Gnawers: Drifters, vagrants and the salt of the
earth, Bone Gnawers are survivors who see everything but
garner little respect.

Initial Willpower: 4

Backgrounds: Bone Gnawers may not buy Past Life, Pure
Breed or Resources.

Beginning Gifts: Odious Aroma, Stone Soup

‘# Children of Gaia: The Children of Gaia seek unity
among all the tribes, and consider themselves the defenders of
humanity. Theirs is the choice to heal before harming.

Initial Willpower: 4

Backgrounds: No restrictions.

Beginning Gifts: Mother's Touch, Resist Pain

‘@ Fianna: Immigrants from the British Isles, they violently
protect the land settled by their Kinfolk and believe that they
are the only ones who can fix the Storm Umbra.

Initial Willpower: 3

Backgrounds: No restrictions.

Beginning Gifts: Persuasion, Resist Toxin

‘v Get of Fenris: Honorable but ferocious berserkers, the
Get still preserve their Norse tribal culture. They have great
courage and are willing to battle the Wyrm no matter how
small their chances.

Initial Willpower: 3

Backgrounds: Get of Fenris may not buy Contacts or Mentor.

Beginning Gifts: Razor Claws, Safe Haven

‘# Iron Riders: The least trusted among other Garou, this
tribe has embraced human technology. They live in large
settlements and speak out in favor of "civilization."

Initial Willpower: 3

‘w Red Talons: Composed entirely of lupus, the Red Talons
have come to the New World to protect it from the human
pioneers who would destroy it.

Initial Willpower: 3

Backgrounds: Red Talons may not buy Allies, Contacts or
Resources. Their only Kinfolk are wolves.

‘w Shadow Lords: Domineeringand demanding, the Shadow
Lords seek to wrest the position of leadership from the Silver
Fangs. They care nothing for the unfit or ignorant.

Initial Willpower: 3

Backgrounds: Shadow Lords may not buy Allies or Mentor.

Beginning Gifts: Aura of Confidence, Fatal Flaw

Silent Striders: Mysterious travelers, the Silent Striders
spend little time in one place. They have secret knowledge of
the mortal and spirit worlds.

Initial Willpower: 3

Backgrounds: Silent Striders may not buy Past Life or Resources.

Beginning Gifts: Sense Wyrm, Speed of Thought

w Silver Fangs: The most prestigious of the tribes, the Fangs
only mate with the noblest of humans and wolves. Though they
are badly inbred, they still seek to unite all Garou under their
leadership. The Silver Fangs are often blinded by pride.

Initial Willpower: 3

Backgrounds: Silver Fangs must spend at least three Back-
ground points on Pure Breed.

Beginning Gifts: Eminent Domain, Sense Wyrm

*w Stargazers: The rarest of Garou, the Stargazers search the
world for enlightenment and truth, staunchly opposing the
Wyrm wherever they encounter it.

Initial Willpower: 5

Backgrounds: Stargazers may not buy Allies, Fetish or Resources.

Beginning Gifts: Balance, Threads of the Weaver

w Uktena: The Uktena are one of the two surviving
American tribes. They are the most capable mystics of the
Garou, and are mistrusted and feared by European werewolves.

Initial Willpower: 3

Backgrounds: No restrictions.

Beginning Gifts: Cloud of Night, Sense Medicine

4w Wendigo: The Wendigo are fiercely independent and wage
war to drive the European invaders from North America. Only the
threats of the Weaver, Wyrm, and the Storm Eater take precedence.

Initial Willpower: 4

Backgrounds: Wendigo may not buy Contacts or Resources.

Beginning Gifts: Call the Breeze, Song of the Seasons

Chapter Five: Character Creation

Backgrounds

See also Backgrounds, pg. 134.

v Allies: Your friends among normal humans and wolves.

‘¥ Contacts: The contacts you possess in human society.

‘# Fetish: A magical object containing a bound spirit. See
the Appendix for examples.

‘¥ Kinfolk: Your Delirium-free human or wolf relatives and
friends.

‘w Mentor: A strong elder who guides and watches out for you.

‘# Past Life: Your connection to your Garou ancestors and
the past.

te Pure Breed: Your lineage and pedigree among the Garou.

w Resources: The amount of wealth or belongings that you
possess.

w Rites: The number and/or potency of the rites you have
learned. See Rites, pg. 166.

‘¥ Totem: The amount of power you have invested into
your pack totem. The greater the total number of points spent
by the pack, the more powerful the totem. See Totem, pg. 137,
for point costs and the Appendix for sample totems.

Gifts

See Breed, Auspice; and Tribe above for beginning Gifts;
choose one from each list. See also Gifts, pg. 137.

Renown
See Auspice for beginning Renown. See also Renown, pg. 178.

Rank

All characters begin as Rank One.
Rage, Gnosis, and Willpower

See auspice for Rage, breed for Gnosis, and tribe for
Willpower. See also Rage, pg. 180; Gnosis, pg. 182; and
Willpower, pg. 183.

Homid

Those born of humanity are the most common ofall the Garou.
Their relative numbers have been increasing ever since humans
invented agriculture and began to despoil the natural world.
There are many advantages to being a homid, and the human-
born haven't hesitated to make use of these advantages and their
superior numbers to emerge as the dominant Garou breed.

Although their natural form is physically inferior to the
wolf, homids have access to all kinds of abilities and attitudes
of which the lupus are ignorant. Though much technology is
the realm of the Weaver and the Wyrm, the homids can master
such sciences and devices and use them for Gaia. What's more,
as humans and wolves come into greater competition, the
humans almost universally win out; there are simply more
human Kinfolk than wolves these days

Though their initial connection to Gaia is more tenuous
than that of the lupus, and their understanding of Garou
society is less developed than that of the
metis, homids can overcome these defi-
ciencies and bring their
natural strengths to

bear. Although

SSSSSSS To)

humans are almost enemies of the Garou, homids have man-
aged to mostly avoid responsibility for their human kin's deeds.
Homid werewolves almost never have any idea of their true
heritage until the First Change. The stress of this change often
destroys any hope of living as they once did in their childhood
homes. Some homids, though not many, maintain contact with
their human families and friends, though this is never a simple
enterprise. Humans don't take well to supernatural relatives.
The best chance a human has for something approximating a
normal family is a family of Kinfolk. Unaffected by the Delirium
and often aware of how things "really are," Kinfolk have the best
chance of accepting a werewolf, Rage and all. Sometimes these
Kinfolks trustis horribly misplaced, and sometimes it's replaced by
jealousy — but even so, a homid's chances of happiness with
human kin are far better than with the rank-and-file of humanity.
Initial Gnosis: |
Beginning Gifts: Jam Gun, Persuasion

Metis

Spumed, shunned and ostracized, the metis are the pariahs of
Garousociety. Universally reviled as symbols of Gaia's disapproval,
metissuffer for their parents' sin. Born of the forbidden union of two
werewolves, these outcasts are sterile and deformed. Most metis are
disowned by even their shamed parents, and must eke out a living
on the fringes of the sept. They can't have cubs of their own, which
makes them even more useless in the eyes of many elders, Their
story would be irredeemably tragic — save that a werewolf with all
the anguish, pain and bitterness that isa metis'
inheritance has the potential to be one of
the most potent foci of

ue

Metis

Spumed, shunned and ostracized, the metis are the pariahs of
Garousociety. Universally reviled as symbols of Gaia's disapproval,
metis suffer for their parents' sin. Born of the forbidden union of two
werewolves, these outcasts are sterile and deformed. Most metis are
disowned by even their shamed parents, and must eke out a living
on the fringes of the sept. They can't have cubs of their own, which
makes them even more useless in the eyes of many elders. Their
story would be irredeemably tragic — save that a werewolf with all
theanguish, pain and bitterness that isa metis'
inheritance has the potential to be one of

the most potent foci of

pes gies \

S


The metis, always rare, though some say their numbers are on
the rise, have not always been allowed to survive. For all memory
they were often killed at birth, and this practice rarely continues
among the more severe tribes. At the firstsign ofa metis deformity
anewbor cub might have its head dashed against a rock. More
horribly, the child is sometimes allowed to grow to adolescence so
the severity of its disability may be judged and its fate decided on.
There's no real reason why a metis could not be part of a sept, and
many are just that. Sadly though, prejudice and pride bar them
from ever truly feeling part of the pack. Metis themselves are
physical manifestations of the imperfections that plague the
Garou. Perhaps their violent treatment is a symptom of the guilt
withinall Garou. There are those who hope that one day metis will
be accepted and treated with understanding.

In some ways the metis most truly represent the balance
between wolf and man. Unlike homids and lupus, metis have
never been human or wolf. Their anger is twice as potent as
that of othershapechangers. Born in the Crinos war-form, they
may shift to it with no effort. Having grown up among
werewolves, metis suffer no restrictions on initial skills, and
understand the complex ways of Garou society.

Some say that the purity of werewolf blood, distilled from
the grace of Gaia, is far too potent unless alloyed with that of
humans or wolves. Perhaps it is this, or maybe it is the
disapproval of Gaia herself that marks the metis. Whatever the
reason, the metis are branded. Each metis bears a deformity
from birth, a result of the inbreeding which produced it. These
disabilities vary and few are completely crippling, but each
brings its own inconveniences as well as ridicule and hatred from
other Garou. A list of possible deformities is provided below,
along with their effects. Players and Storytellers should

use theseasa guide forcreatingindividual metis, with
unique deformities.

The final insult that a metis must bear is that the twisted
Wyrm-tribe, the Black Spiral Dancers, are largely metis; many
other Garou thus wrongly and maliciously associate all metis
with the Wyrm. The Dancers care nothing for Gaia's Litany,
and produce metis with abandon. Unfortunately, this makes
them an even stronger tribe, as every generation sees a new

influx of powerful, furious warriors.

Initial Gnosis: 3

Beginning Gifts: Create Element, Sense Wyrm

Deformity: Every metis begins with at least one deformity
chosen by the player during character generation. Below are
some suggestions. This list is by no means meant to be defini-

tive or limiting.

4 Albino: Your eyes are red and you have no pigment in your
fur or skin, in any of your forms. You sunburn easily and have an
intolerance for bright lights; unless your eyes are somehow
protected (by smoked glasses or the like), yousuffer a +2 difficulty
on all Perception-based rolls under such conditions.

+w Facial Feature Drift: Your features are asymmetrical, mis-
aligned and unappealing. Thiscan vary froma crooked nose to truly
bizarre rearrangements. Your Appearance can never be higher than
2, and the difficulty of all Social rolls is increased by one.

4 Fits: You suffer from periodic lapses of consciousness and
loss of muscular control. Often when you roll a botch in a
stressful situation, you enter one of these fits and must make a
Willpower roll (difficulty 8) each turn to regain control. Until

you succeed on one of these rolls, you can do nothing but

writhe on the ground.


@ Hairless: Your body, in all forms, lacks hair and fur. Your
Homid form is as bald as your Lupus. This is not only downright
ugly, butmightbe dangerously coldinsome situations. Your Social
Dice Pools are reduced by one die (save intimidation attempts),
and you may not purchase the Pure Breed Background.

‘w Hard of Hearing: You have a weak sense of hearing and
must use your other senses to experience the world. Although
you've learned to live with this, it's obviously difficult for you
to communicate, at least in Homid form. You suffer a +2
difficulty to all hearing-based Perception rolls, and may have
asimilar penalty in certain social situations.

‘¥ Horns or Hooves: You have either cloven feet in Crinos
form, or a horn or horns in all forms. Hooves add no modifiers to
yourmovement. Horns may take the shape of goat'sorram's homs,
or possibly even small antlers (which may earn some respect from
Fianna) orasingle unicorn's spiral horn (which may impress some
Children of Gaia). However, you are at +1 difficulty to all Social
rolls among Garou (or anyone else, in the case of horns) — horns
and hooves are the mark of prey, not a predator.

‘w Hunchback: Your spine, crimped and twisted, is bent
over, resulting in an unsightly lump. Your mobility is impaired,
and the difficulty of all Dexterity rolls (and most Social rolls)
is increased by one. Many hunchbacks are physically strong,
almost by way of compensation.

‘W Madness: The sin of your parents has infected your mind
and you are unstable. Each mad metis is different, but your
insanity might take the form of hallucinations, paranoia or a
split personality. When under stress you must make a Will-
power roll (difficulty 5) or succumb to your insanity.

‘W Malformed Limb: One of your limbs is twisted, gnarled
or withered. All Dexterity rolls involving that limb are at +2
difficulty, and your movement speed is halved in Hispo and
Lupus (and possibly the other forms as well, if your leg is
malformed).

4¥ Tailless: The laughingstock of your pack, you have no
tail in any of your forms. Although there is no effect on Homid
or Glabro form, your Dexterity difficulties are at +1 in all other
forms due to your problems balancing. Botches tend to be
embarrassing lapses of balance, and other werewolves probably
consider you pathetic.

Lupus

Arguably closest to Gaia, those born of wolf are not as
populous as they once were. Though there are still many lupus
among both the invaders and the native Garou, no more than
one in five are from this breed. The lupus has the advantages
of extraordinary senses, natural grace and physical poise, which
homids must strive to match. However, rational thought and
many other "human" abilities are strange to the lupus. Some
don't want to learn and others can't.

The lupus spends the first years of its life as a wolf, livingand
running with a pack and facing the trials of the wild. Then the
wolf discovers she is Garou and is confronted with a homid-

Werewolf: 7

dominated society where she must find her place. Coming from
ademanding world of hierarchy, natural laws and instinct, the
lupus is suddenly confronted with logical thought, technology
and complex human social interaction. It is perhaps no wonder
that a good many lupus are separatists.

Initial Gnosis: 5

Beginning Gifts: Heightened Senses, Jackrabbit Jump

There are a number of abilities which aren't available to
lupus characters at the time of character generation, except
through the expenditure of freebie points. They can be learned
later through experience, though many lupus never bother.
The following Abilities are restricted for lupus:

Skills: Crafts, Etiquette, Firearms, Ride, Melee

Knowledges: Law, Linguistics, Medicine, Politics, Science

=
Auspices

Though life is drawn from Mother Gaia, our life-paths, the tracks
we tread come from another: Luna, the Moon. Each of her five faces
brings a different spirit to those born beneath her gaze. am Galliard,
Gibbous Moon, so I am storyteller, bard, wanderer and pathfinder.
So itis that your auspice will determine your path — if you have the
wisdom to follow it.

— Dawnsong, Uktena Galliard

As Gaia is the womb from which all werewolves are deliv-
ered, so is Luna their cradle. The path that a Garou's life
follows, whether long or short, is to a large extent defined by
what moon-phase he is born under. This auspice determines
the role he is destined to play in Garou culture.

Auspice flows deep in the blood of the Garou, running
throughout the social rituals and responsibilities of tribe and
sept. The threads of the five auspices together twine the rope
that is Garou society, each adding its own strengths, the whole
stronger than the individual strands. Each and every werewolf
has a role to play and each his place. Though a significant part
of these tendencies are almost biological, tradition and society
in no small way reinforce and strengthen these natural lean-
ings. To a werewolf it is only right that to some extent her
character is determined by the circumstances of her birth —as
with breed, so with auspice.

Though the Garou accept these character trends, remember
that they are only tre~ ds. It's possible to resist or break them. One
may encounter' wious' Ragabashor a laughing Ahroun:
Indeed, some ¢ che greatest Garou have been those who have
come to terms with both their birth sign and an individual
personality which transgresses and transcends theses limits.
Furthermore, through the Rite of Renunciation, a Garou may
abandon her auspice, name and Renown and begin anew under
the moon of her choice. Such individuals are not widely trusted
or respected, though again, there are notable exceptions.

More than social class, auspices are something to hold
dear during difficult times. To the Pure Ones, fighting

desperately against the relentless encroachment of »

he Wild West


colonization by the invaders, it is important to know one's place
in society. It is no less significant to the European tribes, warily
struggling to push back the mysterious frontier.

Five Moons and five shades have the Garou

Each has its place in the Old World and the new

In this land of uncertainty, auspice is our hitching post...

— Verse of the Five Moons, Speaks-With-the-All-Voice,
Fianna bard

Ragabash: The New Moon

Any fool knows that attracting someone's attention by
throwing a mule-muffin at the back of his head is better than
letting him get trampled by a stagecoach. You're that fool.
Every court needs a jester and in Gaia's kingdom the Ragabash
holds this honor. You whip away the veil of pretension, throw
it in the mud and dance cackling out of reach of retribution's
claws. If it weren't for your singular insight and irreverent way
with exposition, those who call themselves leaders would be
forever marching blindly into disaster.

Should Garou | permitted to lead their people into
calamity, merely,

way to warn thei quiet wordsa idered counsel?

andridicule. jarou appreciates a gc

Though youare tol lérated, youare less
usually don't lead. Your insightisa ipcelves oneand youare liable
to make few friends. Although all Garou agree that you're a
necessary part of the pack, few personally appreciate you
pointing out their failings. Fewer still understand you. Never-
theless, they treat you with care lest you focus your attentions
on a particular individual, and the freedom you have is envi-
able. Whilst every other auspice has a strict role to follow in
society, the New Moon can go where she pleases, attending to
none but her whim.

Initial Rage: 1

Beginning Gifts: Scent of Running Water, Spider's Song,
Walk Under the New Moon.

Chapter Six: Traits

Theurge: Crescent Moon

The Theurge is a seer, a mystic, a shaman. Born under the
crescent moon which casts little light, you are the most
closely connected to the spirit#World. You have wisdom
gained from dark dreaming'ar ind | long study, insight into your
own black depths, andit ysteries of the spirit realm. No
other knows the Umibraas you do; the paths you have trod, no
other can follow. You've made a friend of horror and fear rides
shotgun by your;

The Garou ate nothing without their connection to Gaia
and Her spirits. You, the Theurge, maintain that link; there-
fore the Garou thing without you. You are their
spirit-guide, their tracker and Umbral pioneer. The
Garou look to you ‘Yin all)spiritual matters, You stand aloof,

el ‘ked by your wisdom — wisdom
gained from hellish insi have no time for sitting
around fires and chewing tobacco; You're always occupied

talking with that spirit, meditating on this lore.

Ifthe Garou need ariddle answered they come to you; if they
need comfort they turn to others. Your position makes others
nervous; even great Ahroun fear the Theurge. Who knows
what allies you have? Though unquestioned and feared, you
must learn what it is like to possess something others cannot,
to have your packmates avoid your gaze. Without you the tribe
is doomed, and there would be none to foretell this doom. You
can both heal and harm. Yours isa grave responsibility, but you
would accept nothing less.

Initial Rage: 2

Beginning Gifts: Mother's Touch, Sense Wyrm, Spirit Speech
Verse of the Five Moons:

I speak lost language

1 am the spirit world's voice

I know the future and the past

I make new paths through the frontiers of the Umbra

Tam Theurge

Philodox: The Half Moon

The Philodox is judge. Balanced as is the half moon,
between light and darkn lfand man, Rage and Will-

ance, strength and comfort. It is your responsibility to recon-
cile the extremes of Ragabash and Ahroun, to balance the
emotional Galliard and Theurge and forge an effective and
deadly pack. If you fail in your task then no matter what the
strength of the individual members, the pack will surely fail.

Such responsibility can weigh heavily on your shoulders,
and many Philodox find they can't be effective in their roles
without removing themselves from the everyday life of the
pack. You may find you must be aloof and untouchable; but
such strength can be brittle. You must balance yourself and all
things around you. One slip could be fatal — not only for you,
but for your entire pack.

Galliard: Gibbous Moon

Singer, poet, Storyteller and dancer — these are faces of the
Galliard. Werewolves don't rely on books and writings to recall
their history, to retell their tales; instead the memories of the
great Galliards hold all their lore, and the voices of these bards
sing the stories of their proud past. Around every European
caern-fire can be found a Galliard with a guitar, and the Pure
One silhouetted against the moon, howling on the mesa—she
too is Galliard.

Initial Rage: 4
Beginning Gifts: Beast Speech, Call of the Wyld, Mindspeak

Verse of the Five Moons:

I am Galliard

The moon is reflected in my eye

The songs well up from the earth at my feet
My howl is heard by friend and foe

My life is as bold as the stories | tell

It is I who will sing the ballad of your life

I am Galliard

Ahroun: The Full Moon

Warrior prince of a warrior race, the Ahroun knows nothing
but battle. To her, there is but one solution to all problems.
Fiercest among the savage, filled with more hate than any other,
the Ahroun has deadly claws too often steeped in gore. She is the
gunslinger in the dusty street, the brave with the fresh scalp in
her hand, the wolf with, ibe ted muzzle. The teeth and

ough few come to an

ye e they call when fear grips

them. Your iron will and steadfast courage inspire the pack and

protect them. Nootheris bettersuited to withstand the deadly attacks

of the Wyrm's scions. Strength is your birthright and your tool, used

to defeat your enemies and control your friends. Any who cross you
had best be able to account for themselves with tooth and claw.

Taisho

Tam the hunter and the slayer

Tribes

The tribe is the basis for all of Garou society. The social
instincts of both wolf and human are bred into the Garou, and
the tribes provide a sense of identity the werewolves can get
from nowhere else. Most werewolves are not truly at

home among their human or lupine kin, and only
with their tribes can they find that missing sense of a

14 Werewolf: The Wild West


TLEEL

larger community. The Garouneed the support of their tribes, without which
most would go mad during the First Change. The fierce loyalty most werewolves
feel for their tribes is rivaled only by the loyalty they feel for their packs. In
ancient times, when packs were comprised of Garou of the same tribe, this did
not pose any problems. As more werewolves form mixed-tribe packs, these
potentially conflicting loyalties could pose problems.

Each tribe has its own creation story, in which the tribe plays a prominent part
in the protection of Gaia and the salvation or guidance of the other tribes. Most of
these legends agree that in the beginning, all Garou were of one tribe. The stories
diverge from there, but the Galliards generally agree that the tribal divisions were the
result of fighting between different factions of werewolves. Ever since these first divisions,
the Garou have fought each other with almost the same fervor with which they
fight the Wyrm. Some among the Garou claim that this divisiveness is the
Wyrm's greatest trick; keeping the skinchangers fighting amongst them-
selves leaves them less time, energy and resources to fight the Wyrm. The
split between the Pure Ones and the immigrant werewolves is just the
latest symptom of a hostility that keeps Gaia's greatest warriors from
uniting and destroying the Wyrm's forces once and for all.

In times past, Gaia had other tribes of defenders. The Croatan,
one of the three Pure Tribes, sacrificed itself to destroy the
Eater-of-Souls. The White Howlers were lost to the corrup-
tion of the Wyrm, becoming the hated Black Spiral Dancers.
The remaining tribes see these vile creatures as traitors to Gaia
and all Garou. The European werewolves also speak of another
tribe, the Bunyip, who live in far-off Australia. The mysterious
Bunyip are said to never venture out of Australia, and there has
been no sign that they have ever come to the New World.

The 13 tribes of Garou are described below. Each tribe has a totem
spirit which guides the tribe as a whole. This totem teaches its
children Gifts and may grant them other abilities as
well. Unlike pack totems, tribal totems are not
bought with background points; each Garou
has a connection to her tribal totem. Each
tribe also defends certain areasand people, its
territories and protectorates. These are very
serious obligations of each tribe member.

Backgrounds: Some tribes have Background
restrictions, which are listed with each tribe. A character
with such restrictions may not begin play with these Back-
grounds, nor may she buy them with freebie points. However,
the Storyteller may choose to grant them to the character
through roleplaying. For example, if a Bone Gnawer
character befriended an eccentric millionaire, the Story-
teller could choose to have the Bone Gnawer discover,
upon the millionaire's death, that she is his sole heir. The
Storyteller could then decide to grant the Bone Gnawer
points in Resources.

Black Furies

Greek by origin, the Black Furies look to the New World for

a place to defend Gaia and fight for women's rights. The Furies
believe that women have an inherently stronger bond
with Mother Gaia than a man ever can, and as such the

cu Reibesis eommprizediriesly exclustvely' of tentale Garou:

Chapter Six: Traits


The few males that the Furies do tolerate in the tribe are their
own metis cubs. Though once known for killing their male
children, the Furies now usually give their sons to other tribes
to raise in recognition of the need for more Garou.

Women in the Savage West demand more of a voice and more
of an opportunity to enjoy the same rights and privileges as mei
do, as the United States grows economically more prosperousand
more people expand westward. The Black Furies actively encour-
age and support this growing movement. The Furies often work for
several activist goals, including abolition and especially women's
suffrage. Though many live on the more populous EastCoast,
where more reforms are possible, the Furies have begun moving
West. The rigors of frontier life and the open land provide fertile
ground for the Furies' ambitions. What's more, the frontier is a
direct link to their second responsibility — the Wyld.

To the Furies, the Wyld embodies the Mother. Every acre of
untouched land, every pure stream resounds with the essence of
Gaia. Protecting and nurturing these places is the sacred duty of
every Black Fury. The Furies' rites and Gifts reflect this under-
standing of the Wyld, and are as captivating and complex as the
Wyld they mirror. The Black Furies also place a high value on
honor; their duty to the Mother demands it. Even those Garou
who hate the Furies know they can be trusted.

The Black Furies often have strained
relations with the other tribes.
The male-domi-
nated tribes
tend to
see their
rejection

xX)

of male Furies as a weakness and a crime against Garou. Even the
more moderate tribes don't understand the Furies' reasons for their
practices, and many wrongly believe that the Furies simply hate
mien. The Children of Gaia often work with the Black Furies in
abolitionist and women's rights movements, and frequently vol-
unteer to accept their male children. The Furies have a special
distaste for the Get of Fenris, Garou the Furies feel are without
honor or understanding of the Goddess.

Tribal Totem: Pegasus

Backgrounds: No restrictions.

Physical: The Black Furies live up to their name with coats of
deep black fur, sometimes with lighter markings of silver, white or
gray. While many still show traces of their ancient Greek origins,
the Furies now encompass women of all ethnic groups.

promote women's rights and support the abolitionist movement.

Bone Gnawers

Every society has its downtrodden poor, and the Garou are
no exception. The Bone Gnawers have survived centuries of
oppression and hardship, living on the streets of human cities,
getting by through wits and determination. The Gnawers get
little help from most other werewolves; in fact, they get little
of anything but contempt
though, and thrive wh

Bone Cus do

rain cloud over their parade,
New World was supposed to be ferceees to prevent the sort
of oppression they were accustomed to, and they're doing
everything in their power to stop it. The Bone Gnawers, along
with the Black Furies and the Children of Gaia, are one of the
most active tribes in the abolitionist movement.

The Bone Gnawers have spread throughout the world from
their origins in Africa and India, breeding with humans ofall races
and ethnic backgrounds. Once possibly related to jackals, the
Gnawers' lupus blood is now thinning a touch. Centuries of living
in cities have diluted their wolf heritage, though the Bone
Gnawersamong the mountain men and scavengerson the frontier
have been helping to reintroduce wolf blood to the tribe.

The Bone Gnawers often find more than food while scav-
enging. Gnawers usually know the tidbits of gossip, the latest
rumors on riches to be found, or when a new wagon train is due
in town. The Bone Gnawers who live in the cities and large
towns help supply the Iron Riders with rumors of inventions
and experiments. Many pure-bred werewolves dislike dealing
with the low-born Gnawers, but reluctantly swallow their
pride and negotiate with the mongrel tribe when they need to
learn something that can't be discovered in the wild.

Though Bone Gnawers usually wear dirty, tattered clothes and
eat whatever they can scrape together, they do know how to take
care of themselves and others. Gnawers measure their status by
what possessions they've accumulated and how generously they
share their treasures with others of their tribe. They often extend
this munificence to the down-and-outs of human society as well.

Tribal Totem: Rat

Initial Willpower: 4

Backgrounds: Bone Gnawers may not buy Past Life, Pure
Breed or Resources.

Beginning Gifts: Odious Aroma, Stone Soup
Physical: Bone Gnawers in Lupus form often look

r ) like large stray wolf-dogs, mangy and disheveled from

Chapter Six: Traits

their scavenging, their coats rough and mottled. Though small
for Garou, they are remarkably resilient. Bone Gnawers can now
be found in any ethnic or racial background, and so represent the
full spectrum of human types.

Territory: The Gnawers claim boom towns, railroad ghet-
tos, hermits' shacks and all sorts of not-quite-rich communities
for their own. Considering the number of less than well-to-do
towns in the frontier, it's not surprising that the Bone Gnawers.
have quite a bit of influence west of the Mississippi.

Protectorate: The Bone Gnawers have appointed them-
selves guardians of all those everyone else would like to forget.
They watch out for all those who have nothing, and consider
themselves champions of the true salt of the earth.

Children of Gaia

Through all the bloody conflicts that have divided the
Garou, one tribe has called for unity. Through all the hatred
that fires the Rage of the wolf-changers, one tribe has called for
understanding and compromise. The Children of Gaia believe
that violence is not the only means by which the Garou must
defend the Mother.

While they concede that it is not always possible, or even
desirable, to avoid a fight, the Children know that the Wyrm
finds an open gateway for corruption in every heart tainted by
hatred, in every act of vengeance and malice, in every moment

ger or hatred, but

e the only option.

tribes wasatrick ofthe Wyrm, meant to weaken Gaia's defend-

supernatural creattites can be ho allies in fighting the
Wyrm. Most other tribes look on ae expansiveness as soft-
headedness, but the Children press their point none the weaker.
The Children of Gaia feel well at home in the United States,
a nation whose ideals and very Constitution they influenced.
The Children encourage everyone, even their cubs, to express
their opinions and to act on their beliefs. The Children of Gaia
hold peculiar beliefs for werewolves; they feel that even elders
can learn from cubs. Dissenting opinions are not only permitted,
but encouraged. This respect for the right to disagree allows the
Children to avoid the power struggles some of the other tribes

experience, and permits them to rapidly put aside their personal
differences in the face ofa threat that requires immediate action.

Tribal lore tells that in the early days of the New World,
a Child of Gaia aided the Croatan in their fight against the
Wyrm, and the tribe is now encouraging the Uktena and the
Wendigo to join with the European Garou they so resent. The
Children are faced with a great dilemma. Most want to
actively work against slavery, relocation and murder of the
Pure Ones, as well as the lack of women's rights. They are also
aware that pushing these issues too much could plunge the
nation into war. The Children of Gaia have been polarized on
this issue. Through a grand moot, the Children decided to
compromise by speaking against these things, while working
against them subtly and secretly by aiding such works as the
Underground Railroad. The majority have accepted this
compromise, and also work through their extensive network
of Kinfolk and human allies.

Some renegades feel that not actively fighting against these
issues allows the Wyrm to gain too much of a foothold in the
Pure Land. These Children take the fight into their own claws,
despite opposition from the rest of their tribe. Because the

Children of Gaia have never believed in reprisals against those  s u

taken action to stop them. 4

Fianna

The Garou hold their history and legends dear. No tribe is more
aware of the need to remember the old tales than the Fianna. The
Fianna claim to have devised the Garou language, and point to
similar words in ancient Gaelic by way of proof. The Fiannaare the
keepers of Garou history, well-versed in the knowledge of the
Silver Record. Even the Silver Fangs consult with the Fianna
when they need toknowsomefine pointof legend, or want to trace
a werewolf's lineage back to ancient times.

Descended from the ancient Celts, the Fianna came to
America with the influx of Irish, Scottish and Welsh immi-
grantseThoughsthe. Fianna are well-known for their Galliard
bards anid loremasters, there are few who want to rouse the ire of
an t. Their Ahroun are fierce fighters, and the tribe's
ity and will to fight is renowned among the Garou.

ore, the Fianna have always been notorious for keeping
nong the fae — although that's starting to change.
> Fig u wa love their Kinfolk, and crossed the ocean with their
kin who! -ihg oppression in Britain and famine in Ireland.
The Fianna have spread across the country, seeking the land and
security that was unavailable to them in their native homes. This
desire to grab land has brought them into conflict with the Uktena
and Wendigo, as it is often the Pure Ones who suffer from the

sexpansion of the Fianna and their kin. Some Fianna have become

decided the time has come for violent action should not bef defenders of their lands, often killing any unexpected

Tribal Totem: Unicorn

Initial Willpower: 4

Backgrounds: No restrictions.

Beginning Gifts: Mother's Touch, Resist Pain

Physical: Everything about the Children of Gaia's forms
echoes their connection to Gaia. Their fur tends toward earthy
colors of brown and grey, often with white markings. Majestic
and powerful, they radiate calm and inner confidence rather
than the savage ferocity of other Garou.

Territory: The Children of Gaia can be found almost every-
where. In the new cities and towns, on reservations with the Pure
Ones, acting as wilderness guides, the Children range across the
whole of the new territories, trying to ease the tensions and
growing pains of the nation.

Protectorate: All of humanity. The Children of Gaia try toact
as the conscience both of werewolves and humans. The Children
have been working for millennia to enlighten the human popula-
tion about Gaia's gifts, and continue to try to influence the actions
of humanity and Garou, primarily by example.

Moving to the Savage West with their human Kinfolk has
eant leaving their fae heritage behind. Some among the Fianna
speculate that their tribe is slowly being driven mad, having been
cut offfrom the faeries that remain in Europe. Galliards gather the

cubs around campfires, spinning tales of Fianna so desperate to
contact their fae allies that they have been found inscribing

cient Celtic symbols into the ground in their own blood. Some
even claim to have proof that their tales are based in fact.

The Fianna easily succumb to their passions, and get themselves
in trouble that way more often than any other. They are quick to
laugh, quick to love and quick to anger. Traditionally, Fianna enjoy
the company of others, and can often be found around a campfire
telling stories, or in a saloon singing along with the piano player, or
playing the piano themselves. In the Savage West, some Fianna lose
themselves in booze, gambling, and fighting, occasionally resorting
to thievery and banditry to gain a bit more of what they feel is owed
tothem. Loyal to their friends, the Fianna swiftly and ruthlessly deal
with those who cross them. Many Fianna ballads recount the tales
of loves and fights lost and won.

Tribal Totem: Stag

Initial Willpower: 3

Backgrounds: No restrictions.

Beginning Gifts: Persuasion, Resist Toxin

Werewolf: The Wild West


Physical: The Fianna have coats of deep black or red fur.
Their wolf forms are large, even for Garou, often looking like
Irish wolfhounds in Lupus form. The human stock is generally
Celtic; the Fianna tend to mate with their Kinfolk and with
immigrants from Ireland, Scotland and Wales.

Territory: Fianna prefer to live in rural, picturesque areas.
They can be found anywhere they might be able to get some
land. Rovers and adventurers, the Fianna also trek into the
mountains looking for gold, and live on the outskirts of towns
with good saloons.

Protectorate: The Fianna watch after their immigrant kin
whom they came to protect. They travel along the railroads
with the Irish laborers. By the nature of their circumstances,
they have found aid from the Bone Gnawers and the Children
of Gaia, and help keep an eye on their protectorates as well.

Quote: Lad, there's land aplenty out here, and I was content to

To the Get of Fenris, strength and hard work are power.
Nothing is more important than the battle except victory.
Only the strongest deserve to survive, because only the stron-
gest and finest will be able to best the Wyrm. If the Pure Ones
were really so pure, if they were truly worthy, they would have
the strength and ability to prevent the Get from taking their
lands. While the Uktena and Wendigo blame the Europeans
for bringing the Wyrm to taint their pure land, the Get blame
the Uktena and Wendigo for allowing themselves to grow
weak and lose their ability to fight the Wyrm.

The Get constantly challenge themselves, their packmates
and others of their tribe. For them, life is a cold, harsh struggle,
and nobody deserves any special consideration. The Get have
no patience for the abolitionists and women's suffrage advo-
cates, or for the complaints of the Pure Ones who whine about
the loss of their land. The Get believe the only rights you have
are the ones you earn for yourself. When the slaves free
themselves, when the women win the right to vote, then
they'll truly be worthy of their new rights.

The Get of Fenris, like most of the other tribes, have spread
across the land with their Kinfolk. Some are recent
immigrants, some the descendants of families who have

lived in the Northeast for decades. The Get prefer the
northern lands, as they find the cold, harsh life
reminiscent of their homelands. This
has brought them into direct ter-
ritorial conflict with the

Beginning Gifts: Razor Claws, Safe Haven

Physical: The Get of Fenris are usually huge, grey-coated
wolves, their huge, muscled forms monuments to savagery and
ferocity. Most have battle scars of some sort, and many bear
ritual scars and mystical runes they have carved into their flesh.
Most Get are of Germanic and Nordic origin, descended from
the Vikings, Angles, Saxons and Jutes.

Territory: The Get have any territory they can take. Most
prefer northern lands, close to their Kinfolk. Although they are
among the worst land-grabbers, they won't press for a territory
if the inhabitants put up a strong enough fight.

Protectorate: The Get of Fenris are extremely protective of
their Kinfolk, and become even more savage than usual when
their relatives are threatened. The Get have also taken some
caerns they felt were not being adequately protected; this has
infuriated many other tribes, including many Europeans. Any
place they feel is in real danger of being corrupted by the Wyrm
is open to them.

that — I haven't been beaten yet, and frankly, you aren't the one
to beat me. What matters is that you tried. Here, pup, have
something to drink. It may not take the sting away, but it'll put you
in a better mood about it.

Iron Riders

New towns are popping up everywhere, and the Iron Riders
are right there with them. Technology is developing rapidly,
telegraphs and railroads are becoming more widely used, and
the Iron Riders are there, encouraging this development and
learning as much as they can. The Iron Riders claim to have
found spirits of money and metal that they understand and
learn from.

The Iron Riders are unique among the Garou in that they
don'tdread the spread of humanity. They see human civilization
and culture, not untamed wilderness, as the future of the world.
They claim that the Technology-spirits are proof that Gaia

‘is may not purchase Contacts or

120

Werewolf: The Wild West

encourages human achievements, and are a sign that the Garou
must adapt to the changing world. Most of the other tribes think
the Iron Riders are at best crazy, and at worst aiding the Wyrm.
The rapid spread of human civilization has required that
werewolves learn better to walk among humans and their
settlements. The Iron Riders have been of great assistance to the
tural kin of other tribes who have been forced to deal with
encroaching human towns, so mostare unwilling to accuse them
openly of aiding the Wyrm. To the amazement of the other
tribes, the Iron Riders have even been able to establish caerns in
towns and cities, which the Iron Riders use as further proof that
they have neither abandoned nor been abandoned by Gaia.
However, many Theurges point to the upheaval in the Storm
Umbra as evidence that the Weaver-energies pouring into the
region are far from completely benign. The Riders themselves
agree that the Wyrm is indeed working its ways on many of the
local Weaver-spirits, but refuse to blame technology and its
associated influences for the way that some might abuse them.

The Iron Riders in the Savage West ally themselves with
human visionaries investing in new technology, laying the
foundation of the telegraph system, and extending the railroad
from coast to coast. While this has earned them the enmity of
some other tribes, especially the Wendigo, Uktena and Red
Talons, they do have support from the Bone Gnawers and
interested tolerance from the Children of Gaia.

Red Talons

The spread of humanity has often left young cubs with the
mistaken impression that all werewolves are born as humans.
Notso. In particular, the Red Talons are born only to the wolves
of the wild, and these Garou feel that Gaia would be better off
if humanity stopped crawling around on Herskin entirely.

The first Red Talons came through'the Umbra to the Pure
Land around the same time the Euro

who had claimed all of the wi
there, ifstill cruel, fallible a
sacred Earth. The Taloy
native werewolves, aj
Though fighting began.
Ones and the Europe
attention to these affairs.

The Red Talons g cy mingled with the large American
wolf population, and began to establish dominance over the
wolf packs and find suitable territory. As the humans have
continued to encroach on the lands of the Pure Ones, the
influence of the Weaver and the awakening of the Storm Eater
have become too powerful for the Red Talons to ignore. Some
of the Talons have suggested an alliance with the Pure Ones to
drive out the offending Europeans, Garou and human alike.

Tribal Totem: Griffin

Initial Willpower: 3

Backgrounds: Red Talons may not buy human Allies,
Contacts or Resources. Their only Kinfolk are wolves.

Beginning Gifts: Beast Speech, Eye of the Hunter

Physical: The Red Talons are large wolves, with red to
ruddy brown fur. They prefer to stay in Lupus or Hispo form as
muchas possible, and many try to live their entire lives without
changing to the hated human form. When they do change, the
Talons are often awkward, stumbling and slouching in their
unaccustomed stance. They tend to be very hairy, almost
brutish and primitive humans. No matter the form, a Talon
always has a patch of red fur on his body.

Territory: The Red Talons live among the native wolf
population, staying as far away from the encroaching human
civilization as possible. They still have great freedom and space
in the former Pure Land, but the growing taint of the Weaver
and the tumult of the Umbra have disturbed them.

Protectorate: The Red Talons have appointed themselves
protectors of the unspoiled wilderness. They are determined not to
let the old poison take thisnew land. They have adopted the native
wolves as their packs and protect them as well. Many Talons feel a
duty to teach the lupus of the Wendigo and Uktena of the evils of
humans, but for the most part itisnotsucceeding. The lupus of these
tribes are too accustomed to dealing with humans who understand
the role of the Garou and who respect Gaia.

Quote: You are neither welcome nor safe here, ape. A

pity you have no time remaining to regret your mistake.

Chapter Six: Traits

Shadow Lords

Gaia made some to serve and some to be served, and those
who don't know or won't assume their place must be elimi-
nated. Ruthless and cold, the Shadow Lords believe absolutely
in submission and dominance. They are dominant; all else are
subordinate. Those who don't submit serve no purpose. In their
haughty arrogance, the Shadow Lords know they are the only
ones fit for leadership of the Garou.

The Shadow Lords seek power endlessly, and will stop at
nothing to get it. They show no mercy and expect none in
return. They are formidable physical fighters, but are also
renowned for their political skill. Most Garou prefer the
honesty of a straight-up brawl, but the Shadow Lords are well
aware that some fights are better won with guile and cunning.

The Lords' quest for leadership and authority serves them
well in the Savage West. The wild, untamed land cries out for the
Shadow Lords to take control and claim it for their own. This
domination takes many forms. Some Lords have formed outlaw
gangs, while others have become sheriffs and deputies who rule
their towns with an iron fist, dictating law and order. The
Shadow Lords see their chance to gain true power and authority
in America. A land that has cast off the authority of royalty may
not be so eager for the rule of the Silver Fangs. Of course, the
democratic ideals may cause problems, but the Lords are certain
they can work around that. For now, they are content to be the
councilors and executors of Garou law, bringing the other tribes
in line with a steel hand.

Rumors of the Storm Eater have also roused the interest of the
Shadow Lords. They see the creature as a direct insult to
Grandfather Thunder's supremacy and a challenge to their
dominance. Some among them wonder if the Storm Eater is a
ploy of the Silver Fangs to destroy their influence, and perhaps
their very tribe. Evidence so far seems to deny this, but the Lords
are slow to let go of their suspicions.

Tribal Totem

the Striders unable to penetrate. The Silent Striders
themselves remain true to their name and say nothing on the

wagon paths and the moon paths of America as eagerly as
they travel the rest of the world. The
appearing from nowhere and vanishing as suddenly as they

Silver Fangs

Every group must have its leaders, and the Silver Fangs have
long been the leaders of the Garou. They are widely believed
to have been the first tribe, and this tradition has given them
the respect of most of the other tribes.

Atfirst glance, the Silver Fangs embody the werewolf ideal.

Their regal bearing ap pee still awe even
x aré legendary, their

cynical Garou
martial prowé ir leaders! strong and

just. But someof their rivals whisper of the weakness of age and
the frailty of their inbred line. /
The Silver Fangs claitti the''Réssian Empire as their home-

land, and for centuries have bred among the nobles of Europe.
Just as the human nobility is widely inbred among the monar-
chies of Europe, so too is the nobility of the Garou. Some
among the Silver Fangs saw the journey to the Pure Land not
only as their duty to the Garou there, but also as a chance to
infuse their tribe with new blood. Very few of these were able
to both shed their habits of breeding with the wealthy, power-
ful elite, and gain the trust of the Pure Ones and their kin.
Those who did were able to breed with the chiefs of the native
tribes, but most simply continued breeding with the rich and
powerful among the immigrant Europeans.

The Silver Fangs have taken it upon themselves to reunite
the Uktena and Wendigo with their brethren. The Silver
Fangs do not understand the reluctance of their newest sub-
jects to follow; they can't imagine that the Pure Ones want
nothing to do with the Fangs' noblesse oblige. The Silver Fangs
also have difficulty with the Garou born in the States, who
don't have the proper respect for nobility or the courts of
leaders. These Garou, born into a democratic system, have
resisted the Silver Fangs' attempts to impose the courtly, regal
hierarchy of the European courts.

Tribal Totem: Falcon

Initial Willpower: 3

Backgrounds: Silver Fangs must spend at least three of their

Background points on Pure Breed.

Beginning Gifts: Eminent Domain, Sense Wyrm

Physical: The Silver Fangs have sleek white or silver coats,
and most have blue or green eyes. Their majestic forms radiate
apowerful grace. In Homid form, the Silver Fangs represent the
full range of world nobility, though the majority of the tribe
descends from European courts.

Territory: The Silver Fangs choose places that reflect the
majesty of their tribe as their own. A breathtaking panorama
of mountains and forests or a cliff overlooking the Pacific
Ocean would be ideal.

Protectorate: The Silver Fangs are the leaders of the Garou,
and as such feel the traditional obligation of a lord to protect
his vassals. The Garou as a whole are the protectorate of the
Silver Fangs, though the other tribes are expected to return the
favor with their loyalty and respect.

Quote: Get the young ones to safety. It's me they're really after,
and I'm damned if I won't make them sorry for it. If | don't follow
after you, tell the others that a Silver Fang died here tonight — in a
river of his foes' blood!

Stargazers

Few Stargazers roam Gaia, and even fewer have crossed the
oceans to America. The story of their tribe's formation has
been nearly lost, and many Garou of other tribes know only
that the Stargazers exist — nothing else. The Stargazers
themselves cite their origin, an old, old story, as taking place in
the dim past, almost before the end of the Impergium. Despair-
ing of the Wyrm-spawned hatred that had crept into the souls
of the Garou, some turned from their tribes to another path.
These werewolves purged the Wyrm-taint from themselves
and found a center of balance and peace within their own
hearts. The Stargazers prefer their own company to that of
other Garou. In this, their great strength has become their
greatest weakness. By rejecting others of their kind, they have
lost the desire to be part of a pack, a great legacy from their wolf
heritage. Their desire for solitude has greatly diminished their
numbers; they are easily the t of the 13 tribes. Many of
them are not even borngto the tri ut are"converted from

Physical: Stargazers have a wide range of fur colors, reflect-

often with markings of other colors. The few Stargazers born to
the tribe come largely from Far Eastern lands, though some
have European and African roots.

Territory: Stargazers wander the unexplored and little-
known regions of the Earth. They seek seclusion in which to
discover more paths to harmony, and enlightened Garou to
teach their wisdom.

Protectorate: Stargazers favor rovers and seekers, solitary
philosophers and any who seek wisdom and awareness of Gaia.
Many watch over Chinese ghettoes and isolated, introspective
tribes of Native Americans. Stargazers sometimes teach their
truths to humans whom they trust as well as Garou, but are
careful to veil much of their arcane speak in metaphor — there
are some things that humans are safer not knowing.

Uktena

When the Pure Ones came to the Pure Land, the Uktena spread
their charges across the southern parts of North America, and even
farthersouth where appropriate. There the Uktena began exploring
the strange spirits of the land, and leamed the ways of the other
Changing Breeds. They soon discovered that there was Wyrm-
corruption even here. Thotigh the Ukrena destroyed what Banes
and other Wyrm-spirits they could, sortie Were too powerful; so the
Uktena called on the Wendiggand Eroatan to bind them instead.
The three tribes joinedit@create a net ofpowerinthe Pure Land,
drawing on this power to seal thé Banes below the Earth in their
slumber. With the destruction of the Groatan and the arrival of
European Garou, these sealshaye beenibroken, and the Uktena fear
that the ancient Banes may sgon be free.

With the aid of the Silene)Striders, the Uktena have
discovered that one ancient spirit, the Storm Eater, has
already torn itself free. The Uktena do not know what effect
the Storm Eater will have on the already disturbed Umbra or
on the physical world, but they fear that its wrath may help
free other Wyrm-creatures as well.

‘The Wyrnibringers have destroyed many of the sacred sites,
and are decimating the Uktena's Kinfolk through disease and
forced removal from their lands

adopted the other victims of the
Wyrmbringers: the

Asa result, the Uktena have


slaves they brought to work the land. The tribe is still primarily
comprised of the Pure Ones, but is incorporating more and more
refugees of the Wyrmbringers' oppression. As the physical world
becomes less hospitable to the Uktena, they spend more time
searching the Umbra for answers and guidance. In the process,
they have begun to learn many secrets of the Wyrm, which
disturbs the other tribes, who fear the Uktena may take their
search for a way to fight the Wyrm's predations a bit too far.

The Uktena trust few of the European Garou. Too many of
the Wyrmcomers have preyed on the Pure Ones. Too many
caerns have fallen, and too many Banes have been unwittingly
freed. The Uktena deal with the Silent Striders only because of
the great threat from the Banes and the Storm Eater. They
entertain the occasional Child of Gaia missionary, but are
loath to completely accept the Children's policy of forgiveness
and tolerance. They respect the Children of Gaia for their
acceptance of the Pure Ones, but are mildly dubious of any
Europeans who bring talk of compromise and peace.

Tribal Totem: Uktena (a Native American water spirit combin-
ing the features of serpent, deer and cougar)

Initial Willpower: 3

Backgrounds: No restrictions.

Beginning Gifts: Cloud of Night, Sense Medicine

Physical: In their wolf forms, the Uktena usually haves
brown coats and dark eyes. They are medi
