Protectorate: The Uktena care for the Pure Ones they have
tended for millennia. They have also adopted the displaced
natives formerly protected by the slain Croatan and the former
slaves who have joined them. Almost above all else, the tribe
tends the old sacred sites and guards the locations of the
sleeping Banes, trying to keep the old seals empowered.

Quote: Foolish ones! Don't you see what it is you do? Your clumsy
footsteps tear webs, break cocoons. Every step frees another spirit, and
every spirit feeds our great enemy. Why must you be sightless and deaf?

Wendigo

When the three Pure Tribes parted ways, the Wendigo chose
the northern parts of the continent. They participated in creat-
ing the great spiritual net that bound the sleeping Wyrmspawn
in the Pure Land, and then they lived in peace, growing with
their Kinfolk and enjoying the bounty Gaia provided for them.

When the net began tearing, all that changed. The

Wendigo have now spread south in an effort to fight
the Wyrmbringers and protect the Pure Land.

Chapter Six: Traits

The Wendigo disliked the Wyrmbringers from the time
they arrived, and the loss of the Croatan strengthened their
resolve to chase the Wyrmbringers out of the Pure Land. The
Wendigo now fight a valiant and desperate, but losing, battle.
The Wyrmbringers claim all of the ancient places, leaving the
Pure Ones to eke out their survival from land that is foreign to
and often incompatible with their customs. The Wyrmbringers
claim the land as their own, denying the bounty of Gaia to all
others, as if they have some right to keep a piece of Her for their
own. The Wyrmbringers have even started to corrupt Older
Brother Uktena, who has adopted some of the newcomers as
his own. To the Wendigo, the fight against the Wyrmbringers
and the taint they bring, both of Wyrm and Weaver, is for
nothing less than the very survival of themselves and their kin.
They can see that the land they protect for Gaia will soon be
yet another conquest for the Weaver and the Wyrm if they
don't stop the spread of the Wyrmbringers now.

The Wendigo refuse to deal with most other Garou. Of the
newcomers, the Wendigo associate most freely with the Red
Talons, sympathizing with their desire to kill invading humans —
as long as the natives are spared. They sometimes speak with the
Children of Gaia, as Middle Brother Croatan would have wanted.
Otherwise, the Wendigogenerally associate solely with the Uktena,
who are the only other Garou they consider pure enough. The
ndigo harbor a particular hatred for the Get of Fenris and
whocompete for their ancient territory and are among the

thlessly turn them out of their lands.

fackgrounds: Wendigo may not buy Contacts or Re-
Ources.

Beginning Gifts: Call the Breeze, Song of the Seasons

Physical: The wolf blood of the Wendigo comes from the
great northern timber wolves. Their coats are gray, brown,
ora mixture of the two. They have stout, powerful muscles
and deadly fangs. Their human blood is exclusively of the
Pure Ones, as they have refused to breed with any of the
Wyrmbringers.

Territory: The Wendigo prefer the deep forests of the
northern territories, though some have ventured south, either
traveling with the Pure Ones or aiding the Uktena in fighting
the Wyrmbringers.

Protectorate: All of the land. Surveyors, expeditions, and
pioneer trains are all fair game for their Rage. They are often
among the more warlike tribes of Native Americans, and feel
close to the Apache and Sioux. Any of the Wyrmbringers
who would try to claim another bit of land are legitimate
targets to the Wendigo — who don't limit themselves to
humans, either. Any intruding werewolf is likely to find her
entrails sacrificed to the noble Rage of the Wendigo.

Quote: Your filth will not spread any further across this land,
pale cousins. If you mean to cross this plain, you will nourish it with
your blood.

T


Attributes
Specialties

For each Attribute and Ability Trait rated 4 or higher, a
player can select a specialty. A specialty is some area of
expertise where a body can lay legitimate claim to some
bragging rights — a character may be an able horseman, but
might be more a long-distance traveler than a swift rider. A
specialty allows the player to reroll 10s scored on actions
directly involving the specialty. The player gets to keep the
success indicated by the 10, and may subsequently try again for
another success, rolling another die for each 10 obtained.

Physical

These Traits tell how tough, nimble and brawny a char-
acter is, and are the primary Traits of action-oriented
characters. Physical Traits concern only your body's strengths
and weaknesses.

Strength

This Trait measures your brute strength — how large a bale
you can lift, how long a barge you can tote, how much
punishment you can dish out. Folks with high Strength ratings
tend to be big fellows or strapping wenches, rather than punier
types of people. ‘Course, you can probably think of a tough-as-
nails hombre or a brave cut from stone who barely comes to
your shoulder, but every rule has its exceptions.

Strength comes in when you want to haul, heave or bust
something. In melee combat, your Strength Trait is added
to your damage Dice Pool. Strength is also used when you
take a hack at any kind of leap or jump.

Specialties: Burly, Bearish, Barrel-Chested, Rugged, Grip
Like a Vise, Vigorous, Solid, Potent, Imposing, Full of Snap

. Poor: You can lift yourself onto your horse, barely.
oe Average: You can lift somebody else onto your horse.
ree Good: You can lift somebody else onto your horse

while you're sitting on it.

Exceptional: You can lift a laden barrel onto your
horse (not advisable).

veeee Outstanding: You can lift a pony.

"exterity

This Trait rates how well you move — speed, spryness and
surety of step. It also tells how well you can move other things
around, the care and calculation you bring to handling all
sorts of stuff. Dexterity includes sleight-of-hand trickery,

ancy footwork, ducking, darting and dancing.

Specialties: Limber, Snaky, Lithesome, Agile, Supple,
featly, Quick-as-a-Wink, Like the Wind, Expeditious,
Greased Lightning

. Poor: Better let somebody else load your six-shooter
for you, partner.

oe Average: You get around okay.

Good: You're a hoop dancer without peer.

Exceptional: You can logroll with the best.

Outstanding: You can logroll your way through river

rapids.

Stamina

Stamina gauges your fitness and stolidity. It rates how
long and how far you'll push yourself, and how much distress
or discomfort you can endure. Stamina is the toughness of
your mind and your body. The strength of your will to live
is at its heart.

Specialties: Stubborn, Unbending, Relentless, Unbeat-
able, Sturdy, Flinty, Leathery, Bull-Headed, Iron-Plated, Built
Like a Brick Outpost
. Poor: You are spindly, even consumptive.
ee Average: You are in the pink.

Good: You are fiddle-fit and ever-ready.
Exceptional: You could cross a desert afoot.

© Outstanding: Nails could take pointers from you.

Social

These Traits describe your comeliness (or lack of it),
social graces (or lack of them) and your understanding of
why folks are the way they are. Social Traits determine
whether your dealings with people will lead you to windfalls
or pratfalls, whether you're likelier to wind up a general or
a foot soldier.

Charisma

Charisma reckons how well you attract others and com-
mand their attention. You use it to gain folks' trust and to
earn their tender feelings. This Trait is not about wiliness,
but about how genuinely winning a person you are. Cha-
risma is a matter of your comportment, of how much loyalty
you inspire in others, of how much people want to do what
you want them to do.

Specialties: Dashing, Debonair, Inspirational, Lordly, Cul-
tured, Down-to-Earth, Sweet-Natured, Amiable, Trustworthy,
Vivacious, Enchanting

. Poor: Folks say they'd take a rattler's company over
yours.

ee Average: You're liked, but not well-liked.

eee ~~ Good: You're everybody's huckleberry.

eeee Exceptional: Your friends call you their beacon in
the night.

eeeee Outstanding: You could bring together all the tribes

in the West.


Manipulation

This Trait comes in handy if you ever need to homswoggle a body.
Manipulation is laying down palaver, keeping a poker face or pulling
the wool over someone's eyes. ‘Course, it's not something
that wins you friends. Folks who know you know you walk
on your belly and speak with forked tongue. This art is best
practiced on greenhorns and total strangers.

Manipulation comes into play whenever you try to
cozen or cajole anybody. How charming they think you
are doesn't matter a whit (what you're trying to do can
become more or less tricky, though, depending).

If you bollix up a Manipulation attempt, and if your
target tumbles to your aims, she's likely to turn hot on you.
Folks get manipulated day in and day out, and usually don't
squawk about it. If they get their noses rubbed in it though, it
can be trouble. Manipulation can get things done, but if you
earn a reputation as an inveigler, your name is pretty much
mud. Characters with high Manipulation ratings are not
much trusted by the folks who know them best.

Specialties: Subtle, Guileful, Irrefutable, Foxy, Talkative,
Histrionic, Fine-Spoken, Blandiloquent, Soft Soap, Gift of Gab
. Poor: You couldn't lie on the ground.

oe Average: You are sometimes thought a fibber.
Good: You should be named "Walking Firewater."

Exceptional: You could sell snake oil.
e@eeee Outstanding: You could sell snake oil to snake oil
salesmen.

Appearance

Appearance is a measure of how handsome a buck or how pretty a filly you
may be. It also rates that quality even some homely folks have that just draws
people to them. Appearance tells what kind of figure you cut in the great, wide
world, and whether people find you magnetic or pathetic.

Appearance is key anytime you can't fall back on flattery. It
counts a lot more than most folks think. You might tell yourself
looks don't matter to you, but it's human nature to be put off by
gruesome features.

Be aware that the Old West's laws and attitudes about "misce-
genation" and "half-breeds" are very different. Persons obviously
of mixed racial heritage are usually trusted by no one and perse-
cuted by anyone who takes notice. Bigotry is rampant, so
consider any visible ethnicity a disadvantage,
unless a character is surrounded by
her own kind.


Appearance lets the Storyteller determine how people
respond to you on first sight. It can affect all your Social rolls
with respect to a particular person (sometimes your rating
limits the number of successes from a Social endeavor that
can actually take effect; this restriction can make it impos-
sible for an unsightly person to get much more than a foot in
the door, so to speak).

Specialties: Well-Made, Luscious, Spruce, Beauteous, Dap-
per, Fair, Sleek, Blessed, Spirited, Sniptious

. Poor: Folks usually throw things at you to get your
attention.
ee Average: You're a plain-looking jasper (or jane).

eee Good: You're nice to look at, and folks let you know it.
eoee

Exceptional: You need a big stick to fend off your
many admirers.

Outstanding: You are fresh-sprung from the brow of
some god.

Mental

These Traits indicate your character's braininess. In-
cluded here are what goes on between your ears and how
attentive you are to what comes in through your ears (and
eyes and nose, too).

Perception

This Trait tells how aware you are of the world around
you. Sometimes you do concentrate on what your senses
are telling you, say if you're hunting. More often, it hap-
pens that you just notice something. Perception is essential
on the frontier, where noticing the temperature of the air
can matter to your survival. Folks insensitive to the differ-
ent kinds of signs don't stay that way too long — chances
are, they end up bad hurt or plain dead.

Perception comes into play when you need to make
sense of some detail or to be aware of some change (or its
absence) in your surroundings. It reckons how alert you are
to being bushwhacked, to the mood around the card table
or to the news carried by the local flora and fauna.

Specialties: Eagle-Eyed, Perspicacious, Heedful, Cogni-
zant, Wary, Watchful, Circumspect, Vigilant, Cautious, A
Regular Bloodhound

. Poor: You've been known to miss your hand in front
of your face.

ee Average: You miss things that don't much matter to
your day-to-day.

ooo Good: You miss little, and can distinguish one horse

from another at great distances.
eeee Exceptional: You miss nothing, and are regarded
with some awe.
Outstanding: You can tell what's over yonder by

tasting the wind.

Intelligence

Intelligence is how well you can remember stuff, how easy you
soak up learning and what youcan do with the stuff youremember.
Ir'simportant when youuse Abilities thatdependon working with
your noggin. Some folks say Intelligence is having a steel-trap
mind or being able to make snap decisions, but there's more to it
— think of it as the difference betwixt man and beast.

Common sense, frontier savvy and wisdom don't fall under
Intelligence; a character has as much of this stuff as a player
brings to the table. Low Intelligence, though, earmarks a
character who cannot contemplate the meaning of life or
similar notions. A character of this sort never mastered book-
learning, and is a simple soul at heart.

Characters with high Intelligence are another kettle of fish.
These folks can use twenty-dollar words without embarrassing
themselves, and have a grasp of rhetoric, logic, history, phi-
losophy and all the rest. They have enjoyed the full benefits of
education. They know who's posing and who ain't. Intelli-
gence produces thoughtfully considered conclusions, not hastily
made choices (which derive from the Wits Attribute).

Specialties: Shrewd, Sagacious, Sapient, Whip-Smart, Sharp
as a Tack, Adept, Profound, Learned, Solon, Mental Wizard
. Poor: You have trouble telling right from left.
ee Average: You have the three R's down.
eee Good: Your mastery of the winter count is legendary.
e@eee Exceptional: You could teach Pasteur a thing or two.
Outstanding: You could teach all there is to know,
because you know it.

Wits

The Wits Attribute rates how fast you can adjust to new
things as they come your way. It's also a measure of how keen
and constructive a mind you have. Put simply, it tells how
speedy a thinker you are. Deeper minds think of Wits as your
handiness in applying Ockham's Razor to a predicament.

Characters possessing low Wits sometimes can't act on
account of being surprised. Low Wits can mark you as someone
easily duped. You tend to accept any old gobbledygook as
gospel. High Wits, on the other hand, lets you soak up stuff
lickety-split. Only rarely do you get caught with your trousers
down, and even then you manage to keep a level head.

Specialties: Acute, Sensible, Ingenious, Canny, Rational,
Pragmatic, Cool

. Poor: You're a favored client of snake-oil peddlers.

ee Average: You know when to bet and when to fold at
the card table.

eee Good: You know when to leave the card table to

avoid a shoot-out.

eeee Exceptional: You know when to start a shoot-
out at the card table.
e@eeee Outstanding: You invented card-counting.

128 Werewolf: The Wild West


Talents

Talents are those untrained Abilities a body takes to
naturally. Talents can't be come by from book learning,
mostly; the school of hard knocks is usually their only teacher.
‘Course, for a character that means experience comes during
play. If your character does something that calls for a Talent
he lacks, your roll doesn't change, but there's no penalty.
You'd roll a number of dice equal to the base Attribute in
question. Since Talents are such ingrained activities, it's
assumed a character has some facility for each one, however
tiny that facility may be.

Alermess

Frontier life has taught you the value of circumspection,
whatever may be going on around you. You are a good scout,
one who can keep keen straight through a night watch.
Alertness is the notice you give to the goings-on around you.
Ittells how resistant you are to distractions, weariness and the
like when paying attention is what matters.

. Novice: You notice changes more than the average
cowpoke.

ee Practiced: You keep an eye out and know your

territory.

Competent: You're like a hawk.

Expert: You're always ready for whatever life throws

at you.

e@eeee Master: You're an all-seeing eye.

Possessed by: Scouts, Trackers, Hunters, Pinkertons, Look-
outs, Lawmen

Specialties: Bushwhacking, Trailblazing, Counting Coup,
Prairies, Woods, Saloons, Prisons

Athletics

The West is no place for wallflowers. This Ability deals
with your basic physicality. It comes into play to judge
whether you can leap a gulch, fire a bow, swim through
rapids, bean somebody with a rock, scramble up a slope or
climb a tree.

Athletics includes those activities that call for some unity of
movement. Anything needing only one sort of motion, like
lifting, doesn't use the Athletics rating. Neither does athletic
stuff covered by another Ability.

° Novice: Wagonmaster
oe Practiced: Mule Skinner
Competent: Cow Puncher
¢ — Expert: Bull Whacker
eeeee Master: Bear Wrestler
Possessed by: Ranch Hands, Native Tribes, Sodbusters,
Teamsters, Miners, Railway Workers
Specialties: Swimming, Climbing, Archery, Herd-
& ing, Thrown Weapons, Farming, most professions

You've done this. This Ability covers stuff like punching,
kicking, grappling, throttling, throwing, gouging, clawing and
biting. Brawling can be low-down and dirty, but generally
won't kill anybody — unless you happen to be biting and
clawing with fangs and claws instead of fingers and teeth.
Werewolves with Brawl have learned particularly effective, if
feral, methods of fighting, and may choose certain special
maneuvers with which to maul their foes (see pgs. 242-244).
° Novice: You've used your fists, but not often.
ee Practiced: Youcan makea body know he's been inafight.
Competent: You can hold your own in a free-for-all.
Expert: You're plenty dangerous without asix-shooter.
Master: You can kill with your bare hands.

Possessed by: Ahroun, Freebooters, Guttertrash, Enfore-
ers, Soldiers, Tribal Warriors

Specialties: Fisticuffs, Wrestling, Scrapping, Sockdollager
Blows, Throws, Bear Hugs, Bullying

Him that's hit least hits longest. Dodge is a vital Talent, which
isanotherway ofsayingit'll keep youaliveand kicking. Yourrating
here tells how well you can steer clear of both melee and missile
attacks. It includes diving for cover and ducking punches.
. Novice: Youdive forthe dirtifsomebody hollers, "Duck!"
oe Practiced: You can avoid flying barstools likea champ.
Competent: [t'sarare tomahawk that's tasted your blood.

Sundays.
e@eeee Master: You make sharpshooting hard work.
Possessed by: Bandits, Toughs, Soldiers, Pugilists, Braves
Specialties: Scuttle, Weave, Dart, Bob, Zigzag

Empathy

You have a ken for folks' feelings, and know what moves
them and why. You can often peep someone's cards, so to
speak, just by giving him a hearing. You also have a nose for
prevarication. Empathy's drawback is that, like as not, you get
yanked into whatever is the mood of the folks around you.
. Novice: Folks treat you like their father confessor.
oe Practiced: You can tell at a glance when somebody's

caught in a funk.

eeve

Brotherly/Sisterly Love, Truth-Telling

Expression

Expression is making yourself understood, whether through
correspondence or utterance. Characters with high Expression
touch your soul, but don't necessarily engage your mind;
intelligent or meaningful expression falls under other Traits.
Expression at its best can be art.

likewise. Youknow where outlawsare tobe found, youknow the lingo
once you get there, and you know who's safe to cross and who ain't.

Primal-Urge

Thistellshow close youare to your roots, in more beast ways than
human. Primal-Urge is a rating of how well you get along as a wolf
andasa half-wolf. Garou with Primal-Urge skills tap the bestial part
of their being. Primal-Urge is raw instinct, not rational thought.
With practice, the Primal-Urge Talent allowsashapechanger more
control over the assorted werewolf forms. Primal-Urge often gets
added to Perception rolls when you take on wolf or Crinos form;
plus, it's used for tracking, overland navigation, and root-
ing out hazards whose signs most two-legs forgot ages ago
(like the scent ofa predator or the unique feel of thin ice). ®

Subterfuge

You can stay poker-faced indefinitely; what's more, you can
see through other folks' poker faces to your own advantage.
Dirty little secrets fascinate you, and you can sniff out weak-
ness. Mastery of this skill makes you the perfect interlocutor, or
the perfect spy.

. Novice: A few fibs won't kill you.

Skills

Skills are those Abilities that you usually learn from some
old hand, or through the liberal application of your own elbow
grease. Anytime you take a hack at some feat needing a Skill
you have no rating in, your difficulty increases by one. You are
simply unschooled in the methods behind that Skill, so you
have a harder time accomplishing the task than does a body
who has at least some notion of what he's up to.

Animal Ken

Animals and humans don't behave in the same ways, even
when facing the same situation. Being able to understand
animal behavior can prove right handy, as a consequence.
Characters skilled in Animal Ken can read critters' brutish
thoughts. Additionally, they can rile or soothe their savage
breasts. Animal Ken is also the Skill needed to train animals.

° Novice: You can pet a stray dog and not get bit.
oe Practiced: You can bring dogs to heel.
eee Competent: You can make mongrels into
hunting dogs.

Expert: You can teach critters tricks.

Crafts

You're great at tinkering with things of all sorts, from wagon.

simple carpentry to mechanics. You always need the right tool
to get a job done, but given a knife and a dead buffalo, the
prairie is your oyster.

You grasp the little details of social life, and can comport

Etiquette comes into play during activities like dancing, dick-
ering and seduction. Etiquette is also used in diplomacy.

Skill in Firearms means you have broad knowledge of all

yoked with Charisma.

Your repertoire includes a range of crowd-pleasers like
singing, banjo plucking and acting. You know the ins and

performance on which you concentrate, more than likely
the first type of artistic expression you ever pursued.

You can ride a horse, and may be able to handle some
wagons and buggies, too. Having a Ride rating doesn't make
you expert on all animals, or with all conveyances. Your
difficulty may be raised or lowered depending on the extent of
your experience with one breed or one kind of contraption.

Stealth is how well you skulk around or conceal yourself without
raisingan alarm. It'soften rolled against another character's Perception.
° Novice: You can hide in the woods.
oe Practiced: You can hide on the prairie.

Survival

The wilderness can be a great school or an open grave for
greenhorns. The Skill of Survival includes seeking shelter,
finding your way in the wilderness, and tracking. When you use
Stealth in the wilderness, you can't roll more dice for your
Stealth Dice Pool than you have in Survival.

. Novice: You know your way from town to town.
oe Practiced: You know the lay of the land.
Competent: You could live off roots and berries.
© Expert: You could dwell in the wilderness.
e@eeee Master: You could prosper in the desert.
Possessed by: Indians, Hunters, Scouts, Trailblazers, Anchorites
Specialties: Tracking, Hunting, Prairie, Desert

Other Skills

Archery, Boating, Cooking, Disguise, Meditation, Tracking

Knowledges

Knowledges are those Abilities that make you don your
thinking cap. Mental Traits can therefore be used to boost
Knowledge rolls. Many of these Abilities call for a mentor, but
autodidacts are out there, too.

Without a Knowledge, you cannot even attempt a roll

involving it. Exceptions are when the Storyteller

decides that the roll relates to trivial information
that anybody might've picked up.

Enigmas

You favor brain teasers and riddles. Your love of mental
exercise makes you a natural at resolving all sorts of conun-
drums, from knots to secret plots. The Enigmas Knowledge
helps players unknot the tangles presented by the Storyteller.
It is invaluable for uncovering routes to lost Realms, solving
puzzles posed by sphinxes and the like, or to divine the
elaborate land schemes of railway barons.

The law may be a ass, but that ass has teeth. Folks with

Linguistics

You're presumed to be versed in your natural language. Any
languages beyond it that your character is fluent in must be
bought. Werewolf characters know their native human tongue,
the Garou language and "wolf words" without having to buy
any Linguistics. Linguistics does make for greater fluency in
these languages, though. Each level of Linguistics lets your
character speak another language like a native. Linguistics also
permits you to identify folks by their accents.

Lupus characters, it should be noted, cannot communicate
in human tongues without at least one dot in Linguistics (these
are bought with freebie points). Lupus are presumed to be
illiterate, while homid and metis Garou may or may not be
literate. The player can choose whatever suits his character's
concept best; plenty of folks weren't literate in the Old West,
but a surprising number were.

. Tenderfoot: One additional language

Practitioner: Two additional languages
Professional: Three additional languages
Authority: Four additional languages
Expert: Five additional languages

Possessed by: Translators, Professors, Diplomats, Ambas-
sadors, Vagabonds

Specialties: Cussing, Engineering, Diplomacy, Politics

Medicine

Medicine is the study of folks' innards and ailments. While a
werewolf is built different from a human, the knowledge of
medicine carries over for treating werewolves' most serious (you
know, aggravated) wounds. The Medicine Knowledge includes
an understanding of the aggregation and workings of the body,
the use of medicine, and the diagnosis and treatment of disease.
° Tenderfoot: You can soothe common aches.

ee Practitioner: You can remove limbs ably.

eee Professional: You can set fractures.

eeee Authority: You are a doctor who can recognize and
treat symptoms.

eeeee Expert: Doctors visit you after nightfall for your

herbal cures.

Rituals

Rituals are a werewolf' grasp of the traditions, history,
myths and liturgies of the Garou. This Knowledge confers
understanding of how werewolves in other tribes differ from
your own, how to read Garou glyphs and proper honorifics for
addressing your elders. You need the same number of dots (or
more) as the level of any rite you're privy to.

You have at least a basic understanding of chemistry,
botany, zoology, geology, astronomy and other sciences. Your
Knowledge lends practical understanding of the physical
world. Higher levels almost always point to a specialized field
of endeavor.

Backgrounds

These Traits describe your character's unique particulars.
You must decide why and how you came by this set of Traits.
How did you get ahold of your fetish? Who was your mentor?
Once you make sense of your Backgrounds, fold them into your
character mix.

A Background sometimes can be conjoined with an At-
tribute, like rolling Wits + Resources to get one more advance
against your inheritance from Pa, or Charisma + Allies to
spread your message across a town.

Backgrounds can't be increased with experience points.
They can be raised only through what occurs in the course of
the story. Excepted from this restriction is the Totem Back-
ground (see Totem, below).

Allies

Your Allies are folks who aid and abet you — they could
be your kin, your chums or even some fraternal group you're
partial to. Allies have lives of their own, so don't think you
can treat them like property. You can on occasion, though,
fall back on their good graces, hospitality or what have you.
They can also be routes to Contacts or Resources. Allies are
basically friends who give you an assist now and then out of
mutual tenderness or because y'all have some area of accord.

Counteach point of Allies you take as one Ally. This person
has influence in her town or territory. An Ally might
bea sheriff, an aide-de-camp to the mayor or even (if
your rating is a 4 or 5) the mayor himself.

Werewolf: The Wild West


LIER EL.

Allies typically are the salt of the earth. Alliancesdon'tspring
up overnight, though, and friends sometimes ask favors of their
own. Chances are they likely don't know you're a werewolf, but
they may be privy to some of your powers and so might ask that
you use them. This can be the start of a story all by itself.

. One Ally, having modest influence and power.
ee Two Allies, both of them having modest power.
eee Three Allies, one of them being quite influential.

Four Allies, one of them being very influential.
eeeee Five Allies, one of them being extremely influential.

Contacts

You know folks of all sorts; together, they constitute a web
connecting you to high society and low, and everything in betwixt.

Contacts include not only folks you can manipulate or bribe
for information, but also trustworthy friends who volunteer
nuggets to you (in their areas of expertise). You should flesh out
these Major Contacts as complete characters, either in the
course of the chronicle or before it begins. You also have a
handful of Minor Contacts spread throughout the vicinity.
When you want to reach a Minor Contact, make a roll using
your Contacts rating (difficulty 7). Each success means you
have tracked down one of your
Minor Contacts. Of course,
bribery or sweet-talk
on your part will be
tequired to get any
information
you're after.

° One Major Contact
Two Major Contacts
Three Major Contacts
Four Major Contacts

Five Major Contacts

Fetish

You possess a spirit fetish, a physical object inhabited by
a bound spirit. It is a highly valued object of legendary
import to the Garou, and it has a number of powers be-
stowed by the invested spirit. The greater your score in this
Background, the more potent the fetish. See the Appendix
for a list of sample fetishes.

. You possess one Level 1 fetish;
ee You possess one Level 2 fetish or two Level | fetishes.
eee You possess one or more fetishes with a total of three

levels.

You possess one or more fetishes with a total of four

levels.

You possess one or more fetishes with a total of five
levels.


Kinfolk

You are in contact with certain humans or wolves who,
though descended from the Garou, are not full-blooded
werewolves. They are, in fact, earthly creatures. They do
withstand Delirium, though, and know your roots; they'll help
you any way they can, though most are not high muckety-
mucks by any reckoning (influential folks are classed as Allies).
Bunches of Kinfolk can be a prime asset for Garou wanting to
deal with the human world.

Itshould be stressed that youcan pick your friends, butyoucan't
pick your family. Most of your Kinfolk will probably feel some
measure of filial affection toward you, but relatives have been
known to grow jealous of Garou power now and again. Whether
human or wolf, your kin are people, and should be treated as such.
Kinfolk are the lifeblood of the werewolf race, and often your only
chance atanything resembling romantic love ora partially normal
life. Get to know them — it's usually worth the effort.

° Two Kinfolk
Five Kinfolk
10 Kinfolk
20 Kinfolk
50 Kinfolk

Mentor

This Background indicates one or more elders who take an
interest in you. Each rating point in Mentor you possess tells
how powerful your Mentor is within the tribe, and what Rank
he orshe has risen to. Your Mentor can advise you, protect you,
speak for you with other elders, teach you, steer you away from
tribal transgressions, and clue you in to means of advancing
your own Rank. A powerful Mentor might be a group instead
of a lone wolf (so to speak). A pack, by that reckoning, could
be a collective Mentor, as could a sept's ruling council.

Mentors may expect some boon in compensation for the aid
they lend. It may be they just like the sight of your smiling face,
but in a pinch they may call on their "apprentice." This can
lead to some rousing good stories. Mostly, though, you are the
beneficiary in such relationships.

° Mentor is Rank 2
oe Mentor is Rank 3
eee Mentor is Rank 4

Mentor is Rank 5
eeeee Mentor is Rank 6 (or even higher!)

Past Life

Ancestral memory, for the Garou, is like a live thing. Many
werewolves can actually recall moments from the lives of their
distant forebears. Some can even invite the spirits of their ances-
tors to take over their bodies. When a werewolf is possessed in this
manner, she can sometimes do stuff way beyond her abilities.

A werewolf with this Background may, once per game session,
roll his Past Life Background (difficulty 8; or difficulty 10 ifhe tries

to contact the spirit of a particular ancestor). Each success allows
the character to add one die to the Dice Pool of any Ability, or to
create a Dice Pool (of one die per success) for an Ability that the
character does not even possess. Say a Garou with a Past Life of 4
is in a desperate battle; he's no Geronimo himself (Dex 2, Brawl
1), but he rolls his Past Life rating (difficulty 8) and scores three
successes. Look out! He's just summoned Scares-the-Wind, his
warrior ancestor, who guides him in battle, and so adds three to his
Brawl score. Even if the character had no Brawling Talent
whatsoever, he would now have an effective score of 3.

As noted, it's more difficult to contact specific ancestors, but
success may bring on presentiments or advice at the Storyteller's
whim. All effects of the Past Life bonus last for one scene only.

A botch on the Past Life roll may mean that the character
becomes overwhelmed by the memories of thousands of lives
(effectively making the character a vegetable for the rest of the
scene). Worse still, the character may be possessed by the spirit
ofanancestor who likes his new home and won'tgive itup. The
ancestor will stick around for as long as the Storyteller says so.
. You get bits and pieces from the distant past.

ee A few faces and places stand out in your mind.

eee You know some of your distant ancestors by name.

e@eee You chew the fat with your ancestors like they're
standing next to you.

e@eeee You've earned a name among the Ancestor-spirits;

they often come to you.

Pure Breed

This Background indicates your "pedigree" — your lineage and
ancestry, your markings and carriage, and other features of birth.
If you have a high Pure Breed score, other Garou see you as an
incarnation of werewolf heroes of yore, and they expect you to
behave accordingly. The higher your score, the greater your
latitude in traversing the territory of other Garou unmolested or
in gaining hospitality from a foreign tribe. It is the closest thing to
status in Garou society — each point of Pure Breed adds an extra
die to Social or challenge rolls involving other Garou (even Black
Spiral Dancers!). Pure Breed is an almost mystical trait; eons of
instinct tell other Garou who is Pure Bred and who isn't.

The migration of Europeans across the New World is
diluting bloodlines, though, and the White Man's entry into
Indian Lands is wiping out many native Pure Bred. Tribes
expect more from those few who are pure of blood, and they
frown upon those who don't accept this heritage.

° You carry yourself well.
oe Your distant cousin in the distant past was the
sidekick of a great Garou warrior.

eee Other Garou part to allow you passage.

eeee = The bards of your sept blessed your birth as a sign
from the spirits.

e@eeee You area legendary hero of the past in the

flesh again.

Werewolf: The Wild West


Resources

This Trait describes your monetary worth or your ability to
tap such resources. Your Resources are not totally liquid assets,
but you can often sell them to gain money. It may take weeks or
even months to do so, depending on how much needs to be sold.

This Trait presumes that the character gets a monthly
stipend appropriate to his level of Resources. The source of this
income must be stated, though, as the well could easily run dry
depending on a given chronicle's events.

° Small savings: You rent a room and may owna horse.
If liquidated, you would have $100 in cash. Allow-
ance of $10 a month.

ee Middle class: You own a lean-to or sod house. If
liquidated, you would have $500 in cash. Allowance
of $20 a month.

ooo Large savings: You own a cabin. If liquidated, you

would have $4,000 in cash. Allowance of $80 a month.

Well off: You own a ranch or perhaps a country

manor in fair repair. If liquidated, you would have

$50,000 in cash. Allowance of $500 a month.

eeeee Fantastically rich: You're a millionaire in a time
when a million dollars is all but unimaginable. If
liquidated, you would have at least $1,000,000 in
cash. Allowance of $5,000 a month.

Rites

This Trait tells how many rites the character is privy to at
the beginning of the game. This rating may represent one big
rite ora number of lesser ones whose total levels equal the Rites
rating; remember, though, that a character's Rituals Knowl-
edge rating must at least equal the level of a given rite before
the character can learn it at all. A list of rites is given later in
this chapter. Note: Two Minor Rites can be purchased in place
of one Level One rite; see Minor Rites, pg. 177.

Character knows four levels of rites
eeeee Character knows five levels of rites

Totem
This Trait does not apply directly to the character, but to
the pack's totem. The Background points invested in this Trait
by all the characters in the pack are pooled to determine the
power of the totem. Pack totems are avatars of the totem they
represent. They cannot be destroyed or bound into fetishes
unless a pack is disbanded. If a totem is reduced to zero Power,
it will Reform elsewhere; during this period, the totem may not
be reached by pack members.
Each totem has a Background Cost rating; the pack must
spend that amount to ally with that totem. Some totems
€ are willing to lend great powers to their devotees; these

Chapter Six: Traits

totems are more costly than others. See Pack Totems, pg. 284, for
a list of possible totems. The base totem spirit has seven points to
divide betwixt Rage, Willpower and Gnosis, and a base Power of
20. The totem also has the Charms of Airt Sense and Reform.
Unless a special relationship is purchased (see below), the totem
isa bit snooty toward the pack and is of minor influence within the
community of spirits. Some pack totems, however, have become
the totems of whole septs or even tribes; such stories abound
among the Galliards.

Totems lend special potencies to pack members, such as Gifts
or Abilities. However, these powers may be used by only one pack
member at a time (per turn). Each turn, the pack member
"borrowing" the powers must declare upon whom he endows them
next tum (or ifhe'll keep them). The exception is Renown, which
is awarded to all pack members when they ally with the totem.

Any extra points spent (after the totem's Background Cost has
been spent) can be added to the totem's powers as listed below:
Cost Power

1 Per three points to spend on Willpower, Rage and/or
Gnosis
1 Per 10 points of Power
1 Totem can speak to the pack (the pack does not
require the Spirit Speech Gift)
Totem can always find the pack members
Totem is nearly always with the pack members
Totem is respected by other spirits
Per Charm possessed
Per extra pack member who can use the totem's
powers in the same turn
4 Totem is mystically connected to all pack members,
allowing communication among them even at great
distances (Storyteller's discretion)
5 Totem is feared by servants of the Wyrm

The Totem Trait is the only Background that can be
increased through experience (see Spending Experience, pg.
214); the cost is per the chart above. However, the Storyteller
should only allow this in special situations, and only if the pack
in some way achieves a revelation about their totem, or some
new link to it during play. Such a special situation could be
whena pack member gains a new Rank or when anew member
joins the pack.

Gifts

Creatures as much spirit as flesh, werewolves have learned
many secrets from long years of walking the Umbra and com-
muning with its denizens. Most of all, the Garou have learned a
variety of supernatural "tricks," shortcuts for doing things in a
more magical way that they call Gifts. Gifts are the way in which
werewolves can affect the world through Gnosis, using their
spirit heritage to work magic. Gifts are divided by breed, auspice
and tribe — each group of Garou has its own particular secrets.

wrnnne


SSN

ZLLL;

ox

Gifts are divided into levels, with Level One being the weakest Gifts, those
taught to the raw cubs, and Level Five Gifts being the greatest secrets, only taught
to those heroes who have proved themselves time and again. A beginning character

may choose one Level One Gift from each of her breed, auspice and tribe Gift lists
— three Gifts in all. There are far more Gifts in existence than those depicted here,
and traditions of teaching vary from time to time and place to place. The following
Gifts are simply those which are most commonly passed on in the Savage West.
Although Gifts are purchased with experience, no werewolf can buy a Gift of
a level higher than her Rank. Garou may learn the Gifts of other breeds, auspices
and tribes during play, provided they can find a spirit to teach them, but these have
a higher cost (see the Experience Chart, pg. 214).

Learning Gifts
Gifts are normally taught only by spirits. A werewolf must either petition a particular
spirit to teach him its powers (usually by offering chiminage) orask an elder tosummon
that spirit and petition for him. Only spirits allied to Garou will teach Gifts, and they
will not teach them to werewolves who have not attained the proper Rank.

Werewolves may learn Gifts outside their breed, auspice or tribe, but
these cost extra experience points due to the lack of that primal insight
into what it is to be, say, Ragabash. Besides, finding a spirit or elder
willing to teach these secrets to an "outsider" is a real challenge.
In order to find a spirit teacher, a Garou must either travel toa
caern equal in power to the Gift's level (a Level Three caern for
Level Three Gifts) to seek the spirit personally, or ask a Theurge
tosummon the particular spirit sought (such as a Night-spirit for
the Uktena Gift: Cloud of Night). If neither option is viable,
the Garou must quest through the Umbra in search of the
desired spirit and then convince it to teach him. Spirits will
often be wary of requests not put to them through the proper
channels; they must often be appeased with chiminage of
an appropriate fashion (cleaning a stream for a Water-
spirit, an offering of prey to a Hawk-spirit and so on).
Each Gift lists either the type of spirit that first taught
the Gift to the Garou, or a more accessible teacher in the
Savage West (Tiger-spirits may be adept at teaching Gifts
of ferocity, but they're rather rare in the Storm Umbra).
Some of these spirits are detailed in the Appendix. The
spirits listed with each Giftare not necessarily the only ones
capable of teaching the Gift; the Storyteller may decide
whether or not to allow another spirit to teach the Gift. The
Storyteller is free to create any spirits not detailed. Spirits
encountered while learning a Gift are meant to be roleplaying
opportunities, allowing a character to interact with another
being and learn its ways. Almost anyone can figure out how
to roleplay a Fire-spirit without the need of Traits.

Sometimes a Garou may teach another Garou a
Gift he knows. Unlike spirit teaching,
which only takes a short time
(about an hour for the

spirit to impart

its wisdom),


this can take quite a while, during which the Garou tries to show the other how to
perform the trick. The training takes a month, at the end of which time the student
can spend experience points and acquire the Gift. However, acquisition in this
manner costs one more experience point than normal.

There are other drawbacks to Garou-taught Gifts; for example, the Gift has
to be practiced by the student before he can master it (this is not the case when
spirits teach). Thus, it can be quite painful to learn a Gift such as Silver
Claws. Certain Gifts, such as Call of the Wyrm, are forbidden to be taught
except by spirits — no sept can afford to have wayward Wyrm creatures ®
coming around just so a student can practice. ‘\

Systems

Gifts are highly variable in effect, depending largely on the whims of the spirits
who grant them. If no set difficulty exists for a given use of the Gift, the Storyteller
may assume difficulty 6, or pick anything more appropriate. The Storyteller is also
encouraged to make botches interesting, embarrassing and unique.

There are rumors of Gifts beyond Level Five, but such Gifts, if they indeed exist, are
known only to the wisest elders.

Breed Gifts

Breed Gifts are usually taught by spirits who owe the breed a favor in accordance with
an ancient pact or deed. For instance, an ancient tale tells of a metis who helped a mole
hide from predators. In return, the mole taught the metis how to burrow through the earth,
and since that time, Mole-spirits will teach metis this Gift.

Homid

Humans have always been at odds with nature, in some way or another. What's , "
more, as consummate tool-users, humans have always aimed to shape their surround-
ings to their own liking. As a result, homids are often much more distant from the
primordial side of nature, and their Gifts usually reflect their natural inclinations
toward controlling things and people. This spiritual dearth also means that all too
often, the spirit most willing to instruct a homid is one of his own Ancestor-
spirits; Nature-spirits just aren't as friendly to the human-born. ee

misfire with the use of this Gift. This Gift is taught by certain Wyld-spirits by
way of revenging themselves on the invading Weaver forces.

rolls Manipulation + Crafts (difficulty 5). The,
gun becomes unusable for one turn per success.

System: The werewolf rolls Manipulation + Intimidation
against the target's Willpower. For every success scored, the
Garou may give a target one command that he must follow.
Extra successes allow the werewolf to affect more targets or

that would cause harm to himself or others.

‘¥ Staredown (Level Two) — When in a Mexican stand-
off, the first person to look away or flinch is usually the first to
die. Using this Gift not only allows the Garou to cause the
target to flinch, but usually causes the target to drop his gun and
run away in terror. All the werewolf has to do is look into the
eyes of his target. The Gift can be used against Garou, but a
Garou will freeze in place rather than flee. This Gift is taught
by a Ram-spirit or a Snake-spirit.

System: The Garou rolls Charisma + Intimidation (diffi-
culty 6). The victim flees for one turn per success. Should the
Garou score five or more successes, the victim flees for the
duration of the scene. Garou targets will not flee, but they
cannot attack while this Gift is in ee un cked |

ingof the Weaver, the Garoucan reshape cnorlivng material (but

‘w Weave of Steel (Level Four) — The homid can mysti-
cally reinforce any manmade structure or material, ing
planks the strength of steel and steel the toughness of diamond.
This Gift is taught by a Weaver-spirit.

140

System: The werewolf must concentrate for a turn to
reweave the object's strength. The player spends one Gnosis
and rolls Strength + Crafts, difficulty 7. The effects are largely
up to the Storyteller to adjudicate; one success will make a shirt
alot more durable, while five successes will reinforce a rickety,
half-rotted trestle enough to support an ore-laden train. Gen-
erally speaking, objects gain the equivalent of one Health
Level per success, making them harder to destroy. If the homid
chooses to affect his clothing with this Gift, he gains an extra
die to his soak pool for every success; this clothing counts as
armor (and can be used to soak silver damage), but is effectively
weightless. However, such restrengthened armor is only effec-
tive versus cutting or piercing attacks, as it offers no rigid
protection against heavy blows.

is taught by an Ancestor-spirit.

Metis

The shunned metis are actually able to draw strength from
their miserable lot. Raised in Garou society, they have an
uncanny insight into werewolf affairs; what's more, their too-
potent blood fuels them with anger and pain that they can
channel outward. Undiluted by human or wolf blood, they also
are much more primal creatures, and enjoy a deep connection
with Gaia's heart.

‘w Create Element (Level One) — The Garou can create a
small amount of one of the four basic elements —fire, air, earth
or water. In this way, a Garou can summon a breath of fresh air
in a poisonous mine, make a stone to drop on someone, create a
fire without flint or fuel, or call up water in the middle of Death
Valley. The Gift can't create precious metals (especially silver),
lethal gases or acid. This Gift is taught by an elemental.

System: The Garou spends one Gnosis point and rolls
Gnosis (difficulty 6). One cubic foot of the desired element is
created per success, to a maximum weight of 100 lbs.
element is now permanent until used up (breathed in the¢
of air, or burned up in the case of a fire without fuel).

w Eyes of the Cat (Level Three) — The werewolf may see
clearly in pitch darkness; his eyes glow a lambent green while
this power is in effect. This Gift is taught by a Cat-spirit.

System: The player need only state when this Gift is in
effect; there's no roll or Gnosis cost. The Garou suffers no
difficulty or Dice Pool penalties from darkness.

‘ Rattler's Bite (Level Three) — The metis' fangs lengthen
when she learns this Gift; she can then secrete a deadly poison
from her fangs. When the Garou bites, she can paralyze and
even kill a victim. Some elder Garou look down upon this Gift
as a deformity. Naturally, Snake-spirits teach this Gift.

System: By spending a Rage point, the werewolf can inject
her target with a powerful snake poison. If the bite succeeds,
any remaining damage after the soak roll is doubled. If the
attack fails or causes no damage, the Rage point is still lost.

the insanity lasts. The Storyteller selects the form of insanity,
but the supernatural madness should be plenty severe.

‘v Totem Gift (Level Five) — The metis can draw on
her link with her tribe's totem, beseeching the totem to lend
her some of its power. The effects of this Gift are up to the
Storyteller, but should be in keeping with the totem; thus,
Wendigo might send an ice storm against his child's foes,
while Cockroach might grant a metis Iron Rider the strength
and speed of a locomotive. This Gift is taught by the
werewolf's tribal totem.

System: The Garou spends one Gnosis point and rolls
Charisma + Rituals (difficulty 9).

Lupus

Lupus Gifts reflect the primal side of the wolf-bom, drawingon the
lupus' connections to the wild placesand the creatures that live there.

‘@ Heightened Senses (Level One) — The werewolf
sharpens her senses beyond their normal reach; in Homid or
Glabro form, her senses become as sharp as those of a wolf,
while in her wolf forms, her senses become incredibly potent.
This Gift is taught by a Wolf-spirit.

System: The Garou spends a Gnosis point. In Homid and
Glabro forms, her Perception difficulties are reduced by two; she
may also roll Perception + Primal-Urge (difficulty 6) to perform
sensory feats impossible to humans. In all other forms, her Percep-
tion difficulties are reduced by three, and she gains an extra die to
Primal-Urge Dice Pools. The effects last for one scene.

‘w Jackrabbit Jump (Level One) — The werewolf may leap
incredible distances, well beyond the range of normal humans
or wolves. This Gift is taught by Hare- or Cat:

‘w Sense the Unnatural (Level Two) — The werewolf can
pick out any unnatural presence, gauging its approxima
strength and type. This can include magic, spirits, the fae,
Wyrm-beasts, etc. This also detects vampires, but only those
with Humanity scores below 7. This Gift is taught by any spirit
servant of Gaia.

System: The Garou rolls Perception + Enigmas (difficulty
6). The more successes rolled, the more information is gained.
However, deciphering the information might require an Intel-
ligence + Occult roll (Storyteller's option).

w Catfeet (Level Three) — This Gift gives the Garou the
agility of a cat, allowing him to land on his feet after falls of less
than 100 feet without harm. He also has perfect balance even
on the most slippery surfaces, and the difficulties of all combat
actions involving body slams and grappling are reduced by two.
This Gift is taught by a Cat-spirit.

System: The Garou does not need to expend points or roll
for this ability; it becomes innate to those who learn the Gift.

‘@ Healing Sleep (Level Three) — The lupus can fall into
a state of hibernation for a day, healing even the most severe
wounds while he sleeps. This Gift is taught by a Bear-spirit.

System: The player spends a Gnosis point and rolls Stamina +
Primal-Urge. The werewolf falls into hibernation, and is unaware
of his surroundings while he rests. If injured, he will awaken, but

lupus awakes with all Health Levels healed, even if they were
aggravated damage. This Gift cannot heal battle scars, however.

‘w Gnaw (Level Four) — The Garou may strengthen her
jaws to the point that, given time, she can chew through just
about anything. In addition, her bite does grievous damage to
any unlucky foes. This Gift is taught by a Wolf-spirit.

System: The playerspendsa Willpower pointand rolls Stamina
+ 4 against a variable difficulty (3 for wood, 6 for iron bars, 8 for
a heavy safe). The length of time it takes to gnaw through
something is up to the Storyteller. When employing this power in
combat, the werewolf's bite does an extra die of damage.

‘w Lord of the Wilds (Level Four) —The lupus can call the
beasts of the wild to do her bidding, speaking with and
commanding them as she likes. If there are no animals of the
sort desired in the vicinity, none can be called. This Gift is
taught by any Animal-spirit.

System: The player spends a Gnosis point and rolls Charisma
+ Animal Ken (difficulty 7). One success affects animals of the
chosen type within a 10-mile radius; each additional success adds
10 miles to the radius. The effect lasts for one scene, but the time

prove the Beast's disposition.

Ragabash

Ragabash are the pranksters and deceitful among the Garou.
Their tricks work best under cover of darkness, especially when
the night is moonless. Luna allows New Moons to break the rules.

‘@ Scent of Running Water (Level One) —The Garou can
completely mask her scent, making herself virtually impossible
to track. This Gift is taught by a Fox-spirit.

System: The difficulties of all rolls to track the character

increase by two. This Gift is an innate ability to the

Garou who learns it; she need not spend any points or
make any rolls.

Werewolf: The Wild West


‘w Spider's Song (Level One) — The Ragabash can put her
ear toa telegraph pole or wire and eavesdrop on any messages
sent over the line. The Garou need not know Morse Code to
understand the message; it is whispered to the werewolf by a
Spider-spirit that tends the lines. This Gift is taught by a
Spider-spirit.

System: The character puts her ear to a telegraph pole or
wire. The player spends a Gnosis point and all messages sent
along the line are translated to the Garou. The character can
eavesdrop as long as he remains in contact with the line; he
can't intercept or change the messages

‘w Walk under the New Moon (Level One) — The Garou
seems to disappear from sight, becoming little more than a
shadow. This Gift allows him to pass virtually unseen. However,
once the werewolf has been spotted, this Gift is negated unless the
viewer is again distracted. This Gift is taught by a Moon-spirit.

System: The playerrolls Manipulation + Stealth (difficulty 8).
Each success increases the difficulties of rolls to detect him by one.

@ Carried on the Wind (Level Two) — This Gift garbles
communication between anyone within the Ragabash's vicin-
ity, whether by the sound of the wind, ringing echoes or
otherwise overwhelming background noises. Mes-
sages carried by wolfor Garou howls, even messages
sent on telegraph lines are made incomprehen- Js
sible. This Gift is taught by a Wind-spirit.

System: The player rolls Manipulation + Expression (diffi-
culty depends on activity surrounding the conversation).
Attempts at verbal communication are useless for the duration
of one scene or until the Ragabash leaves the area. Visual
communication is still possible, as is communication through
scent. Conveying a message by means other than talking or
writing requires a successful Intelligence + Expression roll
(difficulry depends on the complexity of the message; "go over
there" is 4, "take this gun and wait for my signal" is 6).

‘w Hide in Plain Sight (Level Two) — The werewolf
becomes completely invisible to all senses
stands still. This Gift is taught by a Lizard- or Sn:

System: The player rolls Charisma, Manipulation or Appear-

accept the Ragabash's most blatant lies as truth, at least for a
little while. This power can also be used to cheat at a game
before someone's very eyes and get away with it. This Gift is
taught by a Fox-spirit.

‘9 Luna's Blessing (Level Four) — When the moon is visible in
thenightsky, silver doesnot cause the werewolfaggravated damage.
Indeed, silver weapons may cause more harm to their wielders than
to the Garou they're used on. This Gift is taught by a Lune.

System: For each strike against the Garou, the attacker
must roll three extra dice on the attack; these dice are read to
check for botches only. The Garou can soak and regenerate
silver as if it inflicts normal damage, but only when the moon
is visible.

‘w Mark of Shame (Level Four) — The Ragabash may
invoke a terrible curse upon an opponent's body, causing it to
weaken or wither, often in retribution for some act, as a
reminder to be more alert to deception or as a frustrating
reminder of embarrassment inflicted upon the target. This Gift
is taught by a Pain-spirit or a spirit of Disease.

System: The player spends a Gnosis point and rolls Gnosis
in a resisted roll against the target's permanent Gnosis. The
Garou's difficulty is the victim's Willpower, while the victim's
difficulty is the Ragabash's Willpower. (If the victim has no
Gnosis, the Garou makes an uncontested roll against the

¢ Garou has the
the permission

victim's Willpower.) Each success achieved by the Garou
allows the player to remove one point from any of the victim's
Physical Attributes. The effect is permanent, although the
victim may restore these lost points through experience. The
Gift may be used only once per scene against a given opponent.

‘w Thieving Talons (Level Five) — Often bitterly called
"Broken Treaty" by the Pure Ones, this Gift lets the Ragabash
steal what could never have been hers — the powers of others
— and use them herself. These powers can be Garou Gifts,
spirit Charms, vampiric Disciplines or even mage Spheres.
This Gift is taught by a spirit of Vengeance ora Trickster-spirit.

System: The player must gain three successes on a Wits +
Stealth roll (difficulty of the target's Willpower). If the roll is
successful, the Garou can use the specified power for each
successive turn that the player is willing to spend a Gnosis point.

Inhabitants of a "ghost town" and newcomers do not recog-

can be moved around by both parties, though, which reinforces
the ghost town image or mystifies and aggravates citizens. The
effect lasts for one story.

Theurge

The Crescent Moon holds a deep link to the Umbra and its
denizens, and is possessed of spiritual wisdom few others could
bear. The Theurge's Gifts allow him to affect spirits and the
very stuff of the Umbra. Although many Theurges are finding
their Gifts less effective in the shifting Storm Umbra, they
nonetheless search for further insight into the changes that the
Storm Eater has wrought.

‘¥ Mother's Touch (Level One) — The werewolf can heal
the wounds of others (but not herself), aggravated or other-
wise, simply by laying hands over the afflicted area. This Gift
is taught by a Unicorn-spirit.

System: The Garou spends a Gnosis point and rolls Intel-
ligence + Medicine (difficulty of the wounded individual's
Rage, or 6 for non-Garou). Each success heals one

Health Level. Even battle scars may be cured in this *

‘w Spirit Speech (Level One) —This Giftallows the Garou
tospeak with encountered spirits. This Gift doesn't compel the
spirit to answer, or prevent it from leaving. This Gift can be
taught by any spirit.

System: Once learned, this Gift allows the Garou to under-
stand spirit-speech intuitively. Some spirits, such as Banes or
Enigmatics, may not always be understood.

‘w Command Spirit (Level Two) —The werewolf can give
simple commands to encountered spirits and be obeyed. This
conveys no summoning power — the spirit must be present to
be commanded. This Gift is taught by any Incarna avatar.

System: The player spends a Willpower point and rolls
Charisma + Leadership (difficulty of the spirit's Gnosis). Ea:

"w Sight from Beyond (Level Two) — When dangerstall
Garou, or momentous events are in the offing, visions come to P
without warming. When the Storm Eater was freed, Theurges across
the land were struck by visions of the sky ripping open; even now,
many Crescent Moons hope to gain clues of how to best the great
Bane in their dreams. This Gift is taught by a Crow-spirit.

System: Interpreting these signs is best handled through
roleplaying, but the Storyteller can require the Garou to roll
Wits + Occult (difficulty 7) if appropriate. The visions cannot
be called; they come only of their own accord.

‘w Exorcism (Level Three) — This is the Gift of ejecting
spirits from places or objects, whether they are there voluntar-
ily or are bound there. This Gift is taught by any Incarna avatar.

System: If a spirit doesn't feel like leaving, the player must
roll Manipulation + Intimidation (difficulty of the spirit's
Willpower). If the spirit has been bound to its lodging place (or
into afetish), then the Exorcist must make a Wits + Subterfuge
roll (difficulty 8) and gain more successes than the binder did
when tying the spirit to its location.

Pulse of the Invisible (Level Three) — The Theurge
need no longer focus to communicate with spirits upon master-
ing this Gift; she is now constantly aware of all that spirits do
around her and can interact with them at will. While most

spirit-business is hardly of note, she will notice any
dramatic changes in the environment. This Gift is
é taught by any spirit.

Chapter Six: Traits

System: If the Garou's Gnosis equals or exceeds the Gaunt-
let, she can automatically see into the Umbra. Otherwise, she
must roll Perception + Occult (difficulty of the Gauntlet) to
pierce the invisible wall. Only one success is required. The
effect lasts for an entire scene or until the character enters an
area with a higher Gauntlet.

‘w Grasp the Beyond (Level Four) — The Theurge may
take things to and from the Umbra without having to dedicate
them to herself (see Rite of Talisman Dedication, pg. 173). This
includes humans and animals, both willing and unwilling.
Garou often use this power to take loyal Kinfolk with them on
Umbral quests or to heal injured kin — both body and soul —
in mystic Glens. This Gift is taught by an Incarna avatar.

System: The werewolf must grasp the object or person he
wishes to take to the spirit world. The player must then spend a
number of Willpower points: one for small items, two for larger

(Svords, etc.) and three for huge items (including people).
makes the usual Gnosis roll to pierce the Gauntlet and step
ideways; if successful, both Theurge and the desired object or
person pass into the Umbra. An unwilling target may resist with
a Willpower roll; each success subtracts one from the Garou's
successes. A Theurge attempting to abduct an unwilling victim
must achieve at least three successes on his Gnosis roll.

Any person taken to the Umbra, unless she has the al
step sideways, must rely on the Garou to escape, or else find an
area with a very thin Gauntlet, such as a caern. The Gauntlet
rating must be 3 or lower for a victim to exit of his own accord.
If he does exit, he may not reenter the Umbra, no matter what
he Gauntlet rating. Some people can manage to be spit out of
mbra by passing Wyldlings, but this is as often as not fatal.
Drain (Level Four) — The Theurge may drain
power from a spirit to feed his own resolve. This Gift is taught
by a spirit servant of the Uktena totem.

System: If the Garou succeeds in a resisted Gnosis vs.
Gnosis roll while combating a spirit, the spirit loses one Power
point per success for the rest of the scene. For every two points
drained, the Garou gains a temporary Willpower point, but
loses any points exceeding her maximum at the end of the
scene.

‘e Mold the Spirit (Level Five) — The Garou can change
aspirit's form or purpose. This Gift is taught by a Chimerling.

System: The Garou must best the spirit in a resisted Gnosis
vs. Gnosis roll. The Theurge's difficulty is based on his goal (see
below), while the spirit's difficulty is the werewolf's Gnosis.

‘w Savage the Mind (Level Five) — With but a thought,
the Theurge can shred an opponent's human reasoning, giving
him the effective mind of a beast. This Gift is taught by a spirit
servant of the Griffin totem.


System: The Garou spends Gnosis (see below) and rolls
Wits + Empathy (difficulty of the target's Willpower + 3;
maximum 10). If successful, the Garou can permanently de-
stroy a target's Intelligence Attribute. Two Gnosis points must
be spent for each point of Intelligence destroyed, and the
werewolf cannot destroy more points than she rolls successes.
Additionally, the more Intelligence the target loses, the more
animalistic his behavior becomes.

Philodox

The Philodox are the arbitrators and judges of Garou
society. They are chieftains and medicine workers among
native tribes, and lawgivers (and takers) among Easterners and
Europeans. Their Gifts allow them to perceive truths, call upon
ancestral wisdom and make decrees. The Half Moons are
trapped between the rage of their primal selves and the respon-
sibility of their ordered selves.

‘w Resist Pain (Level One) — The Garou is able to resist
the pain of his wounds and continue acting normally. ThisG
is taught by a Bear-spirit.

System: The player spends one Willpower
character is not affected by wound penalties for
der of the scene.

Scent of the True Form (Level One) —
can smell what a subject really is; she can supe:
up the scent of her target's true nature. This Gift
any spirit servant of Gaia.

System: The Garou can automatically tell wl
is a werewolf, and can detect vampires and fae

be detected (difficulty 9).

‘¥ Truth of Gaia (Level One) — Philodox, as judges of the
Litany, can sense if others speak truth or lies. This Gift is taught
by a Falcon Gaffling.

System: The player rolls Intelligence + Empathy (difficulty
is the subject's Manipulation + Subterfuge). This Gift reveals
only whether the subject speaks truth or falsehood; it doesn't
reveal the actual truth, whatever that may be.

‘Call to Arms (Level Two) — The Garou can summon
low-powered spirits of a certain type — animal, air, water —
and may give them one, and only one command. Once the
spirits have fulfilled this request, they depart. This Gift is
taught by an Incarna avatar.

System: The player spends one Gnosis point and rolls
Charisma + Leadership (difficulty is the spirits' highest Will-
power) for the character to summon low-powered spirits of the
specified type. The Storyteller decides what spirits respond and
what they are capable of doing.

w King of the Beasts (Level Two) — Animals within
sight, scent and earshot of the Garou follow his orders willingly
and unconditionally (assuming he does not order their sui-
cide). They understand his orders and want to carry them out.
This Gift is taught by a Lion- or Falcon-spirit.

xX

System: The player rolls Charisma + Animal Ken (diffi-
culty depends on the scarcity of animals in the character's
vicinity). Even if animals are around, they might not be
suitable for the task at hand; that doesn't mean they don't try
to help, though, which can prove problematic in itself.

‘W Mend the Forked Tongue (Level Two) —The Garoucan
commanda target to speak the truth, despite the victim's desires.

System: The Storyteller rolls Manipulation + Intimidation
(difficulty is the victim's Intelligence + Subterfuge) on the
player's behalf. On a successful roll, the werewolf forces a
particular person to speak the truth about a specified subject.
The subject has to spend two Willpower points to resist the
command. If the Philodox's roll fails, the subject refuses the
command. Ifthe roll botches, the subject may lie to the character
(thus the need for the Storyteller to make the roll for the player).

‘w Balance Man and Beast (Level Three) — A Philodox
needs to keep a tight grip on his bestial side for fear of losing
control and, perhaps worse still, allowing his passions to control
However, a Garou cannot deny his animal nature;
n not reign alone. The Garou can calm or enrage

If in times of need. This Gift is taught by a Water-spirit.

System: The character concentrates and meditates on her
role as arbitrator and fair judge among the Garou. The player
makes a Gnosis roll (difficulty 8). For every success, one point
of Willpower or Rage is gained, as needed. Thisself-control can
only be used once per scene.

This Gift also allows a Philodox to avoid entering frenzy by
putting the character in a profound state of calm. The player

pends one ne Willpower point and the character won't frenzy for

an entire scene, no matter what happens, unless

seeks to make the change. Alternately, ifa frenzy

ever necessary, the player can spend one point of Rage and
the character automatically becomes a killing machine.

‘w Wisdom of the Ancient Ways (Level Three) — By
tapping into the racial subconscious of the Garou, the Philodox
dredges up ancient facts and lore. This Gift is taught by an
Ancestor-spirit.

System: The Garou must meditate for a short time, concen-
trating on the past. The player rolls Gnosis (difficulty 8, -1 for
each point of Past Life that the Garou possesses).

+w Rally to the Cause (Level Four) — At times a Philodox
needs to rally Garou to achieve a goal, but as events in the
Savage West prove, Garou can rarely agree on anything, let
alone work together. This Gift helps a Philodox inspire other
Garou to follow his cause, despite tribal or personal differences.
This power is most appropriate for inspiring Garou, but also
affects humans. This Gift is taught by a Falcon-spirit.

System: The character makes a speech before his audience.
The player then rolls Manipulation + Leadership (difficulty is
the highest Willpower of any dissenting targets).

Individuals who willingly follow the Philodox auto-
matically rally to him and gain the benefits conferred
by the Gift. If the player's roll succeeds, Garou who

Werewolf: ie Wild West


aaaee

TIL

disagree with the character's plan feel compelled to aid him
despite their differences of opinion (although the expenditure
ofa Willpower point may allow an individual to break from the
pack). If the roll botches, even a Garou's dedicated followers
may abandon him.

Rallied Garou each receive an extra point of Willpower to
be used in achieving the Philodox's goal. They may also be
quicker to frenzy if that suits the mission ahead (a call to battle,
for example); only three successes on a Rage roll are required
to frenzy. Rival Garou have better relations as well, and put
their differences aside for the common goal; difficulties of
Social rolls are reduced by two.

The effects of this Gift last until the Philodox's mission is
complete or for the remainder of the scene, whichever comes
first. The Gift can be used to inspire packs, members of a sept
or attendants at a moot; even native and European tribes can
be convinced to work together, assuming that the Philodox's
goal is not anathema to any party.

w Roll Over (Level Four) — The Philodox's presence
demands respect and submission. Should she succeed in a
test of wills, she dominates her foe, causing humans to fall
to their knees and wolves to roll onto their backs. This Gift
is taught by a Wolf-spirit.

System: The player must score at least three successes more
than the opponent in a resisted Willpower contest.

w Honor Bound (Level Five) — The Philodox compels
an individual or pack of Garou to perform a mission that
may not correspond to an individual's desires. Only a hand-
ful of subjects can be affected by this Gift at one time. The
Philodox's order is so compelling that few can resist, assum-
ing that it doesn't defy Garou basic instincts. The leader
could make a pack of European Garou restore a caern to its
native owners, but could not make werewolves eat human
flesh. This Gift is taught by an Incarna avatar.

System: The player spends a Gnosis point and rolls
Manipulation + Leadership (difficulty of the target's Will-
power or the highest rating in a group). Three Willpower
points must be expended to resist the order, and doing so
results in the loss of three Honor Renown. The command is
compelling until the act is accomplished to the best of the
Garou's ability.

‘@ Wall of Granite (Level Five) — The Philodox's word is
so compelling that the earth, the very foundation upon which
her people live, responds to her command. While in contact
with earth or rock, the werewolf can summon a wall to move
with her, defending her from all angles. This Gift is taught by
an earth elemental.

System: The player spends a Gnosis point. The wall's
dimensions are three yards high, two yards long and one yard
thick; it can soak damage as if it has 10 dice, and 10 Health

Levels must be inflicted to penetrate it in any one spot.
The wall lasts for one scene or until released back into
the earth by the Garou.

The Moon Dancers keep the lore and songs of the Garou
alive, and their Gifts reflect their ability to sing someone's
dreams and emotions into whatever form they choose. Passion
is the Galliard's fuel, and the voice is his weapon.

‘w Beast Speech (Level One) — The werewolf may speak
with his animal brethren, whether docile, domesticated sheep,
furious catamounts or cold-blooded rattlers. This doesn't change
the critter's basic reactions —an enraged buffalo isn't likely to
spend time in conversation, and will probably attack regard-
less. This Gift is taught by a Nature-spirit.

System: The Garou rolls Charisma + Animal Ken (diffi-
culty 6). This roll must be made for each type of animal and for
each encounter.

Call of the Wyld (Level One) — The Galliard can
summon aid by howling; werewolves even beyond the range
of hearing sense the call and may answer. This Gift can
enhance the effect of any of the normal Garou howls (s
Howls, pg. 50); it can also be sounded to summe
Garou fora moot. Finally, Call of the Wyld is of

W Mindspeak (Level One) — The Galliard can call a
waking dream into existence, sharing it with selected charac-
ters and thereby allowing them to silently communicate. This
Gift is taught by a Chimerling.

System: The Garou spends one Willpower point per
sentient being chosen; if a target is unwilling, the player rolls
Manipulation + Expression (difficulty of the victim's Will-
power). All those included in the dream may interact normally
through the Mindspeak, though they can inflict no damage
through it. Their real bodies can still act, though all Dice
Pools are reduced by two. The Mindspeak ends when all the
participants want it to, or on the turn the Galliard fails the
roll against an unwilling member. The beings affected must
be within line of sight.

‘w Call of the Wyrm (Level Two) — This frightening
cry acts as a siren song to Wyrm-creatures, drawing them to
the howler. Galliards use this Gift to create ambushes or to
flush prey from hiding. This Gift is taught by any spirit
servant of Gaia.

System: The Garou makes a resisted roll of Manipulation +
Performance vs. the Wyrm-creature's Willpower (both rolls are
difficulty 7). Ifthe Wyrm-creature loses, it mustcome to the caller.

‘¥ Distractions (Level Two) — The Garou can make
annoying yips, yelps and howls to divert the attention of his
target. This Gift is taught by a Coyote-spirit.

System: The player rolls Wits + Performance (difficulty of
the victim's Willpower). Each success subtracts one from the
target's Dice Pool next turn.

‘w Dreamspeak (Level Two) — The Galliard enters
another's dream and can thereby affect the course of that
dream. The werewolf need not be close to the target; all she
needs is to know or have seen the dreamer. This Gift is taught
by a Chimerling.

System: The player rolls Wits + Empathy (difficulty 8). If
the dreamer awakens while the werewolf is still within the
dream, the Garou is thrown out of the dream world and loses
a Gnosis point.

‘@ Come Hither (Level Three) — The Galliard can draw
anyone to his side with a mere glance. This Gift is taught by a

ake-spiri

's Willpower). The Garou needs three successes to
get to his side; fewer at least start the victim
right direction.
