Seasonal
Minor Rites

the sept is in great danger and performs a vital rite might earn
great Honor. Certain Ahroun retain the right to perform rites
of warand battle, such as the Rite of Wounding performed after
acub's first battle, saying that these areas of a sept's life are the
province of the Ahroun. Although Theurges might argue
differently, there's little evidence that the spirits themselves
have any preference for a particular auspice in the performance
of rites. This prejudice might simply be aresult of Garou culture
and tradition. As such, there's no reason why such trends can't
change with sufficient pressure from the new generations.

Rites of Accord

Rites of Accord are ceremonies of reconciliation and for-
giveness. Through ritualistic cleansing, rebirth and apology,
werewolves use these rites to purify themselves and small parts
of Gaia that have been harmed or damaged.

System: A successful rite of Accord must be performed with
a pure, untainted piece of Gaia's work. This symbol of Gaia, be
it branch, stone or creature, must come from beyond the frontier
where the humans have not yet gone. Generally the ritemaster
makes a Charisma + Rituals roll against a difficulty of 7.

Rite of Cleansing

Level One

The very first European, stepping from his ship, brought the
taint of the Wyrm to the New World, or so the Pure Ones
believe. But the forces of change that come with the Europeans
are nothing compared to the true corruption spread by the
Wyrm. This rite ensures the purity of any person, place or
object afflicted with such a taint. The rite commences with the
ritemaster loping counterclockwise around the afflicted crea-
ture or object. In one hand she holds a flaming branch, in the
other a birch rod dipped in pure spring water. Head down, arms
held high, the Garou anoints the object to be cleansed with

alternating flicks of ember and water. As the speed of her
circling increases, any other Garou participating start a low
howl. This slowly rises in pitch until, at the culmination of the
rite, all present are screaming like banshees, thus frightening
away the corrupting influence. Although it can be performed
at any time, this rite is at its most powerful at sunrise.
System: For each additional person or object to be cleansed,
the ritemaster must spend one Gnosis point. Only one success
is required; the difficulty is equal to the Gnosis of the creature
that caused the taint. The difficulty is reduced by one if the rite
is performed at dawn. This rite only removes an existing taint;
it cannot heal wounds or damage caused by contamination.

Rite of Renunciation

Level Two

Many werewolves who've come to the New World call this
rite by a different name: the Rite of New Hope. The purpose of
the rite is grave: a Garou who performs this ceremony has some
reason to want to give up his old auspice and begin again with
another. Whatever the reason, be it past disgrace or deep
dissatisfaction, the decision is a difficult one to make and the
cost is high. The rite is performed in the presence of the
ritemaster and supplicant alone. The ritemaster strips the
subject ofall former Rank, renown and title, and the supplicant
is symbolically born again under the light of the moon he
wishes to adopt. After such a ceremony, the supplicant is again
treated as a cub and must undertake another Rite of Passage
and find a new place in the sept. The New World has seen a
small influx of Garou who, having performed this rite in
Europe, have made the journey to the Americas where their
past doesn't haunt them and they can truly begin again.

System: A Garou who undertakes this rite loses all Re-
nown and Rank. He must start again at Rank One, and
though he retains all previous Gifts and rites, he can no
longer learn Gifts of his former auspice. Gifts of the new
auspice can now be learned as normal. It's possible that a
werewolf might want to symbolically renounce something
other than auspice — his name, his tribe or even his breed.
This rite can theoretically perform these functions, though in
practice such a request is very rare.

Rite of Reconciliation

Level Three

An unfortunate truth of Garou society is that feuds occurall
too often. If not for rites such as this, werewolves might be even
scarcer than they already are. Two warring Garou who have
decided to end their feuding must confirm their resolve in the
eyes of Gaia. This also applies if an elder or one of higher Rank
intervenes and demands an end to the bickering. The prepara-
tions for reconciliation involve clearing a circle of ground, in
the center of which a pit is dug. Placed next to this hole is a
ribbon of twined hair from the heads of the rivals.
Under the direction of the ritemaster, the two ob-
serve the three stages of Reconciliation.

168 Werewolf: The Wild West


First is Intent: The two Garoustand on either side of the hole
that symbolizes the rift between them. Their arms outstretched,
empty hands palm upward, each in turn loudly declares to the
assembled sept his determination to end the feud: "Before my
brothers and sisters I, (Garou's full name), say my actions have
been base. I say | have wasted my blood-fire and I say I have
offended mother Gaia, my pack, my sept and my tribe. I declare
my intent toend here and now this useless feuding and take again
to my heart my sister (enemy's full name)."

For the second part of the rite, Substantiation, each Garou
must choose a symbol of aggression. This is most commonly a
weapon: a favorite knife ora firearm. Both items are placed in
the hole and buried by the ritemaster.

The third stage is Confirmation, in which the rivals must signal
their acceptance of one another. As the two clasp each other's
forearms, their wrists are tightly bound with their braided hair.
The two now join in asingle howl of appeasement directed at the
heavens. The reconciliation is complete. If a Garou chooses to
break the bond-pact she may be declared a criminal in the eyes of
thesept. This rite isalso used when larger groups are at odds; in this
case, each side chooses a representative to participate. Europeans
tend tomerely break their chosen weapons rather than bury them.
Fetish weapons are never sacrificed in this way.

System: To attract Gaia's attention, the ritemaster must
make a Charisma + Rituals roll, difficulty of 6. Ifsuccessful, Gaia
approves and the rite has "taken." If the ex-rivals sincerely wish
to settle their differences, each must convince the sept with a
Willpower roll, difficulty equal to his Rage. If successful, the
antagonists gain 1 Honor each; otherwise, they must convince
the sept with their actions over the coming months.

Caern Rites

Accaem is the spiritual focus of a sept, the heart of its Garou and
aplace of purity and peace on the surface of Gaia. Caern Rites have
no meaning outside of a caern, and generally only succeed if
performed atits very heart. Their purpose is torenew and refresh the
‘caer, as well as to tap the energy stored there ina constant and fine
balance of giving and receiving between Gaia and Her children.

System: Although the individual rolls vary with each
Caern Rite, the ritemaster's Dice Pool may never exceed her
Gnosis. The difficulty is 7 unless otherwise stated. Many
European werewolves find these rites difficult to perform in the
caerns that have been taken violently from the Pure Ones. To
represent this unfamiliarity, the difficulty for European Garou
in ill-gotten caerns is one higher than normal (usually 8).

Moot Rite
Level One
This rite is called to begin a moot. Although Garou gather
all the time in groups of various sizes, no gathering is officially
a moot, as far as any spirits are concerned, until the Moot
Rite is performed. The rite varies from the extremely

& simple, such as a unified howl to Luna led by the

ritemaster, to the complex and creative, such as weaving
intricate dance patterns and reciting the Litany. The Moot

Rites of European Garou and the Pure Ones vary so greatly that _

moots involving both groups have only been possible if a
special rites were devised for the occasions — a rarity at best.

Individual tribes have many variations on this rite. Some
tribes, such as the Uktena, keep some Moot Rites for
beginning secret moots and use others when members of
other tribes are present. Other tribes, such as the Red
Talons, tend to be minimalist about their Moot Rites,
scratching the ground and howling together more asa signal
of beginning than anything else.

System: The Moot Rite has a role in the lifeblood of the
caern. Once every cycle of Luna, at least half of the caern's
Garou must call a moot; otherwise, the caern begins to dwindle.
During the rite, the participants must refresh the caern with
five Gnosis points per caern level. The Wyrmcomers have
discovered that this cost is higher for them in captured caerns
as they struggle to adjust to the new land; these newcomers
must often expend double the normally required Gnosis.

Rite of the Opened Caern

Level One

A caern is not merely a place of worship and a focus of
spirituality for the Garou. Caerns have practical purposes too,
and a Garou who knows this rite has learned to tap the mystical
power of a caern and use it for her own purposes. The European
Garou refer to this practice as "opening" a caern while the Pure
Ones, who use this rite infrequently, call it "borrowing" from
the caern. The power available from a caern varies with each
particular place. Some caerns provide extra dice in a certain
Ability while others directly refresh a Garou's Gnosis, Rage or
Willpower. Strange and rare caerns may be even more potent.

Caerns do not freely give their assistance to Garou; indeed,
the practice of opening a caern is dangerous to the ritemaster.
The Garou, through performing this rite, tries to show that she
is worthy of the caern's energies, and coaxes them from the
heart of the caern's spirit. The exact form which this rite takes
differs according to the nature of the caern's energies. At a
caern of Leadership the supplicant might demonstrate, through
tales of her deeds, how she is a mighty leader of Garou. At a
caern of Rage the supplicant might prove her burning anger
and inflame the caern's spirit with descriptions of the horror
she wishes to avenge. In every case, the ritemaster must
convince the spirit that her cause is worthy of its help.

System: The rite takes the shape of an extended contest
between the ritemaster and the spirit of the caern, as the Garou
tries to prove her worthiness through whatever method is most
likely to impress the spirit. The player rolls Wits + Rituals
(difficulty 7). The number of successes required is the caern's
level. The caern spirit rolls a number of dice equal to the level
of the caern with a difficulty of the ritemaster's Gnosis, and
needs a number of successes equal to the Garou's Willpower.

Chapter Six: Traits 169


The first contestant to gain the necessary number of suc-
cesses wins. A successful ritemaster can partake of that caern's
power, either adding its rating to an appropriate Dice Pool, or
refreshing herself from the caern's store. If the character is
unsuccessful in supplicating the caern spirit, the angry spirit
wounds her, causing a number of Health Levels of damage
equal to the successes by which the caern beat her. If the
titemaster rolled a botch, these wounds are aggravated.

The Trodden Track
Level Three

A caern's history is of great importance to the Garou. The
caern's past, its roots and its lineage can be a source of comfort and
strength, invigorating the sept in its struggles. The Trodden Track
enables the Garou to observe visible and audible echoes of the
caern's past. The echoes of times gone reverberate in the Umbra,
anda good ritemaster can snare these echoes and tame them. The
Trodden Track requires a map of the caer and its surroundings as
they appeared when it was first created. Placed in the center of the
caern and burnt, the map symbolizes the passing of time that
affects all things. As the map crumbles to ash, the other Garou
present growl deep in their throats, producing a grinding, asyn-
chronous rumble. The ritemaster begins his litany, a recitation of
the caern's life. The off-center growls combined with the sono-
rous drone of the ritemaster's recounting have a disorienting
effect on those present, easing the transition from present to
past. Like a fever dream, the caern's history is replayed hazily
about the participants, looking like reflections in a murky pool.
The nature of the rite ensures that only joyous and important
events from the caern's past are revealed. Watching these events
fills all present with strengthened resolve and renewed vigor, as
well as a deeper connection with their ancestors. Only one who
has known the caern and its history has any chance of success-
fully enacting The Trodden Track.

System: The successful completion of The Trodden Track
requires an Intelligence + Rituals roll against a difficulty of 8;
this difficulty is reduced by one for each Past Life point the
ritemaster has. Any participants in the successful rite gain an
extra point of Past Life until the end of the current moon. The
successful rite also "primes" the caern, reducing the difficulty
of the next Caern Rite by one.

Rite of the Opened Bridge

Level Four

One of the most complex and important rites, the Rite of the
Opened Bridge provides the mechanism by which many of the
new Garou in the New World came to be there. Among the
European Garou, this rite has become more important than ever
and more and more Theurges are being instructed in its complex
details. The Rite of the Opened Bridge creates a Moon Bridge,
aconnection between two caerns, a path along which Garoucan
travel in a hundredth of the time that the real-world journey
takes. The bridge must be renewed once per year in a ritual
enacted simultaneously at both participating caerns.

To create a Moon Bridge the ritemaster must possess a
Pathstone (or Moongem), a flat, paw-sized, very rare white
rock. The retrieval of a Pathstone is often the object of a quest;
the only other way to obtain one is to steal it, which destroys
its previous bridge and inevitably leads to bloody conflict
between a sept and the thief. The bridge exists as a spiritual
connection between the two caer spirits through the foci of
the stones. At the height of the rite the bridge is opened and
Garou can travel freely both ways across it.

No Moon Bridge could be established until the first Euro-
pean Garou reached the Americas and commandeered a caern
there. After several unsuccessful attempts, during which a few
Garou became lost in the Umbra, a caern opened a bridge to
the Black Forest in Germany. Since then, several more bridges
have been opened up and travel is virtually unrestricted.

System: Even though there is a great call for Theurges who
can perform this rite, it's not generally the stuff of younglings
to perform it. The enacting of this ritual is an important
decision for a sept, as any caern may only have one bridge.

The ritemaster rolls Wits + Enigmas against a difficulty of 8
minus the level of the caern at which she enacts the rite. If the
ritemaster's pack totem is the same as that of either caern, she
receives a bonus of three dice on the roll. If either ritemaster
has tried and failed previously, the difficulty is increased by
one. A number of successes equal to the caern's level isrequired
to open the bridge successfully.

If the ritemaster is successful, then the bridge is established,
connecting the two Pathstones. Once the initial rite is performed,
the bridge can be accessed by any werewolf using the Rite of the
Opened Caer or the Ragabash Gift: Open Moon Bridge. The rite
mustbe renewed every year, and ifany of these rituals (including the
first) fails, then there can be no bridge until the rite is attempted
again, 13 moons later. If the rite is botched, both Pathstones are
destroyed and the other sept will most likely demand recompense.
The Theurge who commits this grave offense is most likely ostra-
cized by her own sept to pacify the offended Garou.

Rite of Caern Building

Level Five

The Rite of Caern Building is the most important rite in the
Garou repertoire. Without it the cycles of destruction and
renewal, death and rebirth would fail and the werewolves
themselves, eventually drained of all Gnosis, would fade and
die. Although many caerns are ancient, none are immortal;
every year a few are destroyed by the Wyrm, infighting or by the
capricious nature of time. Some doomsayers argue that this is
an irresistible progression toward the Apocalypse, and that all
caerns will soon perish, but the Rite of Caern Building gives
others the hope to establish something worthy.

The rite creates anew caern, a nexus between the Umbra and
the physical world, through the exertions and devotions of a
powerful ritemaster and many supplicants. The attempt
is dangerous for many reasons. First, creatures of the
Wyrm, both physical and Umbral, are known to be i

Werewolf: The Wild West


able to detect the enacting of the rite and almost always try to
disrupt it. In particular, Black Spiral Dancers often attack in
great numbers and with insane ferocity. Some Theurges figure
that the enacting of this rite causes pain to the Wyrm directly.

The other danger is direct physical damage from the back-
lash of energy should the ritemaster fail. The area meant to be
the heart of the caern is chosen, and many Garou must gather
on an important night of the year (Samhain, the spring
equinox, etc.). At least 13 Garou are required, and more are
usually needed to pool enough energy to perform the cer-
emony. The ritemaster must focus the Gnosis of all the
participants throughout the course of the night, making one
roll per hour for eight hours.

System: The ritemaster's extended roll is Wits + Rituals,
difficulty 8. The ritemaster may never roll more dice than she
has Gnosis. She must accumulate 40 successes over the eight
tolls during the course of the night.

Once the leader obtains 40 successes, all the Garou partici-
pating in the rite must spend Gnosis points to draw a spirit into
the caern. The rite requires 100 Gnosis points in all from the
participants (including the ritemaster). If there isn't 100 Gnosis
available, the assembled Garou begin to sustain aggravated
damage in the form of great welts. Each level of damage counts
as three Gnosis points toward the required total.

Ifthe rite fails (the ritemaster doesn't
achieve 40 successes), all involved
suffer five Health Levels of dam-
age. Those who have been
previously wounded and are
forced below Incapacitated
must roll on the Battle
Scars chart. If the rite
is botched, this
damage is aggra-
vated, probably
killing most
of the par-
ticipants.

Asuccessful rite creates a new caern. If more than 40 but less
than 45 successes are achieved, a Level One caern is created,
and the ritemaster is given the honor of naming it. The
Gauntlet of the area is now 4, and the caern's spirit is capable
of granting powers equivalent to low-level Gifts. Every five
successes above 40 increase the level of the caern by one. Every
10 successes above 40 decreases the area's Gauntlet by one
(minimum 2).

The ritemaster must finally sacrifice a number of permanent
Gnosis equal to the new level of the caern. If this isn't possible,
she's instantly slain but the rite is considered successful. If she
survives, the ritemaster gains 3 Glory, 5 Honor and 7 Wisdom
and is thereafter acclaimed as a great hero and leader.

Rites of Death

A werewolf's life is often tragically short, but tends to burn
very brightly. The Garou have a realistic attitude toward
death: They do mourn for the departed, but also, after a rite of
Death is performed to show the connection of all with Gaia's
cycle, they harden their hearts and continue with life. Death
rites are vital as the vehicle for Garou mourning. Death is
common on the frontier, and sometimes European werewolves
perform this rite for a valued Kinfolk pioneer or settler as well.

System: The ritemaster must succeed ina
Charisma + Rituals roll. The diffi-
culty is 8 minus the Rank of

the departed Garou.


ro~

OXX)

Wake Rite

Level One

Families of European Kinfolk and Garou are close on the
frontier. This rite is held when one of their number dies. The clan
gather round and the ritemaster, always a werewolf and often a
Galliard, leads it in mourning and then in celebration of the life
of the departed one. The Kinfolk then depart and the Garou, who
until that time have remained in Homid form, shift into other
forms and howl their grief at the death of their packmate.

Other tribes, notably the Pure Ones, perform very different
rites, and seldom are any Kinfolk present at all. Their rites are
lonely and mournful, solemn and spiritual. The ritemaster is the
focusof the ceremony, although many contribute, especially those
who knew the warrior well. Whatever exact form the ceremony
takes, the aim is the same: to remember the fallen Garou and her
deeds, and to speed her spirit back to the womb of Gaia.

System: If the ritemaster achieves five successes on the
standard roll, the mourning is so poignant that the departed
Garou's spirit stays on, after a fashion. The werewolf closest in
kin to the deceased receives his fallen comrade's spirit as a Past
Life (bestowing an extra point of Past Life, even if he had no
Past Life previously). Ifno close kin are present, this honor goes
to the ritemaster herself.

Rites of the Frontier

The European Garou have come from the soft greens of the
Old World to a harsh new land. Rites of the Frontier are not a
group that Garou recognize, but loosely represent some ex-
amples of rituals from the homelands which have an important
effect in the New World. These rites are typical of the
werewolves who are intent on changing the frontier to suit
them; they aren't necessarily part of the repertoire of every
European ritemaster.

System: The ritemaster must roll Charisma + Rituals against
a difficulty of 9 to successfully perform these rites as, in some
places, the very land resists them.

Rite of New Territory

Level One

In their colonization of the New World, some Garou from
the Old World have found the need to define their territories
in a way that they can understand. They don't recognize or
acknowledge the ways in which the Pure Ones delineate
territorial boundaries, and don't tend to respect these bound-
aries when they do perceive them. The resulting conflict has
manifested itself in open war between the tribes. Among the
European Garou, however, distribution of new territory isn't
always an easy matter of consent and cooperation. Some areas
of the New World are more desirable than others, and in the
race to claim new territories for tribe, sept and Kinfolk,
conflicts do occur. This rite is designed to ritualize and regulate
these conflicts; it isn't used to determine the ownership of
caerns or other significant sites, but to allow Kinfolk rights to

various frontier outposts and towns. Because of its close con-
nection to Kinfolk, the rite is performed in Homid form and in
full view of the affected kin. Each of the competing parties, be
they packs, tribes or septs, chooses a ritemaster. This ritemaster
need not have any Rituals Ability at all. The two ritemasters
circle each other as the rite begins, surrounded by Kinfolk and
Garou. They then strike each other with human fists, each
giving and receiving punches. The first to fall is the loser, and
his group must lead their Kinfolk elsewhere. Although the
onlookers are responsible for preventing these contests from
getting out of hand, several have become lethal tooth-and-
claw brawls. Despite this, the Rite of New Territory remains a
safer form of dispute resolution than klaive dueling.

System: Each ritemaster rolls a normal Dexterity + Brawl
attack to determine the success of his punch. The Garou who
is struck rolls Stamina + Brawl to resist the punch; initially this
difficulty is also 6. If the receiving Garou fails or botches this,
then he falls to the ground and the contest is over. Otherwise
the contest continues, and the difficulty levels to resist the
blows are raised by one.

Rite of the Homeland

Level Two

This is not so much an old rite brought to a new land but
rather a ritual adapted by Theurges for a new purpose. This rite
brings some of the ritemaster's homeland to the new land in
which he now lives. To successfully enact the rite, the werewolf
must have some natural item —a seed, soil, a stone — from the
area in which he lived before. Also, all the Garou enacting this
rite must belong to the same tribe. These werewolves stand ina
circle, nose to tail, while the ritemaster begins the ceremony.
The ritemaster takes the token and buries it in the center of the
circle of Garou, who then dance and howl about the site for some
hours. The rite has no immediate effect, but slowly, over the
length of a few days, a small patch of landscape the size of the
circle of Garou changes subtly to resemble a patch of the Old
Country. The changes are not blatant, butare enduring. Perhaps
a tree grows and a cactus dies; perhaps the soil softens and tulips
bloom. Whatever the outward effect, it's not without repercus-
sions; the area's Gauntlet increases by one. Some say that certain
Pure One Theurges know magics which can counteract and
dispel this rite, but they have yet to produce any evidence.

System: The limit in area which can be affected is only the size
of the circle formed by touching Garou when the rite is enacted.

= =
Mystic Rites

Mystic Rites are most closely related to the mainstream idea of
shamanistic ritual. They contact and deal with spirits and the

Umbra. These rites are often performed bya lone Theurge, though
it isn't unheard of for the Theurge to have an audience. The Pure

Ones tend not to perform these rites alone.

System: To enact a Mystic Rite, the Garou must
make a Wits + Rituals roll (difficulty 7 unless
indicated otherwise).

Werewolf: The Wild West
Pp


Rite of Binding
Level One

This rite binds a spirit to a werewolf, creating a temporary
spirit servant, or binds it into a talen. Forcing the spirit into an
talen is the only way to make this rite's duration permanent.
There are some European spirits loose in the American Umbra
that made the journey bound to a Theurge but later were
released or escaped.

The rite is only possible because of the ancient bonds
between spirit and Garou, and many Garou see this rite as
taking unfair advantage of this sacred link. Spirits can be bound
into people, places or objects — with different implications for
the spirit. For example, if the place is a logical home for the
spirit, it may not protest being bound there, but all spirits resist
being bound into objects unless such a duty would serve their
patrons (suchas tribal totems). There's a certain amount of risk
associated with this rite. If the ritemaster is unsuccessful in
binding the spirit, he is confronted by an angry foe who attacks
if capable, or escapes to plot revenge.

System: Once the spirithas been encountered, the ritemaster
expends Gnosis to attempt to force a connection with the
spirit. Each point so spent reduces the spirit's effective Gnosis
by one. The ritemaster then attempts to complete the binding
by rolling Willpower against the spirit's new Gnosis rating. A
botch indicates that not only will the spirit attack the Garou,
but is likely to be assisted by one or two of its kind who come
to its aid. The number of successes achieved determines the
duration of the spirit's servitude.

Successes Duration of Servitude
1 One Day
2 One Week
3 Three Weeks
4 Two Months
5 A Season
Rite of Talisman Dedication
Level One

This rite enables a Garou to dedicate an object as a
personal effect so that when the Garou changes physical
form, the object, usually an item of clothing, grows or shifts
with him and isn't destroyed or discarded. Although the
general foci of this ritual are garments, other objects can be
dedicated successfully. Fetishes and talens are automatically
considered to have undergone this rite. The Garou must
solemnly intone the importance of the dedication before
Gaia and then change slowly through all of his forms. After
this, the item can remain with the Garou in some form or
other in any form the Garou wishes to take.

System: The rite costs one Gnosis point per item to be

dedicated (three if the object includes silver). The diffi-
culty of the roll is 5 for clothing, 6 for accessories, 7 for
weapons and technological devices and 8 for items

Chapter Six: Traits

which include silver. A werewolf can never have more objects
bound to him than his permanent Gnosis score. The specific
effects of the rite are left to the player and Storyteller to
determine. Some items may merely stretch to accommodate
the greater bulk of the larger forms, while others are partially
absorbed into the Garou's hide, perhaps becoming brands or
tattoos. In the latter case, the Garou can spend a Willpower
point to retrieve an item while in a form other than the one in
which the rite was enacted.

Rite of Summoning

Level Two

Garou mystics are closely connected to the Realm of spirit.
They are linked flesh and soul to the Umbra and its inhabit-
ants. Proper application of this knowledge combined with
the correct rituals can enable a Garou to actually call forth a
spirit into the physical realm. Although the vast majority of
these creatures are of the lesser order, such as the Umbral
vermin Gafflings, the Penumbra is also home to beings of
unimaginable power, such as the Incarna and the godlike
Celestine Avatars.

The procedure for summoning a spirit varies, not only from
spirit to spirit, but also with the individual mystics. Still, the
inhabitants of the Umbra demand certain constants, as is only
proper. A summoning always has its quotient of complex
rituals, long meditations, chanting and ritualized dancing. The
longer the elaborate web of rituals continues, the more likely
the spirit is to respond.

Tosummon a spirit, one must pierce the Gauntlet, creating
ahole for the call to reach the spirit and enabling the creature
toanswer. Obviously if the mystic performs the rite while in the
Umbra, there isno need tocreate this gateway; the spirit simply
follows the mystic when he returns to the mundane world.

Some wonder why entities of such potential might and
intellect obey their summoners (when they do), but Theurges
don't find this one whit peculiar. They know full well that
spirits have a place in Gaia's plan, and that spirits are more than
willing to play their parts. More intelligent spirits often re-
spond out of curiosity or bemused indulgence; lesser spirits
don'tseem to have much ofa choice. Itall comes from the Pact,
the understanding between Garou and spirits that was settled
at the dawn of time. Both spirit and werewolf are forged of Gaia.
Both are Her tools; their goals are Her goals. Unfortunately in
these days of strife in the West, the chaos in the Umbra has
caused summoned creatures to respond in unexpected ways.

The success or failure of a summoning is affected by the
mystic's skill, the spirit's power and the strength of the
gauntlet in the area.

System: First, the barrier between matter and spirit must
be pierced with a Gnosis roll against the local level of the
Gauntlet. The difficulty of the summoning roll itself is
further affected by the relative power of the spirit called, as
per the following table.


BANK | Ee ihe

Wg

Spirit type

Gaffling

Jaggling 5
Totem Avatar i
Incarna 8-9
Celestine Avatar 10

For each extra hour the mystic spends calling the being, the
difficulty drops by one; it can never fall below 3. Successful
summoning of an Incarna or Celestine Avatar gains the mystic
2 Wisdom. The effects of the ritemaster's Gnosis roll are

described below.

Successes Effect

1 The spirit takes up to an hour to
arrive and is initially hostile.

2 The spirit arrivesin under 10 minutes
but is initially unfriendly.

3 The spirit arrives immediately and
is wary and reserved.

4 The spirit arrives immediately and
is passively benign.

5 The spirit arrives immediately and

is actively helpful.

The specific results of a botch should be determined by the
Storyteller, buta flagrantly failed attempt mightsummon the wrong
(and often hostile) spirit, attract the attention of Black Spiral
Dancers or temporarily mark the ritemaster as a nuisance to spirits.

Obviously, using the above tables, a werewolf sometimes
has no chance of success. At others it's all but impossible to fail.
The Storyteller should feel free to adjust the numbers, treating
each performance of this rite individually.

Rite of the Fetish
Level Three

This powerful rite is the province of Theurges and Philodox.
With it they create fetishes — items with powers granted by
spirits bound within. Fetishes vary greatly in power and poten-
tial as do the specifics of this rite among the tribes. One version
of the rite uses coercion and brute force to instill the spirit in
the fetish. An Uktena ritemaster may prepare an item for
inhabitation by notching it with patterns specific to the task it
is to perform and then anchoring it with twine to a great
bulkhead of Gaia in the physical world — a huge tree, an
outcropping of rock — for at least three days. After this time
the ritemaster calls the spirit and forces it into the fetish-to-be.
The Silent Striders favor a more cooperative approach; many
of their fetishes have a lifespan resulting from bargains made
between spirit and ritemasters. Once this time has elapsed, a
spirit is freed and may negotiate with the fetish's currentowner.

System: The system really depends on the ritemaster's
preferences. It should be noted that many Theurges who have
come to the New World used to good relationships with spirits
have found those here to be unwilling to enter into pacts and

SSSSEN

ZLLA,

XX

have been compelled to resort to force when creating fetishes.
The Storm Umbra has also distorted spirits normally friendly
to the native Garou, making them harder to deal with.

The roll for a cooperative ritemaster is Wits + Rituals against a
difficulty of 10. Each permanent Gnosis pointsacrificed to the spirit
reduces the difficulty by two. The forceful ritemaster mustdefeat the
spirit in spirit combat and reduce it to zero Power before attempting
the same roll, although in that case the difficulty is then the spirit's
Gnosis. Thespirit then needs torecharge before the fetish can begin
to function. The specific powers of a fetish depend on the spirit
inhabiting it, and are generally in keeping with the importance and
associations of that particular spirit.

Rite of the Totem

Level Three

Mostcommonly performed at the formation of anew pack, this
ite binds a group of Garou to its pack totem. Any werewolves who
want to hitch their fate to a totem must participate in this rite.
Each Garou coats his eyes with a mix of spit and mugwort, the
better to focus his attention from the mundane plane to the
spiritual. All Garou participating then step sideways into the
Umbra, where the ritemaster leads the pack ona ritual huntforthe
spectral scent left by the totem spirit. This spoor will vary with
eachspirit, and, of course, is only perceptible to those worthy of the
totem. The hunt is a mutual pursuit, with each werewolf calling
herpersonal cry and the spirit answering, its criesslowly becoming
more clear as the hunt progresses. Eventually the Umbral hunt
ends with the pack and the spirit confronting each other. How-
ever, theconclusion of the hunt doesn't guarantee acceptance; the
totem must still consider the worthiness of the supplicants. A wary
spirit may even require a quest from the Garou, but this rarely
happens if the pack has successfully completed a Rite of Passage.

System: Although all Garou who have purchased the
Totem Background (individually or more usually as a pack)
have by default succeeded in this rite, it can be evocative and
rewarding to play through, giving the characters and players a
deeper understanding of the totem that watches over them.
Each Garou participating rolls Stamina + Enigmas. A failure
indicates that this hunt fora totem is unsuccessful and the pack
must try again after the passing of a moon. A botch indicates
that the spirit sought will never agree to be their totem. If a
particular Garou achieves five or more successes, her rating in
the Totem Background is increased by one, and she may decide
which aspect of the totem this new level enhances.

Punishment Rites

The society of the Garou is surprisingly regimented and
regulated. When a serious transgression of its rules occurs the
offender is sanctioned by a Punishment Rite. The severity of
the particular rite chosen varies with the crime committed.
Punishment Rites are as important for those who perform
them as for those who are their targets. The accusers
strengthen their commitment to each other by
defining what is not Gaia's will.

174 Werewolf: The Wild West


System: The ritemaster, often a Philodox, must make a Cha-
tisma + Rituals roll (difficulty 7 unless stated otherwise). The
consequences of failing a Punishment Rite vary. Sometimes this is
seen asa sign of Gaia's forgiveness of the punished individual, and
the matteris dropped. But ifthe transgression issufficiently serious,
a failed rite is no different in the eyes of the sept than a successful
tite; however, it carries no spiritual conviction.

Rite of Ostracism

Level Two

If a Garou commits an act that offends her sept, but is not
sufficiently heinous to warrant death, the Rite of Ostracism is
enacted. The wrongdoer is estranged from her sept, tribe and
sometimes even her pack. Once this rite has been performed the
offender is ignored, treatedasanonentity and left to fend forherself
in every way. Sept members are discouraged from direct hostilities
against the offender, but accidents have been known to happen.
Tribe members who are related or close to the ostracized one
sometimes choose to aid her in a life-or-death situation.

The Garou performing the rite forma circle about the offender
(ifshe is present). One by one the participants step forward; while
pointing stiffly at the offender each werewolf intones: "Hear me,
Gaia, I know this Garou called (the offender's name) no longer, nor
will [help her." Theaccuser then raises both paws over hereyesand
slowly turns her back and walks away from the offender, symbol-
izing her "nonexistence" to the pack. The ritemaster is the last to
let her howl fall silent. She leaves without the ceremonial rejec-
tion, for by this stage the accused is considered without the wolf.
The transgressor is left alone in the night.

System: This ritual is commonly performed at night. The
exclusion lasts from one phase of the moon to the next. If the
crime is particularly serious, the exile may be declared perma-
nent. Any Garou so ostracized loses one point each of Glory
and Wisdom, and five of Honor.

Sanction of Dishonor

Level Two

If a pup has, through acts of dishonor, offended an elder or
the sept at large, the elder may prescribe this rite. This rite is
basically looked on as a chastisement and a way for those
involved to vent their frustrations so that all may go about their
business. It's also a way to show Gaia the sept's disapproval, thus
ensuring that Her anger isn't stirred.

The elder who calls for the sanction isalso the ritemaster. He
escorts the subject to the place of punishment in silence,
offering no hint of what is to come. Even if the pup guesses his
fate, he is unlikely to attempt escape. It's his duty to stoically
receive his just punishment, thus avoiding shame for himself
and his sept. There the other Garou involved in the insult stand
shoulder to shoulder, in two rows on either side ofa path strewn
with sharpened stones. The accused whelp stands at one end of

this gauntlet, the ritemaster at the other. All present,
with the obvious exception of the offender, carry
& stout branches, sticks or rocks. The rite commences

Chapter Six: Traits

in earnest with the declaration of grievances; the elder states
his full name, the offense (often in shameful detail) and the full
name of the accused. When he has finished, the punishment
begins. The violent heartfelt howl loosed by all is the signal for
the youngster tostart his run. Feet slashed by stony shards, body
bruised by clubs and hurled rocks, the guilty one must reach the
end of the gauntlet. At the far end, between agony and
freedom, stands the elder, weapon of choice brandished. If the
cub has comported himself well, the elder may refrain from
taking a shot himself. The European version of this rite is
somewhat less ritualized and consists mainly of vicious pum-
meling; the stony shards are replaced with broken glass.

System: The ritemaster must make a Charisma + Rituals
roll against a difficulty of his own Rage to stop the ceremony
from turning into a bloodbath and bringing more shame down
upon the heads of the sept.

Rites of the Pure Ones

The following rites are examples of some of the ceremonies
that are peculiar to the Uktena and Wendigo. These rites are
common to some Pure One septs, and many others practice
variations. Often more elaborate and intricate than rites from
the Old World, they are examples of the lengths which
werewolves must go to convince and cajole the capricious
spirits of the West.

System: The ritemaster's role is always that of leader of a
pack (or more) of Garou in these rites. She must convince the
spirits involved with words, actions and meticulous prepara-
tion. The roll is Manipulation + Rituals against a difficulty of
7. Although these rituals are highly complex, the results of
failure are generally not catastrophic.

Seam Between Worlds

Level One

This rite was devised by the Pure Ones as soon as they
realized what colonization would mean to them, Gaia and the
Umbra. It is used to inspire fighting spirit, to warn pups and in
attempts to convince the Europeans of the wrongness of their
actions. The rite must be enacted within sight of the actual
boundaries between "settled" lands and native country. A
branch is taken from the oldest tree in the bawn of the caern
and wrapped in copper wire. The ritemaster uses this talisman
to draw a line in the dirt paralleling the frontier. All present
step into the Umbra, where they behold a vision of the frontier
itself, palpable and terrifying. A roiling line, glowing with
freakish energies, it eats away at the yet-untouched spirit
realm, leaving behind a blasted, web-filled parody of the land
that once was. Most horrifying of all, the seam's movement is
visible, slow to be sure but relentless and implacable. Any
Garou who allows the creeping wall to wash over her will
notice a unwholesome change in herself, as if a healthy shine
was replaced with an oily film. Some Old World Garou see the
changes wrought as a fine sheen of progress and coherence.


System: The ritemaster must make a Manipulation +
Rituals roll against a difficulty of 7, and can't use more dice
than she currently has in Gnosis. All other Garou must
successfully step sideways. The effects on non-Pure Ones are
intangible and depend on the individual; Pure Ones have
their Rage replenished and their commitment reaffirmed.

Facing the Final Journey
Level Two

Death comes to the Garou often, frequently painfully and all too
suddenly. Yet a Garou might have an inkling of his coming demise.
When a Garou knows he may be about to die, this rite is indispens-
able. It reconciles the doomed Garou to his fate, allowing him to
calm his dread, collect himself and marshal his resolve. The subject
and ritemaster together must construct a small hut from baked clay
in a secluded locale. Both now enter, and a volunteer seals the
entrance with more clay. At the center of the interior is a deep
firepit; the supplicant bestrides the pit and the fire is lit. Howling a
long, low keen of mourning, the ritemaster presents him with a
specially prepared draught. This potent mix of spirit blood, rattle-
snake venom and peyote soon puts the Garou into a trance. He
spends the rest of the long hours of the ritual straddling the flames
andslipping in and out of the Umbraas his trance waxes and wanes.
The closed environment in the hut is especially important to this
ritual. The concentrated, smoky air evokes Gaia's breath, the fire
personifies Rage and the spirit of Gaia is given form by the potion.

The ritemaster slowly recites a prepared speech, re-
counting the story of the Garou's life, reinforcing his sense
of pride and worthiness as a servant of Gaia. The rite
doesn't touch on the doomed one's future, but the knowl-
edge that one is facing probable death, having lived a
worthy life is certainly empowering. At the rite's comple-
tion, the subject breaks out of the hut, shattering the clay
in a symbolic rebirth.

System: The ritemaster must make a Manipulation +
Rituals roll, difficulty 7. For each success, the recipient
receives a temporary point in each of Willpower, Rage and
Gnosis. A botch indicates the ritemaster's narration touches
on painful or unfortunate memories, driving the Garou
into depression; the subject loses a temporary Willpower
point for each botch.

Rites of Renown

Enacted to acknowledge accomplishment or achievement,
rites of Renown bestow honorand praise upon Garou who have
earned them. Sometimes these rites involve tests, makingarite
both trial and reward. On the frontier, not all recognize the
status conferred by such a rite.

System: The ritemaster, having judged the recipient
worthy to undergo the trial or receive the honor, must
make a Charisma + Rituals roll (generally against a
difficulty of 6).

178 Werewolf: The Wild West


Rite of Accomplishment

Level Two

When a werewolf has distinguished herself by her words and
deeds, herseptenacts thisrite to honor heraccomplishmentsand
call upon the spirits to acknowledge her Renown. An elder calls
the subject forward, just as he might summon her to face
punishment or censure. As she advances, the elder recounts a list
of her recent deeds, and tells the sept that the Garou has rightly
earned her standing. With that, the ritemaster enacts the Rite of
Accomplishment, allowing any others who desire to step forward
and speak on the recipient's behalf to do so. In conclusion, the
elder says something along the lines of, "She is made greater in
her tribe, her sept, and among all the People. Let this be known."

System: This rite converts 10 points of temporary Renown in
a category into one point of permanent Renown. The difficulty
isonly 4; 6 ifanother werewolf disputes the rite (see below). Only
‘one success is necessary, but the spirits consider failure a sign of
the subject's unworthiness, and grant signs of the applicant's
failing to the ritemaster. If the roll botches, the sept realizes that
something must be truly wrong, and the applicant must undergo
a penance before anyone will perform this rite for her again.

Sometimes, though, another Garou doubts the applicant's
worthiness, and disputes the rite. This practice is particularly
common among Shadow Lords, who want to ensure that only the
truly worthy are granted such an honor. All a werewolf need do to
dispute the rite is to heckle the ritemaster and applicant, brazenly
pointing out the negative qualities of the applicant. The insulted
applicant must make a Rage roll; if she frenzies, the rite ends. But
ifshe keepsacool head, and the rite succeeds anyway, then nobody
can rightfully question her worthiness for at least three moons; her

septmates are forbidden to dispute any Rites of Accomplishment
enacted on her behalf for the next month and a half.

Rite of Passage
Level Two
The first rite any werewolf ever participates in is his own Rite
of Passage. Although the rite is easy to perform, ritemasters are
chosen carefully for this rite. Garou understand the critical nature
of the ceremony, and the impression it makes on cubs. During the
rite, which may last for several days, the young werewolf undergoes
trials which are designed to prove his worth to the pack, to the sept
and to the tribe. These trials, often undertaken by a pack of young
Garou, are decreed by the ritemaster at the opening of the rite. A
wise ritemaster chooses trials that are both challenging and
attainable, stressing upon the young cubs the difficulties facing
their kind, but not disheartening them with impossible tasks.
The tests themselves vary from tribe to tribe and ritemaster to
ritemaster. Some stress individual achievement, notably those of
the Get of Fenris and the Stargazers. Other tribes prescribe goals
which can only be achieved through pack coherence and coopera-
tion. On the frontier, as the war reddens and scars deepen, some
ritemasters subscribe to the ugly practice of sending their
cubs across the frontier (from either side) with various
missions of mayhem and destruction. Needless to say,
this practice is discouraged by responsible sept leaders.

Ifand when the cubs are successful, the ritemaster completes
the rite, often inscribing them with a ritualistic tattoo or scar to
indicate their full membership in the sept and tribe (although
they remain cubs in the eyes of their elders). If the cubs fail they
are generally offered asecond chance. Few tribes allow their cubs
a third such rite, and those who fail twice are lucky to survive.

Brand of Honor

Level Three

The deeds of Garou on the frontier are as bold and powerful
as the landscape itself. The septs who live there exist ina rough
environment, with little subtlety or finesse. This rite has been
developed by these frontier Garou. Although the practice was
begun by the Get of Fenris, it has caught on among Garou on
both sides of the frontier. The rite is performed to commemo-
rate a great and invaluable deed by a single Garou or, less often,
an entire pack in the service of a caern.

Each caem that practices this rite has its own brand, a variant of
its pictogram forged in iron. The hero is summoned into the caern's
bawn, where the brand is heated in a ceremonial fire. At the closing
of the rite, the caern's brand is burned into the Garou's shoulder,
marking him forever as a champion of the sept. Some werewolves
have taken to wandering the frontier looking for opportunities to
perform deeds which are worthy of these brands, and collecting them.

System: The ritemaster need make no roll to successfully enact
this rite. The Garou so rewarded sustains an aggravated wound and
must makea roll to avoid frenzy. If the roll is successful, the werewolf
gains 2 points of temporary Glory. Any subsequent brands achieved
from different caerns by the same Garou are worth an extra point of
temporary Glory (i.e., the second brand is worth 3).

Seasonal Rites

Performed at special times in the annual cycle of Gaia, these
rites are highly variable between septs, packs, tribes and places.
Each has its own local seasonal influences and requirements, and
the rites have been adjusted over time to accommodate these.
These rites have a critical social and spiritual function in the
affirmation of a Garou's connection to Gaia and Her seasons.

System: Each rite can only be successfully performed at the
proper time of year. Although the specifics vary, the required
roll is Stamina + Rituals. The difficulty is 8 - the level of the
caern where the rite is held.

Rite of the Equinox

Level Two

This is not so much a specific rite as a generalized description
of the celebrations that Garou hold at the turning points of the
year. Among the tribes and septs, different emphasis is placed on
different celebrations. The ritemaster is generally the caern
leader, and all Garou participate in the rite. These celebrations
signify the renewal of the Garou's duty to and faith in Gaia.

The night of the winter solstice, the longest night of the
year, is a solemn time for the Garou. The emphasis of the
festival is on vigilance and duty, and its passing is denoted by
anight-long vigil over the caern and its environs.

Chapter Six: Traits 177


ZOLE:

ox

The spring equinox is a festival of rebirth among many
humans as well as the Garou. Kinfolk mix with Garou on these
nights, and many cubs are conceived on the equinox. Inevita-
bly, the festivities stretch long into the night.

Midsummer is a serious occasion which is connected to the
Garou's strength and power. The rite involves many tests of
ability, and contests between members of the sept. Laced in
recent times with political intrigue, this rite is recognized as an
appropriate time to challenge leaders and elders, and has been
known to descend into violence.

The autumnal equinox is a sad affair as the werewolves face
the realities of the coming Apocalypse and their waning power,
just as the year dies and looks toward winter. The ritemaster
tells tales of old as Garou solemnly listen. Many bold and overly
brave oaths are sworn during this rite as the sept members try
to conquer the fear which threatens to overwhelm them.

Minor Rites

Spiritual creatures such as the Garou are constantly affirming
their connection to the world at large, its cycles and currents.
Almost every werewolf knows a few minor rites, small prayers to
Gaia and other superstitions. Generally these rites involve only
‘one werewolf, who performs the ceremony in private.

System: These rites can be purchased at one half the normal
Background cost: One point of Rites Background grants two
learned Minor Rites. They take only a fraction of the time to
learn and enact than other rites do, and quickly become part of
a werewolf' daily routine.

‘ Tear for the Prey

Although the spirit of the hunter is strong in the Garou,
most try not to blindly kill, orslay for no reason. This rite allows
a werewolf to make peace with the spirit of a being he has just
slain, either in battle or in the hunt. The Garou must take time
to kneel over the corpse and close his eyes, silently thanking
the spirit as it departs the body. This rite is never performed
over slain Wyrm-things.

System: The player must make a Charisma + Expression
roll to be sufficiently eloquent to console the fleeing spirit.
The difficulty is 6 if the Garou needed to kill to survive; 8
otherwise. If he is successful, the spirit is mollified and
understands that the werewolf needed to take its life. If the
Garou fails, or does not bother to perform this rite over his
kills, Naturae begin to mistrust the Garou and all Primal-
Urge rolls in the wilderness become one higher in difficulty
as the Garou is ostracized by the natural world.

Hunt Blessing

Before any hunt, the werewolf takes a moment to solicit
Gaia's grace and to praise Her and all Her works, principally
Her creatures. The Garou must choose a talisman to hold her
prayers; it may bea trophy of combat or an item of clothing, but
it must be carried on the hunt. This rite has almost as many
different forms as there are individual Garou, but it's always
performed immediately before the hunt, and in private.

System: Once this rite has been performed before each hunt
over three full cycles of the moon, the werewolf receives an
additional die in all tracking rolls. This boon lasts as long as the
Garou continues her prayers. If she hunts without praying, her
run is broken and she must commence again.

¥ Hail the Sun

The werewolf sings out a joyous welcome at dawn, following
thesun's long journey through the darkness beneath the world.
The werewolf howls an elaborate ululating greeting while
squatting atop the nearest high ground. The tones, rhythms
and duration vary with the season.

System: The adherent must greet the returned sun for nine
consecutive days. At high noon on the ninth day the Garou is
granted increased sensitivity to Wyrmspawn or Wyrm-taint; he
receives an additional die when attempting to sense any evi-
dence of the Wyrm. This ability is available as long as the sun is
in the sky. The benefit continues as long as the sun receives his
praises. If the character misses a day, the rite must begin anew.

v Welcome Luna

This greeting must be performed at Luna's zenith. When she
reaches her peak, the Garou must howl as loudly as possible to
attract her attention. The moon's phase determines the length
of the howl; the fuller she is, the longer the howl.

System: By performing this rite each night for one complete
phase, the Garou gains one die in all social dealings with
werewolves of that auspice. This blessing takes effect the next
time the moon is in the phase in question.

‘¥ Prayers for Rain

This rite can be enacted by a single werewolf or a pack.
Dancing in circles and pleading to Gaia, the Garou tries to
bring rain. Her pleas take whatever shape she thinks might
convince Gaia, from the ritualistic poetry of the Silver Fangs
tothe conversational supplications of the Bone Gnawers. Each
Garou tends to have a personal theory about what works best.

System: Although there is no roll as such, and also no hard
evidence that this rite works, it does seem to rain more often in
the days following its invocation. Repeated rites over several
days seem to have an increased likelihood of bringing rain,
though in some of the more severe desert areas, no amount of
pleading is enough.

‘ Chant of the Run

Thisrite isused in the New World before any longjourney. The
werewolf stamps and shuffles in one spot while turning a slow
circle, face toward the horizon. All the while, she chants to Gaia,
entreating Her for guidance on the coming trek. Upon completing
a full circle, the Garou steps through the Gauntlet. Glowing
footprints across the Penumbra indicate the best path to take.

System: If the rite is performed at the start of each day, the
knowledge of the best place to set one's paws makes any difficult
journey considerably easier. The werewolf gainsan additional die in

any travel-related task. This might be Stamina + Survival

to maintain a steady pace through difficult territory, or
Intelligence + Occult to navigate by the stars.

Werewolf: The Wild West


Renown

A Garou's Renown is measured in three distinctive areas: Glory
(physical prowess, boldness in the face of danger and victories in
combat), Honor (a Garou's sense of duty, ethics and morals) and
Wisdom (erudition, patience, cunning, insightandspiritual balance).

Renown isn't meant to work like experience points; it's a
roleplaying system. It represents cultural esteem, and measures
how wella character lives up to his peers' and elders' expectations.
A werewolfs Renown is therefore inextricably tied to his auspice,
since his moon sign defines his expected role in society. While a
character can gain experience from actions that contradict his
auspice role, he will probably not gain much Renown by doing so.
Werewolves respect Renown, and are expected to honor indi-
viduals who have proved themselves particularly courageous,
honorable or wise. Sarcastic backtalk to one's betters usually earns
one frequent losses of Honor, and often Wisdom as well.

Renown comes in two forms: the Renown rating (shown in
dots), which measures the character's permanent Renown, and
the Renown pool (shown in squares), which shows how many
temporary points have been gained. Each character has a sepa-
rate rating and pool to represent Glory, Honor, and Wisdom.
Unlike the Rage, Gnosis or Willpower pools, a Renown pool can
exceed Renown rating. Permanent Renown is a mostly static
thing, fluctuating only in dramatic circumstances. Temporary
Renown is much more fluid and fluctuates a lot.

Gaining Renown eventually allows a Garou torise in Rank. For
more information on this process, refer to Chapter Eight: Systems.

Beginning Renown

Garou characters begin with three dots of permanent Renown,
distributed according to auspice (see the Rank Chart on pg. 229).
This Renown has been gained over the course of the werewolf Rite
of Passage; if the Storyteller plans to roleplay this rite, players should
not assign this Renown until the rite is completed. Once this
Renown is assigned, the characters are considered to be Rank One.

Glory

Gaining distinction among one's people by winning brutal
battlesand accomplishing great things is the path of glory. Garou
with high Glory ratings possess larger-than-life reputations, and
carry themselves in a heroic manner, earning reverence from
other werewolves. Glory is the respect an individual is given for
martial prowess and brute ability to get things done. Werewolves.
gain Glory only by accomplishing great deeds.

Glory Creed
(as stated by Cyrus Canyon, Fianna Ahroun)
I'll be brave.
I'll do what I say I'll do.
Pil help other folks out when I'can:

T'lllook out for them that's not strong enough to do it for
themselves.

I'll stomp the crap outta the Wyrm.

Honor

Honor is integral to the Garou's adherence to the laws of
their own society. It is a combination of pride, dignity and
personal integrity. Gaining Honor among the Garou is a
recognition of one's dutifulness, honesty and steadfastness. As
such, it isa sign of deep respect and a source of immense pride.

ApersonofHonorholdstothe highest moral standardsandethical
principles, standing firm in the face ofadversity, danger or temptation.
The most honorable actions are those taken despite all detriments,
and even in the face of absolute defeat. Honor denotes a refined
sense of, and a strict conformity to, a rigid code of conduct — the
Litany. Deceit and fraud aren't viewed kindly in those seeking
Honor. What's more, Honor is also imperiled by frenzy, as a berserk
werewolfis likely todoall sorts of things that might prove dishonorable.

Code of Honor

(as stated by Cyrus Canyon, Fianna Ahroun)

I'll show respect to most.

T'll be loyal to the folks I care about.

I'll be fair-minded.

I'll keep my word.

If someone thinks they can prove something to me, I'll give them
that chance.

Wisdom

Although everyone gives a werewolf with Glory room, and
Honor is a fair measure of a person's character, the Garou with
high Wisdom is given an uncanny amount of deference. Some-
timesthe path of Wisdom conflicts with the demands of Glory, but
the greatest heroes, even among the Get and Wendigo, have
always been able to balance both. A person with high Wisdom is
thought to possess keen judgment and strong discretion, as well as
a certain eloquence and an ability to see truth and falsehood.

Wisdom is gained only by proving one's judgment and
knowledge in the midst of crisis.

Creed of Wisdom
(as stated to Cyrus Canyon, Fianna Ahroun, by Chattering-
Bird-Who-Foretells-Snow, Cherokee-Uktena Theurge)
Always keep a peaceful heart.
Seek the proper wisdom of each moment.
Donotabuse the many gifts of the world; keep respect anda grateful heart.
Mercy is a greatness of spirit; never forget this.
Think before you act and watch before you think.

Renown Rolls (Optional)

Renown rolls can be used to simulate Garou society's reac-
tions to a character. Such rolls are particularly useful during
social situations. The Storyteller can have a player make a roll
based on a Renown rating, or roll a character's rating to surrep-
titiously check how far the werewolf's fame has spread. Some
examples are described below by category of Renown.

178

Chapter Six: Traits

A

(=~ "ale

OX

Glory Rolls

A Glory roll may be used to determine whether or not a
character (and/or his pack) is allowed to accompany a posse or
to requisition combat equipment. It is also a measure of how
"famous" the Garou is, for those with high Glory are those
discussed in tales around the fire. A Glory roll may also
influence Garou in leadership positions and may affect situa-
tions when a character is leading other Garou. Characters with
low Glory aren't well-known, and those with no Glory atall are
often dismissed as spineless whelps.

Honor Rolls

An Honor roll may be used to determine whether a character
is considered trustworthy and honest, as well as whether others
believe he adheres closely to the Litany or not. Honor is of great
importance when dealing with elders, for itis Honor, not Glory,
that impresses these old ones. Honor rolls may also determine
how equitably the character is treated in certain circumstances,
and the Storyteller may even allow an Honor roll fora character
who might need a "sense of honor" about something. Those with
low Honor scores are viewed with suspicion; those with no
Honor at all aren't trusted any farther than arm's reach.

Wisdom Rolls

A Wisdom roll may be used to determine whether Garou
society considers a character wise or spiritually attuned. It's of
utmost importance when a character seeks to gain secrets about
the Garou. It can also be used to see whether a werewolf is trusted
with a sacred fetish or allowed to attend a special moot. Those
with low Wisdom scores often have their questions met with
stony silence; those with no Wisdom atall are viewed as buffoons
who might be dangerous to themselves or to others.

Rank

Where Renown accords what a werewolf is notable for,
Rank is the very definition of who's a doer and who isn't. The
tribes' greatest secrets are taught only to those Garou who have
truly proven themselves worthy, and the best way to do this is
by gaining Rank. Nothing earns respect more than a high
Rank; such werewolves have proven time and again that they
are the most competent and steadfast servants of Gaia.

All characters begin at Rank One, and may increase Rank
thereafter by earning Renown. Gaining Rank is fully discussed
in Chapter Eight: Systems.

Benefits of Rank

‘w Gifts: When a Garou attains a new Rank, he gains the
opportunity to learn more powerful Gifts. To learn a Gift, a
character must not only possess the required experience, he
must have a Rank equal to or higher than the level of that Gift.
The spirits' trust isn't earned by life experience, but by deeds.
The greatest secrets are reserved for the greatest and most
trustworthy Garou.

LO Ee

Werewolf: The

Bh

‘w Respect: Werewolves of low Rank are expected to defer
to those of high Rank, particularly those within one's tribe.
Werewolves of notable Rank (3+) are actually known through-
out Garou society.

w Challenges: Rank is also a factor in who may honorably
challenge whom. A Garou may only challenge another Garou
if there is not more than one Rank's difference between them.
Any deviation from this will cause the challenger to lose
temporary Honor (and possibly Wisdom, if he's picking a fight
way out of his league).

‘w Responsibilities: Garou of high Rank are expected to
lead, protect, and often mentor low-Ranked Garou; guard the
caern from invaders; and undertake great quests to further the
sept and tribe.

‘w Self-Control: Rank, as the sum of a Garou's Renown,
also indicates the "virtue" of that Garou. Werewolves of higher
Rank have, by their deeds and experiences, tempered them-
selves to the point that they are less likely to frenzy. The chart
below gives the frenzy bonus a Garou gains with each Rank.

Rank Frenzy Bonus

3: +1 to frenzy difficulties

4 +2 to frenzy difficulties

5 +2 to frenzy difficulties; 5+ successes needed to frenzy
6 +2 tofrenzy difficulties; 6+ successes needed to frenzy

Werewolves are, at heart, creatures of instinctand hot blood.
They have a connection to a primal side of life that humans just
can't understand. They're constantly at war, and their emotions
fit their role. Garou have no time to get complacent or lackadai-
sical — they literally live on the edge of the abyss. Alas, like
many good things, this has a less-appealing side as well.

Rage is a measure of a character's passion, as well as his
capacity for all-consuming fury. It's the violence and ferocity
and lust that churn directly from the dark wells of primeval
instinct, the unreasoned terror of the unknown, and the hot-
blooded hatred of that which destroys and despoils all that the
Garou loves. It's every dark and bloody impulse, every savage
thought, every headlong dive into madness possible, all re-
strained solely by the werewolf's strength of will.

Rage is important to the Garou; when channeled, it allows
them to perform extraordinary feats, but when it seizes hold of the
mind, itcan move them to deeds they later regret. Rage is also the
force that allows them to shapeshift between human and wolf.

Whatever its origins and nature, Rage dominates a Garou's
attention and energy. Much of a werewolf's existence is
spent trying to direct her Rage to good purposes, while

trying desperately to harness the dark impulses it brings. *

Wild West


LORI L

Rage comes in two forms: the Rage rating (shown indots), which
indicates your character's permanent Rage, and the Rage Pool
(shown in squares), which indicates how many Rage points your
character has left to spend. The Rage Pool can never be greater than
the Rage rating. When you usea point of Rage, youremove a square
from your character's Rage Pool, nota dot from his Rage rating. The
rating remains constant, while the Pool is depleted during the story.

Using Rage

Rage points may only be expended during the initiative
stage of a turn (see Chapter Nine), and only during times of
stress (for examples of such times, see Stress, pg. 260). Rage has
a variety of effects and uses in Werewolf:

tw Extra Actions: A Rage point may be spent to take an
extra action. A Garou may not use more Rage points ina single
turn than her Dexterity score. See Multiple Actions, pg. 234.

‘@ Changing Forms: A Rage point may be spent to assume
any desired form instantly, without requiring a Stamina +
Primal-Urge roll.

te Recovering From Stun: When a Garou loses more
Health Levels in one turn than his Stamina rating, he is
stunned and can't act for another turn. However, the
character may spend a Rage point to ignore this effect.

tw Remaining Active: When a Garou falls below
the Incapacitated Health Level, he may elect to make
a Rage roll in an attempt to remain active. The
difficulty of this roll is 8. Each success instantly heals
one Health Level — even if the wounds are aggra-
vated! However, a Garou may attempt this roll only
once, and if it fails he does not recover. Also, as with
any Rage roll, the risk of frenzy is ever-present.

There's an extra hazard for drawing on one's
anger to remain active. If the roll succeeds, the
Garou must roll an extra die on his Battle Scars
roll, adding the results of the two rolls (see Battle
Scars Chart, pg. 221).

‘w Frenzy: Frenzy is the primary danger of Rage. It's
the violent bloodlust that surges over Garou minds in
times of great passion, hunger or conflict. Whenever a
character makes a Rage roll and scores four or more
successes, he entersa frenzy. See Frenzy, pg. 221, formore
details about the causes and resolutions of frenzies.

‘w The Eyes of the Wolf: For every point of
Rage above his Willpower rating, a char-
acterlosesonedie onallrollsinvolving
social interaction with non-Garou.
The wolf-spirit is simply too
near the surface, and the
werewolf's presence
discomfits and even

frightens
those

around him.
,


4 Losing the Wolf: Ifatany time a Garouhasneither Rage nor
Willpower points, he has "lost the wolf' and can no longer gain
Rage. He cannot transform into any form but that of his breed until
he once again possesses Rage. He must first regain at least one
Willpower point before he may regain Rage. See Willpower, below.

Gaining and Regaining Rage

Rage Pools will fluctuate constantly during a Werewolf
game, as characters use Rage to gain extra actions, change forms,
etc, Rage points are regained in one of the following ways:

‘w The Moon: When the character first sees the moon at
night, something stirs deep in his soul. The character gains one
Rage point underanew moon, two points undera waning moon,
three points under a half or waxing moon, and four points if the
moon is full. If the moon corresponds to the character's auspice,
he regains all of his Rage. This occurs only upon first seeing the
moon on any given night. For this reason, Garou with high Rage
ratings are best left alone or treated cautiously during their
auspice moon. Full moons are literally the nightmarish times of
legend, as the high-Rage Ahrouns are tempted to just cut loose
and destroy by even the smallest things. More than one small
frontier town has vanished from the map on a full moon....

‘w Botch: At the Storyteller's discretion, a character who
botchesa roll should receive a Rage point to represent frustration,
provided the situation is stressful enough. Remember that social
situations can be as stressful as combat, and a botched roll is
even more frustrating during some social circumstances.

‘w Humiliation: The character may gain a Rage
pointduringa particularly humiliatingsituation. The
Storyteller, not the player, judges whether or not
the humiliation is enough to award a point.
Werewolves donotlike being laughed at. Thiscan
becombined with the rulesabove forbotching—
a werewolf who screws up in public is likely to
become quite irate.

‘@ Confrontation: At the Storyteller's dis-
cretion, a character may receive a Rage point
a‘ter the first turn ofaconfrontational situation,
j stbefore combat really begins in earnest, or
in any really tense roleplaying situation.
"his represents the anticipation of
danger, the raising of hackles as an-
ger rises to the surface.

XX

‘w New Stories: At the beginning of each new story, each
player should roll one die to determine how many Rage points
the characters has (up to her Rage rating). The points could be
more or fewer than those possessed at the end of the previous
story. This represents the inconstancy of Rage, as the anger can
fluctuate wildly over a number of days or as the moon turns.

The player is free to explain the Rage loss or gain, fleshing out
the life of his character between stories. If the stories are creative
or the excuses inventive enough, the Storyteller might be more
liberal with Rage points in the future, or might even add points
to the roll. Ifa player complains that her character doesn't have
enough Rage, she shouldn't worry — her character will get it
back when her auspice moon is in the night sky.

Gnosis

Gnosis represents the link between the Garou and the Sacred
Mother; it'sthespirithalfofawerewolfthat balancesouttheflesh. Gnosis
is the key to understanding the spirit world and the cycles of Gaia.

Gnosisallows werewolves to interact with the entitiesand objects
ofthe spirit world, for Gnosisis the literal substance ofspirit. Without
Gnosis, one can't contact the spirit world at all. Low Gnosis
scores make it difficult to touch the Middle Lands, while Garou

with very high Gnosis scores may find that the

boundary between the worlds of spirit
and matter blurs, making it diffi-
cult at times to distinguish
what is spirit and

what is
not.


Gnosis, like Rage and Willpower, comes in two forms: the
Gnosis rating (shown in dots), which represents your
character's permanent Gnosis, and the Gnosis Pool (shown
in squares), which indicates how many Gnosis points the
character has left to spend. The Gnosis Pool may never be greater
than the Gnosisrating. When youusea pointof Gnosis, youremove
asquare from your character's Gnosis Pool. The dots in your Gnosis
ratingremain constant, while the Pool isexpended during the story.

Using Gnosis

Gnosis can be used to do a variety of things, all related in
some way to nature or the world of spirits.

‘w Using Gifts: Many Gifts require either a Gnosis roll or
an expenditure of Gnosis points.

‘W Fetishes: Gnosis is used to attune to or activate a fetish.

w Rage and Gnosis: You may not spend Gnosis points or
make a Gnosis roll in the same turn that you use a Rage point
or make a Rage roll (though certain Gifts are exceptions to
this rule; Might of Thor, for instance). Tapping white-hot
anger tends to bollix one's spiritual concentration a mite.

‘w Carrying Silver: For each silver object he carries, a
character effectively loses one point from his Gnosis rating.
Some objects cause him to lose more. This is only temporary,
lasting about a day after the silver is discarded. A character
can also be penalized for any silver objects carried by his
companions. For more information, see Silver, pg. 219.

Gaining and Regaining Gnosis

There are several different ways in which characters may
regain spent Gnosis points.

‘w Sacred Hunt: Garou moots often involve a sacred,
ritualistic hunt for the good of the sept and the caern. The prey,
an Engling spirit, is summoned and then hunted down, either
in the Umbra or on Earth. After it is "slain," the Garou
reverently thank it for giving of itself, thereby allowing the
Garou more power in their battles against the Wyrm. All
participants in the hunt have their Gnosis Pools completely
teplenished. Because of the rites performed during this hunt,
the Engling is not actually destroyed, instead Reforming else-
where in the Umbra. See the Appendix for details on Englings.

‘w Bargaining with Spirits: Characters may attempt to find
a spirit, such as an Engling, on their own and try to convince
it to give them some of its Gnosis. The characters must be able
to communicate with the spirit (the Gift: Spirit Speech is
required), and the spirit will ask a small sacrifice in return, such
as a short quest or other favor.

‘@ Meditation: A character may regain Gnosis by spend-
ing time in meditation. He must spend at least an hour doing
so, remaining in one place and focusing on matters of spirit;
he may do nothing else during this time. The player then rolls

Wits + Enigmas (difficulty 8); one Gnosis point is
regained per success. However, a maximum of one

€ Gnosis point can be regained per hour that the

Chapter Six: Traits

character spends in meditation, so if a character spent only
one hour, but scored enough successes to regain three points,
he still gains only one point. This form of meditation may
only be done once per day, and the difficulty is increased by
one for each extra day this is done within a week (maximum
difficulty of 10). Meditation doesn't come easy to all cultures,
and many Garou have to be taught how to look inward.

‘w Between Stories: Players are allowed to make a Cha-
tisma + Enigmas roll (difficulty 6) between stories. Each
success indicates one Gnosis point regained.

Willpower

Willpower measures the character's ability to overcome
the urges and desires that might otherwise control his behav-
ior. It's probably the most self-explanatory of the three
"Finishing Touches" Traits — it's pluck, orneriness and
strength of will, pure and simple.

Willpower, like Rage and Gnosis, comes in two forms: the
Willpower rating (shown in dots), which indicates your
character's permanent Willpower, and the Willpower Pool
(shown in squares), which indicates how many Willpower
points your character has left to spend. The character's
Willpower Pool may never be greater than his Willpower
rating. When you spend a point of Willpower, you remove a
square from the Willpower Pool, not a dot from the Will-
power rating. The rating remains constant, while the Pool
will be depleted during the story.

The Willpower Pool will fluctuate greatly during the
course of a story. It is decreased by one each time the player
uses a Willpower point to have his character do something
extraordinary, such as retain self-control or gain an auto-
matic success. Eventually, the character will have no
Willpower left, and will be unable to exert himself the way he
could before. The character becomes mentally exhausted and
can't rouse himself enough to give a tinker's damn.
