Using Willpower

Willpower is one of the most active Traits in Werewolf; there
are a lot of ways to use or regain it. There's a powerful focus on
Willpower in the game; you'll be paying a lot of attention to it,
so you really need to understand the following rules.


‘w Automatic Success: A Willpower point may be spent to
obtain one automatic success in an action. Only one point of
Willpower may be used in this way per turn, but it does give a
guaranteed suc In this way, it is possible to succeed
automatically in any simple action just by concentrating. For
extended rolls, the extra success can make the critical differ-
ence between success and failure. There are some situations in
which the Storyteller may not allow this use of Willpower.

‘w Uncontrollable Urges: At times, the Storyteller may tell
you that your character does something because of instinct, primal
urge or gut reaction ("You lose your head and attack the miners.").
You may spend a point of Willpower to negate this and do as you
please. The impulse might return, however, and you may have to
spend another Willpower point to keep fighting the urge. Some-
times it will return more times than you have Willpower, while at
other times you may completely overcome the desire.

@ Halting Frenzies: A character who rolls four or more
successes on a Rage roll flies into a frenzy, unless he makes a
successful Willpower roll to keep his head and control himself.
For more information, see Frenzy, pg. 221.

Recovering Willpower
Characters recover their Willpower whenever they are
able to rest or getachance to restore their self-confidence. It's

up to the Storyteller to decide when and how a character
recovers Willpower.

194

WO:

Listed below are three ways in which a character may
recover spent Willpower points. This does not affect the
Willpower rating, which may only be changed through
experience.

w At the end of a completed story (not a game session),
characters regain all their Willpower Pools, up to the amount
of their Willpower ratings. The Storyteller may wish to restrict
this recovery to packs that have at least retained some modi-
cum of dignity or who have achieved at least a partial success
in the story: , "You ripped the Banes to shreds, but didn't
manage to save the Brookshire ranch from the fire. Things
could've gone better, but they could've gone worse too. Regain
all your Willpower."

*@ (Optional) Your character achieves some notable suc-
cess during the story, and the Storyteller figures this would
cause him to recover his self-confidence, and thus all or some
of his Willpower; e.g., "Even with three silver bullets in your
belly, you managed to catch up to the train and rescue the
children. Regain three Willpower points."

‘W (Optional) Your character fulfills some criterion of her
auspice, thus regaining from one to three Willpower points,
subject to Storyteller judgment. This must be achieved through
roleplaying and approved by the Storyteller; e.g., "You're a
Philodox, so your success in averting a battle with the
other tribes filled you with pride. Regain two Will-
power points." &

Werewolf: The Wild West


SSSSSSss3

If none of the above options suits your style of play, consider
allowing characters to regain one point of Willpower after they
wake up each day. It's simple, and it guarantees a steady
recovery of Willpower.

Health

The Health Trait measures how hale and hearty a character
is, There are various levels of injury, each applying a different
penalty to the character thus injured. A character who is Hurt
has one less die to roll on all his rolls, while a character who is
Crippled has five fewer dice to roll. If this leaves the player with
no dice to roll, then the character can't attempt that action.

Awerewolfwhois broughtto Incapacitated by aggravated damage
is very close to death; if he loses one more Health Level, he dies.

Of course, werewolves are creatures of impossible grit. They're
a part of Gaia, and therefore part of the land itself. As Gaia's
medicine workers against the Wyrm's sickness, they aren't as
affected by the ravages of disease and injury as are other creatures.

[Though werewolves are often thought to be immune to
wounds, in truth they just heal so quickly that they don't seem
to get hurt. A Garou can automatically regenerate one Health
Level each turn. However, if he is involved in strenuous or
stressful activity, like combat, he must roll to do so (Stamina
roll; difficulty 8). See Healing, pg. xx, for more details.

Garou do have one major (and unfortunately, not all that
secret) weakness: silver. Wounds inflicted on a werewolf with
asilver weapon can't be automatically regenerated. Similarly,
injuries resulting from fire or the attacks of supernatural crea-
tures (vampires, mockeries, other shapechangers) do damage
beyond the realm of mundane threats. These wounds can't be
regenerated automatically, either; they're called aggravated
wounds, and are the bane of a werewolf's existence.

More details on taking damage and healing wounds can be
found in Chapter Eight: Systems.

When the moon spreads her pale light over the frontier, the
land changes. You feel it in your spine. You see the change as
dark shadows pool in the standing frames of ghost towns. You
hear nervousness in the snorts and whinnies of horses, and in
the pounding of your own heart. You smell the smoke from
burning campfires where people huddle close, afraid of what
lurks in deep shadows. You taste settlers' fear of the night, an
irrational fear born of dreams and ghost stories.

The spirit world reaches out for us in the moonlight. The
native tribes understand what the settlers deride in the day-
light as myth and superstition. Less than the thickness of a
spider strand away lies the Umbra, a place where dreams and
meanings live. The Umbra is the home of the totem spirits and
the place where nightmares live and breed.

Some say the Umbra is a mirror image of the physical world;
it reflects all the things in the physical world, and in turn, the
physical world reflects the spirits in the Umbra. The spirit
world is like a calm lake, reflecting the world around it, but if
you dive beneath the surface, you discover that it is far more
thana reflection. The Umbra is a mightier wilderness than any
other. There are entire worlds to explore, spirits of ancestors
and fallen gods to speak with, long-lost knowledge, ancient
artifacts and fetishes to recover. However, the greatest treasure

a wanderer can discover in the Umbra is himself. Only
by confronting the spirit world can a werewolf truly
understand what he is and his purpose in Gaia's plan.

Werewolves are creatures of both worlds. They are spirits
with bodies of flesh and blood. Garou still keep the ancient
knowledge of calling spirits across the Gauntlet, the barrier
that separates the physical world from the Umbra. Werewolves
know how to look beyond the physical world and enter the
spirit lands. The Garou need contact with the Umbra to feed

their spirits and empower their Gifts.

As the logic-driven sciences and philosophies of Europe
invade the Pure Lands, the gulf between the spirit world and
mundane reality widens. Belief ties the Umbra to the material
world, and as science "disproves" the substance of dreams and
spirits, the Gauntlet dividing the spirit lands from the physical
lands grows stronger. American Indians retain many ties to the
Umbra, revering many spirits. They strive to sustain the ties of
the spirit to the physical world, and most tribes understand that
one world is not complete without the other.

Now there's a war waging for control of the spirit world, as
colonists, pioneers, and settlers bring their philosophies to the
New World, strengthening the Gauntlet, separating the Earth
from the Umbra. Diseases of the spirit wrack the American
Indians and members of the Wendigo and Uktena tribes of
Garou. Native Americans and Garou fight back, using weap-
ons of the spirit against the tools of science.

Thisconflict crosses into the Umbra. The spirit worldchurnsand
boils in the American West. Forgotten entities awake from their
ancient slumbers. The Umbra itself seems to join in the battle to

Chapter Seven: The Storm Umbra 109

RPL REP

ODO


SY.
AY

SY)

preserve its ties to Earth. Great storms rage through the Umbra, and clouds of
darkness fall over many spirit lands. Angry spirits crawl across the Gauntlet
seeking revenge for being disturbed. Some Garou tell tales that the
physical world is cracked and the laws of nature no longer hold true in
these broken lands. The spirit world of the Savage West has eamed a
new name. It is the Storm Umbra, and its fury knows no bounds.

The Umbra

The Umbra is made up of spiritual energy. In the First Times,
the spirit world and the material world were one. After the
Severing, a powerful barrier called the Gauntlet separated the

two worlds. Each world cries out for the other, piercing the
Gauntlet in many places to reunite. The Gauntlet is part of
the Pattern Web, the structure of reality, and as such is too
strong for the worlds to shatter. A few beings, such as the

Garou, have the ability to break through the Gauntlet and

pass on to the Umbra. Many spirits, benign or malevolent,

also have the ability to reach the material world from the

Umbra, and to act on their ambitions.

Anything one can imagine exists in the Umbra; some
believe that everything exists in the spirit world in some
form or another. Dimensional pockets called Realms float
through the Umbra, and have their own physical laws.
Some defy all logic and reason, but most Realms resemble
Earth. They have ground, sky, sun, moon and stars, and
convey a sense of up and down. Garou experience the
Umbra with the same senses they use in the physical world
— things just seem a little...sharper in the spirit lands.
Colors are more vibrant;

me

ire more intense.

Some native Garou believe that the spirit world exists as
it appears, but they also believe that a greater spirit world lies
beyond the reach of the living. Werewolves of other tribes

have more complicated views. The Stargazers believe that the
Umbra is unknowable, for the traveler's own beliefs shapes her
perceptions of the spirit world. Others believe that those
closest to Gaia have the clearest awareness of the Umbra.
One portion of the spirit world, the Penumbra, closely re-
sembles the material world. The influence of powerful actions,
desires and beliefs seep through the Gauntlet into the Penumbra,
molding the ephemera, or spirit material. Extremely powerful or
repeating emanations travel beyond the Penumbra into the Near
Umbra, where they coalesce into distinctive Realms based around
ideas or concepts. The Deep Umbra is far too removed from the
physical world to be affected by emanations. The members of the
Triat— the Weaver, the Wyld, and the Wyrm— dwell in the Deep
Umbra, separated from the rest of the spirit world by the Membrane.
Many spirits live in the Umbra. They have different shapes and
powers. Purpose and meaning define the nature of spirits. Each spirit
isan incamation of its purpose, a meaning given substance. Some are
evil, antagonistic beings, while others are allied with the

Garou. Most werewolves divide spirits into two types
natural spirits related to Gaia, such as those of animals,


plants and wind; and supernatural spirits that serve other forces, such as monsters and aa
Avatars of the Triat. Most spirits have no physical form and cannot exist in the

material world without possessing or being bound into people, objects or creatures.
Some spirits can harness enough spiritual power to create bodies for themselves.
However, all but the mightiest of spirits find materializing exhausting.

One of the most powerful spirits in the Savage West is the Storm
Eater. This ancient monstrosity grows more powerful with the arrival
of the Wyrmcomer Garou to the West. Once a potent Bane, it now
fuses its essence with powerful Weaver energies, gaining enough
strength to dominate the Storm Umbra.

The spirit world offers Garou a source of energy and a sense of
renewal. Although born in the physical world, Garou have strong
ties to the Umbra. Places where the Gauntlet thins are called
caerns. Werewolves guard and tend these sacred sites where they
can more easily perform their rituals and touch the Umbra. The
spirit world offers hope and infinite possibilities for those who
can resist the fear of the unknown and the formless.

Gaia vs. the Tellurian

The werewolves refer to all of reality as the Tellurian
when they speak of the parts of the universe where mon-
sters dwell and dark evil things spawn. When they
emphasize the good and living parts of creation, they refer
to all of reality as Gaia. Both Gaia and the Tellurian are
the same — just with different emphasis.

The Garou believe that the whole of creation cannot
be understood by any single mortal. The only way to
comprehend the Tellurian is to become one with Gaia, the
soul of the universe. Too much emphasis on the here-and-
now spoils that unity; the way to reach Gaia and gain
spiritual understanding is through the Umbra.

The Nature of the
World

Werewolves call the center of the universe Gaia. She
anchors creation, and all things come from Her. The Umbra
surrounds and encompasses Earth, stretching out beyond this
center of life. The Umbra has three distinct layers: the Middle Land,
the Underworld and the Heavens. The Garou and all living
things dwell in the Middle Land. Dead things, demons and
monsters lurk in the Underworld, emerging only to terror-
ize the world of the living. The Heavens are distant and
unfathomable. Powerful entities live in the Heavens, and
bold travelers sometime seek out the powers above, but
Garou belong to the Middle Land and should not seek
things beneath the earth or in the sky.

Earth lies at Gaia's heart. The World Tree grows from the
center of the Earth, stretching its branches up into the Heavens
and digging its roots deep into the underworld. The North

American tribes say that the World Tree grows on the back of df a

agreat turtle thatswimsin thegreatoceanofthe Umbra. The . *
e shell of the turtle makes up the surface of the Earth.


Allother parts of reality are composed or shaped by the Triat:
the Wyrm, the Weaver, and the Wyld. Insimple terms, the Wyld
represents raw undefined potential — it is the energy of creation
in constant flux. The Weaver takes the potential of the Wyld
and givesit formandstability. Although the Weaver can't create
on her own, she can give order to the creations of the Wyld. The
Wyrm is the power of destruction that breaks down the forms of
creation. The Wyrm removes the dead limbs from the World
Tree and prevents stagnation — or at least, that's how it used to
work. Now the Wyrm savages the whole of the World Tree,
ripping apart living and dead alike, tearing at the vibrant as well
as the atrophied. The relationship of the Triat moves the
universe. If the Triat works in harmony, all things enjoy the
blessings of Gaia. If the Triat conflicts, all suffer its ravages.

The Umbra is the place where truth lives. If the spirit can be
healed, then the body will follow. The Garou believe that if the
spiritual source of a problem is found and changed, then the
problem is solved; cleaning the spiritual taint on Penumbral bluff
cleans the physical-world bluff in turn. The Garou have an
opportunity to fix the underlying causes of wrongs in the world,
through the Umbra. Werewolves can alter destiny and shape
reality; it's a heavy responsibility, but they take it on gladly.

How Things Began

It's impossible to put words to the truth of creation, for creation
happened at a time before words. The wisest shamans say that a
turtle surfaced on the great sea of eternity. The sun rose and warmed
the waters, and so the turtle fell asleep. Raven dropped a seed on the
back of thesleeping turtle and from there a great tree grew. The tree
reached up into the clouds and the realms of the spirit. Dead things
lived in the belly of the turtle, which became the realm of death.
Although the specifics of this story differ in the telling, most Garou
agree that this is very close to how things came to be.

The First Times

At the beginning of time, all of Gaia was unified and whole.
Matter and spirit were one. The Triat was in balance, and the cycle
of reality flowed without interruption. Individual creatures, both
matter and spirit, lived in this harmonious whole. Although they
possessed their own sentience, they understood their places and
purposes in the universe. Happiness and sadness were parts of
existence, and the first creatures shared their pleasures and pains
with all of Gaia. In turn, they felt the joy and pain of the universe
and suffered or celebrated with the rest of creation.

Life was eternal, but it was also ever-changing. Entities
could take new forms and live new lives, but they could always
restore their old consciousness and awareness. Nothing truly
died; instead it existed in acycle of eternal rebirth. This was the
time of the "true Gaia," when all knew that they were of the
Mother and Her universe. All was one, yet each individual
maintained its own identity. Anything was possible and no
limits bound creation. The Garou maintain that all living
beings have a memory of this time of wonder within their souls.

Werewolf: The Wild West

Then a great cataclysm shattered the Edenic tranquillity of
the First Times. Creation was torn apart; matter and spirit were
tipped from each other. A great fossilized barrier, the Gauntlet,
rose up between the spiritual and the material worlds, All
beings screamed as one in agony and terror. Never again would
they know the pleasure of the whole. Gaia was split, forever.

The werewolves maintain that all evil stems from this
Severing of the spirit from the physical. Before that time, pain
was an accidental warning of unintended consequences. After
the Severing, Gaia learned anger and rage, which she passed on
to Her children. There was death in the material world, and
dissolution in the spirit world. Creatures learned to inflict pain
on others for their own pleasure. Mortals and spirits knew fear
and isolation. Utopia was destroyed.

Neither Pure Onenor European can say forcertain what caused
the Severing. Some Garou blame the Wyrm, and say that the
Severing came with the birth of the Wyrm itself. A few believe
that when the Wyrm went mad, its berserk struggles were what
tore spirit from flesh. Others believe that the Wyrm deliberately
chose to sever the spirit world from the material world.

Most Garou lorekeepers say the Weaver went insane. She
began to spin her Pattern Web faster and faster. The Wyrm
couldn't keep pace with her, and the balance of things tipped
in favor of order. The Wyrm struggled harder to destroy the
Weaver's creations, but in a fit of blindness, it stumbled into
the Pattern Web. As the Wyrm struggled to escape, it grew
more and more tangled. Soon it was trapped for good. Unable
to fulfill its purpose, the Wyrm fell prey to delirium. The
mindless creation of the Weaver met random destruction by
the Wyrm. The cycle was broken.

The imbalance shattered existence. The order and matter of
the universe gathered in one world, while the creative spiritual
energies coalesced in another. They still interacted, but the
Gauntlet held the worlds apart.

Now most living beings, save for shapeshifters and some
others, are separated from their spirits. Animating spirits keep
mortals alive, but their true spirits lie in deep slumber unaware
of the cosmos surrounding them, unaware of their true place
and true purpose. The living can perceive and communicate
with each other's bodies, but only rarely can their souls perceive
each other and communicate. Many sentient beings have
distant memories and can touch their spirits in dreams, but most
of them cannot traverse the Gauntlet. They cannot reach the
Umbra. They cannot regain their souls.

The Wolves that Walk
Between the Worlds

The Garou can still cross into the spirit world. They retain
access to the Near Umbra and the spiritual Realms that float
within its boundaries. Although the Garou can pen-
etrate the Gauntlet, they have yet to find a way to
dersy ie barieeand etre Cale *


as

Werewolves mayslip out of the material world into the Umbra.
There isadanger, however, that each time a Garou passes between
worlds, she may be caught in the wall between worlds. The Garou
call the act of crossing the Gauntlet "stepping sideways."

Garouseekanswerstospiritual questionsin the Umbra. They hope
to resolve the problems of the material world by untangling the true
meanings and roots of those problems. A few werewolves seek power
inthe Umbra, butmany travel toseek ways to reunify Gaiaand restore
the heritage of the Garou. Unfortunately, the grim realities of the
Savage West lead many to think that if matter and spirit are ever
reunified, it won't be in their lifetimes, or the lifetimes of their cubs.
The Wyrm has grown too strong, and things look bleaker by the day

The Umbral
Environment

Garou are the finest scouts and pathfinders of the Storm
Umbra from the physical side of the Gauntlet. Werewolves open
Moon Bridges to travel from one caern to another via the
Umbra. The Garou step sideways into the Umbra and follow
Spirit Tracks and Moon Paths.

Although werewolves are the best of Earth's Umbral wan-
derers, they are not the only beings who can enter the spirit
world. Powerful shamans can enter the Umbra, as can mages
who may or may not respect the spirit world. What's more,
other shapeshifters can often navigate the Storm Umbra with
varying ease; the few remaining children of Coyote, in particu-
lar, seem to have no trouble getting around in the spirit world.

The fury of the Storm Umbra has torn rents and fissures in the
Gauntlet of the Savage West. Mundane creatures sometimes
even accidentally stumble into the spirit world. Most humans
who discover the Umbra don't live long enough to tell tales.

The Earth

Gaia sits at the center of the spiritual cosmos. Werewolves
believe the Umbra radiates outward from the physical universe. The
Gauntlet surrounds and permeates the natural world, dividing the
mundane from the spirit. Werewolves usually cross over at places
where the Gauntlet is weak, places they call caerns, or wherever
necessity demands stepping sideways. During powerful Umbral
storms, the spirit reunites with the physical world, opening a gate
between the two. The Garou call places where this happens
frequently the Broken Lands. The Earth is the mostimportant place
inall creation, and the Garou sometimes refer to it as "the Realm,"

aterm established by the Silver Fangs during medieval times.

The Gauntlet

Matter and spirit crave unification and pull toward each other.
The Gauntlet strains to keep the two apart. In some areas, the
Gauntlet has grown powerful enough to make the land

spiritually dead. In other areas, the laws of reality break
&: down and the Umbra bleeds into the Realm. As pio-

neers cross North America, map the land, build railroad

tracks, establish roads and communities, they bring the Weaver
with them. The unknown areas of the world, those brimming with
the possibilities of the Wyld, become fewer and fewer. Now the
remaining Wyld energies of the Savage West are out of control.
Storms rage that could destroy all that the Weaver has created,
throwing the physical and spiritual worlds into chaos.

Werewolves circumvent the Gauntlet in a number of ways.
They travel to the spirit world by focusing on their spiritual
selves and stepping sideways. They also enter the Umbra
through dreams. What's more, Garou often locate areas where
the Gauntlet is weak — caerns — and form septs to protect
these places and hold off incursions of the Weaver.

Every place on Earth has a Gauntlet rating from 2 to 8.
Where the Gauntlet is highest, entering the spirit world is
almost impossible. Areas where the Gauntlet is weak allow
easy access for those who know the way.

Most settlements

Well-traveled frontier

wruan

Of course, these are measures of the Gauntlet in the Savage
West. In Europe and more populous areas, the Gauntlet is
higher. Over the last few centuries, the strength of the Gauntlet
has increased steadily throughout the world, causing a few
visionaries to cry that the end of all things draws near. Many
Garou travel to the American frontier because the Gauntlet
remains weak here. Some hope that they can reunite the Umbra
with the material world and rejuvenate all of Gaia in the process.

Caerns

The Gauntlet weakens in wild, primeval areas. At the thinnest
points of the Gauntlet, spiritual energy flowsdirectly into the physical
world. Theso-called lawsof nature give way in these areas to wondrous
and magical events. The Garou respect such sacred sites. Werewolves
guard their caerns from those who would leech spiritual energy from
the land, and jealously hold them against others who would twist
caems into their own images. Often, this means defending caerns
against otherGarou. Asthe EuropeansadvanceacrossNorth America,
native and foreign werewolves fight for caerns, wreaking devastation.
Many of the Pure Lands' caerns are lost to infighting and the influx of
powerful Wyrm and Weaver-spirits brought by the Europeans, spirits
which the Wendigo and Uktena have little recent experience
fighting. When the Croatan tribe was destroyed, a third of the caems
in North America were left untended, and neither of the surviving
Pure Ones tribes have the numbers to reclaim them.

Some caerns embody specific aspects, such as leadership or
combat. Spirits attuned to a caern's purpose inhabit the place.
Werewolves can often invoke arcane powers at such a site,
powers that are aligned with the nature of the caern.

Chapter Seven: The Storm Umbra 188


Caerns are rated in potency from | to 5. A Level One
caern of visions might provide a Garou with vague stirrings,
while a Level Five caern of visions could fill the minds of an
audience with detailed images of the future. The weaker the
Gauntlet, the more potent the caern.

The Periphery

At the edge of the Umbra, spiritual energy trickles into the
material world. This area, the Periphery, is the only part of the
Umbra that most living beings can experience. The Periphery
doesn't provide enough spiritual energy to aid the Garou, but
for those with unawakened spirits, the Periphery is a blessing.
Many humans will use any means possible, including opium or
peyote, religion, self-deprivation, or meditative sweat lodges to
access the edge of the Umbra.

When a mortal becomes aware of the spiritual side of the self,
she can touch the Periphery. She feels spiritually awake; the
spark of life within her brightens. Problems seem distant; her
mind feels free. Her perceptions are raised, and she feels a true
unity with all things. Anyone of any culture can access this
altered state of consciousness through a variety of means. Kin-
folk have a stronger tie to the Periphery than do most humans
and wolves, and some legends suggest they can use the Periphery
to contact Garou who travel through the Near Umbra.

Contacting the Periphery causes a person to enter a "magical"
state of awareness in which everything seems possible. A person
takes his expectations with him into the Periphery. A depressed
individual grows even more depressed. A person searching for
answers may suddenly realize the solutions to his dilemma. Spirits
cancontact humans who touch the Periphery. Sometimes, people

contact beatific spirits who can aid them; in other cases, people
draw the attention of Banes and other malevolent entities.

IfaPeripheral travelerencountersan evil spirit, he needs toleave
immediately, or he's easy prey. However, many of those who reach
this state of consciousness through drugs can't stop the effects. Ifa
human stays in the Periphery too long, he won't be able to handle
the shock of reality. Such psychic damage can permanently scar
one's personality. Only those who enter the Periphery by force of
will safely interact with it, since they can leave at any time.

The Garou believe that the Storm Eater can mold the bounds
of the Periphery at its whim, snatching unwitting targets over the
edge. Some humans in the Savage West who touch the Periphery
thus draw the attention of the mighty spirit, which entices them
further into their newly altered awareness, only to surround and
enslave them. Such victims often become mockeries, but others
return tothe physical world with their perceptionssubtly. . .altered.
The latter serve as some of the most subtle pawns of the great
Weaver-Bane, as they usually have no idea what it is they serve.

The Penumbra

The Penumbra is the part of the spirit world that corresponds
to the physical world. Within the Penumbra lie the spiritual
reflections of creatures and objects of the physical world.
Werewolves often enter the spirit world to reach the Penumbra,
where they battle Banes intent on polluting and changing Gaia
from the other side of the Gauntlet. If the Banes can alter or
corrupt the spirit ofa thing, the material likeness is soon affected.

The Penumbra, sometimes called Earth's Shadow, is a gray,
phantasmal place illuminated only by Luna, the Celestine of the
Moon. The weaker the Gauntlet in an area, the closer the Penum-
bra resembles the spirit world. The tone of a place, the emotions it
stirs — these things are echoed in its Umbral reflection. A hillside
seems more stark, the air sharper. A telegraph line drips with
glistening webbing, with the occasional machine-spider scurrying
along the line like a bolt of lightning. A desert seems bleak and
lifeless, or teems with the spirits of animals and the elements. A
copper mine bleeds acrid, metallic sludge. Whatever is most affect-
ing about a given site — beauty, pollution, tranquillity, menace or
what-have-you— becomes almost overpowering in the Penumbra.

The Penumbra holds the ephemera, or spirit matter, which
corresponds to the physical matter of the Realm. Only physi-
cal objects that hold large amounts of spiritual energy manifest
in the Penumbra. Old structures often have counterparts on
Earth's Shadow, but most (if not all) of the new settlements
on the frontier are too recent to cast spiritual reflections.
Spirits of the Triat move through the Penumbra, staying close
to objects aligned with their masters. Only spiritually Awak-
ened beings such as mages or werewolves have strong enough
spirits to cast shadows in the Penumbra.

Although a spirit can survive for a long time without a

physical reflection, a physical object or item cannot last
long without a spiritual counterpart. If a totem pole is
& chopped down in the Realm, its spirit lingers in the

Chapter Seven: The Storm Umbra

Penumbra for many years. However, if the spirit of the totem
pole is destroyed, the physical pole quickly rots and decays. It's
for this reason that the Penumbra is so often a battleground, as
werewolves fight to defend their territory on the spiritual front.

The Penumbra is dotted with Domains, small patches of
spiritual ground that correspond to sites in the material world
and the Near Umbra. These areas are almost always inhabited
by spirits. Domains siphon energy from related areas in the
Near Umbra to sustain themselves.

‘@ Blights: A Blight is an area of mixed Wyrm/Weaver
energies. The Uktena attribute most of the Blights in the
Savage West to the Storm Eater. The Blights appear in particu-
larly soulless or heavily populated towns as contaminated,
twisted, web-coated townscapes. Rumors abound that deep in
the Middle Umbra, a potent Blight has swelled into a Near
Realm, providing a new source of power for these Domains.

@ Chimares: These are dream Domains connected to the
Dream Zone. Every being that dreams may access a chimare. The
nature of a chimare depends on the types of dreams it represents.
Someare peculiar, surreal landscapes with bizarre inhabitants; others
are nightmarish parodies of the mortal world and its inhabitants.

‘w Epiphs: These Domains represent abstract ideas and con-
cepts. They are exceedingly rare and sought after by Stargazers who
claim to find great insights by visiting them. Most werewolves
cannot understand such places or the abstract entities that dwell in
them; Garou nature leaves little time for mulling over abstractions.

‘Glens: Glensare areas empowered by the Wyld. They appear
as ideal examples of the natural world, with talking animals, and
sentient trees and plants. They are found in any environment from
forest to plain to desert; a Glen may resemble an idealized watering
hole, a rugged mountaintop eyrie, a forested island in the center of
a river, or any number of similarly primal sites.

4w Webs: These are locations where the Weaver touches the
Penumbra. They are found in technologically advanced areas, such
as places connected to telegraph lines or railyards. Glistening metal
webs hang throughout this sort of Domain, and strange, insectoid
Weaver-spirits crawl along the strands. Webs are becoming more
common, but are still rare in the Savage West. Most immigrant
tribes are better versed in negotiating Webs than the Pure Ones.
The Uktena and Wendigo have almost no experience with these
Weaver-controlled zones. What's worse, the Storm Eater often
rends Webs with its very touch, tangling them into strange half-
Blights that are twice as dangerous to unwary travelers.

*¥ Wyldlings: These are places where pure Wyld energy touches
the Realm. Nothing stays constant in these areas, and dangerous
‘Wyld energies transform those who enter such Domains. With the
coming of the Weaver and the release of the Storm Eater, many
Wyldlingsare growing dramatically or moving through the Penum-
bra, twisting and destroying all Weaver creations they touch. The
incursion of European Weaver spirits into the Pure Lands seems to
have made Wyldlings even more chaotic than they were before.

There are other Domains, such as Trods (faerie circles) and
Hellholes (heavily polluted Wyrm Domains), but these and
others are rarely found in the Savage West.


SSSSSESN

Umbral Storms

As the influence of European colonists supplants the beliefs of the native
peoples, storms brew in the Umbra. The Weaver spins her strands of science
and technology across the Savage West, most visibly in the form of railroad
tracks that sprawl for miles across the previously untamed frontier. The
spirits of Europe follow the settlers, attacking native spirits in
a struggle for dominance. The resulting maelstroms
have created the Storm Umbra. Not even in the songs
of the Galliards can the Garou recall a time or place
when storms raged as they do in the American West.
The Pure Ones know that the Umbra touching the
Savage West wasn't always this volatile, but they have
little luck convincing the newcomers of this.

Umbral storms can force a pack off course, shatter
Moon Bridges, and wreak spiritual devastation on a locale in
the physical world. When a storm strikes an area, it manifests in disease,
famine and strife; gentle people start massacres; buildings collapse without
reason; booming towns are abandoned to ghosts. Storms tear holes in the
Gauntlet, creating sections of Broken Land, strange places where it is

possible to wander in and out of the Umbra. A few s| that Broken

Lands are small Domains of Gaia, twisted by the ‘orm Umbra.

‘The Broken Lands

Broken Lands are a strange form of Umbral terrain found only in the
Savage West. They are places where the Gauntlet has temporarily fallen
to 1. These patchwork locales shift from the Umbra to the Earth and back
again. The Periphery is very strong in Broken Lands, and many of those
who have returned from such places describe them as dreamlike.

Broken Lands travel with the winds of the Storm Umbra. Violent
"Umbral storms rend apart the Gauntlet for miles, leaving vast stretches
., of freakish terrain. Most encounters with the Broken Lands involve
taking a wrong turn ona trail and discovering a town or mesa not on any
map. At first, most wanderers don't realize that they've slipped into a
section of Broken Land. The inhabitants of such places seem uncon-
*-¢emned about goings-on outside their parts of the world.

Small coincidences and slight reality problems are the first hints that all is
* ‘pot right. In a saloon in the Broken Lands, a thirsty drifter may notice that his
drinkneverrunsdry. Themusic ftom the pianomayhaveahauntingecho. Low
cards may be beating high cards in poker at the next table, and nine aces could
_ te in play with no one complaining. Horses get skittish when they slip into the
Broken Lands, which is perhaps the first sign that something just ain't right.
The weirdness escalates, too. A gunfighter may drag the body of a Pattern
. * Spider into town to collect a bounty. A werewolf may transform in public without
"any residents being affected by the Delirium — or even noticing. In the wilds,
jackrabbits may chase and eat coyotes. The sun may not rise for days. All Broken
Lands have something unnatural about them. They are not healthy places where
the physical and spiritual worlds have rejoined, much as most Garou hope will
someday happen. Broken Lands are more like the result of taking a festering wound
and pressing another festering wound against it — in other words, a real mess.
Broken Lands are at the center of an Umbral Storm. This storm shreds the
Gauntlet, holding the laws of reality, as most folks know them, at bay. In most

strength. A forgotten caern, an ancient fetish ora sleeping spirit are possible sources.

Broken Lands, there are sources of spiritual energy from which the storm draws its &

Werewolf: The Wild West


Most often, the cause of the storm is an ancient or forgotten
menace lurking beneath the surface of a Broken Land, hoping to
be released by those attracted by a storm. If the source of spiritual
energy can be removed or cut off, then the land heals itself.

The Middle Lands

When a werewolf heads outward from the Penumbra, he
arrives in the Middle Lands, or Near Umbra. This is a land of
swirling mists, broken by hundreds of floating Realms. The
different Realms revolve around Gaia, just as the moon re-
volves around the Earth. These other realities are the homes of
many powerful secrets. They hide materials needed to con-
struct fetishes or perform rituals. Some Realms keep forgotten
knowledge within their depths. Most of the Realms have
rulers, who are usually powerful, unpredictable spirits.

The strongest of these regions are the Nine Near Realms. Each is
surrounded by a membrane that holds its spiritual energy together.
When a werewolf enters a Near Realm, he feels as if he has stepped
out of the Umbraand back into the physical world. All of the Garou's
natural abilities work, although certain Near Realms slightly alter a
werewolf's abilities. Each Realm has its own "physical" laws that
govern the local reality. There are many rumorsofotherNearRealms,
but they are not commonly known among the Garou.

In addition to the Near Realms, the Umbra is filled with
Domains, sub-Realms and Zones, including the Dream Zone.
Allofthe Domains described in the Penumbra have strongerand
larger equivalents in the Near Umbra. Each Garou tribe has its
own Tribal Homeland in the Umbra, a representation of the
tribe's ideal protectorate, and these are some of the strongest
Domains in the Near Umbra. Vistas float along the edges of the
Near Umbra, offering glimpses into the embodiments of abstract
concepts, such as Death, Chaos, and Stasis. Vistas cannot be
entered, but many believe they are windows to the Heavens.

The Near Realms

The Nine Near Realms formed when emanations from Gaia
combined with the energies of the Triat. Some of the Near
Realms lean more closely to Gaia, while others are closer to the
Triat. These are the most stable and spiritually potent of all the
Realms of the Near Umbra, although rumors abound of two
other Near Realms forming in the spirit world. One, the Atrocity
Realm, is rumored to be dedicated to the deaths and suffering of
the displaced Native Americans. The other is a growing Blight
Realm, called the Scar, where energies of the Weaver and Wyrm
mix with emanations from the factories of Europe.

te Abyss: The Abyss is a crack in the fabric of reality. A few
Wendigo say that the Eater-of-Souls, a gigantic Wyrm-spirit slain
through the sacrifice of the Croatan tribe, died here. Others say the
Abyss is where the Tellurian has come unraveled, opening portal to
places beyond the Heavens. The Abyss contains nothingness, no

light, nosound, nofeeling. Nothing that falls into the Abyss
ever comes out. Spirits refuse to enter this Realm, and
abandon any companions who insist on visiting the Abyss.

Chapter Seven: The Storm Umbra

 Aetherial Realm: This Realm exists atop the clouds of the
Near Umbra and extends out to the edge of the Heavens. Stars
gleam down over the Near Umbra from the Aetherial Realm, and
Incarna of Helios and Luna hold Domains here. The Courts of the
Spirits are said to gather in the Aetherial Realm, and Spirit Gates
tothe Heavens are rumored tofloat in the clouds. Garoucan reach
the Aetherial Realm by traveling toward the Umbral sky.

‘e Arcadia Gateway: Long ago, the faeries abandoned the
Earth for their own dimension. They left a gate in the Near
Umbra behind, which has grown into a powerful Realm. This
Realm assumed many attributes of the original home of the
faeries, Arcadia. A few faeries dwell here, sentinels assigned to
guard the gate, or outcasts who were abandoned by their fellows.
A few Fianna say that beyond the Gateway, faeries have changed
into something far different from the ones left behind.

‘¥ Battleground: Humanity's glorification of war created the
Realm of the Battleground. Here, spirit warriors continuously
reenact every battle from the First Times to Gettysburg. Blood-
soaked signposts show the way to each battle, and Pathways
provide access to battles from different times and places. In the
center of the Realm lies the Plain of the Apocalypse, where
Theurges know the final battle of creation will take place. Many
werewolves seek the Battleground to gain Rage and study the
spirit duplicates of their enemies. Others come to hone their
fighting skills and release their pent-up Rage on the ever-
renewing spirits, rather than on their packmates or families.

Erebus: This is the underworld of the Middle Lands, It is
aplace of ongoing torment where Garouare imprisoned ina lake
of molten silver. The silver inflicts terrible wounds that would
surely kill its victims, but the Realm sustains them so that no
matter how much they endure, the werewolves cannot die. A
three-headed wolf-creature guards the gate to the Realm.

Somesay that Erebus isacrucible thatpurifies the corrupted Garou
and restores them from Harano. Others believe that Erebus is the
werewolf equivalent of Perdition itself. A few European Garou,
possibly driven insane by the Storm Umbra, travel the Savage West
preaching "fire and brimstone" sermons about Charyss, the Silver
Lady and mythical ruler of Erebus. The sermons usually tum to
Charyss' demons and what they do to those who don't pay proper
homage to Gaia and her priests. These Perdition-preacher Garou are
known to attack those who dismiss their words out of hand.

4 Flux: This isa piece of the Wyld that has broken through the
Membrane. The energies ofthe Flux Realm makeanything possible.
Time shifts uncontrollably in the Flux Realm and Garou lose
controlovertheirtransformations. Werewolvesgather vastamounts
of spiritual energy here, and even shape the mutable fabric of the
Flux Realm as if she were a god. The Flux Realm lies at the center
of the mightiest winds in the Storm Umbra. A few Iron Riders say
tharthe Flux Realm needs to be driven back beyond the Barrier, and
that thisRealm is the cause of the conflict within the Storm Umbra.
Other tribes, like the Black Furies, believe that the Flux Realm helps
tokeep the Weaver from completely ensnaring the Near Umbra in
its webs. A few Uktena Theurges wonder if the Storm Eater itself
doesn't drink from the Flux energies to sustain its mighty bulk...

rrr

XX

‘w Legendary Realm: The legends of the Garou sustain a
Realm of their own. The ancient heroes of every tribe dwell in
this interconnected series of subrealms, living out their heroic
epics again and again. Garou who enter this Realm may assume
the roles of these legends and test themselves against the Realm's
recreations of these myths. A great debate rages among Theurges
as to whether the Legendary Realm represents the true actions
of the past or the interpretations of the legends that the Garou
have inherited. There, it is said, the Pure Lands remain pure,
guarded by the greatest heroes of the Uktena and Wendigo.

‘w Pangaea: The strongest of the Near Realms may be
Pangaea. This primordial world was created over countless
centuries as a reflection of the Earth that once was. All
creatures who have ever lived on Earth dwell on Pangaea,
including the great thunder lizards of long, long ago. Ancient
woods, never cleared by humans, cover the vast land. An
Incarna called the Elder Serpent makes his lair here. Many
shamans believe that Pangaea holds a secret to protecting the
Pure Lands from the Weaver, and quest there for answers.

¢@ Summer Country: Most Garou say that Summer Country
does not exist. In the Savage West, where life is hard and brutal,
this legendary Eden-Realm seems more like a story for young
pups or children than a Realm that Theurges should seek. Most
say that Summer Country reflects the time before the Severing
whenall beings were one with Gaia. They say that ifa Garou has
a transcendental experience and accepts the unconditional love
of Gaia, she arrives in Summer Country. Those who believe the
legends say that all wounds, physical, mental and emotional,
heal in Summer Country. Those who don't believe usually shake
their heads at such stories, and chastise the speaker for trying to
spread a hope that doesn't exist.

(For more complete descriptions of the Near Realms, see
Umbra: The Velvet Shadow.)

Dream Zone

One of the most mysterious areas of the Umbra is the Dream
Zone. It is a place of vast power, terrifying symbolism, and
chaos. Only the foolish enter the Dream Zone casually, for it
seems to defy the natural order of the Umbra.

Theurges often describe the Dream Zone as a beautiful gem
enclosing the Middle Lands and bordering the Heavens.
Werewolves respect it greatly, both asa testament to the power
of dreams and as a way of gaining special access to the Umbra.
Rather than entering the Umbra via Moon Bridges or stepping
sideways, Garou enter the Dream Zone through their own
minds, and move on from there. By traveling deep into his
dreams, a Garou arrives at the border of the Near Umbra and
the Deep Umbra. The werewolf somehow avoids traveling
outward through the Gauntlet, Penumbra and Middle Lands.
This is a potent, but perilous form of Umbral travel, because it
leaves the Garou's body behind, unprotected and asleep. In
addition, the Garou must survive the dangers of his own
dreams. The Uktena in particular make use of the Dream Zone

Werewolf: The Wild West

to go on dreamquests into the Heavens in search of strange
answers. Werewolves also slip into the Dream Zone through
Chimares, small dream Domains in the Penumbra.

The Membrane

A rainbow shimmers in the Heavens above the Middle Lands;
it is somewhat obscured by the clouded skies of the Storm Umbra,
butitisstill visible if you know where to look. Thisisthe Membrane,
the border between the Tellurian and whatever dwells in the Deep
Umbra. It is a much stronger barrier than the Gauntlet; only
certain realms poke through it. These realms are called
Anchotheads, and they are harsh lands terrorized by powerful
servants of the Triat. Some say that mystic realms float through the
Membrane, but although werewolves sometimes see islands in the
rainbow, they've reached no consensus as to their significance.

The Heavens

On the outer fringe of the Tellurian lie the Heavens, also
called the Deep Umbra. Beyond the Membrane, things don't
work as they should. Time and distance don't exist. The realms
of the Triat are the only places out there that any traveler can
relate to the rest of the Tellurian. Perhaps some bold scouts will
one day discover secrets and places beyond the Deep Umbra, but
if they do, it will make mastering the Savage West seem easy.

=
Night

Most malevolent spirits in the Umbra rest at night, making it
an easier time for Garou to prowl the spirit world. Weaver-spirits
slumber at nightfall, exhausted from their daily web-spinning.
Banes retreat to their Blights. Luna's light filters down through
the Umbra, making travel simpler for the Garou. But the Umbral
Storms don't need Slumber, and don't acknowledge day or
night. What's more, the perverted spirit offspring of the Storm
Eater have a dangerous liking for roaming at night, which can
put an unwary werewolf in a dire predicament.

= =
Stepping Sideways
All werewolves have the ability to travel into the spirit
world. They call this "stepping sideways," although many
Garou prefer the more respectful term of "reaching." To a
werewolf, stepping into the Umbra feels like walking through
a veil or curtain. Her body becomes pure spirit-stuff and, to a
physical onlooker, she seems to vanish.
AllaGaroumustdoisconcentrateon his own image inareflective
surface and then focus beyond the image — "projecting" himself
through a reflective surface. Any reflective surface will do: a mirror, a
still pool of water or a polished silver klaive. Some Garou have Gifts
that allow them to enter without gazing at their reflections. Many
native Garou step sideways by concentrating on symbols or totems.
Theyguard thesecret ofusingfetishestoenterthe Umbrafrom
the invaders; however, those who know this secret are so
reluctant to share it that the art is in danger of dying out. eI


To determine whether or not a werewolf can enter the spirit
world, a player rolls his character's Gnosis against the difficulty
of the Gauntlet. The number of successes determines how long
it takes the werewolf to cross the barrier.

Pack members choose a single member to lead them across
the Gauntlet. The leader only takes those willing to follow
him and those capable of stepping sideways; taking humans or
other non-Garou through the Gauntlet can not be done
without the help of a special Gift. All members of the group
must belong to the same pack; their bond allows them to focus
more clearly as a whole. The leader's player (likely the Garou
with the highest Gnosis) rolls for all members. In this way, all
members of the pack arrive in the Umbra together. Other-
wise, they risk being split up by the Gauntlet, or some
members will probably have to wait for others to arrive. If the
leader's player botches his roll, the entire pack suffers the
consequences and becomes trapped in the Gauntlet.

If a Garou is "caught," he is trapped in the Gauntlet, torn
between the material world and the Umbra. The werewolf is
bound in the Pattern Web and cannot move unless he is
helped by another Garou. Terrifying visions, some say the
Weaver's madness, come to the trapped Garou. His being is
half-matter and half-spirit. Physical beings are not able to see
or touch someone caught in the Gauntlet; however, many
spirits stalk the Gauntlet hunting for trapped victims. After
an hour of imprisonment, the Garou can try to complete his
trip through the Gauntlet again. Failure on the second
attempt means that unless the character is rescued, he is
trapped forever in the Pattern Web.

Disconnection

Garou often become lost traveling the paths of ancestors
and battling spirit storms. A werewolf who walks in the spirit
world too long becomes disconnected; the longer she remains
in the spirit world, the harder it is to find her way home.
Although most prudent Umbral travelers never become dis-
connected, all Garou should be aware of the danger.

Nonshapeshifters somehow brought into the Umbra face
even greater risk. Not being half-spirit like werecreatures, they
have a greater dependence on the physical world. If a mortal
spends too long in the Umbra, his form eventually fades into
spirit matter; he gradually forgets his previous life and becomes
aspirit himself. Walking the Storm Umbra is best left to those
meant to do so.

Umbral Travel

When a Garou steps sideways, he enters the Penumbra.
Since the Penumbra reflects the physical world, movement
occurs in much the same manner as it does in the material
world — running three miles in the Penumbra is the same as

running three miles in the material realm. If a werewolf

leaves the Penumbra by stepping sideways, he enters
the physical world in the location corresponding to
& his position in the Penumbra.

——

A Garou can travel out of the Penumbra and into the
Middle Lands through sheer force of will. The Garou just
thinks about going deeper into the Umbra and starts moving.
The Penumbra fades, and the werewolf enters the Near Umbra.

The Middle Lands don't work like the Penumbra. Distance
is no longer constant. There are no landmarks or signs, just
swirling mists. The traveler should decide where she wants to
go in the Umbra and start walking. Time doesn't flow consis-
tently in the Umbra, so the length of a journey is hardly
constant. Travel times in the Middle Lands are at the
Storyteller's discretion. Sometimes, it is not possible to get to
one place from another without finding a trail or pathway.

Moon Paths

Usually, when a werewolf enters the Near Umbra, she must
find a Moon Path that leads to her destination. Lunes guard
Moon Paths during a full moon, but during that time they are
consumed with madness. The route is safe unless a werewolf
stops to talk with the Lunes— then she takes his chances with
the crazed Moon-spirits. Fewer Lunes protect the paths, during
agibbous or half-moon, but they are less of a threat to travelers.
Most Garou feel that Moon Paths are safest during these times.
If a Garou steps off a Moon Path, guardian Lunes may play
tricks on him, or even attack.

During crescent and new moons, Moon Paths are difficult
totraverse. Whenacrescent moon shines, a traveler must roll
Perception + Enigmas (difficulty 6) to follow the path.
During a new moon, Moon Paths are almost invisible. Garou
should not walk Moon Paths during a new moon, because it
is easy to walk off a trail without knowing it and become lost
in the unlit Umbra. The threat of Umbral Storms further
complicates matters, as a werewolf can be blown far off course
and never find his path again.

Spirit Gates

Some Realms have direct connections to other, usually
related Realms. These connections are called Spirit Gates.
They are usually well-marked, appear quite dramatic, such as
sparkling pools of water or swirling clouds of energy that fill
an ivory arch. A Spirit Gate provides instant transportation;
however, they are usually well-guarded. Werewolves seek
permission if possible before using a Spirit Gate, else they risk
making powerful enemies.


Spirit Tracks

Garou can travel the Umbra by following the airts used
by spirits. These tracks have no guardians nor are they
well-marked. Airts only last for a short time, and they may
dissolve beneath a Garou traveling them. If a Garou is lost
in the Umbra, her best bet is to find a spirit and convince

it to lead her to her destination.

Other Paths

There are many other potential methods of
traveling through the Umbra

to another. The Pattern Web it-

avoid the Pattern Spiders. Some

werewolves believe that there is a way to ride the fierce
winds of the Storm Umbra, but risk being swept to parts
unknown or torn to bits by the power of the storms. Many
other means of travel exist, but a Garou should carefully
weigh the potential dangers of little-used methods of travel.

Peeking

Most beings can't see what happens on the other side of

the Gauntlet. Even though every living being has a spirit,
most spirits of the living are in a deep sleep. Only people and
animals with Awakened, active spirits, such as mages or sha-
mans, cast shadows across the Gauntlet.
To see into the physical world from the Umbra, a player
makes a Gnosis roll (difficulty of the local Gauntlet). If he
succeeds, a shadowy version of the mundane world replaces the
Garou's vision of the Umbra. The werewolf normally can't see fine
detail, but can make out images

taring across the Gauntlet is like

looking up at something from underwater. You can see objects, but
as ripples move across the surface
Garou's player scores four or mort

images become distorted. If a
ses, the surface of the Gaunt-

Succ!

let calms and the Garou can see clearly enough to read a wanted poster
or count the beads on a necklace. In addition, the werewolf can smell
and hear goings-on in the physical world.


The downside of spying on the physical world from the Umbra
is that a Garou becomes completely unaware of the spirit world. A
werewolf might see the camp of a bandit gang, but miss the Banes
gathering on its fringes. Spirits that manifest in the physical world
are visible, but the werewolf cannot perceive spirits that are wholly
in the Umbra. Tosee from the Realm to the Umbra requires a Gift.

Most spirits can Peek at will. They can switch their perceptions
without rolls being made, and those that interact regularly with the
material world often do so habitually.

Spirits

A vast number of spirits walk, ride or fly across the Umbra.
They range from powerful and noble creatures who stride across
realms like gods, to pitiful wisps bobbing on Umbral winds like
tumbleweeds. Spirits don't bleed, and they don't understand life
the way physical creatures do. Some spirits have connections to
beings in the material world, while others are unique to the
Umbra. No one really knows how many different types of spirits
dwell out in the unexplored reaches of the Umbra, and no words
can truly describe the nature of spirits.

The one certainty about spirits is that they follow a
hierarchy. Big spirits breed little spirits. Little spirits create
smaller spirits. In some ways, spirits are like ideas. The large

powerful spirits are the overarching concepts, and the little
spirits are the picky details. The more powerful spirits are
patrons of the lesser spirits, which means that if you mess
with enough little spirits, you'll eventually run into some-
thing tougher and nastier. A few Garou say that some spirits
exist outside the normal order of things; those spirits don't
have patrons. This world certainly has enough drifters to
give that idea credence

Shamans regard Gaia as the greatest spirit or as the
mother of all spirits, but as somehow more than just a spirit
herself. Just below Gaia is the Triat. Beneath the Triat are
the Celestines, roughly the equivalents of gods, if you want
to put the power of the first four into perspective. Then you
get the Incarna, the chieftains and presidents of the Umbra.
Below the Incarna are Jagglings and Gafflings, the powerful

warriors and the expendables.

Into this mix comes the Storm Eater, probably somewhere
between Celestine and Incarna. The only thing certain is that
it is ancient and powerful. The Storm Eater breeds its own
progeny, dangerous minions with both Weaver and Wyrm
abilities. Those spirits scream across the Penumbra, twisting
and devouring all they cross, leaving ghost towns, madness and
pain in their wake.

Examples of individual spirits can be found in the Appendix.


Gaia

The Litany holds that: All are of Gaia. The most powerful of
spirits, she makes up all of the Tellurian. The good, the bad, the
indifferent: all belong to Gaia, even if a few werewolves suggest that
only theirdefinitions of good things belong to Gaia. Gaiais the spirit
of Earth, and the spirit of all living things within the Tellurian. She
loves all She has created, no matter what they may have done with
their lives. Gaia's love hasno limits. Life isa celebration of Gaia, and
death is just a transformation of Her spiritual energy.

Many native werewolves snarl that the Europeans have
weakened Gaia. They point to industry and invention as tools
of the Weaver that restrain Her life energy. They see the
violence of the Wyrm tearing at Gaia's soul. On the frontier,
the Uktena and Wendigo view their protectorates as the last
bastions of Gaia. As the pioneers sweep across the West, the
mists of the Uktena are swept aside, and their creators howl
with rage and anger at the loss of the Pure Lands. However,
many European elders say that the Garou of the Americas have
been removed from their brethren for too long, and only
through unity between all the tribes can Gaia survive.

=
The Triat

Although Gaia unifies all spirits, the Triat is the shaper of
existence. The forces of the Weaver, the Wyld, and the Wyrm
define the Tellurian through their constant interactions. They
are distant entities with no regard for anything they warp or
destroy. Unlike living creatures, they have few feelings; they
are raw power given form. The members of the Triat have a
purpose, a meaning to give them direction, but few would say
they have any real understanding of their actions or the effects.

The Wyld

The Wyld sparks creation. It represents change in its purest
form; more than maddening chaos, it is just as much a dynamic
force of transformation. The Wyld is possibility and potential,
for good or ill. Every living being contains a bit of the Wyld,
which allows creatures to make choices and gives them a small
amount of freedom. No shaman or Theurge has ever been able
to measure or control that spark of Wyld, but they know it is
there. The Wyld gives life to Gaia. Without it, She could not
survive. Nothing is possible without the Wyld.

The Wyld flickers like an eternal flame, never shining nor
moving exactly the same way. It isa forge reshaping all it touches.
Due to its nature, the Wyld has little personality or motivation. It
stays in constant motion, changing all aspects of its being with
each passing moment. Although the Wyld is a whole being, it
cannot maintain its creations without the aid of the Weaver. The
Weaver needs the Wyld to provide it with creations to fix into the
structure of reality. The Wyrm once used the Wyld to reforge what
it destroyed, maintaining the balance of the universe.

The Wyld is the strongest member of the Triat in the Deep
Umbra, where laws hold no sway over actions. But in the
physical realm, the Wyld has much less influence. Logic and

© 4

reason force possibility out of the world and impose limits that
keep the Wyld distant. In the Storm Umbra, the Wyld rages to
keep its ties to the Realm. The Savage West is one of the last
places where the Weaver's philosophy and science have not
forced the Wyld into the Umbra. The frontier binds the Wyld,
for it is the essence of change. Each day sees new possibilities.
Large tracts of land remain unexplored, and many regions are
not yet mapped by humanity. As Indian tribes, ranchers,
homesteaders and others try to cope with the changing West,
the Wyld gains power. However, most Garou see the surge of
Wyldenergy on the frontier as feeding the bloated Storm Eater,
giving it more power and strength as the monstrous Weaver-
Bane continues to devour Wyld energies to sustain itself.

Many Garou (especially the Black Furies) venerate the
Wyld. They see the Wyld as the power of change and
transformation, the fire of life, and the hope of a glorious
future. All dreams become possible with the Wyld. Werewolves
whosee dark times ahead as their numbers dwindle and magic
drains out of the world look to the Wyld for salvation. If
anything is possible with the Wyld, then even an outnum-
bered, overmatched and desperate pack can hope that its
actions might change the world and set Gaia aright. Al-
though the Wyld provides no direct aid to the Garou, the
hope and inspiration that werewolves draw from it may be
their strongest weapon in the struggles to come.

The Weaver

The Weaver spins creation into forms. From the turbulent
Wyld comes potential, which the Weaver works into the fabric
of the universe. It adds these newborn things to the Pattern
Web, fixing possibilities long enough to make them realities.
The Pattern Web holds the universe together. The Weaver
brings meaning to the Tellurian. Growth and progress are
possible. Infinity gives way to eternity. Order replaces chaos.

In the First Times, the Wyrm destroyed pieces of the
Pattern Web, preventing stagnation. Like a careful farmer,
the Wyrm took the weeds from the fields of the Weaver's
creation and tilled them back into the soil of the Wyld. In
this way, the Wyrm achieved the balance between the
Weaver and the Wyld. The cycle of the cosmos began, and
all things lived in a time of glory.

The cycle broke when the Weaver gained consciousness.
No living being can say for certain what happened to end
that golden age. According to some myths, the Weaver
looked fully on the Wyld and went insane when confronted
with endless chaos and imperfection. Others believe that
the Weaver looked on its own Pattern Web and saw no
purpose, no meaning within its strands. Some believe that
Weaver entangled and trapped the Wyrm within its webs,
where the Wyrm remains, struggling to free itself and de-
stroying creation from within. Some European Garou use
these myths to show parallels between the Uktena's
imprisonment of the Storm Eater and the Weaver's
imprisonment of the Wyrm, blaming the Uktena 2

Werewolf: The Wild West


for the current strength of the Storm Eater. This argument
all too often becomes a justification for seizing Uktena
caerns and driving the Pure Ones from their homelands.

Other Garou hold that the Weaver isn't merely insane, but
a calculating, mad creature intent on subjugating all things to
its desires. Many believe that the Weaver attempted to change
the order of things by imposing logic and causes for all actions.
The Wyrm was a threat to the Weaver's laws, and so the
Weaver attacked and bound the Wyrm, attempting to destroy
it through logic and causes. The Wyrm retains a bit of its own
free will and destroys creation in a blind pattern of its own
making. This random destruction weakens the Weaver, but in
the process harms Gaia.

A few Garou defend the Weaver, blaming the Wyrm for
breaking the cycle. They argue that the Wyrm was respon-
sible for the balance and failed in its duties. Some say that
the Wyrm tried to destroy the Wyld by turning the Weaver
against it. When the Weaver went insane, the Wyrmybe-
came the victim of its own plans.

world, the Weaver now wields id th
other members of the Triat. Stagni

The Wyrm

The third member of the Triat is the were'
enemy: the Wyrm. In the First Times, the.
balance between the chaos of the Wyld and
Weaver. It destroyed anything that threate
and kept the cycle of life in motion. Lik@™
Wyrm was deadly, but quick to give warnin,

When the Wyrm fell into the Pattern Web, it was bound
by the limits of the Weaver's reality. The Wyrm shattered
into three distinct but mad personalities, each one a de-
mented fount of corruption. The Garou name these three
heads of the Wyrm Beast-of-War, Eater-of-Souls and the
Defiler Wyrm. The Wyrm's madness prevents the Pattern
Web from calcifying it completely, but madness also drives
the Wyrm to tear at itself and the world around it with no
understanding of balance, only a desire for destruction. It is
a snake poisoned by its own fangs.

The Wyrm spawns decay, desolation and corruption. It
lacks the power to bring cleansing fires down upon cre-
ation, so it twists and corrupts all it touches, leading things
down the path of self-destruction. Those who are weak in
spirit fall victim to its power.

The Wyrm has many servants. It reaches into the

dark places that dwell within all beings. It whis-
& pers to them, tempts them and leads them to

destruction. Like a plague, the Wyrm's corruption spreads
through the Realm and the Umbra. Theurges can often see
the Wyrm's taint in the wasted eyes of alcoholic Pure Ones,
in the frenzied eyes of greedy gold-miners and in the slick
expressions of railway overseers and snake-oil salesmen.
Although the Wyrm isn't the source of all evil, every tainted
deed feeds it a little more.

Now hell-bent on its path of madness and corruption, the
Wyrm will not stop until all reality is twisted to its whims.
Once the corruption is complete, the Wyrm will free itself
of the Pattern Web and destroy the befouled creation — all
of the Tellurian.

The Storm Eater

Tales of the Storm Eater haven't been sung for centuries, out
of fear that the naming might awaken the powerful spirit. The
Storm Eater was imprisoned in ages past by the Garou of the
Pure Lands. The Uktena, Croatan and Wendigo created a
great net with the energy of their caerns to trap the Bane.

as their caerns were left untended. The
Banes and Weaver-spirits brought

ieve that the Storm Eater is an avatar of
ger of the Eater-of-Souls. Others fear that
is a more ancient force, a long-forgotten
enemy Of Gaia from the First Times. Still others say that the
Storm Eater is the first spirit of Gaia's Rage, a misbegotten
child driven insane by the fury that birthed it.

Most werewolves figure that the Storm Eater dwells high
in the Umbra above the Courts of the Spirits. Umbral winds
accompanied by fearsome thunder and spiritual lightning
rage across the Penumbra of the Pure Lands, warping and
destroying all who get caught in them. Nothing seems to
escape the wrath of the Storm Eater. Pattern spiders shatter
to crystal. Banes bleed dark ichor while Umbral winds tear
their pus-filled hides from their bones. Wyldlings are sucked
into the storms, empowering the disruptions with their own
spiritual energy.

Theurges say that the worst has yet to come. The Storm
Eater expends most of its anger high in the Umbra among the
tealms of the Incarna, but will turn its full fury on the Realm
before the close of the century. They say it will seek vengeance
on those who trapped it for so long: the Garou.

Celestines

The Celestines are spirits just beneath Gaia and the Triat in
the hierarchy of the cosmos. The most revered of the Celestines
are Luna, the spirit of the moon, and Helios, the spirit of the
sun. Other Celestines include ancient spirits once wor-
shipped as gods by early man, and Avatars of the Triat. The
Garou know little of the Celestines or their motives. Most
Celestines are believed to have their own realms in the
Umbra, where they are all-seeing and all-powerful.

When Celestines choose to appear, they send Avatars to
speak to Garou. The Avatars are portions of the Celestines
that the Garou can relate to and understand. A Celestine's
true form is unimaginable; it is only through an Avatar that
communication between a Celestine and a mortal can take
place. For instance, if a great Celestine of Thunder wishes
to speak toa Wendigo, it shapes an Avatar into the likeness
of the Thunderbird so that the werewolf can comprehend
the Thunder-spirit.

A Celestine simply wills an Avatar into existence.
Celestines have as many active Avatars as they desire.

an entity less powerful than another Celestine. Celestines
have no Traits in Werewolf: The Wild West; they are of a
power that defies game terms. Celestines are limited only by
the Storyteller's good judgment.

Incarna

The Incarnaare the leaders of the legions of spirits that revere the
Celestines as patrons. Incarna have their own desires, powers and
goals. Some dwell in their own Domains, while others rule over
Domainsgiventothem by Celestines. Even iftheirpatron Celestines
are destroyed or devoured, the Incama survive. These mighty spirit-
lords also gain Gnosis and change their purposes. In the past,
some Incarna are said to have risen to the rank of Celestine.

Totem spirits are Incarna, and most are servants of Gaia. They
create many Avatars to interact with werewolves. Pack totems are
totem Avatars, containing a fraction of the power of an Incarna.
Totem Avatars are roughly the equivalents of Jagglings.

Like Celestines, Incarna wield so much power that they are
unaffected by any direct actions taken against them, although
this doesn't hold for their Avatars. A few legends speak of
werewolves who have encountered the true forms of Incarna

Celestine Avatars cannot be harmed or affected in any way by _ and gained great wisdom.

The members of the Wendigoand Uktena tribes cannot agree on how to face the threat of the Storm
Eater. Their Galliards howl songs about how the great Bane devoured Wyldlings until it grew too large
and powerful for any single pack, sept or even tribe to defeat it. Only through the efforts of the Croatan,
Wendigo and Uktena working together throughout their septs were they able to imprison it.

Now, the Wyrmcomers control the caerns needed to entrap the Storm Eater once again. Some
native werewolves want to attack the stolen caerns and take them back, but the Theurges know
that such a course would only weaken all Garou. There are not enough members of the Wendigo
and Uktena to defeat the Storm Eater as they did before, and the Storm Eater has grown more
powerful as strange Weaver-spirits have joined to its essence. The native Garou know that they
do not understand the Weaver these days, or how to fight its modern minions. They blame their
ignorance for allowing the spirit world of their Pure Lands to become the Storm Umbra.

However, if the Uktena and Wendigo do not try and reclaim their caerns, they may never get
them back. If they work with the Wyrmcomers, they may lose more land and traditions to their
invading cousins. It is a conflict of duty. Is it more important to fight the Storm Eater or to defend
their Kinfolk and heritage, even their very tribal identities? The Pure Ones have made many
sacrifices for Gaia, but many do not wish to live in the world that the Europeans are building. Many
native Garou have decided to join with the Wyrmcomers "for the greater good of defeating the
Storm Eater, but they keep their claws sharp and their eyes on their ‘allies'."
