Jagglings

These are the servants of Incarna, and rarely Celestines, who
directly interact with Garou. They form from the spiritual
essences of their patrons. Most Jagglings are completely loyal to
their patrons, and most do not have free will. More advanced
Jagglings create Gafflings as servants. Jagglings teach some Gifts
toGarou, and the most powerful fetishes contain bound Jagglings.
Jagglings have some vestiges of an individual personalities, but
their purpose is inextricably tied up with that of their patron.

Gafflings

The weakest spirits are the Gafflings. These spirits have
little to no sentience. They serve their masters, who generally
think less of the Gafflings than humans think of animals, as
single-minded tools. Many Garou use Gafflings to forge fe-
tishes. Gafflings carry simple messages through the Umbra on
the orders of their patrons. Patron spirits commonly have
complete control over their Gafflings. Most of these underlings
welcome this control; they feel safest as members of a group.
Geafflings with full intelligence are considered dangerous, and
are suspected of serving mysterious and powerful patrons, such
as Celestines. Gafflings can teach Gifts to Garou; they are the
spirits that werewolves most commonly encounter.

== =
Spirit Traits
Spirits don't have the same Traits as physical beings; they
have no Attributes or Abilities. Power is a Trait unique to
spirits, which measures the mystical energy thata spirit can tap.
Charms are also the special magics of the spirits, roughly the
equivalent of Gifts. Spirits do have Willpower, Rage

and Gnosis just as Garou, but they are more defined
by these Traits, and use them for different purposes.

Willpower

Spirits use Willpower to take "physical" actions, such as
chasing other spirits or attacking an enemy. Conflicts between
spirits are resolved with opposed Willpower rolls.

Difficulty Action
3 Easy action
5 Fairly simple action
6 Normal action
8 Difficult action
10 Virtually impossible action

As with Garou, Rage measures a spirit's anger and inner
turmoil. By unleashing its Rage, a spirit damages other spirits
and even physical beings that are in the Umbra. For each
success on a Rage roll, a spirit inflicts one Health Level of
damage, or forces a spirit target to lose one point of Power.

Gnosis

Spirits use Gnosis for all Social and Mental rolls. They may
also use Gnosis to transform the parameters of a situation; for
instance, for a Water-spirit to convince a werewolf to relax a
bit and sit in the shade, a roll of Gnosis needs to performed.

Power

Spirits don't use their own Gnosis to invoke their magics.
They are composed of spiritual energy and could cease to exist
if they drained themselves to do battle. Instead, they store
mystical energy harvested from the Umbra. This supply of
energy is called a spirit's Power. Spirits deplete their supply by
taking damage and using Charms. When a spirit's Power
reaches zero, the spirit dissipates into the Umbra for a number
of hours equal to 20 minus its Gnosis, after that it Reforms with
a single point of Power. Optionally, a Garou who knows the
proper rite can bind a defeated spirit into a fetish; however, the
fetish is not usable until the spirit's Power is replenished
(through Slumbering within the fetish; see below). A Garou
who reduces a spirit's Power to zero may also elect to siphon up
to five points of Gnosis from the spirit to refill her own Gnosis.
This kills a spirit with five or less Gnosis points — a serious
offense to the spiritual community.

Charms

Every spirit has special powers, known as Charms. Most
Charms require the expenditure of Power to use. Most Charms
last for one scene. Combat-oriented Charms, however, last one
turn per use.

Common Charms

w Airt Sense: Most spirits can find their way through the
airts (trails) of the Umbra without much difficulty. It costs one
Power to find any particular thing. This Charm is useless during
Umbral storms.

Chapter Seven: The Storm Umbra


‘ Armor: Some spirits are tougher than others. This Charm
allows a spirit to have one soak die per Power point spent. This
Charm is the only way a spirit can soak damage. Spirits may use
this Charm at any time ina combat turn before damage is rolled.

‘w Healing: Spiritual energy renews material beings. The spirit
spends one Power point per Health Level to heal normal damage,
or three points per Health Level to heal aggravated damage.

‘W Materialize: A spirit with this Charm has the ability to form
a body and enter the physical world. The spirit's Gnosis must be
greater than orequal tothe Gauntlet where it wants tomaterialize.
When the spirit materializes, it spends Power to create its form
and buy that body's Traits. The spirit's player still rolls Gnosis for
Social and Mental actions. The Power point costs are as follows:
Power Cost Trait

1 Per one Physical Attribute dot
Per two Ability dots
Seven initial Health Levels (for the body)
Per additional Health Level after seven
(extra Health Levels increase the spirits size)

1 Per one Health Level healed (damage
regenerated for the physical form)

1 Weaponry: Per die of aggravated damage done
in addition to Strength (Bite is one die, each
claw is one, etc.)

Spirit forms have no limits on their Attribute and Ability
levels; with enough Power, anything is possible.

For example: A Buffalo-spirit wants to materialize into the
physical world to kill a hunter's horse. The spirit spends 17
Power for the following characteristics: Strength 5, Dexterity
4, Stamina 3, Brawl 5, Dodge 3, and seven Health Levels.

A spirit's physical form lasts as long as it likes, but it cannot
enter an area with a higher Gauntlet rating than its Gnosis. A
materialized spirit may not recharge its Power unless it pos-

sesses a special Charm thatallows it to doso. Ifaspirit's physical
form is attacked, the damage applies to the form's Health

Levels. A spirit reduced to zero Health Levels dissipates into
the Umbra and may not materialize to create a new body
for (20 hours minus its Gnosis).

Any aggravated damage aspirit suffers is applied
to Health Levels and Power. A spirit cannot enter the
yy physical world and always escape unscathed.

‘w Reform: Mostspirits use this Charm to escape from enemies. 7/
ao The Charm allows them to dissipate into the Umbra and
Reform somewhere else, usually a great distance
a away. A spirit must spend 20 Power to use Reform.

Specialty Charms
‘w Agony: The spirit may incapacitate a victim by
magnifying its pain. The target incurs double Dice Pool
penalties from its wounds. If the victim tries to take an action
witha Dice Pool smaller than the penalty, she burnsa Willpower
point tomake the attempt (and she has one die for the attempted
action). The duration is one round; the Power cost is three.


‘@ Blast Flame: The spirit has the ability to launch flame at
opponents. This Charm costs two Power per die of damage.

‘@ Break Reality: The spirit can disrupt the reality of an
object and modify the target's Umbral form. It's player must
make a Gnosis roll. The extent of the attempted change and
how plausible the transformation is determines the difficulty.
The number of successes determines how much of a change is
possible. A botch on this roll causes the spirit to lose a Gnosis
point as reality backlashes against it. The Power cost ranges
from two to 10 points (depending on the difficulty).

‘e Calcify: Weaver spirits are the only ones who can call
on this Charm. Calcify binds the target into the Pattern Web.
The Storyteller rolls the spirit's Willpower against the target's
Rage; each success subtracts one dot from the victim's Physi-
cal Attributes (or Willpower in the case of spirits). When
Attributes or Willpower fall to zero, the Pattern Web en-
snares the target, holding it forever. Allies of the victim can
shatter the Pattern Web (rescuers must score a number of
damage successes with attack or Rage rolls equal to the
successes of the Weaver spirit) to release the victim. The
Power cost is two.

‘# Cleanse the Blight: This Charm purges spiritual cor-
ruption in its vicinity. It is similar to the Rite of Cleansing.
Power cost is 10.

‘w Create Fires: This Charm creates small fires with a
Gnosis roll. The difficulty depends on the size of the desired
blaze (3 for a small campfire, 9 for a large-scale prairie
wildfire). The Power cost varies from one to five points.

‘w Create Wind: This Charm allows a spirit to call forth
winds. Power cost varies with the strength of the winds. A
breeze costs only one point, while a twister costs 20. Several
spirits can combine the effects of this Charm, although a
tornado is the most powerful force Create Wind may manifest.

"# Disorient: Wyldlings may completely alter landmarks and
directions with a successful Gnosis roll against a difficulty of 6 or
the Gauntlet, whichever is higher. The Charm costs two Power.

4# Domain Sense: Many spirits are tied to an earthly
domain, such as a forest, a stream or a mountain. This Charm
allows them to sense all that takes place within their sacred
domains. The Power cost is 10.

4 Flood: The spirit calls upon the waters in an area to rise,
creating great floods. This Power cost is five for each one-mile
radius. Affected spirits who use this Charm run the risk of
drawing the attention of water spirits.

@ Freeze: This Charm grants a spirit the ability to lower the
temperature in its immediate area. The cost is three Power points
for every 10-yard radius, and three additional Power points per
die of damage; anyone in the area suffers the damage. The first
three points reduce the temperature in a 10-foot yard area to
freezing; each additional three points reduces the temperature by

an additional 10 degrees Fahrenheit and adds a damage
die. Desert or Heat-spirits don't look kindly on those

= who use this Charm in their domains.

Chapter Seven: The Storm Umbra

‘v Frozen Breath: The spirit exhales a breath of chilling
frost. This Charm costs one Power point per die of damage.

‘w Ice Shards: The spirit flings shards of ice at a target. The
cost of this Charm is five Power per three dice of damage.

"w Lightning Bolts: The spirit produces bolts of lightning
and hurls them at an enemy. The Power cost is two per die of
damage inflicted.

"# Open Moon Bridge: This Charm gives the spirit the
ability to open a Moon Bridge to a desired location. A caern
does not have to be present. The maximum distance is 1000
miles. This Charm costs five Power.

‘w Shapeshift: With this Charm, a spirit assumes any form
it desires. It only gains the appearance of the form, not any of
the natural (or supernatural) abilities or powers. The cost for
this Charm is five.

‘@ Solidify Reality: Only Weaver-spirits possess this Charm.
Solidify Reality gives a Weaver-spirit the ability to spin the
Pattern Web, and reinforce the order of the Weaver upon the
Umbra. To use this Charm, the Weaver-spirit must make a
Willpower roll. With a successful roll, the spirit can erect a
practically indestructible barrier. The difficulty depends on the
plausibility and extent of the creation; it is a lot harder to spin a
Pattern Web ina wide-open desert than in St. Louis. The number
of successes determines the solidity of the Web. Power costs range
from one to 20 depending on the magnitude of the task.

Success makes the object or spirit stronger. An object's
effective "Health Levels" increase by one per success. The
effect lasts for one day. Each spirit may only use this Charm
once per day per target.

4 Spirit Wall: Weaver spirits can increase the Gauntlet in
an area by one. The Power cost is 10.

Tear the Gauntlet: Some Wyld spirits can break the
Pattern Web. This Charm lets spirits reduce the Gauntlet in an
area by one for a number of days equal to the spirit's Gnosis
(this Charm is often responsible for the emergence of new
Broken Lands). Power cost is 15.

w Tracking: The spirit can track its prey unerringly, no
matter where it flees. The Power cost is five.

sw Umbraquake: This Charmallows aspirit to cause the fabric
of the Umbra to convulse violently. The resulting Umbraquake
throws everything standing to the ground. Everyone within the
area of effect suffers damage from the force. The cost is five Power
per 10 foot radius, and two Power per die of aggravated damage.

4 Updraft: The spirit can lift a man into the air. Power cost
is three.

‘w Blighted Touch: This Charm lets a Bane deliver the inflict
of Blight onto a target. If the spirit successfully strikes its target, it
forces the victim's player to make a Willpower roll. Failure
unleashes the dark side of the victim's personality until the next
sunrise orsunset. A botchon the Willpower roll means the twisted
personality takes over — permanently. The Power cost is two.

While aspirit possesses its victim, it retreats toa dark section
of the Umbra, such as a Blight, and remains there, doing
nothing but concentrating on the possession. If the spirit takes
any other action (such as combat), the Charm's effect fails.
Banes surround themselves with others of their ilk as protec-
tion while they attempt possession.

Once the attempt succeeds, the Bane passes fully into its
host body. A Bane that posse: victim manifests powers and
abilities through the victim's body. Such possessed humans are
known as mockeries or fomori.

Recharging Power

Asa spirit loses Power, its form starts to shimmer and fade.
If it has been attacked, its wounds will be visible on its form.

To refresh its Power, the spirit must fall into a sort of
hibernation called Slumber. The spirit seeks out a safe place in
the Umbra and falls intoadeep sleep. For each hour that passes,
the spirit regains a point of Power.

While aspirit isin Slumber, itis relatively defenseless. Ritescan
easily bind the spirit regardless of its strength. If a spirit is bound
into a fetish, it enters Slumber and does not fully awaken until

released; the usercalls
on the powers of the fe-
tish, not the spirit. A new
fetish will not work until the
spirit within has regained its
full Power (which may take a
longtime with powerful spir-
its.) In rare cases, a spirit
may havesome degree of
\, consciousness while
) bound into a fetish.

Movement

Spirits may fly or float
through the Umbra. Some say
spirits swim through the Umbra like fish in the
ocean. Spirits may move up to 20 yards plus their
» Willpower a tum. In difficult conditions, such
‘@ a Umbral Storms or strange Realms, spirits
move less quickly; in fact, they may wind up

tossed about, depending on how strong or weak they
are. Storytellers should use their own discretion.

The Umbra is not a place of exact measure,
* however, and Storytellers should feel free to warp
space as they see fit. Naturally, the Penumbra is
*¢ roughly analogous to the physical world and distances
5 do remain constant there (one yard is one yard). But
when one wanders into the deeper sections of the spirit

@°" world, or when distorting Umbral winds sweep across the

Middle Lands or Penumbra, nothing is quite so certain.

Communication

Spirits have their own tongue, which they only teach to
worthy Garou (in the form of the Gift: Spirit Speech). Spirits
do not use voices to communicate; they share their thoughts
and feelings through the Umbra. The language of spirits is more
a way of understanding others than making oneself under-
stood. Some say this tongue came into being at the birth of the
world, before the Weaver spun words into being.

Spirits with ties to living beings may speak the tongues of
those to whom they are tied. Totem spirits speak the languages
of those who give them respect; for example, Uktena has no
difficulties conversing in Cherokee, nor does Stag have trouble
with Gaelic. Spirits do not always choose to speak the lan-
guages of others, though; many proud spirits refuse to use the
languages of physical creatures that they feel are crude and poor
ways of expressing meaning.

Garou Gifts that allow spirit control or interaction do not
require knowledge of spirit tongue in order to work; the same
tule applies to rites. The inherent nature of rites and Gifts
allows them to function magically.

Werewolf: The Wild West


Fetishes

A fetish is an object that contains the bound essence of a
spirit. The spirit's abilities are at the wielder's disposal. Natu-
rally, the fetish's powers are limited by the abilities of the spirit;
there's no point in beseeching a Fire-spirit to quench your
thirst in the desert.

Fetishes are crafted from the purest natural objects, and are
usually small enough to hold in one hand. Fetishes are often
decorated with symbols and objects that represent the spirit or
do homage to the spirit. Some fetishes are weapons, like knives
and spears; others take the forms of blankets, pouches, or even
dream-catchers or saddles.

All fetishes are sacred because they contain the essence of
spirits. Garou treat them with the utmost respect, and they
protect such items. Minions of the Wyrm often try to capture
and corrupt fetishes; what's more, they fabricate their own
Bane fetishes from the Wyrm's corrupt children.

A Garou must use a Rite of the Fetish to create one of these
items. A fetish almost always requires a willing spirit. Those
who force spirits to become parts of fetishes never fully control
the items they have made. A spirit will rebel in any way
possible, and such an item is considered cursed.

To use a fetish, a Garou must attune himself to the spirit
within the object. He does so by making a Gnosis roll against
a difficulty of the fetish's own Gnosis score. As long as the
Garou scores a single success, he is attuned to the fetish. If he
fails the roll, he may not use or become attuned to the fetish.
If he wishes to try again, he must first find a way to make an
accord with the bound spirit. The Storyteller determines an
appropriate act of respect.

For example, Windchaser climbs into the ruins of an
Anasazi cliff dwelling. As she clambers up the narrow rock
steps toa wall precariously balanced on the edge of the cliff, she
discovers an unexpected treasure, a War Bow, lying discarded
on the rocks. In a rush of excitement, Windchaser grabs the
weapon, claiming it for her own. However, her player fails her
Gnosis roll. The War Bow twists out of her hand and clatters
to the ground. Windchaser realizes that she must pay respect
to the people who once lived in this place and to the warrior
who fell using the weapon (if the War Bow is to accept her).

She kneels on the edge of the cliff and whispers a song of
praise to the ancient ones. She searches the site for symbols
and images of protective spirits. She pays her respects to each
dwelling and to the protective spirits. Finally, she kneels
before the War Bow and offers her respect, asking it for help

against the mockeries that menace her pack. Her player makes
her Gnosis roll, using a point of Willpower, just to be certain.
When she grasps the War Bow again, it feels as if it belongs in
herhand. If the Storyteller issuitably impressed by Windchaser's
actions, he can reduce the difficulty of the Gnosis roll.

Once a Garou has attuned herself to a fetish, she has an
empathic bond with its spirit. The attuned fetish travels with
her anywhere in the Tellurian (as if Dedicated to her; see the
Rite of Talisman Dedication, pg. 173). She also gains a basic
knowledge of the fetish's powers. Depending on the strength of
the spirit, the relationship between the Garou and the fetish
may continue to develop. More powerful spirits do not Slumber
as fully as weaker spirits, and may communicate with Garou
allies. Some may even make demands of wielders or pass on
secret knowledge. A Garou should go to great lengths to care
for a valuable fetish.

Each time the wielder wishes to use one of the fetish's

powers, her player must roll Gnosis against a difficulty of the
fetish's Gnosis. If the player wishes, Gnosis point may be spent
to activate the power without a roll.
Specific types of fetishes or talens may only be created using
spirits aligned with the items' intent. For example, a spirit of
healing will not willingly enter a klaive or other weapon of war.
Most fetish spirits are Gafflings.

Talens

Talens are simple fetishes. They contain the living essence of
spirits, and require Gnosis rolls to activate (but not to attune),
but may only be used once. After a talen is used, the spirit is
released back into the Umbra, and the object loses its potency.
Unlike fetishes, which may only be used by those attuned to
them, talens may be use by anyone (even non-Garou). The Rite
of Binding may create a talen by forcing a spirit into an object
after successfully defeating it in spiritual combat. Even the most
spiritually oriented elders do not consider binding spirits into
talens immoral, because talens are only temporary creations, and
the spirits are not be bound for long.

Atalen receives Gnosis equal to the bound spirit's Gnosis.
For each success received on a Rite of Binding roll, an extra
talen of the same type may be created. Talens are almost
always created with Gafflings, but more powerful spirits can
have their essences spread through multiple talens. A Jaggling
automatically adds two or three extra talens to the total.
There is no time limit to how long a spirit may be bound, but
the talen only has one use.

Sample fetishes and talens may be found in the Appendix.

Chapter Seven: The Storm Umbra 208



LE PR EL:

We should be careful to get out of an experience only the wisdom

that is in it — and stop there; lest we be like the cat that sits down on
ahot stove-lid. She will never sit down on a hot stove-lid again — and
that is well; but also she will never sit down on a cold one any more.

— Mark Twain

You've read all the background material, right? Well then,
let's get on to the business of the character and how she
interacts with the Savage West. A character's statistics are a
representation of what she is and what she is capable of doing,
but that's only the beginning. Once you've built your charac-
ter, he or she changes and learns in day-to-day life. There are
systems for handling these events and changes, and that's what
this chapter is all about.

Character Evolution

This chapter is about change. Watching your character
develop is, ina way, like watchinga child grow. The bonus here
is that there are no diapers to change, nor do you have another
mouth to feed. In Werewolf: The Wild West, however, there

are no guarantees that a character improves with time.

Chapter Bight: Systems

It's sort of like life: Appreciate the good times and do your best
to prepare for the bad. You can't anticipate every change that
your character will undergo. But that's for the best. If you knew
everything about the journey before youstarted, what would be
the point of going?

Character interaction with the world is broken down into
three sections: Character Development, Physical States, and
Mental States. Each focuses on a particular aspect of your
character's growth. Character Development pays special at-
tention to the physical ways your character changes and grows
stronger (or in some cases weaker) as time passes. It also
discusses Renown and how your Garou can rise and fall in
Rank. Physical States deals with injuries, how they affect your
character and how your character recovers from them. It also
deals with permanent injuries and death. Mental States details
systems for frenzy and the effects of the Delirium.

Character

Life is an ongoing series of experiences. Every experience
a Fouave changes you. Theres muchit beat Foreaintagwinton

‘ "a Wy) Anyone who moves through life without change is just existing, not
Be living. A lot of folks never catch on to the difference.
To reflect the way in which experience changes your character, the
Storyteller awards you experience points to spend on your character. These
points represent alterations to your character that result from roleplaying,
and even from what is assumed to happen to your character between
stories. Sure you learn how to fight as a result of running with your
pack and battling the Wyrm, buta werewolf who tends bar during the
day also partakes in a lot of social interaction. Another character
who works part-time for her father, the local undertaker, might
learn a great deal about human anatomy.
The Storyteller usually awards experience points at the end
of astory. Some things are harder to learn than others, though,
and improving yourself often requires maturation and some-
: times grueling exercise. The costs of increasing your character's
SN Traits therefore vary greatly. (See the Experience Chart for
\ specifics.) Costs are usually based on the current rating of an
} attribute, which is multiplied by a certain number. The more

to learn the intricacies that a master takes for granted.
Learning how to clean and bandage a wound is much easier
an learning how to properly amputate a gangrenous leg. A

Trait changes don't have to be based on a character's
successes, either. We learn from all our experiences. If a
character lives in the desert eating snakes and jackrabbits for
a week, she learns from the trial whether she went hungry
most of the time or lived comfortably.

Trait increases should reflect what occurs ina story, or what
is developed about a character's mundane life. Make both the
player and the character work for what the character learns. In
; doingso, you bringa sense of reality to the story. The more
realistic the story, the more powerful the storytelling

event, and the more the character develops as a person. &


Awarding
Experience Points

The Storyteller needs to maintain a balance when awarding
experience points. The numbers should not only reflect how the
players portray their characters, but should also be a check-and-
balance system that ensures no one character overpowers his
packmates. If Kyle's Shadow Lord is far stronger than John's Red
Talon when they've both been in the same number of stories and
both started at the same power level, something has gone wrong.

The following set of guidelines helps maintain experience
balance. Ultimately the Storytellerdecides how experience points
are allocated depending on what types of stories he and his players
want to tell, and at what pace they want the characters to grow.

End of Each Chapter

Give each character one to five experience points at the end
of each chapter or game session, based on the qualifications
discussed below. Everyone gets at least one point, whether the
pack succeeds at its mission or not. This minimal experience
award represents characters' development in everyday life.

‘@ One Point — Automatic: Each player gets one point
after every game session.

‘w One Point — Learning Curve: The character learns
somethingfrom his experiences during the chapter. Thisshouldn't
be awarded automatically; it should be awarded only if the
player can account for what the
character learned during the \
course of the session. \ Hi /

4@ One Point — Acting: The player roleplays well — not
only entertainingly, but appropriately. Award exceptional
roleplaying only; your standards should go up as the story
progresses. This point is awarded to only one or two players in
the group, the ones who do the best roleplaying.

w One Point — Concept: The player acts true to her
character's concept. In most cases, this has less to do with
accurately depicting personality traits and the like (the pur-
view of "Acting," above) and more to do with auspice roles. An
Ahroun who suffers a bout of cowardice and avoids a fight
qualifies for an "Acting" bonus, but isn't as true to his concept.

*W One Point — Heroism: When a character risks himself
for others — perhaps by scooping up a lit stick of dynamite and
carrying it out of amine where the other characters are trapped
— give him an experience point. Don't let the players abuse
your generosity; award for true courage, not for exceptional
acts of stupidity.

End of Each Story

At the end of each story, award one to three additional
experience points to each character to reflect what the charac-
ters learn over the long haul. These points are in addition to
what the characters earn after any chapter.

‘ One Point — Success: The pack is successful in its
mission. Perhaps it wasn'ta complete success or
a Pyrrhic victory, but even if half the
pack died in binding a Pestilence-
spirit forever, it's still
a victory.

SA 4)


‘w One Point — Danger: The character faced true peril and
lived to tell the tale.

‘¥ One Point — Wisdom: The character used his wits to
save the day, possibly coming up with the idea that made the
difference between success and failure.

Again, these are example experience points allocations. If you
feel that characters should receive fewer points, they are forced to
grow ata slower rate. If you want characters to become strong and
skilled quickly, increase the value of these experience conditions or
simply award points based on what you feel the characters deserve.

Spending Experience

There is a variety of things that players can spend their
experience points on.

Backgrounds

Background Traits can't be increased with the expenditure
of experience points. (The only exception to this rule is the
Totem Trait — see below.) Background Traits are left in the
hands of the Storyteller. The Backgrounds that players pur-
chase when they create their characters are merely what those
characters have at the beginning of the chronicle. Events may
disqualify Backgrounds or introduce new ones. For instance, a
starting character who has the Mentor Background might lose
her patron during the course of the chronicle, or an impover-
ished character might strike the mother lode.

Chronicle events and their effects on characters' Back-
grounds can even defy tribal restrictions, if only for a little
while. A Bone Gnawer who suddenly comes into money might
go on an extravagant spending spree for his tribe or sept, but
end up back in the gutter where he started.

The Storytellershould take note ofany changes inacharacter's
Backgrounds and have the player make the appropriate modifi-
cations. A player might ask if her Background Traits should
change, but this shouldn't happen very often (especially in the
case of Pure Breed, which is impossible to change).

The Storyteller may design a list of requirements for Back-
ground Trait changes. Characters must meet specifications for
each Background Trait before increasing score. To gain a new
Contact, for example, a character might have to meet the
individual and befriend him or perhaps buy his services.

| Experience Chart

214 Werewolf: The Wild West

Changes in Background ratings, for better or worse, should take
place during a story. Background Traits normally change one dot
at a time, but major events can lead to dramatic gains or losses.

The exception to these rules is the Totem Background. It can
be raised through experience points, but the players should still
roleplay the spirit's increase in power. A pack typically strength-
ens its patron spirit by heeding its Ban and offering it chiminage
from time to time. (Such chiminage takes the form of reciting
songs of praise, or making offerings appropriate to the totem's
nature — dedicating a portion of a kill to Fenris, for instance, or
presenting small, shiny objects to Raven.) Just as each playercan
chip in on the initial cost of a pack totem, each can also choose
to invest further experience points into the totem-spirit itself.

Gaining Rage

Players can buy permanent Rage with experience points.
This allows a player to spend more Rage during the course of a
story, and increases his character's chances of entering frenzy.
This can be justified by becoming more in touch with one's
warrior self, by witnessing horrible atrocities (which are com-
mon in the Savage West) or by losing loved ones.

Gaining Gnosis

Permanent Gnosis is purchased with experience points, in-
creasing characters' ability to step sideways, and strengthening
their spiritual connections with Gaia. Such an increase might be
rationalized with meditation, special insight or spiritual guidance.

Gaining Willpower

Players spend experience points to increase permanent Will-
power scores. However, gaining Willpower is moderated by the
Storyteller. The player doesn't simply add dots to his sheet; the
character must earn Willpower in the context of the chronicle. The
points are justified by a victory in a story, or used as inspiration for
a story unto themselves. The Storyteller might account for extra
Willpower points by sending a character on a quest to retrieve a
long-lost family heirloom, or by having her undergo the Sun Dance.

Characterscanalso lose permanent Willpower. A character loses
a point whenever she botches a Willpower roll. The only exception
to this rule is that characters cannot lose permanent Willpower
while using Gifts that must be activated with Willpower rolls.

Awarding Renown

Renown is the foundation of Garou society. Werewolves
cannot establish places for themselves within their societies nor
rise in tribe or sept hierarchy without increasing Renown. Re-
nown reflects other werewolves' opinions of a character. A
werewolf not only can improve his standing according to his
behavior in day-to-day life, he can also pursue status. Garou often
seek to make names for themselves.

The Storyteller awards three specific types of Renown:

Glory, Honor and Wisdom. If a werewolf slays a Wyrm

creature, he gains Glory. Ifhe preventsa violation of the i


Litany, he gains Honor. If he solves a riddle set forth by a spirit, he
gains Wisdom. Players can't choose where awarded Renown goes,
theStorytellerdecides. Likewise, aplayercan'texchangean award
of Glory for Wisdom Renown simply because his character needs
more Wisdom to increase in Rank. Renown reflects a character's
deeds. Ahroun naturally strive for Glory Renown over Wisdom,
while Theurges are more concerned with Wisdom. Players need
to pay attention to their characters' auspice roles and concepts if
they want their characters to rise in Rank quickly.

Whenever werewolves work together toaccomplish an act, they
share the Renown earned. A pack that brings peace to two warring
septs wins Honor for each member. The Storyteller is encouraged
to award an appropriate bonus to each werewolf, rather than divide
areward amongst the pack — if the pack is suitably rewarded for its
teamwork, it is more likely to work well together in the future.

Carrots and Sticks

Renown is awarded at the end of agame session orat the end
of a story, whichever the Storyteller prefers. In Garou society,
werewolves earn Renown at moots, where they are recognized
for their deeds. Storytellers can also award or revoke temporary
Renown during play. Use of temporary Renown makes for easy
bookkeeping, but requires that the Storyteller be familiar with
the Renown system so that play isn't interrupted while appro-
priate Renown awards are looked up.

G sos g
ainin
Temporary Renown

Temporary Renown is only awarded during play. It is indicated
on the character sheet by the squares listed under each Renown
type. One square represents one point of temporary Renown. A
character's temporary Renown is often higher than his perma-
nent. Temporary Renown isa simple means by which to measure
when acharacter is due for an increase in permanent Renown. As
atule of thumb, 10 temporary points of Renown are worth one
permanent dot. When temporary Renown is cashed in for perma-
nent, the character is reduced to zero temporary Renown.
Otherwise, temporary Renown has no direct effect on play.

Refer to the Renown Awards Chart at the end of this
chapter for sample Glory, Honor and Wisdom awards. This list
is in no way exhaustive, and should only be used as a guideline.

Gaining
Permanent Renown

In order for a character to increase her permanent Renown in
aspecific category, she must accumulate 10 points of temporary in
that category and then convince another Garou of equal or greater
Rank to perform the Rite of Accomplishment for her. (See Rite
of Accomplishment, pg. 177.) The assisting Garou cannot be a

member of the character's pack. In some cases, the character

can challenge another Garou to acknowledge her, in-
stead. Most, however, seek to gain esteem in a polite
= fashion, all the better to stay in their elders' good graces.

Losing Renown
Losing Temporary Renown

Just as Renown can be gained according to characters' deeds,
it can be lost as well. It's very easy to lose temporary Renown.
This loss is an effective means of reminding players that their
characters' actions have repercussions in Garou society. The
Storyteller should consider a character's breed, auspice and tribe
when deducting temporary Renown. For example, Ragabash
can get away with more extreme trespasses against Honor than
most characters — but if Ragabash perform honorable acts,
others usually suspect them of having ulterior motives.

The Storyteller decides when temporary Renown is lost. For
guidelines, see the Renown Awards Chart.

Losing Permanent Renown

Werewolves only lose permanent Renown when they violate
the fundamental rules of Garou society. A Garou who goes into
frenzy and actually feeds on a wolf or a human is likely to lose
permanent Renown. Offenses may be overlooked in extreme
cases. If the feeding werewolf was riddled with silver and fighting
agang of mockeries, he might be given a little more leeway. Even
if permanent Honor is not lost, temporary Honor probably is.

Werewolves who face the loss of permanent Renown can
contest, but must prove themselves in a series of trials. Success
convinces the elders that the shame was undeserved. The Story-
teller devises an appropriate ordeal for a penitent wretch, then
decides if the character handles himself well enough to avoid a
permanent Renown penalty. The Storyteller's decision is final.

Ifa character loses enough permanent Renown so that her score
falls below the minimum Renown required of her current Rank, she
is "demoted" and loses all benefits and privileges associated with her
former Rank. She does not lose any Gifts that she has learned, but is
forall practical purposes considereda Rank loweruntilshe restores her
name. To return to her former status, she must again earn the proper
amount of Renown; most septs require a fairly stringent task of
penitence as well before the werewolf challenges to regain lost Rank.

Advancing in Rank

To gain Rank a character needs permanent Renown points
and must win a challenge.

‘w Renown: The character must meet her auspice's mini-
mum requirements in permanent Renown to rise to the next
Rank. This isn't the only requisite for increasing Rank, just a
benchmark that the character has to meet before she's consid-
ered for advancement.

‘w Challenge: Once she has the necessary Renown, the
character must face a Garou of higher Rank ina contest of some
sort.and win. The challenged (the higher-ranking Garou)
picks the contest — a physical test, duel or test of wills or
gamecraft —and its difficulty. Tribes and septs have their own
standard contests for each Rank, but an elder can decide to
ignore the usual contests and devise something of her own. The

Chapter Eight: Systems 216


challenge can be as simple as a contest of dominance or as
difficult as rooting out and destroying an elder vampire. If the
character bests the elder in the ritual challenge or succeeds at
the elder's test, the elder is honor-bound to recognize the
character as an equal.

Should the character lose to the elder, she may not chal-
lenge that elder again. High-ranking elders seldom teach those
whom they have beaten, at least those who repeatedly attempt
to rise to the same Rank. The character loses no permanent
Renown for losing a challenge, but it's exceptionally poor form
to go out and challenge another elder immediately; most elders
will outright refuse any challenges from someone who's re-
cently failed his test of Rank.

Example of Gaining
Renown and Rank

Thunder's Howl is a Rank One Uktena Theurge with 1 Honor
and 4 Wisdom. If he gains one more permanent point in each of
Glory and Wisdom, he may challenge for the righttoeam Rank Two.

Aftera hard battle against an Arizona death-cult, Thunder's
Howl finally achieves 10 temporary Glory. To gain recogni-
tion, he asks an elder, Talking Snake, to perform the Rite of
Accomplishment for him. Talking Snake demands the tale of
the young Theurge's deeds, and after listening closely, deems
the youngster worthy of his claim. At the next moot, Talking
Snake performs the Rite of Accomplishment for Thunder's
Howl, who gains one permanent Glory Renown; he loses all
temporary Glory immediately, starting again at zero.

Inthe months that follow, Thunder's Howl dedicates himself to
hisauspice duties, summoning many spirits and beseeching them for
advice. He soon accumulates 10 temporary Honor Renown. As
before, he asks Talking Snake to recognize hisaccomplishment, and
the elder agrees. His Wisdom Renown rises to 5 at the next moot.
Thunder's Howl is now ready to attempt to rise in Rank.

However, Talking Snake advises the young Theurge to chal-
lenge another elder for his new Rank — in this case, Ten-Tails,
Wendigo Theurge. Wily old Ten-Tails agrees to the challenge.
She has done this many times, and quickly ascertains that the
young one is worthy. She doesn't like to make things easy for cubs,
though, and decides to test the Garou's worth for herself.

"Well, Thunder's Howl, the contest is mine to choose. Here,
take this cup of water. Now climb up that tree andsetit in the bird's
nest near the top — and don't spill a drop."

Thunder's Howl had hoped for a mental contest, and is some-
what dismayed that his perception skills are not tested. He isn't
certain of his ability to meet a physical challenge, but agrees
nonetheless. It takes patience and persistence to get the cup into the
nest, but after a grueling hour the Theurge climbs down in victory.

"Walk proudly with the blessings of your siblings, young
Fostern," Ten-Tails grins. "You're a worthy youngling, and your
tribe ishonored tohavea Cresent Moonsuchas youamong them."

Thunder's How lets loose a cry of mingled relief and pride.
Suddenly refreshed and reinvigorated, he thanks Ten-Tails
and lopes back to Talking Snake to share the good news.

Werewolf: The Wild West

SSSN

XX

Renunciation

Some werewolves, worried that they don't live up to their full
potential, change auspice. This is somewhat acceptable in the eyes of
the Garou, but has certain drawbacks. A werewolf who renounces her
auspice sacrificesall Rank. Additionally, she must chooseanew name,
and loses all but three permanent Renown. In order to complete the
transformation, the Garou performs, or has another perform, the Rite
of Renunciation. From that point on, the Garou she was no longer
exists. She isnowanew werewolf, considered nothingmore thanacub
fresh from her Rite of Passage, despite her physical age.

The Garou lives under her new name, and under new conditions
as well. Most Garou no longer treat her with familiarity, excluding
only her packmates. It is considered improper in Garou society to
acknowledge a werewolf who has undergone the Rite of Renunciation
until she once again achieves her previous Rank. Additionally, while
she keeps the Gifts she has already learned, the renunciatory werewolf
may only learn new Gifts that are appropriate to her new auspice and
Rank. In many cases, sept elders require renunciatory werewolves to
go through second Rites of Passage, ones that are far more tasking than
their first. Ifa Garou survives her second Rite of Passage, she may ask
aspirit to teach her a new Level One Gift of her new auspice.

Renunciation is not only self-imposed. There are other
reasons for changing auspice.

@ It is used as punishment for a crime committed; the
choice is often renunciation, exile or death.

‘@ Harano can sometimes drive a Garou to change auspice.

‘ In rare cases, a Garou chooses to dedicate himself to a new
goal, foregoing everything else to ensure that he attains his prize.
Revenge, or a quest of the spirit or heart, may lead a Garou from
his former path. Many septs accept a Garou with a new auspice
backupon the successful completion of his quest, at the same Rank
he previously held or, very rarely, at an even higher Rank.
(Naturally, this is all up the Storyteller.)

Physical States

Thissection deals with physical health: healing, injury and aging.
Injury

Injury in the Savage West is almost unavoidable. There are
about as many ways to get hurt as grains of dust in the Mojave.
Players record injuries in terms of Health Levels. Each wound
causes the loss of one Health Level. Simply check off Health
Levels as a character loses them, with the last box marked
delineating the character's current Health Level.

Health in Werewolf: The Wild West is rather like a ladder,
with Bruised at the top and Incapacitated at the bottom. As a
character suffers injuries, he scales down the ladder until he
teaches Incapacitated. When a character heals, one mark is
removed foreach Health Level recovered until he ishealthy again.

Each success on an opponent's damage roll indicates
the loss of one Health Level. Successes on a soak roll
naeae these Intel ct darrace Ge kone toons it *


CLE LE EL:

Ifan opponentscores three damage successes, the character loses
as many as three Health Levels. If the player gets two successes
on her soak roll, the character only loses one Health Level.

From time to time, a character suffers enough damage to take
her to Incapacitated and beyond. At that point, a werewolf is
considered critically wounded — not dead —and is out of action
for at least an hour. (Mortals are less fortunate; see next page.) A
critically wounded character does not recover one Health L
per turn, but only one every hour until she returns to Incap:
tated. The player must also make a roll on the Battle Scars chart
(see pg. 221). An Incapacitated werewolf dies only if her injuries
are particularly grievous (see Aggravated Wounds, below).

Werewolves have another truly distinct advantage when it
comes to being Incapacitated. A wounded Garou can focus her
Rage to remain active when she'd other-

wise be out of

commission
for an hour.

Healing

Due to their mystical nature, Garou heal far more swiftly than
humans. Unless damage is aggravated (see below), a character
regenerates one Health Level per turn. Homid and lupus Garou
in their natural forms heal at the same speeds as humans and
wolves, but shifting into any of other forms induces regenera-
tion. Metis in their Crinos form heal supernaturally quickly,
breed form not withstanding. There are some small advantages
to being metis — plenty scarce, but there nonetheless.

Under normal circumstances, a character cannot heal or
regenerate while involved in strenuous activity, but players
may attempt a Stamina roll (difficulty 8) to heal characters
engaged in physical efforts, even in combat. Success means
that a character regenerates a Health Level as usual.

, Aggravated Wounds

Some wounds are too substantial for even Garou to
recover from with ease. These injuries, called aggravated
wounds, are caused by anything that is simply too traumatic
to Garou physiology to be contended with. These grievous
injuries are sometimes caused by natural Banes, sometimes by
overpowering forces. They are one of the few ways to kill a
werewolf and keep it dead — normal wounds are
typically only a hindrance. Aggravated wounds are
inflicted by silver, fire, electricity, weird radia-
tions and by the fangs and claws of other


SSSN

ZLLL

supernatural creatures, including other werewolves. The Sto-
ryteller may also deem certain injuries inflicted by other means
(such as hitting the ground at terminal velocity) to be aggra-
vated. The Storyteller has final say on this matter.

Aggravated wounds may be soaked, with the exception of
those inflicted by silver — the lunar metal has been and always
will be the Garou's weakness.

Aggravated wounds are so substantial that they require a great
deal of time to heal. They heal ata rate ofone Level perday, provided
the character is resting or at least avoiding strenuous activity.

Aggravated wounds are indicated on the character sheet
with an X instead of the usual check mark. It's best to simply
convert checks into Xs where necessary, adding the normal
wounds later. For instance, a werewolf takes three Health
Levels of damage from normal weapons, and becomes Injured.
He then takes an aggravated wound from fire, hitting Wounded.
The player replaces the check in the Bruised box with an X, and
fills in the Wounded box with a check — after this werewolf
heals his three Health Levels of unaggravated damage, the one
aggravated wound he takes keeps him at Bruised, not Wounded.

i

% +
,

Mormus amo Mormartry

Sources of Injury

Despite the preternatural durability of Garou, there are
still many ways to harm them. Werewolves can be hurt in
many of the same ways as humans, and have to watch out for
most of the usual hazards to life and limb. There are excep-
tions, however, silver being the most obvious.

Combat

Combat wounds are addressed in the Melee and Firefight
sections of the Drama Chapter. Each success on an opponent's
damage roll forces the victim to lose one Health Level. Most
forms of attack can be soaked.

Falling

A long drop to hard earth hurts humans and werewolves alike;
the chart below determines damage. All creatures, even humans,
make Stamina rolls (difficulty 8) in order to soak damage from
falling. Each success means one less Health Level is lost. Each
botch — that is, each extra one after all the successes have been
taken away — means the loss of an additional Health Level.

4 Pe Whena human or animal reaches Incapacitated, he's one Health Level away fromdeath. If he suffers one
Tmore wound, he dies. If a human or animal falls to Incapacitated and has open wounds, loss of blood kills hima
Fe ifit isnot stopped. Without the supernatural endurance of werewolves, a person's life can end abruptly.

* for eacl

W6unded. An additional month and 11 days is required to reach full health. All this presumes decent medical

Distance (in feet) Injury

5 1 Health Level

10 2 Health Levels
20 3 Health Levels
30 4 Health Levels
40 5 Health Levels
50 6 Health Levels
60 7 Health Levels

Damage increases to a maximum of 10 Health Levels (90
feet), at which the body reaches terminal velocity and suffers
the same amount of damage no matter how far it falls.

Fire and Electricity

Fire and electricity are extremely hazardous to Garou; they
cause aggravated damage and can potentially kill a werewolf.
However, a player can roll Stamina against a difficulty that
depends on the intensity of the attack (see the chart below) in
order to resist burn damage. The player must make a soak roll
for every turn her character is exposed to fire or electricity. The
damage sustained ranges from one to three Health Levels per
turn (see the second chart, below). Each successful Stamina roll
reduces damage sustained by one. If the roll botches, additional,
more-devastating damage is inflicted (the character is blinded
until the wound heals, or perhaps maimed).

Difficulty Heat of Fire
3 Candle flame (first-degree burns)
5 Torch (second-degree burns)
7 Bonfire (third-degree burns)
9 Chemical fire
10 Molten metal
10 Lightning*
Wounds Size of Fire
1 Torch; part of body burned
az Bonfire; half of body burned
3 Raging inferno; all of body burned
4 Lightning; all of body burned

* Electricity, especially lightning, is severely damaging. The
worst burns occur inside the body and cannot be seen or
recognized immediately without a Medicine roll (difficulty 7).

Extreme Heat

Extreme heat (in excess of 200 degrees Fahrenheit) can
injure a werewolf just as surely as fire. Use the fire system for
sustaining damage, but modify it to suit the situation (always
only one Health Level lost per turn).

Disease

Werewolves are not immune to disease, but their amazing
recuperative powers and high Stamina ratings make them
highly resistant. Garou can still carry diseases and
spread them to others. Damage caused by diseases is

& Not normally aggravated.

Poison

Poisons generally act much like diseases do on Garou.
They're seldom fatal and, with rare exceptions, are recovered
from as though they were normal wounds. It's rare for a
werewolf to be bothered by a rattler bite, but characters should
still be careful; certain supernatural critters and Wyrm-min-
ions deliver poisons that cause aggravated damage.

Suffocation and Drowning

Werewolves need to breathe. But werewolves are better at
surviving suffocation and drowning than most animals. The
length of time a Garou can hold his breath is determined by his
Stamina rating, as per the following chart:

Stamina Holding Time
30 seconds
One minute
Two minutes
Four minutes
Eight minutes
15 minutes
20 minutes

DIYIAKHEWNE

30 minutes

Garou expend Willpower to continue holding their breath;
each point of Willpower allows fora full minute if Stamina is 4 or
higher, another 30 seconds if the character's Stamina is 3 or lower.

When a Garou can no longer hold her breath she begins
to suffocate or drown. The character suffers one Health
Level per turn. This damage isn't aggravated, but can't be
healed until the character can breathe again. When a Garou
reaches Incapacitated she falls unconscious and dies after a
number of minutes equal to her Stamina. After reaching
Incapacitated, she can't recover without resuscitation.

Silver

Silver causes one level of aggravated damage per turn of
contact. Successful attack rolls with silver weapons auto-
matically inflict at least one level of aggravated damage
regardless of the result of the damage roll. Damage caused by
silver can't be soaked.

Certain Garou don't take special damage from silver
when in particular forms. Lupus Garou in Lupus form take
normal damage from silver weapons. Lupus, homid and
metis Garou in Homid form also suffer no special damage
from silver weapons.

Each silver item carried by a werewolf reduces her Gnosis
rating by one or more, depending on the size of the object. Form
has no affect on this penalty. While the effects are not perma-
nent, the loss of Gnosis lasts at least one day even after the
disposal of the silver. Additionally, every five silver articles
carried by a pack causes each member of the pack to lose one
Gnosis point. (Obviously, many Garou resent the members of
their packs who insist on carrying plenty of silver bullets.)

Chapter Eight: Systems 218


SR

The size of an object or the quantity of silver in it can
increase the Gnosis penalty. The following chart gives a few
examples.

Item Gnosis Lost
Silver Bullets 1 point each
Silver Dollars 1 point each (find a safe place to
keep your money or carry gold.)
Klaive 1 point
Grand Klaive 2 points
Balefire

Galliards whisper stories of glowing caverns deep beneath
the earth, of vile, miasmic flames that warm the Wyrm itself.
They call this stuff balefire. Werewolves aren't likely to
encounter balefire save in the most wretched, tainted hives of
Wyrmlings or in forgotten places far underground. Those
unlucky few who do are in for a rough time. Balefire does the
same damage as normal fire, but its effects are more lasting —
the aggravated wounds it inflicts take twice as long to heal,
and any battle scars that result are rolled at +10 on the Battle
Scars Chart (next page). Most horrifying of all, balefire
damage can't be soaked — the best a body can do is avoid it.

Critical Wounds and
Battle Scars

A human dies if her Health Level drops below Incapaci-
tated, but a werewolf is made of sturdier stuff. She only dies
if she drops below Incapacitated and all the wounds she has
are aggravated.

When a werewolf is wounded beyond the Incapacitated
level, she is considered "critically wounded." The werewolf is
either unconscious or barely conscious. She can't perform
any actions, but may speak by expending a Willpower point.

The critically wounded werewolf no longer heals at her
normal rate, regenerating one Health Level per hour (exclud-
ing aggravated damage, which requires much longer). Once
she has recovered above the Incapacitated level, the Garou
can act again and begins healing at her normal rate of one
Health Level per turn. Naturally, homids and lupus in their
breed forms can't regenerate — if caught in their natural
shape, they can be killed about as easy as a man or wolf.

A wounded character may attempt to focus his Rage and
continue to remain active. The player makes a Rage roll
(difficulty 8) in an effort to channel the character's fury.
Every success on this roll restores one Health Level, even
those lost to aggravated damage. This effort can only be
tried once in any situation, and failure means that the
character remains too injured to act. There are other dan-
gers as well, as the character may give into her Rage and
enter a frenzy (See Frenzy, below). In either case, the
character must still make a roll on the Battle Scars Chart,
adding one additional die to the roll.

A Garou who drops below Incapacitated and recovers suffers
the effects of a roll on the Battle Scars Chart, on the next page.
Battle scars are permanent injuries or disfigurements caused by
severe circumstances. A player usually rolls only one bone on the
chart, but certain factors (like balefire or trying to fight past
Incapacitated) add to the roll, increasing the severity of the
scarring. A werewolf trying to recover an incapacitating wound
caused by balefire can theoretically roll two dice and add 10 to
his total on this chart, yielding a result anywhere from 12 to 30!

.
Aging

Despite their regenerative abilities, Garou are still mortal.
They die eventually, even if they never see combat (which is
nearly impossible). While werewolves age at a slower rate than
humans or wolves, they suffer the effects of old age. With
advanced years (65+ for Garou) comes the loss of Physical
Attributes. Senility is also a danger, reducing Wits and Intel-
ligence. An old Garou finds it difficult to Rage and runs the risk
of losing the wolf unless she chooses to gracefully pass on before
this occurs. The effects of age are up to the Storyteller to devise.
Still, most Garou die in battle, commit suicide or retire before
time can wear them down — as the Litany tenet prescribes.

Even so, not all werewolves fight the good fight until they die;
some choose to return to their mortal families or packs to live out
the rest of their years as humans or wolves. These weary heroes are
typically high ranking and simply feel they've done their duty.
They retreat almost entirely from Garou society, taking part in
tribalactivities only during yearly rituals and in times of greatneed.
Retired Garou are usually old, mellow and experienced enough to
control their Rage, which helps them live in peace.

Mental States
Frenzy

The ferocity that burns in even the meekest Child of Gaia's
heartis practically incomprehensible to normal folks. Some say
that Rage is just the animal in a shapeshifter, but it's a lot more
potent than that. Rage is a white-hot fever of supernatural
pitch, a link to the most destructive and primordial energies of
the Universe. In fact, some go so faras to call itthe Wyrm's own
gift to the Garou — but if that's so, then it's a gift that's been
turned against its giver countless times.

Rage rolls are, in effect, a werewolf' attempts to reach deep
into himself to draw forth the primal, bestial warrior-nature
instilled in him by Gaia. This savage power — when focused —
allows a werewolf to accomplish epic deeds. But as the old saying
goes: Be careful what you ask for, you just might get it. When too
much of the animal emerges, the character falls into a berserk
rage called frenzy. He becomes a creature of pure fury, with no
reasoning faculties to counter-balance his killer instinct.
When a player gets four or more successes on a Rage

roll, his character loses control and slips into frenzy. ®

Brain Damage: You lose a point of Intelligence, Perception or Wits, as well as one die roll's worth of points

frend Gnosis, Willpower and/or Knowledges (all Storyteller's choice). You may even suffer memory lapses.

Frenzied Garou switch immediately to either Crinos or
Hispo form and blindly attack those around them or flee into
the wild. A character can avoid frenzying with the expenditure
of a Willpower point, but loses all other actions in that turn.

The instincts of "fight or flight" are very strong in Garou. A
character entering a frenzy is overcome with one or both of
these emotions. If the character bolts, she's said to be overcome
by the "fox." Ifshe fights, she entersa berserk frenzy. The player
can decide which form of frenzy her character enters, but once
that decision is made there is no turning back. Fox or berserk
persists for the duration of the frenzy.

A berserk werewolf might turn on her own pack or any
helpless bystanders if all of her opponents have fallen or fled.
Berserk Garousometimes lapse into unconsciousness when their
frenzies end. Garou whose Rage scores exceed their Gnosis never

remember any details of what happened while berserk

— these werewolves often come to their senses in a
& heap of bodies and have blood-matted fur.

as
.

While ina berserk frenzy, a Garou must charge her opponent and
attack. While in a fox frenzy she must run away in the opposite
direction. In either case, she attacks anything in her path — anything;
friend, foe, packmate, archenemy or loved one. A frenzying Garou
can't use any special combat maneuvers, weapons or even Gifts.

There is no set time limit for a frenzy. As long as the
character senses danger of any sort, she continues to frenzy. If
the character can escape from combat and hide fora minimum
of three turns, she calms down enough to reason again. Frenzies
typically last twice as long if a character is hungry.

Frenzy can be difficult to roleplay. If the Storyteller feels that
the player is exerting too much control, he can either remove
one Willpower point automatically (the character is obviously
trying to control herself) or can choose the opponents that the
character attacks next or flees from. The simple thing to remem-
ber is that the character is in a primal state. He wants to escape
from an overwhelming danger, either by fleeing from it or
destroying it, and won't allow anything to stand in his way.

Chapter Eight: Systems 221

—S7


The primary advantage of a frenzy is that the character
suffers no penalties for lost Health Levels while in a frenzied
state. She receives her full Dice Pool regardless of her injuries.

Rage Rolls

The Storyteller may call fora Rage to determine ifa character enters
frenzy when any of the following conditions occurs:

¢ Embarrassment

¢ Humiliation

¢ Any strong emotion, even lust, passion or envy

¢ Extreme hunger

* Confinement; helplessness

© Taunting of an enemy

Being personally wronged

Witnessing an atrocity, no matter who the victim is

© Silver is in the vicinity

¢ Territory is seriously compromised

(Optional) The first wound received in combat; difficulty
is decreased by one if the weapon is silver

© (Optional) An important roll is botched

Difficulty of Rage Rolls

The difficulty of a Rage roll depends on the phase of the
moon. Luna's effects on Garou are powerful, and legends of
werewolf attacks during a full moon are no coincidence.

Difficulty Moon Phase
8 New
7 Crescent
6 Half
5 Gibbous
4 Full

If a Garou is in Crinos form, the difficulty is automatically
reduced by one.

Though itis dishonorable to lose control, sometimes a werewolf
vith her back to the wall needs the manic power of a frenzy. If she
© sires, she can summon up her inner demons and deliberately go
berserk, ignoring her wounds ina last-ditch effort to cut her way to
s fety. A Garou who deliberately tries to induce frenzy makesa Rage
roll as usual, but the difficulty is reduced by one. However, this is
x suallya last resort. Because every time a werewolf loses control, she
rens herself up to the possibility of something else taking over.

=
Wyrm-Rides
Sometimes a Garou's Rage grows so strong that it can consume
him. Some dark spot deep in his soul emerges and the Garou
; ommits depravities unimaginable under normal circumstances.
‘ome say the bestial quality of Rage is evidence of Wyrm-taint
.n Garou, and that the Wyrm sometimes demands its due.
Whenever a Garou receives six or more successes on a
Rage roll (two more than required to frenzy), he is said to be
"tidden by the Wyrm." He goes into a berserk frenzy and not

OX

even the expenditure of Willpower can rein him in. This
frenzy is played out as usual with regard to effects and
duration, but assumes a darker bent.

When the Wyrm rides a werewolf, the unlucky Garou
responds in various ways. This depends largely on breed. The
Wyrm's taint has touched even the purest warriors in the
smallest of ways, planting the seeds of atrocity in all breeds.

‘ Homid: Although the Croatan's sacrifice banished Eater-
of-Souls from this world, the taint that the creature buried deep
within humanity remains. Those Garou born to humans are
susceptible to the Wyrm's lures. Petty rivalries develop into
full-blown hatred, and racial tensions explode into violence
when the Wyrm rides a homid.

More commonly, though, homids in the thrall of the Wyrm
are prone to acts of cannibalism, not just on Garou, but on
humans and even wolves. If the homid incapacitates or kills a
foe, the player must make a Wits roll. If it botches, the character
stops for a turn, even in the middle of combat, and feeds his
hunger. Horror stories of man-eating wolves are the results of
such frenzies, mercifully veiled by the Delirium as they are.

‘@ Metis: Born of sin, metis begin their lives with the Defiler
Wyrm's hooks already in their souls. Indeed, many Garou believe
that the breach of the Litany that produces metis is the result of
temptation by the Defiler Wyrm. Metis ridden by the Defiler Wyrm
are capable of acts of vile perversion on their victims. If a metis
incapacitates or kills a foe while under the effects of a severe frenzy,
a Wits roll must be made. If it botches, the metis stops raging for a
tum, even in the middle of combat, and lets his unspeakable urges
take over. This isnot necessarily a character trait of metis Garou, but
the curse they must endure, the taint of an outside enemy.

‘w Lupus: The animal nature sometimes makes a Garou
vulnerable to the rages of Beast-of-War. This ravening entity
turns extreme lupus frenzies into raw, bestial rages, and it
foments bitter resentment for human despoilers into full-blown,
unforgiving mania. Lupus in the thrall of the Wyrm lose all sense
of mercy, even for their own packmates. They are as likely to
attack their own as their enemies. If any opponent or even a
packmate in the lupus' vicinity falls to Incapacitated, a Wits roll
must be made. If the roll botches, the lupus immediately attacks
the fallen combatant, even turning from another foe to do so.

When a Garou's extreme frenzy ends, the Wyrm-taint
leaves the character. All Garou fear such frenzies, for they strip
away what Garou hold dear about themselves and reveal them
to be as vulnerable to corruption as any other creature. Some
werewolves even kill themselves after such frenzies, choosing
death over the possibility of committing such atrocities again.

The Curse

Garou are predators and their predatory nature is difficult to
conceal. Even when they walk down the street in human form,

there's an animal under the skin. Despite the millennia
that have passed since the time of the Impergium, hu- ‘

mans subconsciously remember beingculled. Asa result,

Werewolf: The Wild West
NP)


whena Garou's Rage exceeds a bystanding human's Willpower, the
human instinctively feels uncomfortable around the Garou regard-
less of the werewolf present form. Anyone who comes near a
high-Rage werewolf avoids his gaze and tries to avoid him entirely.
Werewolves call this effect "the Curse," for that is exactly what
it is. The Curse forever separates Garou from humans and wolves,
preventing stable relationships, marriages or even matings. Even
close friendshipsare almost unheard ofbetween the races. Although
werewolves attain positions of great esteem in the human or wolf
world, there's a difference between being respected and accepted.
Many homid Garouare raised in broken homes. Garousecretly
watch over such children, and use spirits to monitor youths in the
hopes that they breed true. The Curse affects wolves as well, who
sensea stranger in their midst, and are often wary of oreven violent
toan unnatural wolf. A Garou can hope to find true companion-
ship only among her own kind, and even then the
consummation of love is forbidden by the Litany.

Family Tragedy

Curse orno Curse, werewolves
just aren't suited for long-term
family life among humans. In
time, their Rage always gets
the best of them, and a
spouse or child might be-
come a victim of the
primal family member.

Atthe Storyteller's dis-
cretion, a character who

tries to live among humans
can berequiredtomakea Will-
power roll between stories
(difficulty equal to the

character's Rage). If
the roll fails,

something happens in the character's life to hinder his relation-
ship with the humans. Something snaps, wells to the surface or
the Delirium engages. The Storyteller may reduce this penalty if
a character is fortunate enough to live among Kinfolk.

The same Willpower roll is made when living among wolves,
but the general dominance of the Garou reduces the difficulty by
one. Although wolves are leery of werewolves in their midst, few
are fool enough to provoke the generally dominant character.

If the character's Willpower roll botches, someone else

Delirium

Whenever humanssee an honest-to-God werewolf in Crinos
form, they go a crazy for a little while. The precise reaction
differs from person to person, but 3,000.

years of being preyed upon has
left indelible marks on the
human psyche. Humans'
fear of their ancestral
predators is instinctual


— there's no reason or logic to it (although a person should
logically be scared by an eight-foot-tall man-wolf, anyhow).

Werewolvesoncecalled thisoverpoweringreaction "night-fear,"
but now call it the Delirium. Ifnothing else, it's the main reason why
werewolves haven't been "discovered" by modern humanity. The
Delirium protects Garou, shrouding them ina disbelief that is deeply
rooted in the human mind. Those humans who repeatedly encoun-
ter Crinos "monsters" tend to end up in asylums.

A person's reaction to seeing a Garou in Crinos form is
determined by his Willpower (see the Delirium Chart, below). If
characters want to avoid inflicting the worst effects of the De-
lirium, players can roll Wits + Occult (difficulty 7). Each success
brings victims up one step on the Delirium Chart. This slightly
meditative, slightly manipulative act of concentration is called
"lifting the Veil," and is a direct violation of the Litany.

If the Storyteller needs a quick method of determining the
effects of Delirium on an individual, roll one die and refer to the
Delirium Chart. This is hardly an accurate reflection of the
normal human's Willpower score (one out of 10 does not have
a 10 Willpower, not even in the Savage West). Of course, there's

&

Willpower — "% of Population
ly 10%

_ ie set

always dramatic license; many stories involve impressive coinci-
dences, anyway. The town drunk may have an 8 Willpower —
you certainly wouldn't expect so, but anything is possible.

Note thatsome human culturesare lessaffected by the Delirium
or are even immune to it. A few human cultures were spared the
ravages of the Impergium, and don't have the Delirium in their
subconscious make-up. Examples of such people include isolated
Native American tribes, the Aborigines of Australia and certain
tribes in the Amazon. That doesn't mean these people accept
werewolves into their societies; it just means they see Garou for
what they are and are suitably afraid of them.

The Veil

The effects of the Delirium, and the shocking sights that
impose them, are normally forgotten by a person after she is
removed from a Garou. The person can't recall exactly what
happened. They eventually replace the image of the changed
Garou with a memory of something else, like a bear or a shaggy-
bearded mountain man in patchwork furs. This memory loss is
known as the Veil, and Garou value it highly.

Chapter Bight: Systems
