———— —_a at

I think that, as life is action and passion, it is required of
aman that he should share the passion and action of his time
at peril of being judged not to have lived.

— Oliver Wendell Holmes, Jr.

This chapter is designed to make the role of the Storyteller easier
and to provide players with a list of possible actions for their
characters. Other chapters detail the basic rules of Werewolf: The
Wild West; this chapter concentrates on permutations and compli-
cations, We can't cover every possibility that can arise ina game,
but this chapter offers enough information to help the Storyteller
design systems of her own, and tofill any blanks. Indeed, any of the
systems provided here can and should be modified to fita situation
or your game. Use what works and discard the rest.

This chapter also offers guidelines for pacing events in your
stories. This includes basic ideas on organizing a story, and ways
to decide who draws first in a shoot-out. The Action Sequence

is an outline of the order of events in a turn.

Chapter Wine: Drama

Knowing when to impose systemsand dice rolls to determine
characters' success at actions is as important as knowing when
not to. If it's doubtful that a character can perform an action,
call fora roll. If the action is straightforward and simple, assume
success. Minimizing dice rolls and rules makes for a faster game
and a seamless story.

If rules and dice rolls are called for, make sure the event is
unique and suspenseful. If players have to make the same rolls
every time their characters cross the dusty street, the game
becomes boring. However, if you call for different rolls that make
demands on characters' different abilities, and those rolls are
crucial to the progress of the story, the players will hang on each
bouncing die, feeling as much anxiety as their Garou characters.


Time passes in the World of Darkness just as it does in the
real world. However, measuring that time can be difficult.
There are five ways to organize time in a story

‘w Turn: The basic unit of time. A turn
three seconds to three minutes. A turn represents enough

n last from

time to take one action.

4 Scene: A number of rurns linked together by a com-
mon setting. A scene is structured according to turn-by-turn
actions or free-form roleplaying.

w Chapter: A series of scenes and downtime that forms
an independent section of a story. A chapter usually takes
one game session to complete.

‘@ Story: One complete adventure encompassing several
chapters and multiple game sessions. A complete story in-
cludes an introduction, build-up and conclusion.

‘w Chronicle: The ongoing escapades of a certain pack,

including all of its stories from start to finish. A
chronicle is a series of

>»

related stories
linked by the

same char-
R) acters or

\ setting.

SSeS

ZIELs

Player interaction and the Storyteller decide how long
each of these units of time lasts. Fast-paced, action-packed
stories blur from scene to scene and from chapter to
chapter. Long, suspenseful stories may demand whole
game sessions to complete a single scene. The turn itself
can also vary depending on characters' actions and the
pace of the story.

Turns

The range of possible actions that characters can at-
tempt in a turn is virtually unlimited, and the Storyteller
can easily be overwhelmed. Each character, whether he
belongs to a player or the Storyteller, will want to take an
action in each turn. Figuring out when things happen in
a turn — who shoots first, when the dynamite explodes —
is vital to the outcome of a

cene or story.

Turns are used to organize and stage the events of a
scene. Each character typically gets to attempt one a
tion in a turn. The nature of these actions determines

the duration of a turn. At the beginning of the turn each
player announces what her character aims to accom-
plish. The Storyteller also decides what his characters
attempt. The outcome of each action is resolved in order
aracters with high initiative
act first and might get the drop on their opponents.
Players resolve their turns in order until everyone has
used all of their actions, and then the process is
repeated in the next turn.

of initiative (see below).


ELL:

=
Taking Actions

Anything can happen in the Savage West and some
fools will try just about anything. The characters have as
many options in their situations as real people do in real
situations. Most actions do not require dice rolls and can
be roleplayed or stated plainly. It's not necessary to roll
dice when you want to have a conversation or walk down
a street.

Dice rolls are only applicable when characters take
actions that are challenging, dangerous or both. When the
Wyld-spirits of the plains grow restless or the poisonous
beasts of the Wyrm ooze from the Earth, you can bet that
characters respond with challenging and dangerous ac-
tions, and the dice determine their success or failure.

Simple Actions

In general, simple actions don't require dice rolls; the
character is automatically successful. The Storyteller does
have the option to ask for a roll if the circumstances warrant
it, though. Simple actions quickly become complex if a
scene proves stressful. Here's a list of typical simple actions.

Yield: Don't do anything. The character allows
others to go before her, even though she has the higher
initiative. The character can decide to take her action at
any time after she yie' If everyone yields, nothing
happens that turn. Yielding can be strategically beneficial,
but a character also runs the risk of losing an opportunity.

‘@ Healing: Werewolves' connection to Gaia allows
them to heal much faster than any normal person or wolf. If
acharacter does nothing else during a turn, she may choose
to regenerate one Health Level, subject toa few restrictions.

A werewolf can't automatically regenerate aggravated
wounds. She also can't heal quickly in her natural form
unless she's metis. (Metis can regenerate in any form; just
one of the reasons why they're so dangerous.) If the Garou
takes any action in addition to healing, a successful Stamina
roll (difficulty 8) must be made to heal. It's a difficult thing
to do, but sometimes necessary when surrounded by a pack
of ravenous Black Spirals.

‘W Moving: A typical human can walk seven yards in a
turn, jog 12 + Dexterity in yards, and run 20 + (3 x
Dexterity) yards. Garou are capable of much faster move-
ment than the typical human. If a Garou assumes the
Crinos, Hispo or Lupus forms, she can cover greater dis-
tances on all fours. It may be dirty business, but any Garou
worth her hide knows it's better than strolling upright across
the countryside. The following chart details the differences
in movement rates between the many forms. Don't forget to
include any Dexterity modifiers for each form.

A roll isn't usually ne: y for a character to walk
from here to there. If the going is rough, exceptionally
long, or pits two characters against each other, you might
have to break out the dice. If a character wants to turn tail
and run from battle, he might have to do some dodging
before he gets out of trouble.


wal

\\

ta

‘w Reversion to Breed Form: All Garou can shift to
breed form automatically. No dice are rolled and the shift
occurs immediately. A lupus Garou can shift to wolf form for
a quick getaway; a homid can change to Homid form to fire
off a few shots. A metis...well, a metis can shift to Crinos
and the rest isn't pretty. For complete rules on changing
forms, see Shapeshifting, pg. 251.

Complex Actions

Complex actions require skill, concentration and a bit of
luck to perform. Whenever a character wants to try something
and has a chance of failing, the Storyteller should call for an
appropriate dice roll. Alternatively, the Storyteller may wish
to award automatic successes to characters with superior skills.
The Storyteller shouldn't demand that a character who has
years riding experience roll Ride every single time something
unexpected happens. He's done this before and the player
probably has a lot of dice to roll. The attempt will probably
succeed anyway, so why slow the game?

‘w Attack: Sometimes negotiating isn't enough and only
your claws and your Rage can keep you alive. Even the most
peaceful Garou is a slave to his animal nature. Whenever a
character wishes to punch, scratch, kick or claw an opponent,
a roll is required; automatic successes don't apply to combat.

w Dodge: Of course, getting out of the way is a good idea,
too. A character can abort another action and dodge to avoid
getting hit, assuming she still has any actions left. This requires
a Dodge roll.

‘w Change Form: A character may attempt to shapeshift.
This requires a roll, except when changing to one's breed form.

‘@ Climb: Garou are always climbing all sorts of things:
barns, trees, stagecoaches, Banes and mountains. Doing so isn't
always safe.

‘w Leadership: Keeping your cool while Banes creep out the
woodwork is not an easy thing to do. Keeping control of a pack
when Kinfolk bodies lie on the floor is even tougher.

‘w Reload a Gun: It's tricky to get bullets into a cylinder
when you have about two seconds left to live.

ra al e-

Multiple Actions

As much as some might deny it, werewolves are born to cut
through their enemies. As born and bred warriors, they are
among the fiercest combatants on the planet.

Players often take advantage of their Garou's combat
skills by performing as many actions as possible in one turn.
A player has to split her character's Dice Pool to stage
multiple attacks, or spend Rage for extra actions, or both.
Combining these options simulates the devastating preda-
tory powers of the Garou.

Multiple and Rage actions can also be used to do things
other than fight. The rules described below apply to combat
and more esoteric actions.

Splitting Dice Pools: A character can perform multiple
actions in a single turn, such as shooting two pistols or dodging
and shooting at the same time, but the player has to divide the
dice rolled. The player takes the Dice Pool for the action that
has the fewest number of dice and splits it among the actions
performed. A character with a Dexterity + Dodge total of five
dice and a Dexterity + Firearms total of seven dice receives five
dice to be allocated between dodging and shooting. You're only
as good as your least practiced skill.

‘w Rage Actions: In addition to splitting Dice Pools to stage
multiple actions, players can spend Rage points to gain extra
actions. Each Rage point spent enables a character to perform
one more action with a full, normal Dice Pool.

For example, if a player spends two Rage points, his charac-
ters can perform three full actions (one regular plus two Rage
actions), each with its own Dice Pool. The player may then
break down these full actions according to the rules for splitting
Dice Pools, increasing the number of actions even further. A
player can never spend more Rage per turn than his character
has Dexterity points.

Normal humans don't have the option to use Rage for extra
actions. Only the Garou, born of an angry mother, can unleash
their hatred in such a flurry of activity. A werewolfs Rage
makes her heart pound and ushers her forward when every-
thing else deserts her.

Werewolf: The Wild West
ww


The players and the Storyteller must respect Rage for what
it represents. Spending Rage means unleashing primal fury. It
means the Garou has let go of herself. The Storyteller should
control when players can spend Rage. Only stressful situations
warrant the expenditure of this powerful force; most combat
falls into this category, but any time a fight's too easy, a
werewolf won't feel the need to tap her Rage.

‘@ Order of Multiple Actions: When a character's Dice
Pool is split for multiple actions, resolve all the actions at the
same time. This simulates a character devoting her attention
to multiple tasks. If a character receives multiple actions from
Rage, the actions are resolved at different times.

When players spend Rage to take additional actions, things
can get confusing. The Storyteller has two options. First, each
character and combatant takes his first action in order of
initiative from highest to lowest. Once everyone has taken
their first action, characters with extra Rage actions may take
their second actions in order of descending initiative. This
proceeds until all characters have taken all of their actions.
Resolve any split Dice Pools within individual Rage actions.

The Storyteller's second option more accurately represents
Rage actions in relation to the turn sequence. The Storyteller
counts down initiative as usual, with the highest-rolling player
taking her action first. If she has spent Rage to receive more
actions, her second and subsequent actions occur immediately
after her first. Treat each additional action as if it is one point
lower in initiative than the previous one. A player who takes
her first action when other players are resolving Rage actions
takes precedent and resolves her action first.

Forexample, Runs-Swiftly attacks two vampires whohave killed
one of his packmates. Runs-Swiftly's player spends one Rage point
foran extra action to tear both Ticks to pieces. One of the vampires
also gets an extra action from one of her Wyrm-spawned abilities.

Runs-Swiftly gets five initiative successes. The female vam-
pire receives four, and the slower vampire receives two. The
vengeful werewolf takes his first action on five and his second
on four. The bloodsucker takes her first action on four, before
Runs-Swiftly takes his Rage action, and takes her second
action on three. The slow vampire will probably never see his
first action, but if he survives he will resolve his action on two.

According to the first method of resolving multiple actions,
the slow vampire would have been able to take his first action
before his compatriot, and Runs-Swiftly would have taken
their first and second actions.

Initiative
There are two types of people in the Savage West: the quick
and the dead. It's acliché, but those who don't believe it usually
end up six feet under — and some of them get back up again!
Sometimes it's just a matter of who shoots first and who dies.
The Storytellerneeds to determine the order in which

events take place at the beginning of every action turn.
In anormal situation, each player makes an initiative

roll and the Storyteller rolls for the characters' adversaries.
Initiative is resolved with a Wits + Alertness roll (difficulty 4).
(Wits + Ride, Wits + Firearms or any other fitting Ability might
be more appropriate in certain circumstances.)

The character who receives the most successes acts first.
Failure (no successes) indicates that a character hesitates and
acts last (initiative is effectively zero). Actions are resolved
simultaneously when there's a tie.

A botched initiative roll means a character cannot act this
turn; something unexpected happens — a gun jams, a horse
stumbles — and the character is momentarily out of action.
The Storyteller may let a character act ona botched initiative,
but may reduce the player's Die Pool by the number of Is in the
botched Initiative roll. (In this case, the player's initiative is
considered zero.) This option keeps the story moving and keeps
everyone in the game.

If a player decides to yield, his character allows others with
lower initiatives to perform first. The yielding character may
take his action at any time in the remainder of the turn.

Action Sequence

Runningasmooth action scene requiresa certain amount of
organization. Just as turns help organize a scene, the following
sequence helps organize a turn. The Storyteller's goal is to
enhance the story with action, not slow it down with charts,
turns and dice rolls.

¥ Initiative

The Storyteller describes the current situation from each
character's point of view at the beginning of the turn. The
players will want to know what their characters are able to
accomplish, and have a sense of what their enemies might try.
A recap of the last turn's events and its outcome is often the
most effective way to describe the current situation. Keep in
mind that a character may not be able to see everything — a
fight can be confusing and disorienting. Furthermore, there
may not be enough time for characters to take in everything.

It isn't necessary for the Storyteller to list the players' options.
The Storyteller should describe their situation in as much detail
as possible and let the players make their own decisions. The more
information that's provided, the more dynamic and realistic the
scene is. Yet there are times when the absence of detail, such as
when a character frenzies, is more appropriate and dramatic.

Once the Storyteller has described the circumstances, each
player rolls initiative and the Storyteller rolls for the charac-
ters' adversaries and allies (Wits + Alertness, difficulty 4).
Each player declares what he or she attempts in ascending
order of initiative (the player who gets the fewest initiative
successes declares his action first). Once everyone has declared
actions, the player with the most initiative successes acts first,
and so on in descending order. Use the multiple-action guide-
lines above when players declare more than one task ina turn.

Ifa player wants to spend Rage points ina turn, the act must
be declared in the initiative phase.

Chapter Mine: Drama


A character can't normally change her action once the
action phase has begun and all actions have been declared.
However, a player may want to, for example, stop trying to
crack a safe when she discovers that a frenzied Black Spiral
Dancer is about to tear her throat out. If a player changes her
character's action, the Storyteller should impose a penalty. All
difficulties of the new action increase by one. The only excep-
tion to this penalty is when a character aborts an action in order
to dodge. When a character reacts to an incoming attack by
getting out of the way, no penalty should be imposed. Option-
ally, the Storyteller may require a player to spend a Willpower
point to change actions with no penalty.

wv Ambushes

Occasionally it isn't necessary to roll to determine the order
of initiative. If a character gets the drop on her opponent, via a
well-planned ambush or surprise attack, she automatically gets
to move first. The Storyteller determines what entails a success-
ful ambush and can call for Perception or Stealth rolls as she
deems appropriate. The Storyteller can, in special circum-
stances, determine the order of attack without need of Initiative
rolls. When a successful ambush is staged, the attackers have a
full turn to act against their opponents without a counterattack.

wv Actions

Once initiative has been determined, each character ex-
ecutes his or her action. The Storyteller determines the
appropriate roll and sets the difficulty. A player rolls, splitting
dice for multiple actions if necessary, and announces the
number of successes.

Most combat actions tend to be attacks. The number of
successes rolled determines the accuracy of pistol shots or
punches. The Storyteller determines the results of actions in
the resolution stage.

¥ Resolution

The Storyteller determines the outcome of events that
occur in a turn. While dice determine the magnitude of
success, the Storyteller determines the actual results of actions.

Inacombat scene, the Storyteller determines the effective-
ness of a hit or the quality of a dodge. The number of successes
on an attack roll indicates the effectiveness of the attack. A
successful hit means the player makes a damage roll.

The type of attack determines how many damage dice are
rolled. In brawling combat and melee, the character's Strength
plus any modifiers determine the number of damage dice
rolled. The number rolled for a gun is equal to the damage
rating of the weapon plus the number of successes rolled on the
attack. The difficulty of damage rolls is always 6.

Each success rolled has the potential to inflictone Health Level
of damage. Successes rolled on damage dice do not automatically
affect the victim. A target can attempt to shrug off damage with
Stamina (difficulty 6). Each success on this soak roll reduces the
inflicted damage by one Health Level. A Garou's form and some
Gifts can modify Stamina or provide extra protection.

Note: Damage rolls and soak rolls cannot be botched.

Combat Systems

Gaia created werewolves as her last line of defense against the
Apocalypse. While that day seems far off, the battle for su-
premacy between the Wyrm, the Weaverand the Wyldis fought
every day. The Savage West is one of the last places left in the
world where the Wyld still thrives, but it is encroached upon
constantly. An increasing amount of settlers press west like a
juggernaut, bringing the Weaver and her children with them.
Meanwhile, the Wyrm lies buried beneath the ground, ready to
rise and cull the herd, not only of the weak and sickly, but of the
strong and healthy. The Garou are trapped between these forces
and feel that the only way out is to cut their way free.

Violent conflict is the easiest means by which a Storyteller
can create drama and suspense. The following mechanics
determine the outcome of combat. The rules are designed to
simulate the dynamics of battle while still remaining a game.

The three basic forms of combat — gunfighting, melee and
brawling — all use the same basic system.

‘e Gunfighting and Ranged Weapons: A gunfight, the
most cinematically popular way to settle disputes, is any
combat in which opponents draw iron and try to shoot each
other down. Gunfighting can also include cannons. The basic
system is Dexterity + Firearms, and with the difficulty depend-
ing on the accuracy of the weapons used. The roll for bows and
thrown weapons is Dexterity + Athletics with difficulty de-
pending on the distance to the target.

4W Melee: Melee concernsall combat wherein the combatants
use hand weapons. Wielding a saber or a similar weapon isn't
easy or natural as simply punching someone; it requires training
and skill. Melee also covers all combat with makeshift hand
weapons such as bottles, chairs or other people. Each weapon
brings its own dynamics toa fight, but the basic system is Dexterity
+ Melee, and the difficulty to hit depending on the weapon.

te Brawl: Brawling is the most common type of combat. It
involves any situation in which one person seeks to strike
another with her bare fist or any other part of her anatomy.
Opponents must be within reach of each other. There are
humerous complications and special maneuvers for brawling
combat, but the basic system is Dexterity + Brawl.

Combat scenes can, of course, be a mix of these three
systems with additional complications thrown in. One charac-
ter might have a rifle while another uses her klaive and yet
another enters frenzy and swings wildly with her claws. Char-
acters may choose not to enter combat directly, but sneak
behind their opponents or step sideways into the Umbra.

Complications and
Permutations

Combat almost never involves characters who simply
stand in place and beat each other. That is unrealistic
and makes for dull action scenes. Instead, combat-
ants dive for cover, try to outflank each other, shoot

Werewolf: The Wild West


objects out of each other's hands and bring Gifts into play.
Most of these complications are detailed here, and these rules
provide a foundation on which to improvise even more
complex combat scenes.

General Complications

The following complications apply to all action situations
regardless of the type of combat or weapons used.

w Changing Actions: If a character decides to change her
intended action once the turn has begun, she suffers for her
indecisiveness. The new action may still be attempted, but its
difficulties increase by one. The Storyteller should limit charac-
ters' chances to change actions; it's difficult to be fully aware of
everything that goes on ina combat situation. Changing actions
should be reserved for dramatically appropriate moments or
when a character's first action has simply become impossible.

Thisrule doesn't apply when a character switches toa dodge
action (see Dodging, below).

*w Coordinated Attacks: A pack that has trained
together and that has a strong leader (minimum
Leadership of 3) can coordinate its attacks on a
single opponent for greater effectiveness. The
leader must make a successful Leadership roll
(difficulty 6), and each pack member must re-
serve his
(the coordinated attack is staged on the low-
est initiative achieved by the pack members).

ction until the entire pack can act

If successful, each attack has its difficulty re-
duced by one. This system works for both ranged
combat, melee and brawling.

If the leader's roll fails, characters act
normally, based on their own initiative
rolls. The lead character loses his first
(or only) action
turn, though.

w Dodging:
Noncombative or
smart characters

in the

try to avoid in-
coming blows. Any
time that a cha

acter is being
attacked, the
player may

declare a dodge. This can be done even if the player previously
declared another action for the turn. The character does not
suffer the normal penalty for changing actions (see Changing
Actions, above).

A character can also declare a dodge and still attempt to
perform the action declared at the beginning of the turn (if
doing so is physically possible). The normal rules for multiple
actions apply; the lowest Dice Pool of the actions performed is
split among the actions.

The success of a dodge is determined with a Dexterity +
Dodge roll. Each success rolled removes one success from the
opponent's attack roll. In order to dodge multiple attacks, the
player must split her dodge Dice Pool between incoming attacks.

The difficulty of dodging in melee and brawling combat is
6, but it increases by one for every following attacker.

The difficulty of dodging ranged attacks is determined by

the availability of nearby cover and the mobility of the
character. The character doesn't
physically dodge bullets,
but dives for

cover.

Immobilization: Stunned, unconscious or otherwise
immobilized opponents make easy targets. Foes who cannot
move whatsoever can be hit automatically at close range; no
roll is required. If the target is immobilized but canstill struggle,
difficulties to hit it are reduced by two.

‘w Stunning: Werewolves are hardy creatures and are
difficult to permanently put down. They can, however, be
dazed by solid blows. Whenever the actual number of Health
Levels lost in an attack exceeds a character's Stamina, she is
stunned and unable to act in the following turn. A Rage point
may be spent in the following turn to counter this effect.

Gunfight Complications

Am I fast or is Sweden just a very small place?

— The Kid, The Quick and the Dead

The prominence of firearms in the Savage West makes
gunfights an important part of a Wild West story. Not every-
one carries a pistol or rifle, but anyone expecting a fight better
be packing a trusted weapon. The following list of variables can
complicate a firefight.

‘w Aiming: Characters who take the time to aim have a
better chance of hitting their targets. Aiming takes time and
requires that a character do nothing but focus on his shot. For
every turn the character spends taking aim, one die is added to
her Firearms Dice Pool. The player may add as many dice to the
character's Dice Pool as the character possesses Perception
points. A Perception of 3 allows a character to aim for three
turns and gain a three-die bonus. Four turns of aiming for the
same character would provide no additional benefit.

4 Cover: Only foolsand sheriffs stand in the middle of thestreet
and get shot at. While the empty-street gun duel might seem
honorable orromantic, it'sa quick way to get killed. Gettingbehind
cover can save one's hide from a hail of bullets. Depending on how
protective that covers, the difficulty ofa shot increases from one to
four. Cover can also protect a character from most forms of harm.

Being behind cover can work against a character, too. If the
gunslinger needs to spring from cover to fire and duck back
down again, the difficulty of her shots increases by one.

Cover Difficulty
Lying flat (light) +1
Wagon wheel (moderate) +2
Barn door (good) 43
Only head exposed (superior) +4

Werewolf: The Wild West

‘w Cover Fire: By repeatedly firing at opponents without
wasting time trying to hit, a character can force his opponents
to dive for cover. This might allow for the strategic withdrawal
of a packmate or might give the character enough time to
escape into the Umbra in the following turn. The player rolls
Perception + Firearms at a difficulty equal to her individual
opponents' Willpower. (The roll iscompared toeach opponent's
Willpower to determine if each dives for cover.) Only one
success is required to force an opponent to dive for cover and
lose any actions that remained in the turn. (There is no penalty
for such "changing of actions" on the victim's part.)

‘w Duels and Staredowns: The image has been burned into
our collective psyche: The sheriff walks into the street. The
villain awaits him at the end of town. They lock eyes, the clock
strikes and one man falls.

The most important moment of a gunfight isn't the pull of
the trigger. A gunfight is a battle of Willpower. The Storyteller
should use the rules for staredowns when two gunslingers meet
inthestreet (see Staredowns, below). The loser of the staredown
can't perform a quick-draw (see Quick-Draw, below).

w Fanning: Most pistols in the Savage West are double-
action, requiring time to cock and fire. This makes multiple
shots all but impossible, even with multiple actions or split
Dice Pools. However, a character may attempt to fan his pistol
and fire multiple shots. One hand repeatedly strikes the ham-
mer while the other pulls the trigger.

Fanninga pistol requires a split Dice Pool or the expenditure
of Rage to account for shots fired and to pull off the fanning
action. Fora character to fan, a player first needs tomakea Wits
+ Firearms roll (difficulty 8). (The Dice Pool used to fan and
shoot is the lower of either Wits + Firearms or Dexterity +
Firearms.) If the fan roll succeeds, remaining Rage actions or
dice in the pool can be allocated to the number of shots fired.
If the fan roll fails, only one shot can be squeezed off.

Fanning, whether successful or not, increases the difficulties
ofall shots fired by two. Obviously only trained gunfighters can
pull off this maneuver.

Fanning is the only way that multiple shots can be fired with
adouble-action pistol, unless the character deliberately splits
his Dice Pool to fire, cock and fire again.

@ Movement: If the target or shooter moves, the difficulty
of firing increases. If either party moves at a speed faster than
awalk, but slower than a run, the shooter's difficulty increases
by one. If either moves at a run or faster, whether on horseback
or ona train, the difficulty increases accordingly.

‘@ Multiple Shots: If a gunslinging Garou wants to make
multiple shots in a single turn, he has a few options. If he has a
double-action pistol, he has to fan his pistol with a Rage action
or split Dice Pool (see Fanning, above). If he has a single-action
pistol, ora rifle, he may take as many shots as he has bullets, and
as may as his player has dice to split among them.

Particular weapons require special attention where
multiple shots are concerned. -


Shotguns: Shotguns are available in two types: single-barrel
and double-barrel. Double-barrel shotguns can be used to fire
twoshots simultaneously at one target (one roll; +1 difficulty),
or single shots leaving the remaining cartridge loaded and
ready to fire. A double-barrel shotgun can be used to make
multiple attacks, but only if one barrel is fired at a time; a Rage
point can be spent for an extra action or the Dexterity +
Firearms Dice Pool split between the shots, as usual.

Asingle-barrel shotgun can'tbe used to make multiple attacks,
nor can a double-barrel that fires both barrels at one target in a
single action. The loading mechanisms of shotguns simply denies
the multiple shots that are possible with pistols or rifles.

The Damage listed for shotguns on the Firearms Chart is for
a single-barrel. If both barrels of a double-barrel are fired
simultaneously, double the damage dice listed.

Gatling guns: The Gatling gun is the world's first automatic
weapon. If folks in the Savage West ever see one, it's in the
possession of the army; the gun is not available to civilians
(unless somehow stolen).

It is typically mounted on a tripod. It is fired by turning a
crank, and is fed ammunition on a belt that is stored in a box.
It requires a crew of humans to operate it. Superhuman
strength is required to single-handily lift a Gatling gun, let
alone fire it effectively (Strength 6 to carry, 8 to fire).

A Gatling gun sprays bullets. Whether firing from a tripod
or with the Gatling in hand, a character rolls Dexterity +
Firearms (difficulty 8) to hit, but gains 10 additional dice to his
pool, increasing the chances to hit and do damage.

If a Garou in Crinos form plans to move around with a
Gatling gun, he needs an assistant to carry the ammunition
box that the gun-belt feeds from. If no one can help him, the
Garou must remain in place to continue firing.

If a botch is rolled while firing, the gun jams and can't be
cleared until downtime permits; a jam can't be cleared in
combat. If two ormore Isare rolled onabotch, the gun explodes.
All those in its immediate vicinity suffer 12 dice normal damage
(soakable), and that's all she wrote for that gun.

Bows and Thrown Weapons: Attacks made with these weap-
ons are resolved with Dexterity + Athletics. The number of
multiple shots that are possible is usually limited by the
amount of axes or spears that a character carries. Dice Pools are
split among the number thrown.

Bows can usually be used to fire one shot ina turn unless the
archer has prepared to fire multiple times. (He has several
arrows stabbed into the ground before him, ready to be fired.)
In that case, he splits his Dice Pool or spends Rage as usual.

 Quick-Draw: Quick-drawing is the act of drawing and
shooting in one fluid motion in the same turn to beat an
opponent to the shot. This system is restricted to face-off
gunfights only. It can't be used in regular combat, as quick-

draw is as much a matter of speed and precision as it is
anticipating the movements of a particular oppo-
@ nent, without being distracted.

Chapter Mine: Drama

Combatants in a face-off may roll Wits + Firearms (or Wits
+ Athletics for bows or thrown weapons) rather than Wits +
Alertness (difficulty 4) to determine initiative. The rest of the
shoot-out is resolved normally with Dexterity + Firearms rolls.

A face-off doesn't have to be strictly one-on-one; one or two
allies can contend with one or more opponents on an open
street. Skilled gunfighters can still use Wits + Firearms to beat
their opponents to the shot in such situations. The difference is
that when a single character faces multiple opponents, he must
spend Rage or split his Dice Pool to shoot at them.

Practiced gunfighters often specialize in Quick-Draw with
their Firearms Abilities for that added edge that might mean
the difference between life and a pine box.

A character who loses a staredown in a duel cannot perform
a quick-draw in that same fight.

w Range: The distance from shooter to target is a major
factor in gunfighting. The Firearms Chart lists the medium
range of each gun type; shots fired within this range are
resolved normally. A firearm can shoot effectively up to twice
its listed range, but the difficulty for doing so increases by one.

In the case of bows and thrown weapons, range determines
the difficulty of the shot. The distance in yards between shooter
and target is divided by the attacker's Strength (rounded up).
The result is the difficulty of the shot. The maximum range that
acharacter can throw a small object is Strength x 10 in yards.

Point-blank shots, regardless of weapon type, occur up to
one yard away from the target, and have a difficulty of 4.

4¥ Reloading: Reloading a weapon when no shotsare being fired
iseasy, unless you're character's lupus who's never handled gun. In
the heat of combat, however, the click ofa hammer hitting an empty
chamber usually spells disaster. Reloading a weapon quickly and
understress requires Wits + Firearms roll (difficulty 6). The number
of successes rolled equals the number of bullets that can be loaded
inone tum. Failure indicates that something distracts the character
— probably the shots being fired at him — and he can't load his
weapon. A botch indicates that the character dropsall ofhis bullets.

4 Silver Bullets: Given werewolves' allergy tosilver, itmightseem
prudenttoloadapistol withsilverbullets. However, withoutdelvingtoo
deeply into metallurgy or physics, silver bullets aren't easy to come by
andfiringthem isproblematic. Even ifacharactercouldfindagunsmith
who could make silver bullets, they wouldn't be nearly as accurate as
regular bullets. Furthermore, by carrying silver bullets one draws
unwanted attention from the Garoucommunity. Noone usessilver
bullets unless they know about werewolves and are out to get hurt.

Ifa character decides to use silver ammunition anyway, she
suffers from several penalties. All firing difficulties increase by
one, the weapon's range ishalved and botches indicate that the
gun is damaged permanently and cannot fire.

Yet Garou take aggravated damage from successful hits; it is
silver, after all. If the attack does more damage than the target
has Stamina, the bullet lodges itself in the character's body. If this
occurs a Garou takes another aggravated wound every turn until
she reverts to Homid form (or breed form, in the case of lupus).

w Two Guns: Using two guns puts a shooter at a distinct
advantage, but it also has its drawbacks. At the very least, the
shooter must have a lot of ammunition on hand. The real
disadvantage, of course, is that her hands are full and she has
to concentrate on two different guns.

Pistols: Twin pistols are fairly common in gunslinger circles,
Any gunslinger who wants to live should have a back-up weapon,
even if she doesn't use them both at the same time. Firing two
pistols simultaneously requires the player to split her Dice Pool.
The character suffers a -2 difficulty penalty forher off-hand, unless
she's ambidextrous. Fanning is impossible with your hands full of
guns, so using double-action pistols in this manner limits you to
two shots. Ifa character has single-action pistols, she may take as
many shots as she has bullets and dice to split among them.

Rifles: Fewcan wield and fire tworiflesat thesame time, but those
few who can are holy terrors. Fighting with two rifles requires a
minimum Strength of 3 anda whole lot of luck. The shooter has to
spend Rage points for extra actions or split his Dice Pool as normal
among the number of shots he fires. He suffers any appropriate off-
hand penalties as well. And the rifleman needs to swing-cock each
rifle after each shot (spin a rifle in his hand while holding the
cocking mechanism) in order to fire again. Cocking each gun while
firing multiple shots requires a Dexterity + Firearms roll, difficulty
8. Only one success per rifle is necessary, but the dice must come
from the character's Dice Pool or from an extra Rage action.

Werewolf: The wild

Shotguns: Itis possible to wield and fire two shotguns, but it's very
demanding; you need Strength of at least 4 to doso. A single-barrel
shotgun can only fire once before itneeds to be reloaded, so carrying
two guns affords a character two shots in one action. Double-barrel
shotguns can fire two double bursts (+1 difficulty each) or four
individual shots, maximum, in one action. A Rage point isspent for
extra actions or Dice Pools for these shots are split normally. A
shotgun in the off-hand suffers a +2 difficulty modifier.

Gatling Guns: Two Gatling guns? Please!

Bows: Two bows? How many arms does your character have?

Thrown Weapons: Throwing two weapons offers two attacks
and requires a split Dice Pool. Unless the character is ambidex-
trous an off-hand difficulty penalty of two is imposed.

‘@ Trick Shots: Targeting a specific part of a target's body,
a small object or a portion of a larger object is sometimes
required to put the target down quickly or to achieve some
effect (shooting the wick off a stick of dynamite is dangerous,
but could save the Kinfolk who are tied up around it).

Targeting a specific part of a body or object increases a shot's
difficulty, but can increase the effectiveness of the shot. Not
only do called shots cause more damage, they can cause victims
to drop their weapons, lose their sight or fall prone.

The Storyteller should determine the modifiers of shooting
objects based on this chart. The effects of shooting objects are
determined by the circumstances of the story.

Firearms

Firearm Types

‘@ Pistols: Pistols vary in size, shape and model. The main
distinction between pistols is the hammer action. Single-
action pistols don'trequire any additional effort beyond pulling
the trigger; the hammer cocks when the trigger is pulled. Old,
double-action pistols require a shootist to cock the hammer
manually before firing. Double-action pistols require fanning
in order to fire multiple shots, while single-action pistols are
only limited by the ability of the gunslinger.

‘@ Rifles: Rifles, for the purposes of game play, vary mostly
based on theirammunition capacity and range. Some weapons,
such asa buffalo gun, are designed strictly for taking down large
animals. The Winchester and Spencer rifles can be loaded with
several bullets and are generally more effective in shoot-outs
than hunting rifles.

‘v Shotguns: Shotguns are available in two types: single-
barreled and double-barreled.

Range: This is the practical range of the firearm in yards. A chute may fire on a target at up to double that listed

range, although this is considered a long-range shot.

‘w Gatling Gun: The Gatling gun is the world's first
automatic weapon. It is typically mounted ona tripod and is fed
ammunition on a belt.

w Thrown Weapons: Guns aren't the only form of ranged
combat; bows, spears, axes and knives can be thrown. To fire
a bow requires a minimum Strength of 3. Otherwise bows do
roughly the same amount of damage regardless of who fires.

The Dice Pool to hit with a thrown or ranged weapon,
including bows, is Dexterity + Athletics (although the Storyteller
mightallowahand weapon tobe thrown with Dexterity + Melee).

The difficulty of hitting with a thrown object or weapon is
determined by dividing the range in yards to the target by the

Strength of the character (yards/Strength) rounded up.
Thus, if character with a Strength of 3 throws a rock
at a doorway 16 yards away, the difficulty of the
5 attack is 6.

tm
The damage inflicted is determined by Strength. The number
of successes achieved on the attack roll does not increase the
damage roll where thrown weapons and bows are concerned.
The maximum range that a character can throw a small
object is generally Strength x 10 in yards.

Melee and Brawling
Complications

Werewolves typically solve their problems with claws and
teeth. If a problem has a greater scope or deep personal roots,
they might draw klaives. Both options are messier than using
firearms, but Garou aren't usually afraid to get dirty.

Melee covers the full range of armed combat, from swinging
barstools to crossing Grand Klaives. Whenevera fight involves
weapons at close range, the Storyteller should use the compli-
cations and maneuvers described below.

Chapter Wine: Drama 241


Brawling covers any combat in which Garou dispense with
fancy knives and sticks and tear into each with fang and claw. A
list of brawling complications and maneuvers is provided here.

The following complications apply to both melee and brawling.

@ Multiple Opponents: Ganging up on someone can prove
tobea highly effective tactic, while being ganged up on is never
healthy. All dodge difficulties increase by one for each oppo-
nent after first that a character faces (to a maximum of 10)

‘@ Flank and Rear Attacks: Flanking (attacking from the side)
an opponent reduces the difficulty of an attack by one. An attack
from the rear reduces the attack difficulty by two. The Storyteller
may allow the target a Perception roll to avoid the blindside,

Melee Complications

‘Aimed Strike: Justasashootistcanaim carefully tohitaspecific
partofan opponent's body, amelee weapon can be used tostrikea vital
nerve or a sensitive location. A blow to the leg can cripple an
opponent. A strike to the head can daze an otherwise unbeatable foe.

The aimed strike uses the same chart as for Trick Shots, under
Gunfight Complications, above. The one exception is the "drop
weapon" special effect. An aimed strike can force someone to
drop a simple item, but no one in melee combat gives up their
weapon so easily; use the Disarm rules, below, instead.

‘@ Disarm: Klaive duelsand otherfights involving melee weapons
are deadly affairs. Disarming an opponent is a clever means to end a
battle without loss of life or honor. The player rolls Dexterity + Melee
(add one to the weapon's normal difficulty to hit). If at least three
successes are scored, the player rolls the weapon's damage. If damage
successes exceed the opponent's Strength rating, the opponent loses
her grip on her weapon. A botch usually means the attacking
character drops his weapon instead. The target of this action suffersno
damage, whether the disarm attempt succeeds or fails.

4 Parry: Many Garou elect to block incoming blows with their
weapons rather than try to absorb the damage. A character can parry
at any time, just as he can dodge at any time, so long as there are still
dice in his Dice Pool. A character cannot parry with a weapon assmall
asa knife, but can do so with a sword, klaive or even a barstool. The

aan

from the opponent's number of attack successes. A botch on a parry
roll usually means the parrying weapon fliesfrom the character'shand.

Brawling Complications

Brawls might seem the most crude form of combat, but they
canactually be quite complicated. When did you ever know two
brawlers to stand face to face and throw punches until one fell?
Listed under each complication is the range of forms in which
each particular maneuver is restricted. The Brawling Chart lists
rolls, difficulties and damage for these brawling maneuvers.

¥v Bite (Crinos — Lupus)

This is the primary weapon of wolves, and the devastating
teeth of Garou cause aggravated wounds.

¥ Block (Homid — Crinos)

Skilled combatants can divert incoming blows with their
bodies. A charactercan elect to block instead of dodge ifshe wishes
toavoid getting hurt. A character can block atany time, just asshe
can dodge at any time, as long as there are still dice in the player's
Dice Pool. Combatants can only block fists, kicks or blunt
weapons. Sabers, axes and bullets tend to penetrate flesh that gets
in their way. Roll Dexterity + Brawl (difficulty 6). Each success
subtracts one from an opponent's number of attack successes.

‘ Body Slam (All Forms)

The most vulnerable place for opponents is to be is off their feet
and on their backs. Most won't just lie down, though; they have
to be convinced. To perform a body slam, a character charges
forward and hurls his weight into his opponent. The player rolls
Dexterity + Brawl (difficulty 7) and needs three successes for his
character to unbalance the opponent. The opponent's action
difficulties increase by two for the rest of the turn. However, if the
opponentissuccessfully struck and doesnot succeed ina Dexterity
+ Athletics roll (difficulty of the attacker's successes + 3), she falls
tothe ground. The base damage done, whether the opponent falls
down or not, equals the attacker's Strength, and each success
scored on the attack roll above three adds one.

If the attacker does not receive at least three successes, the
maneuver fails. The character falls to the ground, loses his
remaining Dice Pool for the turn and suffers one Health Level
of damage for each success fewer than three. (A botch counts

e y Des Fe eS et Mis as zero successes, three Health Levels damage).

If the attacker misses completely (the player fails the Dexterity +
Brawlroll), shefallsdownand mustspend an action getting toherfeet.

Continuing to grapple over successive turns after the first
requires the combatants to make opposed Strength + Brawl rolls
(difficulty is the other combatant's Strength + Brawl total). Who-
everaccumulates more successes may immobilize the other or break
free. If the character who wins this contest had his opponent in a
hold the previous tum, the victim suffers damage automatically the
following turn. If both combatants score the same number of
successes, neither gains the upper hand during the present turn;
neither suffers damage and the match continues into the next tum.

v Kick (Homid — Crinos)

This isa boot to the teeth; the character delivers a solid foot
to soft flesh. A kick does not cause aggravated wounds.

‘ Punch (Homid — Crinos)

Most frontier folk lack claws or razor-sharp teeth. Common
people ball up their fists and knock their opponents senseless.
Werewolves use their fists when trying to make a point without
killing their students. A punch does not cause aggravated wounds.

Special Maneuvers

Although frontier life requires one to be tough as nails, few
actually specialize in the martial arts. For every dot a
character possesses in
Brawl, the player
may select
one of the
maneuvers
listed below
or may de-
sign her
own.

It is assumed that the character has trained at the maneuvers
and they are at her disposal whenever she needs them. How-
ever, special maneuvers cannot be performed by characters in
frenzy.

Fur Gnarl (Crinos — Hispo)

Thick hide or armor can make an opponent nearly invincible.
This martial technique targets an opponent's special defenses,
making her easier to defeat. The attacker lashes out with claws,
slashing through fur and peeling off layer after layer of protective
hide. Thisattack doesan incredible amount ofdamage and shreds an
opponent's defenses. Figure damage normally, except that it is
aggravated. In addition, for every two Health Levels ofdamage rolled
(damage done before soak), the opponent loses one die from
subsequent soak rolls until he either heals (in the case of natural
armor such as fur) or gets a new suit of armor.

Fur Gnarl is often used as a pack tactic. One werewolf
performs the maneuver, leaving the opponent less resistant to
his packmates' attacks.

‘v Hamstring (Crinos — Lupus)

Anenemy can't run away if his legs are shredded. By sinking
her teeth into the tendons of an opponent's lower leg and
ripping them out, an attacker disables her opponent's move-
ment. The movements of horses or other quadrupeds is
halved byasingle ham-

Leaping Rake (Crinos)

The Garou puts all of her strength into one mighty leap,
taking her within range of her opponent fora fleeting moment.
When in range she strikes with her claws, tearing her opponent
apart as she continues to travel. The momentum of the jump
carries the attacker out of retaliatory range — unless, of course,
the victim has a gun.

The player must spend a Rage point for the character to
accomplish both a leap and an attack; one Dice Pool is used
for each. Calculate the effectiveness of the leap using the
Jumping rules, pg. 249. (Using the Automatic Success rule
might be simplest for adjudicating this jump.) If the character
succeeds in reaching the target, the player may make an
attack roll assuming a claw attack. After the attack, the
Garou sails clear of her opponent — hopefully. If the attack
fails, the force of the leap still carries her to a landing point,
wherever that may be. This attack causes aggravated wounds
unless the Garou punches instead of claws. A Leaping Punch
can be used in Glabro as well as in Crinos form.

‘v Taunt (All Forms)

Nothing breaks an opponent's concentration more than
barking, laughing, growling, leaping or mocking him. Garou
employing this maneuver roll about on the ground, leap high
into the air, and utter the most vivid insults imaginable.
Every two successes obtained subtract one die from the
opponent's next action. If more than one character uses the
Taunt maneuver on the same foe in one turn, the effects are
cumulative. It is possible for a group of characters to paralyze
an opponent through confusion and fear in this way. How-
ever, when one player fails a Taunt roll, no further Taunt rolls
affect the opponent that turn.

Victims paralyzed by taunting may only perform actions
that do not require rolls (although they may dodge). A
Garou subjected to such treatment must make a Rage roll to
avoid flying into a frenzy.

Armor

Armor is rare in the Savage West. Folks don't walk around
wearing metal shirts. Even if they did wear armor, it might not
stop a bullet. Premade armor is almost impossible to acquire;
just try to find it at a general store. Armor can be improvised
in times of need, though. It doesn't cover the body head to toe.
It can amount to a metal plate hung over the neck and worn,
undera blanket, or is limited to an animal-skin shield. Yet even
that impromptu protection can save a character's life.

When a character wears armor, her player adds dice to
soak rolls. Reinforced clothing adds one to soak rolls. A
steel plate or skin-shield adds two dice, but not to areas it
can't cover. A character can even try to wear most of a
Franklin stove for protection, but this adds two to the
difficulty of all Dexterity rolls. It does offer three dice worth
of armor. (The character will also look a blamed fool, but
some'd rather be foolish-looking than perforated.)

Combat in the Spirit
World

The spirit world of the Savage West is even more dangerous
than its physical counterpart. Numerous Wyld-spirits roam the
Storm Umbra, leaping on unwary Umbral travelers. Banes and
Weaver-spirits follow as settlers encroach ever westward.

Most physical-world creatures that wind up in the spirit world
are out of their realm. A human who has somehow become
trapped in the Umbra may swing a punch at a spirit only to
find that his hand passes harmlessly through it. Werewolves
are the exceptions to this rule. As much spiritual as they are
physical, Garou in the Umbra can attack spirits and Banes
with all the powers they possesses in the physical world. Even
their natural weaponry can affect and harm their foes.

Melee weapons and firearms are another matter

altogether. Objects to be taken into the Umbra e

Werewolf: The Wild West


=O

PE Deo

a

must first undergo the Rite of Talisman Dedication. Otherwise,
the Garou steps sideways and is empty-handed on the other side.
Furthermore, only supernaturally treated weapons or magical
attacks can affect spirits in the Umbra. Even in the hands of a
werewolf, a normal knife can't harm a spirit. Fortunately all
fetish weapons, including klaives, can strike spirits. A player
must still make a Gnosis roll to activate a character's fetish, but
the difficulty of doing so decreases by two in the Umbra.

Spirits

Spirits in the Umbra don't have Physical Attributes. In-
stead of attacking with claws or fists, they use their Willpower
and Rage scores. Spirits "strike" by rolling Willpower (diffi-
culty 6). If the roll is successful, the spirit hits its target. The

—30¢4e———_

spirit's Rage is rolled to determine damage (difficulty 6). Each
success causes one Health Level of aggravated wounds. A
werewolf can soak this damage by rolling Gnosis (difficulty 6).
The number of successes is the amount of damage soaked.

Spirits don't have Health Levels, either. They lose Power
points instead. So if one spirit attacks another, a damage roll
made with Rage reduces the target's Power score.

A spirit doesn't actually throw punches, kick or slash with
claws, although it might appear to do so. A spirit's attack is a
mystical assault of pure Rage; the spirit usually flies about its
enemy, engulfing him in its anger.

A spirit can't soak damage; it must take whatever punish-
ment that it is dealt. There is no difference between normal
and aggravated damage for a spirit.

Note: When a spirit materializes onto the physical plane, it
attacks using its materialized Attributes and Abilities. At this
point, combat proceeds normally.

Garou Maneuvers

When a Garou attacks a spirit in the Umbra, the werewolf
can use any attack maneuvers he chooses. The difficulty of a
physical attack increases by one in the Umbra. Any maneuver
with a difficulty normally based on an Attribute of the oppo-
nent (Wits, for example) uses the spirit's Willpower instead.

Damage

Calculate damage for Garou attacks normally, but
apply it to the spirit's Power. Any attack from a Garou,
punch orkick, works justas aws or fetish weapons.
When a spirit has no Power left, it dissolves out of
existence. A Garou can choose to bind a defeated spirit,
too (see the Rite of Binding in Chapter 6). Most spirits flee
when reduced to one quarter or less of their Power for fear
of being bound. But many Banes keep attacking
until either they or their opponents are dead.

Pursuit in the Umbra

When a spirit or a Garou tries to flee battle in
the Umbra, use the Pursuit rules below; the spirit
rolls its Willpower. Spirits begin with three suc-
cesses automatically because of their ability to fly
in the Umbra. Ifa fleeing spirit scores 10 successes
more than its pursuer, it gets away. If the pursuer
matches the spirit's successes, he moves alongside
the spirit and may attack it. A spirit with the
Charm: Reform may simply dissipate rather than
flee and may reform elsewhere.

Dramatic
} Systems

The Rules chapter details the mechanics of
the Storyteller system. The remainder of this

chapter describes numerous ways to extrapolate
on the basics provided in the rules.

A variety of rule permutations are described
here. However, the Storyteller may use dramatic sys-
tems of her own design. In fact, we encourage it. While
the list of systems here is extensive, it can't hope to
cover every possibility. Storytellers who wish to design
their own systems should use what follows as a model.
Furthermore, the Storyteller can modify any of these
systems to suit the flavor of the story. The important
thing to keep in mind when designing new systems or
modifying old ones is consistency. As long as

your systems remain consistent throughout

astory, the players won't feel cheated and
your world will seem more realistic.

248 Werewolf: The Wild West


The following systems are useful when determining the

outcome of physical actions and confrontations. Combat is
dealt with earlier in this chapter.
Climbing

Holding higher ground always offers an advantage, espe-
cially when your opponents don't expect an ambush from
above. Simple climbs don't require a roll; climbing anything
more difficult than a ladder does. A successful Dexterity +
Athletics roll is required when any character attempts a climb.
The difficulty is determined by the gradient of the surface, the
existence or absence of hand or footholds, and other interfer-
ing circumstances (combat, severe weather, frenzy). Each
success indicates that the character climbs five feet.

For example, Runs-Swiftly attempts to climb acliff in order
to escape a band of Black Spiral Dancer bandits. The
surface is rough, but almost perfectly vertical. Bullets
ricochet off the cliff walls all around the Garou. The
penalty for failure will surely be death — or worse.

If the playerrrolls five successes, Runs-Swiftly climbs 25
feet. A failure indicates no progress. A botch indicates the
character might fall and may not continue without expending
a Willpower point. The Storyteller decides the exact difficulty
of a climb, and this one might mean trouble for Falls-Swiftly.

Difficulty Type of Climb
2 Easy: a tree with many stout branches
4 Simple:agentlecliffwith many handholds
6 Straightforward: the side of a building
8 Treacherous: an unstable cliff face
10 Extremely difficult: a sheer, vertical
surface with no handholds

Feats of Strength

Sometimes all the bullets and all the brains in
the world simply can't get a job done. Fortunately a strong se
back and a little sweat can solve some of your problems. The if
following is several ways in which strength affects a story.

‘w Lifting: The connection to Gaia gives a werewolf
incredible strength. If there's a stagecoach in his way or a
boulder blocking the mine, he can pick it up and move it. The
chart below lists the number of Strength pointsa character must
possess to lift various objects. Ifthe character has the appropriate
Strength, with or without friendly assistance, he can lift
theobjectautomatically.Cooperatingchar-
acterscanadd their Strength scores
together to lift an object.
Ofcourse, makingroom for

a whole pack in

Crinos form is a lo-
& gistical nightmare.


&)

"Under normal ‘circumstances, a character can't lift more

than his Strength would indicate on the Feats of Strength
Chart. However, if the player wishes, she may try to exceed her
character's Strength with a Willpower roll. The difficulty is
always 9 and the character only gets one shot at it. Every
success indicates that the character's effective Strength in-
creases by one for the contest in question, up to a maximum of
five additional points. Failure indicates a wasted effort. A
botch causes one Health Level of damage for every | in the roll.

‘w Throwing: If a Garou has just picked up a rock, horse or
stagecoach, why not throw itat somebody? Stagecoaches make
effective weapons.

The difference between a werewolfs Strength and the
rating of the object indicates the number of dice remaining
with which to throw the item. (This Dice can never exceed a
character's Dexterity + Athletics, no matter what.) Difficulty
is 6, barring any interference. The combat section covers rules
regarding throwing weapons.

For example, Stanson, a powerful Silver Fang, has picked up
alarge rock that weighs about 600 pounds (Strength 5 required
to lift it). Stanson's Strength is 8 in Crinos form. If he throws
the rock, his player rolls three dice to make an attack.

If the rating of an object ties or exceeds a Garou's Strength,
he may attempt a Willpower roll (or perhaps a second one if
Willpower was used to lift the object) to increase his Dice Pool
temporarily, as detailed under Lifting. If the Willpower roll fails,
the character is forced to drop the rock. If the roll botches, he
drops it on himself and suffers damage as described under Lifting.

‘w Opening and Closing: Some things aren't meant to be
opened and other things aren't meant to be closed. Whether a
werewolf tries to pry open a safe or push a jammed door closed,

rating of the task at hand. Remember, if the Garou's Strength
exceeds the rating of the task, she automatically succeeds. The
player may also attempt a Willpower roll to increase the
Garou's effective Strength.

Clever werewolves quickly discover that levers and tools
make jobs easier. Appropriate use of tools increases a werewolf's
effective Strength, as deemed appropriate by the Storyteller.
Generally, a lever or pry bar can increase a Dice Pool by at least
one-half, if not more.

Horse Training

Assuming a horse has not already bolted at the mere scent
of a werewolf, commanding it to perform requires significant
talent. To perform stunts on horseback requires a Dexterity +
Ride roll. However, the player may add her character's Ride
Skill only up to the level of Animal Ken score.

The difficulty of such a stunt roll depends on the complexity of
the feat, the training and temperament of the animal, and any
other variables that might disturb or distract the horse. Use the
Horse Training Chart to determine the base difficulty of the effort.
If the horse has been trained for a particular stunt, the difficulty is
reduced. Ifthe animal has performed the trick numerous times, the
Storyteller might do away with the roll altogether.

Training a horse to obey commands, perform stunts or to
remain calm inacombatsituation requires a lot of patience and
skill. A horse can be trained by an expert to perform many

tricks at the command of its owner, although it will

not solve problems creatively for the character. To
train a horse requires the Animal Ken Skill and time.

Werewolf: The Wild West


Stunt

Respond to a summons

Return toa specific point

Kick on command

Bite on command

Throw rider

"9. Ride fearlessly into combat
Open'a door

cone oro

After the character spends several days or months training, the
player rolls Manipulation + Animal Ken. Again, the difficulty
depends on the complexity of the "trick." If the feat is some-
thing the horse might do naturally, the difficulty should be
lower than if the stunt involves complex actions.

For example, Stanson wants to train his horse to kick on
command. Stanson knows the animal well and has years of experi-
ence training horses. The animal takes to the training, as horses
normally kick out when frightened. The tricky part is getting the
horse to kick on command. If Stanson wanted his horse to open
doors on command, that would be even more difficult to teach.

The Horse Training Chart lists a few stunts and the difficulty
required to train the animal. Thischart can be used forotheranimals
as well, but difficulties might be higher or lower depending on the
intelligence of the animal. Mules are intelligent but stubborn. Dogs
are amenable to training but aren't necessarily all that intelligent.

Jumping

Jumping is a simple action; the character uses all of her
strength to launch herself into the air. The player rolls Strength
in dice against a difficulty of 3 (unless circumstances such as
loose gravel or desert sand make the jump more difficult). The
Storyteller calculates how many successes the jump requires
and compares that to the player's roll.

Jumping is an all-or-nothing action. A character either
makes it or falls short, literally and sometimes painfully. If a
werewolf attempts a horizontal jump with a running start, the
Storyteller might allow the player to add Athletics score to the
Strength dice rolled (Strength + Athletics, difficulty 3).

The following chart details how each of the Garou forms
affects the outcome of a jump roll.

Long-Distance Running

The wide-open, undeveloped country of the Savage West
makes long-distance running one of the most reliable forms of
travelavailable to Garou. Wolvesare renowned fortheirstamina
and ability to run for long periods without rest. Werewolves are

even more capable than their cousins, driven by their Rage
as much as their legs. Other than riding horseback or
taking the occasional train, long-distance running is
& the only way to travel great distances.

Feet Per Success

9 _Type of Jump

Homid Glabro  Crinos Hispo Lupus
Vertical 2 3 4 5 4
Horizontal 4 4, 5 6 7
ce eal 4

Werewolves in Lupus form are able to trot at about 16 miles an
hour fora number of hours equal to their Stamina. After that time
they must rest or begin making rolls to keep pace. The player rolls

. Stamina + Athletics (difficulty 4) for every hour beyond the

character's normal limitation. The difficulty can be adjusted by
adverse conditions such as intense heat, rough terrain or cold. If
a rolls fails, the character suffers one Health Level of aggravated
damage from strained muscles and overexertion. If the player fails
two rolls in a row, or botches, she must spend a Rage or Willpower
point for the Garou to continue. At that point, all difficulties for
the run are increased by two until the Garou rests.

Despite pack mentality, sometimes survival of the fittest
dictates that if a werewolf can't keep up, he'll be left behind.

Pursuit

When a character tries to catch up to bandits before they get
away with his prize fetish, he better be quick. Ina short-distance
foot race, one opponent starts with a number of successes as
deemed appropriate by the Storyteller or as indicated by a
Dexterity + Athletics roll (difficulty 6), made for each turn
before the pursuit starts. In each turn after the pursuer begins the
chase, both parties roll Dexterity + Athletics (difficulty 6) and

different than a foot race. Determining whether the
hunter catches up with hissprey involves combining the
tules for long-distance running and pursuit. This system
assumes that the predator knows the prey's course orfinal
destination. If the subject'sfocation is unknown, she has
to be tracked. In this casé, use the Tracking rules below,
instead'of these:
‘Each party, hunter and prey, rolls Stamina,+ Ath-
_ letics (difficulty 4) for each day of travel. The prey *
begins with a number of successes equal to the number.
of days she has:as a lead. When the hunter accumu-
