successes plus her lead successes, he has caught up to
\ within a few miles‘of her: The difficulty and the,rolls
f might be modified by several variables: weather, health,

whether either party has. transportation, intensity of
pace, and familiarity with terrain.


accumulate successes. When the hunter overcomes his deficit,
he has caught up with his opponent. What happens next is
probably best covered in the combat section.

The subject of pursuit escapes if he achieves a certain
number of successes (say 10) or when he gains a certain number
f es more than his pursuer has achieved (say five). The
Storyteller has final say when the subject of pursuit gets away.

Repairs
While Savage West technology doesn't compare to that of the
20th century, railroads do mar virgin countryside, telegraph lines

do cut through forests, and new weaponry does appear in the
ay plains almost daily. Modern technology often falls prey to the
a
nie
\<

elements and the rugged conditions of the Savage West. If a
character wants to fix something, the player rolls Dexterity
+ Crafts or Science, depending on the nature of the
busted doohickey. The difficulty and the required
number of successes are based on the complexity of
the device and the severity of the damage suffered.
Once the player rolls the total number of successes
required, the character's work is done.
Obviously the more successes required, the
more time a task takes. In a combat situation,
time is vitally important (for example, one char-
acter tries to get the train running again while

another holds off the Wyld-spirits that converge on
it). If the situation is more relaxed, a character can
take her time with a repair and make sure it's done
right. The Storyteller decides how much time passes between
repair rolls (turns, hours, days) based on the situation

If the character doesn't know anything about the device she
hopes to fix, the Storyteller should call for Intelligence or Wits
+ Crafts or Science rolls just to figure out what the heck

the problem is. This system also works when damage
may not be obvious.

Of course, there are time when even the most suc-
cessful rolls won't fix things. Ifa vital part is destroyed in
the middle of the desert, it's not likely that characters can
find a replacement or even improvise a quick fix. Certain types of
characters may not be able to attempt repairs, either. A lupus fresh
out of the wild won't know how to fix a plow blade.
Riding
Horses are an important part of life in the Savage West.
Not only do they provide transportation and com-
panionship, they are also a status symbol.
Owning a fine horse equates to owning a
Porsche, but even owning a haggard
old mare is better than walking.
For the people who can't
shapechange into wolves,

horses supply

the majority of

their transportation. ®


Depending on the chronicle's time frame, the railroad may not
be that prevalentan influence, if present at all. Certainly, most
townsfolk would laugh at the very idea of the automobile.

Horses, unlike trains or cars, have individual personalities;
they're living creatures, after all. Regrettably they're skittish, and
werewolves only make matters worse. Garou are born predators;
ahorse can sense the presence of a werewolf just as if the character
were a wolf and the horse was back in the wild. When horses are
nervous, they dance, whinny and sometimes bolt. Garou stir up
horses tied to hitching posts just by walking down a street.

In general, if a werewolf's Rage exceeds his Willpower,
assume that horses react violently in his presence. If a Garou's
Willpower exceeds his Rage and he doesn't pose an obvious
threat, horses are visibly nervous, but might not panic.

Since merely approaching horse inspires a nervous reaction,
it'sno wonder that werewolves often have trouble riding horses.
Ahorse will likely throw a strange Garou who attempts to mount
it, and may even deal out a hoof to the head for good measure.
Many werewolves use their Gifts to make peace with their
horses, or they choose to walk. Over time a werewolf can, with
enough patience and training, gain control over an animal.

Eventually, if treated properly, a horse can be acclimated to a
rider. Oncea the creature overcomes its natural fear, most normal
equestrian actions do not require a roll. This doesn't mean that the
horse has forgotten it hasa wolfon its back; it has just become used
to it. However, when trouble arises, the horse is more likely to
panic under a werewolf than under a normal person. The Story-
teller should increase all riding difficulties for werewolves by two
for this reason. A patient character can compensate for (or even
negate) this penalty with time, Gifts or good training.

Shadowing

Following someone in the small towns of the Savage West
presents many difficulties. Following someone closely in the
open wilderness borders on impossible. There are two require-
ments to covertly shadowing someone: keeping track of the
target and not being spotted.

Shadowing someone in a city or town requires numerous
Perception + Investigation rolls to keep track of the target. The
base difficulty is 6, but crowds, distractions and the erratic
movements of the prey increase that difficulty.

Keeping out of sight while shadowing requires a Dexterity +
Stealth roll (difficulty equals the prey's Perception + Alertness
total). This roll must be made whenever an opportunity arises
for the target to recognize his shadow.

Most Savage Western towns are small enough that it doesn't
take long to trail a target to his destination. However, failing the
shadow's Perception rolls indicates that the target is lost. Failure
on Stealth rolls indicates that the prey is aware of the predator.
In either case, the Storyteller should determine exactly what
happens and how the one being pursued reacts.

Shadowing someone in the countryside usually requires
the ability to track a target, follow its trail. The wide

@ open plains deny a close shadow. Use the Tracking

tules, pg. 260, for wilderness shadowing.

The defining characteristic of a werewolf is the ability to
shapeshift. Garou aren't the only creatures that can change
shape; they're just the best at it. Garou can change into five
different forms, each of which has its own advantages and
disadvantages: Homid (Human), Glabro (Near-Man), Crinos
(Wolf-Man), Hispo (Near-Wolf), and Lupus (Wolf).

A Garou always assumes her breed form (Homid, Lupus or
Crinos) automatically. However, any other change requires a
Stamina + Primal Urge roll. The difficulty depends on the form
the character wishes to change. The character needs multiple
successes in order to change shape — it's not that easy to double
your mass in three seconds. The player must acquire a number of
successes equal to the number of intermediary forms plus final
form plus one for the character to make the transformation in
one turn. If he doesn't get enough successes, the number rolled
determines what shape the character stops. Further attempts can
be made in subsequent turns based on the current form of the
Garou. Complete failure on the roll means the character doesn't
change at all. A botch indicates that the Garou has lost control
and has altered her body painfully. The grotesque transforma-
tion causes one aggravated wound per extra | rolled.

For example, Runs-Swiftly is in Lupus form and stumbles
onto what appears to be a Cherokee camp. In the dim firelight,
he can see several human forms preparing for a hunting party.
Not wanting to be mistaken for dinner, he decides to change
into human form. His player needs five successes (three inter-
mediary forms, his target Homid form + one) to complete the
transformation. He gets only four successes, meaning that
Runs-Swiftly shifts through Hispo and Crinos, but stops at
Glabro. Runs pauses a bit longer to try again. This time he only
needs two successes to shift from Glabro to Homid.

Upon approaching his would-be companions, Runs-Swiftly
discovers them to actually be Bane-infested zombies. He auto-
matically switches to Lupus, his breed form, and takes to the hills.

Clothing and restraining articles of any kind are usually de-
stroyed in a transformation. If such items have been dedicated to
aGarouspiritually (see Rite of Talisman Dedication, pg. 173) they
change along with the werewolf. Nondedicated objects that are
too solid to break (such as handcuffs or barbed wire) injure the
werewolf foolish enough to shapeshift while restrained by them.

In a particularly stressful situation, a player may elect to
spend a Rage point for his character to change shape immedi-
ately. The transformation takes place automatically despite
the number of intermediary forms. This kind of change is
usually undertaken when combat is imminent. Remember that
Garou with no Rage or Willpower cannot shapeshift into any
form but their breed forms; they've "lost the wolf."

When changing forms, werewolves gain the benefits that
come from being larger, faster and stronger. However, the
intermediary forms, Glabro, Hispo and Crinos, complicate
social life with humans and wolves. Each form adjusts a
character's Attributes; these adjustments should be applied to
a character's Homid statistics.

Chapter Mine: Drama 261

7T


SSSSSN

Itshould be noted that Appearance is subjective. While the
Crinos form's Appearance is zero, other werewolves still recog-
nize the inherent beauty of a highly attractive werewolf. When
a Garou in Crinos form interacts with other werewolves, the
werewolf retains his full Appearance rating. Crinos form scares
the living daylights out of humans and wolves, though. With
that in mind, remember that werewolves retain their full
Manipulation scores when intimidating opponents (see In-
timidation, p.254), be they human, wolf or Garou.

Homid (Human)

Statistics Adjustments: None

Shift Difficulty: 6

Description: The standard form that most characters use to
interact with the human world. Most machines, including guns,
can be used only while in this form. A werewolf cannot regen-
erate in Homid form, but may handle silver without difficulty.

Roleplaying: The Homid form is indistinguishable from an
ordinary human in almost every way. Werewolves in Homid can
understand the Garou tongue (see Languages and Customs,
below), but can only speak a few Garou words with difficulty.

Glabro (Near-Man)

Statistics Adjustments: Strength +2, Stamina +2, Ma-
nipulation -1, Appearance -1

Shift Difficulty: 7

Description: With the increase in Strength and Stamina
comes an appropriate increase in body size and mass. The now
heavily muscled body towers over human form by about six to
nine inches. The character no longer resembles a human; hair
grows in spots over his body, and sharp teeth and nails sprout.
A Glabro werewolf can hide some of his beastly features by dim
firelight, but there's no mistaking the creature as something
other than human in broad daylight.

Roleplaying: While still close to human in some ways, the
Glabro form imposes some bestial personality traits. Conversa-
tion is possible but words are gruff and guttural. Glabro
werewolves can speak the Garou language with some difficulty.

Crinos (Wolf-Man)

Statistics Adjustments: Strength +4, Stamina +3, Dexter-
ity +1, Manipulation -3, Appearance 0

Shift Difficulty: 6

Description: AsGaia's chosen warriors, Garou have the ability
to change into the ultimate fighting machine. This terrifying
transformation combines the ferocity of the wolf and the cunning
of the human. A Garou in Crinos form doubles or even triples in
mass and nearly doubles in height. Any resemblance to human
form vanishes as the werewolf grows thick fur over every inch of
his body and gains a wolfish snout, ears and a tail. Razor-sharp
fangs and claws grow long and deadly. The Garou's reach extends
as his arms lengthen and become more massive, suitable for
running on all fours or grappling with enemies.

Roleplaying: The embodiment of the killer, a werewolf in
Crinos form doesn't persuade or negotiate — he destroys. Even
simple human-speak is difficult; Crinos form limits a werewolf
to grunts, snarls and two-word sentences. Wolf-speech is just as
difficult. The Garou language, however, comes easily and
naturally. (The reduction in Manipulation applies even when
speaking the Garou tongue, as Crinos form prohibits pro-
tracted conversation and any form of subtlety.) A Crinos
werewolf may speak one clear sentence in either human or wolf
speech with the expenditure of a Willpower point.

Hispo (Near-Wolf)

Statistics Adjustments: Strength +3, Stamina +3, Dexter-
ity +2, Manipulation -3, Appearance 0

Shift Difficulty: 7

Description: While Crinos form may be ideal for combat,
Hispo is perfect for hunting. This form combines massive size,
keen senses and powerful jaws. A throwback to the prehistoric
dire wolf, the Hispo's hulking frame is something out of a bad
dream. Head and jaws grow to nightmarish size, while a Hispo's
four-legged stance lends it great speed and mobility (and it can
stand on two feet in emergencies). The Hispo also benefits
from the keen senses of wolves (reduce Hispo-Perception
difficulties by one).

Roleplaying: The Hispo does not possess hands and cannot
speak any languages but the Garou or wolf tongues, and even
then communication with wolves and other Garou suffers from
poor social skills and a general lack of good graces. Hispo form's
only purpose is to hunt. Intelligent interaction with humanity
is virtually impossible.

Lupus (Wolf)

Statistics Adjustments: Strength +1, Stamina +2, Dexter-
ity +2, Manipulation -3

Shift Difficulty: 6

Description: The other half of the human-wolf equation,
Lupus form resembles that of a wolf. Each of the tribes re-
sembles a different breed of wolf, but Lupus form is always
similar. Lupus form is smaller and sleeker than the prehistoric
Hispo. Lupus teeth are deadly, though; they still cause aggra-
vated wounds. A Garou''s senses in Lupus form are his sharpest.
All Perception-related difficulties are reduced by two.

Werewolves in this form may also take advantage of
instinctual senses that the other forms lack. A Lupus werewolf
can see in near-darkness and has exceptional hearing and
incredible olfactory awareness. Werewolves in Lupus form
can also use Primal-Urge as a "sixth sense" to be on guard for
traps and ambushes and to sense the temperaments of other
animals. (Using any of these senses calls for Perception +
Primal-Urge rolls.)

Roleplaying: Instinct drives the Lupus werewolf,
almost as if the Garou had completely become his

canine cousin. Werewolves in Lupus are likely to ®

he Wild West


flee froma fight out of sheer instinct rather than stay and claw
it out. Stray scents, noises or movements may distract or even
unnerve a Lupus Garou. Lupus werewolves cannot speak
human tongues at all, but may communicate freely with
wolves. They have access to the Garou language but can only
string together a few words at a time.

Partial Transformation

Although it is painful, difficult and rarely attractive, a
Garou with superior command over shapeshifting may trans-
form parts of her body. A Crinos werewolf might shapeshift her
claw into a hand in order to hold and fire a gun, or shapeshift
her eyes to Lupus form to improve her night vision. A Homid
werewolf might change her ears for improved hearing, or grow
some Lupus fur to protect herself from the elements.

Partial transformations require the expenditure of a Will-
power point and a successful Dexterity + Primal-Urge roll
(difficulty 9). Failure indicates a wasted effort, while a botch
causes lasting physical damage (one aggravated wound per
extra | after failure).

Sneaking

Garou don't usually want to be seen when they skulk into
Bane-infested mines or when they sneak around in the shad-
ows waiting for unwitting vampires. When acharacter attempts
to hide in shadows, sneak through woods or the back alleys of
a town, the player rolls Dexterity + Stealth (difficulty of the
would-be spotter's Perception + Alertness). Atthe Storyteller's
discretion, Stealth may be limited to a character's Survival
score when the Garou is in the wilderness.

The amount of time spent hiding or the distance of ground
that needs to be stealthily covered determines the number of
successes required. If the character remains within perception
of her opponent the player may need to continue rolling as long
as there is a chance the Garou might be spotted. Failure
indicates that the character has been heard or seen. A botch
leaves the character high and dry, in plain view after snapping
a twig or knocking over a liquor cabinet.

Social
The following systems are based on interactions involving
the three Social Attributes: Charisma, Manipulation and
Appearance. While roleplaying takes precedent over any
systematized die rolling, rolling dice in social situations
balances a player's skills with a her character's. While the
player might make a great delivery, her character may lack
the needed social awareness and botch the job. Conversely,
the character may possess skills far beyond the abilities of the
player to emulate. In general, the Storyteller should call for
Social Skill rolls only when the difference between the skills
of player and character differ greatly. Also, the Storyteller
might demand rolls simply to get an objective measure
of a character's talent, even if the player roleplays

& close to the mark.

Chapter Nine: Drama

Garou are both human and animal. On one side, they must
deal with social graces. On the other side, they must contend
with the pack mentality. Werewolves, as the ultimate preda-
tors, threaten all living things by virtue of their very existence.
Overcoming that fear can be difficult. This explains why
werewolves tend to stick to their own.

Yet the animal in a werewolf can attract members of the
opposite sex. Wolves sense a superior creature and potential
mate; humans feel their primal urges stir. Whether it affects a
wolf or human, primal attraction is determined by rolling
Charisma + Primal-Urge (difficulty of the Garou's own Rage).
If the subject is aloof, the Storyteller may require several rolls
over a period of turns and a number of successes equal to the
subject's Willpower. Werewolves use their animal attraction
to mate despite their fear-evoking presence. A failure causes
fear and discomfort; a botch invokes the Delirium.

Animal attraction is not a seduction effort, but simply a
reflection of a character's magnetism and natural ability to
attract members of the opposite sex. It does not apply when
a Garou's Rage exceeds his Willpower score; the predator is
too much at the fore, more frightening than attractive. But
even if a character's Willpower exceeds his Rage, animal
attraction rolls shouldn't be made in every social situation.
The Garou can use his magnetism to attract partners, or the
Storyteller can call for rolls (or make secret ones herself) to
determine if the werewolf has unintentionally attracted
would-be companions.

Fast-Talk

Sometimes the best way to get out of trouble is to run your
mouth and leave your pistol in its holster. Better to talk the
marshal into believing that you're not who he thinks you
are, and that the person he's looking for has taken off for
Dodge. Overwhelm him with more information than he
ever wanted to know, as quickly as possible, and he just
might just let you off easy.

The player rolls Manipulation + Subterfuge (or Cha-
risma or Appearance or Performance) against a difficulty of
the target's Wits + Subterfuge. The difficulty should be
reduced if the player roleplays his character's efforts con-
vincingly. The number of successes rolled indicates how
confused the subject is, and how likely she is to agree with
the character. Failure on the roll means the person disre-
gards the character's line of bull or at least gets a chance to
interrupt. Once a victim sees through the character's smoke
screen, no further fast-talk attempts affect him. A botch
might get the character hanged.

Fast-talk can be used repeatedly to continue to confuse
the same person, assuming preceding efforts were success-
ful. Eventually the poor victim is left standing and staring
like a fool, which gives characters a chance to pass by or
make a getaway.


SSSSSN

Interrogation

The law in the Savage West extends only as far as the local sheriff
can shoot. Beyond that, only personal justice can set things right.
Taking matters into your own hands might be not only wrong, but
necessary. Aftera werewolf has found the outlaw who's been poisoning
the well, or the murderer of his Kinfolk, he might want to find out who
thecriminal is workingfor. The law might punish the perpetrator of the
crimes, but probably won't go after the railroad baron behind the plot.
A Garou tends to find those responsible, and do something about it
himself. Setting thingsright often involves gaining information, and in
the Savage West that means talking to people, perhaps aggressively.

There are numerous ways to get information from those who
won't volunteer it, Interrogation involves a complex mix of
questioning, cajoling, threatening and making promises that may
or may not be kept. Torture might work, but that's likely to lead
to a bad reputation, and is on no account good for one's Honor.

The player roleplays the questions and states the threats or
promises that her character makes. The Storyteller should roll the
Garou's Manipulation + Intimidation (difficulty equal to the victim's
Willpower). The number of successes indicates how much informa-
tion the victim reveals, and how accurate it is. One success indicates
minimal, garbled information. Five successes strikes the mother lode.
The victim may spend Willpower to hold his tongue, but if the
interrogator is patient, the captive's will should erode eventually.

Failure at an interrogation roll leaves the character as ignorant
as when she started. If the player botches, the character never gets
anything out of her prisoner — or worse, is given false information.

Intimidation

Any man don't wanna get killed better clear on out the back.

— Clint Eastwood, Unforgiven

Werewolves, by their very nature, intimidate almost every
creature that comes in contact with them. They are among the
most frightening creatures in the West. The very sight of a ten-
foot werewolf can make even a seasoned gunslinger wet his
trousers. Intimidation can be used ina variety of ways, most often
to get your own way when asking nicely proves ineffective.

Thebasicresultsofintimidation usually come intoeffectautomati-
cally where Garou are concemed. No roll is required. Most humans
are overcome by the Deliriumand most animals flee. However, when
a werewolf wants to manipulate this effect to achieve a specific goal,
the player rolls Manipulation + Intimidation. The Storyteller deter-
mines the difficulty (which is modified by the Garou's form), the
settingand the quality of roleplaying. Difficultiesrange between 4and
9. The Storyteller may also allow the player to spend a Rage point to
getan automatic success; Rage is plenty terrifying in its own right, and
sometimes just a hint at the savage anger within does the trick.

The results of a successful intimidation roll vary. An abun-
dance of successes incapacitates a victim, turning him into a
gibbering mass after the werewolf learns what he wants; the
Delirium can easily kick in. Between one and five successes
usually suffices to intimidate someone.

The Manipulation penalty imposed by some shapeshifting
forms does not apply to intimidation attempts.

254

Languages and Customs

Knowing your way around the Savage West requires knowing
a lot about its people. The best way to learn about people is to
learn their languages and customs. Numerous native tribes, each
with its own language and ways, roam free throughout the plains
and mountains. Mexican society influences much of the Ameri-
can Southwest. And then there are all those Easterners who seek
to settle the plains.

Multiple Charisma + Linguistics, Culture or perhaps Eti-
quette rolls may be required when meeting strangers. Maybe
they've had trouble with yourkind—shapeshifters, thatis. Ifyou
don't know their language and ways, good luck getting by.

Not only do werewolves have to contend with multitudes of
human languages and ways, they must also communicate with
wolves and each other. Lupus-born werewolves may speak freely
with their wolf brethren, but must be taught human languages.
All werewolves can instinctively speak with wolves, but only
when they're in Hispo or Lupus. To overcome these barriers,
Garou have developed a language that they all can speak. To
humans, the language would seem to be a combination of grunts,
snarls and half-articulated words. Werewolves in Lupus or
Homid form have difficulty with the Garou language, but even
they can use it to some extent.

The language skills of the various forms are discussed under
Shapeshifting.

Leadership

Some Garou feel they were born to sit on a throne and lord
over all others. Wolves instinctively look to the strongest,
fastest and smartest to make decisions. The same applies to
werewolves. It takes a certain amount of natural talent to make
others believe that your decisions are the best. While snarling
and biting might work for wolves, most Garou need better
reasons to follow someone.

A pack's alpha commands the group and should expect
nothing short of obedience. If there is disagreement at a crucial
moment, the entire pack may be lost. However, a poor choice of
leader is just as likely to lead to disaster. Werewolves change
leaders when it is obvious that the old alpha has become weak or
foolish. There isa time and place for such upheaval, though. Too
many packs have been destroyed because one Garou has chal-
lenged the alpha's leadership when danger loomed.

Attemptingtoleadapackorany group requiresstrongroleplaying
and successful Manipulation + Leadership rolls. Difficulties vary by
circumstance, but are usually 7. A strong leader is invaluable in a
combat situation. The ability to make quick decisions and order
members of the pack to execute them can mean the difference
between living and dying. A successful Leadership roll in combat
can also allow a pack to coordinate attacks (see General Compli-
cations, pg. 237), as long as the pack has practiced or fought
together before. If the other characters obey the alpha without
question, noroll isnecessary. The lead character may wish
to order Storyteller characters around as well. In that
case, the player has to roll to succeed. *

Werewolf: The Wild West


Performance

Whether used to tell stories around a campfire or to bringa bit
of culture to a bitter and hateful territory, this system provides

auseful measure of a character's ability to entertain. The act
can be formal (on stage or in front of tribal elders), or
=> informal (as when entertaining friends).

The player doesn't have to sing or dance
himself (unless he wants to and possesses such
skill); a description of the display suffices. The
player rolls the appropriate Attribute + Performance
(or Expression) at a difficulty set by the
Storyteller. The Attribute depends on the
act (Dexterity for juggling, Charisma for
singing, Wits for comedy and so on). Difficulty
depends on the receptiveness of the audience. If a
Garou quotes Shakespeare in front of a bunch of
ignorant tin-pans, she better be good if she wants any
response at all (gunshots notwithstanding). The num-
ber of successes rolled indicates the quality of the
performance and the response of the audience.

% Seduction

Taking advantage of one's natural
appeal can get a character into a lot of
{7 trouble, but can also help her get her
way. The player establishes the tech-
nique that is used to seduce the target. The
Storyteller and player need to roleplay the
e, but the dice help establish the outcome of
the event. A word of warning: Subtle, patient seduc-
tion isn't always easy for characters with Rage, so Garou
might be better off trying to use their animal attraction.

The roll depends on the seduction technique used:
Appearance + Subterfuge for straightforward physical
attraction; Wits + Subterfuge for witty, playful banter;
Charisma + Empathy for endearing conversation. Any

one of these techniques can accomplish the desired
Y goal. They also work well in unison and the seduc-
tion effort should become increasingly

| heer easieras the character succeedsat various
L) methods. What happens next isn't suit-

able for young audiences.

Staredowns
Staredownsare staples of the Savage West
gunfight and many other intense one-on-
onesituations. They can be used tounnerve
opponents in order to gain an edge or they
can beused topreventconflictaltogether. A
mean stare brings out the yellow in folks.
Wolves use staredowns to determine su-
= s premacy without loss of life; they establish their
pecking order when the strong dominates the weak.
Two wolves face off, growling and bristling until one

Chapter Wine: Drama 256


backs down. Wolves also use this technique when measuring
potential enemies and prey, waiting for a victim to lose nerve
before striking. Humans and Garou can achieve similar results.

When a staredown begins, those involved roll Rage, or Manipu-
lation + Intimidation (whichever is higher) at a difficulty of the
opponent's Willpower. The first of the opponents to accumulate a
number ofsuccesses equal to the other's Wits + 5 forces that opponent
to break contact. The victor may choose to press the issue further by
intimidating his opponent (see Intimidation, above) or by taking
advantage of hisopponent's broken concentration and filling him full
of lead (see Dueling and Staredown, and Quick-Draw).

Garou of high Rank have an advantage when staring down
other werewolves. The staredown difficulty of a lower-Rank
Garou increases by one for every two levels (rounded up) differ-
ence between the Garou's Ranks. Losing a staredown toa human
causes a Garou to lose a point of Glory Renown; skinchangers are
just supposed to have more mettle than ordinary folks.

Swindling

There's money to be made in the Savage West; fortunes are
found, lost and stolen every day. In order toremain on the fortunate
end of the stick, a character needs to be clever and cunning — or at
least more so than his intended victim. A properly executed scam
usually involves selling something to someone who is too ignorant
or greedy for his own good. This something can be the deed to a
tapped mine or 40 acres of prime real-estate that actually belongs to

the most powerful rancher in the area. Whatever the case may be,
greed makes the con-man's|ifea lot easier and many hucksters have
crawled out from under their rocks to cash in.

Ifa character wishes to pull the wool over someone's
make a few dollars, the player needs to describe preparations
made to the Storyteller. These might include false or stolen

yes to

documents or producing the character's "share" of an invest-
ment or a cooperative party or two.

After preparation work is completed, the player must roleplay
thescene through with the Storyteller. If the scam seems sound and
convincing, the Storyteller might not even need to make a roll. If
the player slips up or the victim catches on, the Storyteller rolls the
character'sCharisma + Subterfuge (difficulty isthe opponent's Wits
+ Streetwise). The number of successes determines the likelihood
of the mark falling for the scam. Failure indicates doubt or disinter-
est. A botch might win the shuckster the business end of a pistol.

Mental

The following systems account for the ways in which players
most often use the mental abilities of their characters. Many
characters possess knowledge that players may not have, and
players may know things that are outside character under-
standing. The Storyteller must balance a player's
knowledge with his character's. Players should solve
problems and puzzles on their own, but should be
allowed the benefit of their characters' experiences. *»

Werewolf: The Wild West


Awareness

Watching your back may be paranoid, but sometimes being
paranoid keeps you alive. To determine if a character notices what
occurs around her or that she has just walked into a trap, roll
Perception + Alertness. The difficulty depends on the subtly of
events or the skill of the person setting the trap. (Note: The
Shadowing rules, pg. 251, use a target's Perception + Alertness as
the difficulty fora stalker to avoid detection. The Storyteller also
has the option to allow the target Perception + Alertness rolls to
spot her stalker, depending on the circumstances of the hunt.)

Werewolves have enhanced senses in their wolflike Hispo and
Lupus forms. Difficulty modifiers for Perception rolls are reduced
by one for Hispo Garou, and by two for Lupus-form Garou.

Wolvesandskinchangersin Lupusform can hearandsee things
imperceptible to normal humans, and can even communicate by
scent alone. Werewolves in this form can switch Primal-Urge for
Alertness in awareness tests, if players wish. Garou in Lupus form
can sometimes sense danger before it hits. The Storyteller deter-
mines if and when this danger sense is applicable. The roll should
only be made if some vital clue, scent or movement could give the
danger away. The Storyteller makes an Alertness roll for a
character to determine if she detects impending danger.

Cheating

Winning at cards sometimes requires an unfair advantage.
Cheating may be dishonorable, but when you need to win, you
need to win. Cheating uses both mental and social abilities. If a
character wishes to apply some underhanded tactic, the player rolls
Wits + Subterfuge. The Storyteller sets the difficulty equal to the
highest Perception + Subterfuge score of the others at the table,
whether playing or watching. For every success rolled, the charac-
ter wins a game. The Storyteller should determine the exact dollar
amounts won. If the character wants to play for high stakes, the
player's difficulty should increase. If a character continues to cheat
thesame opponents, the player's difficulty increasesby one foreach
roll after the first. Greedy cheaters often become dead cheaters.

To lower the difficulty of a cheat attempt, the character can
try to distract her opponents. This should be roleplayed, possibly
using actual cards and the other players as the marks at the table.
Once the player has established how the others are distracted
(seduction, comedy, long boring stories), she rolls Charisma +
Subterfuge at a difficulty equal to the highest Willpower of the
opponents. Each success on this roll reduces the difficulty of the
player's cheat roll by one. In the case of a botch, each | rolled
increases the cheat difficulty by one; the character draws too
much attention to herself and might get caught!

Forgery

Forging documents, whether they are deeds to land or
warrants for arrest, requires a steady hand and the proper tools.
In order for a character to properly falsify a document, the
Storyteller makes a Dexterity + Larceny roll on the
player's behalf. Difficulty is based on the complexity

£ of the document.

Chapter Wine: Drama

If the character doesn't have a copy of the document that
she wishes to forge, the player rolls Intelligence + Investigation
(difficulty 6). This determines whether or not the character
has an idea of what the proper document looks like. The
number of successes rolled is the limit to the number of dice
that may be used in the player's forgery roll.

If a forgery roll made by the Storyteller fails outright, the
character probably realizes that she has made a mistake. If the
roll botches, the character doesn't realize she has made a fatal
error that will be recognized by all but the ignorant or blind.

Anyone familiar with successfully forged documents rolls
Perception + Investigation against a difficulty of the forger's
Dexterity + Larceny. (This detection roll doesn't apply to any
mark on the street, but rather to lawyers, bankers, accountants
and the like.) If the investigator's roll succeeds, the ruse is
recognized. Otherwise the document passes inspection.

Gambling

In the Savage West, a lot of blood has been spilled when
there's money on the table and someone got a little too lucky.
There are all sorts of games to bet money on, from the roulette
wheels in the sleekest casinos to stud poker in the seediest
sodbusters' cabins. Sometimes you can even make a buck.

The simplest forms of gambling can always be resolved with
a die roll, any die roll will do, really, as long as the odds are
heavily stacked in the house's favor. A character may have to
roll two nines or better on two dice to reach the long odds of
the roulette wheel, or maybe even two tens on two dice to make
that one-in-a-hundred shot. That's games of pure chance,
though. The Storyteller may allow a character to make a
straight-up Wits roll, difficulty 10, to accurately guess where to
put his money in a given game.

Of course, poker itself isn't as much a game of luck as it is skill.
To simulate a hand of poker, the Storyteller may have each player
involved in the game make two rolls; one Wits + Empathy,
difficulty 8 (to guess when to fold or call) and one Manipulation
+ Empathy, difficulty 7 (to properly bluff or conceal the strength
of one's hand). The player with the most successes wins the hand.
Toadd inalittle more of Lady Luck's favor, the Storyteller may add
a set number of dice to everyone's Dice Pools (three is usually
about right), to simulate the element of chance.

Perhaps the surest way to act out a poker game is to actually
deal out a few hands and play while in character. This game-
within-a-game can be pretty relaxing, and it can show you who
your real friends are.

Gamecraft

This is a form of riddle and puzzle solving in which contes-
tants ask each other questions or play a mental game until one
of the participants can't answer or continue. When they are
not roleplayed out, such games are adjudicated by rolling Wits
+ Enigmas (difficulty of the opponent's Intelligence + Enig-
mas, +1 for every two Ranks that she is above an opponent,
rounded up.).

257


SSSEN

XX

Gamecraft can also take the form of put-downs and pranks.
The contender who makes the most fun of the other is
victorious. When it is not roleplayed out, this contest is
resolved with Manipulation + Expression rolls (difficulty of the
opponent's Willpower).

Garou Rituals and Etiquette

Werewolf life involves plenty of customs, rituals and eti-
quette. Stepping out of line is liable to get you a swat on the
nose or into a klaive duel — no one really cares if you own one
or not. Small errors in judgment can lead to big consequences,
especially when European and native Garou meet orattend the
same moots. Many unnecessary battles have been fought over
perceived lack of respect for other tribes' customs.

Knowing when to stay quiet and what to say when called
on in Garou society requires a Wits + Etiquette roll (diffi-
culty 8). If the character familiarizes herself prior to the
event or has coaching, a roll may not be necessary. Actually
performing a Ritual, even a simple greeting, usually requires
a roll of Charisma + Rituals (difficulty 6). If the Garou
doesn't know the particular Ritual, she might attempt to
fake it based on what she's seen or heard (Wits + Subterfuge,
difficulty 8). Botching any of these rolls can result in
dangerous consequences. Success might mean a Garou is
accepted into a foreign caern or might simply earn the
Garou a grunt of approval.

Creating new Rituals earns a Garou great esteem in the eyes
of other werewolves, especially Galliards and Theurges. The
exact execution and function of the Ritual must be detailed by
the player. The Storyteller then determines the difficulty of the
Ritual based on its complexity, effect and the character's
experience with other Rituals of the same type. The player rolls
Intelligence + Rituals (or possibly Occult or Etiquette) with a
difficulty between 7 and 9.

Hunting

Even skinchangers need to eat, and food isn't always
available in the Savage West at the corner restaurant or
saloon, especially where there are no restaurants, corners or
towns! Werewolves can find plenty of opportunities to hunt
in open country. Dinner, on the other hand, doesn't want to
be caught. When a Garou is stalking prey, a Perception +
Primal-Urge roll is made (difficulty depends on the scarcity of
game). For every two successes rolled, the hunter catches
enough for a meal for one.

When hunting dangerous prey, such as humans, mockeries or
vampires, werewolves often harry their opponents before taking
them down. A pack of Garou surrounds its victim. One werewolf
chases the prey into the reaches of the next werewolf, who
surprises the victim and takes up the chase. The process contin-
ues until the victim is exhausted. Ifa human or animal suffers this
treatment, he losesa Willpower point wheneveranew werewolf,
after the first, takes over the hunt. If the prey loses all its
Willpower in this way, it falls to the ground, exhausted.

258

Werewolf: The Wild West

Setting upa harrying requiressome planning. Each werewolf,
including the first, makes a Dexterity + Athletics roll (diffi-
culty 5). The prey also makes this roll when another werewolf
is encountered. If each werewolf receives more successes than
the prey, the chase continues. If the victim wins any roll, the
Garou must physically catch the target.

Inventions

Inventors scour the Savage West looking for investors
(read: suckers) to purchase their latest strides in technology.
Many of the items that are commonplace or even outdated in
our time are on the cutting edge of technology in the 19th
century. Hydraulic mining tools, steam trains and the Gatling
gun are all developed in this period. If a character wants to
cobble together a device from her stash of miscellaneous parts,
the player must make several Intelligence + Science rolls over
a period of time. The time, difficulty and number of successes
are all determined by the complexity of the item.

The Storyteller has final say about what characters can devise.
Players have to break the modem paradigm when getting into
their characters' imaginations. Settlers in the West are more
interested in improving the tools and means of tilling fields than
they are in creating devices that can store and summon up
information. Hell, electrical-powered devicesdon'teven existyet.

Memory

Players are as responsible for keeping track of chronicle events
asthe Storyteller is, but players are human, even if their characters
aren't. Players frequently forget what has been said by or has
transpired for their characters. Characters sometimes try to re-
member things that they might have experienced only in passing.
For a character to remember an important detail (whether the
player has forgotten it or not), the player rolls Intelligence +
Alertness. Difficulty depends on the obscurity of the information.
The Storyteller can also use this system as a tool to measure how
well characters keep track of events if they have to recount them.

Research

Universities and research laboratories are rare in the
Savage West. That doesn't make research impossible, just
difficult. The main problem with accomplishing any book-
learning is finding books. Not everybody can read, and
public libraries exist only in big cities. Perhaps the only
storehouses of knowledge on the frontier are schools and the
small newspaper offices.

If a Garou can manage to find an appropriate text, she
can try to find the information she's researching. An
Intelligence + Investigation (or another appropriate Skill,
such as Occult) roll is required. The difficulty varies
depending on the obscurity of the information sought and
the amount of research material available.

Ifa werewolf doesn't waste her time sticking her nose in
abook, but rifles through the paperwork of an institution
or her enemy, the local land baron, it works a little


differently. The following research system works for any kind of
investigation that involves more than book research. To work
through a stack of paperwork and discover something interesting
requires a Perception + Enigmas roll (difficulty 8, but modified by
the complexity of the information sought). The werewolf might
discover interesting purchasing patterns or learn that the railroad
won't be coming to town — but something much worse will.

Under either re
tion might only be found with extended actions and

ch conditions, truly obscure informa-

numerous suc:

Books and papers aren't the only sources of informa-
tion, though. People are full of it — information, that is,
and are probably a Garou's greatest resource in the West.
Although pla
ments, the Storyteller may wish to speed things up. If a
character probes into the general knowledge of saloon

patrons or just wants to learn the latest gossip, the Story-
teller should roll the character's Wits + Empathy, difficulty
7 (or roll for the character with the highest total

in a pack). Etiquette or Politics may prove

more appropriate than Empathy in cer-

successes determines the amount

t

Search

Finding someone in a crowded saloon or finding a clue
in the rubble of a collapsed mine can be a tedious, time-
consuming task. How a Garou searches — on all fours
using wolf-senses, for instance — often determines the
difficulty of a task. The player rolls Perception + Investi-
gation. Difficulty depends on how well the object or
person is concealed. If the Garou searches for a person and
the subject doesn't want to be found, the two must make
opposed rolls. The hunter rolls Perception + Investiga-
tion; the prey rolls Wits + Stealth. The difficulty in such
a roll is the opponent's opposed Skill (Investigation and

Stealth, respectively).
Whether a Garou searches for clues, objects or people,
the number of successes rolled indicates how productive
the investigation is. One success means the target is heard
or asmall clue is found. Five successes indicates the Garou
walks right/tup:to the idiot in-his hiding place or the
werewolf finds all the evidence he needs

to go to the hanging judge.


Stress

Werewolves simultaneously thrive on and are victims of stress. In order to utilize his Rage in any way, a werewolf must be in
a stressful situation, or the animal within will not respond. Too much stress can cause the Beast to gain control and overwhelm
a Garou, though, sending him into frenzy. Any time a werewolf scores four or more successes on any Rage roll, he frenzies.
Stressful situations come in many varieties, but the basic rule is that a Garou suffers stress whenever he
experiences a great deal of tension or is under pressure. When emotional strings are pulled (anger,
fear, love, hate, passion) assume that the Garou suffers stress. Stressful situations don't
always demand Rage rolls, but enough stress might push a werewolf over the edge.

‘w Danger: Whether its combat or hanging off the side of a runawa
a life-threatening situation creates a lot of tension.

train,

‘w Suspense: Sometimes waiting for events to unfold can be more
stressful than the actual event itself. Standing in the street and
waiting for your opponent to draw builds up a lot of tension.

‘w Confusion: Not knowing what to do, being lost in a dark,
dusty mine or being separated from the pack in combat can cause
a werewolf to lose control. Lupus and native Garou often suffer
confusion when in towns or cities.

*w Surprise: Being caught off-guard makes adrenaline flow
and muscles tense.

‘w Frustration: Beating your head « sta brick wall that
won't budge is a sure sign of stress. Being defeated by your
enemies, dealing with bureaucratic Easterners or being
imprisoned can drive a werewolf mad.

There are numerous ways in which a character
might suffer from stress. The Storyteller determines
the exact nature of how stress affects a character, and
when the player may spend Rage. Sometimes com-
bat isn't life-threatening, and the werewolf might
not be excited enough for Rage to be spent. On the
other hand, a simple conversation might be irritat-
ing if the Garou can't communicate properly.

A Storyteller does well to control players' ex-
penditure of Rage points. She doesn't need to
clamp down, but needs to ensure that Rage is spent
at appropriate times; otherwise, the emotional
impact is lost. The best rule is: If the players are
excited or tense, their characters probably are too.

Tracking

Tracking is similar to hunting; the main difference
lies in the goals. The goal of a hunt is to acquire a
meal, and the subject is usually a small, unintelli-
gent animal. Tracking can be used to hunt an
animal, but it's usually applied to following
humans — much more dangerous prey.

Tracking is usually staged over several
hours or days, depending on the pace of
the opponents. It's usually used when the
tracker does not know the location or des-
tination of the subject. If the tracker does
know this information, the rules for long-dis-
tance pursuit can be used.


When tracking people — or things pretending to be people — through the wilderness, a character needs to utilize his animal

instincts. The player rolls Perception + Primal-Urge. The number of successes determines how well the character follows the
trail. The Storyteller determines the difficulty based on the age of the trail and the terrain (typically 7 or 8). If the prey does not
wish to be followed or covers its tracks, it may roll Wits + Survival. The difficulty again depends on the terrain. Whoever
gets the most successes wins the contest — the tracker continues following until he catches his prey or the hunted
loses the pursuer. The time that passes before the hunter arrives upon the prey or the hunter realizes that he
has lost the trail depends on the distance between the opponents — turns, hours or days.

Tracking someone in a town is a task for only the most skilled hunters. Even Garou in
Lupus form can lose a scent in a town. The easiest way to track someone in an
urban setting is by doing it at night. The streets are less
crowded and scents and noises don't distract the hunter as aft
much. The roll to track someone through a town (or an ly
otherwise human-occupied place) is still Perception + Pri- y)
mal-Urge, but the difficulty rises by one to three, depending
on the activity in the town, the age of the scent and any
distractions or complexities.

ee
VY

So how do humans track, being that they don't
have Primal-Urge? Well, even though mortals can't
track by scent, they can still make a decent job of
following a trail. The Storyteller may make an
optional Tracking Skill available to human charac-
ters, or she may let mortals default to Survival or
Investigation when following a trail. In any event,
the difficulty for human tracking is always at least one
higher; having to rely on sight alone tends to hinder
tracking a bit.

Vision Interpretation

Werewolves are creatures of both the physical and
spiritual worlds. Dreams and visions from their spirit
ancestors or mystical guides impart clues to the future. But
visions are obscured by symbolism and are subject to
interpretation. When a Garou is confused by a vision,
the player may make a Perception + Enigmas roll to
understand its meaning. The difficulty depends
on the complexity of the dream, but usually

ranges between 7 and 9. The Dice Pool cannot i

/
exceed the character's Gnosis rating. Knowl-
edge of the occult or possession of the Past Life
Background can reduce the difficulty.

Although this system is helpful, its use is
not encouraged. Let the players piece things
together instead. This system is only pro-
vided so that players don't pull their hair
out trying to understand visions and dreams.

Some native tribes use smoke huts, ritu-
als or natural drugs to induce visions from *,
their ancestors or spirit guides. In order to call |
on spiritual aid in a visionquest, an Intelligence +
Rituals rolls is required. If successful, the werewolf calls
on a spirit who reveals something of the character's (or

another's) fate. The Storyteller decides whether the spirit
& shows the Garou true visions or false images. Either way, the

werewolf needs to interpret what the spirit reveals.

No chronicle is complete without a hearty supply of sup-
porting cast, props, enemies and the like. This chapter gives
plenty of rules and suggestions for thoroughly stocking your
chronicle with all the elements you need.

Antagonists

Mockeries

Mockeries, or "fomori" as many Europeans call them, are
humans who have completely turned to the Wyrm. The
supernatural change from human to mockery often causes
physical changes in the person, transforming him intoa twisted
foul reflection of what he once was and warping his personality
as well. Most often these wretches have obvious deformities,
but some mockeries can hide the changes in their forms,
allowing them to lurk among humans and spread their corrup-
tion. Those who cannot hide their malformed bodies often live
in the desert or in the deep caverns they find in the hills. As
often as not, the desert-dwelling mockeries resort to cannibal-
ism as a means of survival.

A fomor is created when a Bane possesses a body. Most
often it is a human, but sometimes they possess an
animal. Normally the victim's personality and con-
sciousness remain intact, but are fused with the

malevolent disposition of the Bane. If the person was already
corrupt and evil, the resulting mockery is a truly psychotic

creature, uncontrollable and unrelenting in its pursuits.

Some of the less-scrupulousland baronshave discovered darksome
methods for creating mockeries, and use these creatures as guards
and enforcers. While fomori are often unpredictable, the greedy
landowners are willing to take a few risks in order to achieve their
goals. These soldiersare ararity, butthey are becoming morecommon
as the mining industry sinks its teeth into the Western Territory.

The following statistics are a simple guideline for fomori
creation. The Storyteller is encouraged to create new powers
for mockeries in order to make them more personalized.
Physical: Strength 3+, Dexterity 2+

amina 3+

Social: Charisma 1+, Manipulation 1+, Appearance varies
Mental: Perception 2+, Intelligence 1+, Wits 2+

Abilities (typical): Alertness 1, Athletics 1+, Brawl 1-3,
Dodge 1, Intimidation 2+, Larceny 2+, Firearms 1+, Melee 1+,
Stealth 1, Survival 2, Occult 1

Powers: All mockeries have one or more special abilities, "Gifts"
from the Wyrm that help them in their corruptive endeavors. $
the list below for examples, or feel free to make up your own.

ec

Equipment: Most of the fomori hired by the land barons are
issued handguns or knives for combat, and heavy clothes to aid
in hiding their deformities.


SEEN

ZL,

Mockery Powers

Most fomori have around three special powers;
some have more and some have less. The most powerful
mockeries, often the leaders through brute strength, are
considerably more powerful than their brethren. More often
than not, the more powerful fomori suffer side effects that are
debilitating in the long term. While these detrimental
conditions exist (such as a body that's rotting from the
inside, or a form that mutates more as the days go on,
leading to eventual death), they aren't always a factor.
Some take place over the course of months or even years.
*@ Armor—Thefomorhasthick, scaly skin, addingthreedice
to all soak rolls. This flesh is often mottled gray or a pale green.
‘w Berserker — The mockery has five Rage points, and can use
these just as werewolves do. The mockery is also susceptible to frenzy.
‘w Claws and Fangs — The fomor has natural weapons to employ
against his enemies and victims. He may attack using claw and bite
maneuvers, and the wounds inflicted are aggravated damage.
e\(( ‘w Extra Limbs — The mockery has extra limbs growing from his
N body. These are normally in the form of tentacles, tendril clusters or
insectlike arms. These limbs are useful for feinting maneuvers and
adding to the confusion in a battle, but often grow in awkward
places; an arm may grow from the side of the head, or a
) tentacle may sprout from the mockery's stomach.

w Eyes of the Wyrm — The fomor has unusual eyes
that can dilate, revealing visions of the Wyrm's corrup-
tion. Dark images and torturous sights of damnation fill
the minds of anyone unfortunate enough to gaze into the
mockery's eyes. Anyone meeting the mockery's gaze must
makea Willpowerroll (difficulty 8) orbe frozen in horrorforfive
turns minus Wits rating, minimum of one turn. While holding a
victim in thrall this way, the fomor can neither attack nor
1, defend. His friends are another story.

‘w Immunity to the Delirium — Most mock-
eriesare immune to the Delirium. The influence
of the Wyrm has erased their primal fears along
with their humanity. Some still feel the effects

of the Veil, but at a +4 on the Delirium Chart.

‘@ Monstrous Strength — The fomoradds +5

to his Strength.

@ Poison Tumors — The mockery's skin is
covered with heavy, pus-bloated blisters that
erupt on hard contact. Whenever the mockery
is hit with a claw attack or is bitten, the blisters
explode, spilling a powerful venom. The at-

tacker must immediately roll to soak three levels
of damage (five if bitten), or suffer severe burns.
Characters attacking with melee weapons must
make a Dexterity roll (difficulty 6) or suffer the
same fate.

@ Quills — The fomor is covered
with thick, heavy spines that work as
armor (+2 tosoak rolls) and asa weapon.

Black Spiral Dancers

The Croatan weren't the only tribe of Garou who fell while battling the
Wyrm. Long before the Mayflower drifted ashore at Plymouth Rock, the White
Howlers lost their final battle. The Pictish werewolves risked everything by
entering a massive Wyrmhole in the hopes of destroying the foul creatures
beneath the ground and sparing the rest of the British Isles. They were
willing to die in order to save Gaia. They weren't that lucky.

After nearly a month in the Wyrm's lair, the White Howlers came
back to the surface, now twisted in mind and spirit. They became the
Wyrm's own Garou, known today as the Black Spiral Dancers.

The Dancers are vicious, warped monstrosities whose primary
goal is the destruction or corruption of all the other tribes.
From their underground dwellings and their Wyrm-cor-
rupt caerns called "hives," the Black Spiral Dancers seek to
destroy everything the Garou have worked for, and they've
succeeded to a frightening degree.

Their time with the Wyrm and their tendency to
inbreed have created a tribe that seems mostly made up of
metis. The Dancers resemble most other Garou, but they
are malformed, reshaped to make their lives in the per-
petual darkness more comfortable. Many of the Dancers
have ears more like a bat's than a wolf', and their once-
noble faces have changed until they more resemble hyenas
than wolves in their Crinos form. Their oversized eyes

burn with the fires of the Wyrm's hatred, s

me-
times a dark and violent red, sometimes a pale,
sickly green. Even their fur has changed, losing
its rich white lusterand turning the same mottled
greenish-gray as the molds and spores found in
their caverns. The Dancers' totem is Whippoor-
will, whose cry they often mimic while hunting.
They also speak in some other language, now
forgotten by all other tribes.

The Black Spiral Dancers name themselves
after a spirit-maze they follow to the realm of the
Wyrm. The path, the Black Spiral, is a dangerous
course. Successfully "dancing" the Black Spiral issupposedly
asource of great power and insight for the Wyrm Garou, and

surviving the dance is a sign of wisdom in the eyes
ofthe Dancers. However, with this wisdomcomes
= insanity; most Dancersare horribly, incurably mad.

bt

y)
EN)

D3).


Black Spiral Dancers have statistics like any other Garou, but
most of them are metis, having inbred for centuries in their dark
caverns. Their "Gifts" often resemble mutations; the nature of
these Gifts is up to the Storyteller. Some suggestions follow.

w Bite of the Bat (Level One) — The Black Spiral
Dancer develops razor-sharp teeth and an insatiable appetite
for blood. When biting an opponent, the character can
choose to drink the blood of a foe, quickly weakening an
enemy while temporarily fortifying herself.

System: With the expenditure of a Gnosis point and a
Strength + Medicine roll (difficulty 7), the Dancer activates
the Gift. For each success on the roll, the bitten victim loses
one Health Level in addition to the bite damage. The victim
must make a Stamina roll (difficulty 8) to resist this additional
damage. For each Health Level of damage inflicted on an
opponent by this Gift (and only this Gift), the Black Spiral
Dancer gains +1 Stamina for the next turn, but only with a
successful Gnosis roll (difficulty 8).

W Cloud of Night (Level One) — As the Level One
Uktena Gift.

*w Sense Wyrm (Level One) — As the Level One Metis Gift.

‘w Burrow (Level Two) — As the Level Two Metis Gift.

‘w Ears of the Bat (Level Two) — Black Spiral Dancers
with this Gift develop large, batlike ears in their wolf forms.
Even in their human forms, their ears are often oversized. This
Gift allows the Black Spiral Dancer to use sonar like a bat,
sensing objects around him even in complete darkness.

System: This Gift is always active, and has no Gnosis cost.

‘w Wyrm Speech (Level Two) — This Gift allows the Black
Spiral Dancer to understand and communicate with other
sentient followers of the Wyrm. Simple communication, how-
ever, doesn't mean that any sort of bond between the Dancers
and the other Wyrmlings exists.

System: The Black Spiral Dancer rolls Wits + Enigmas in
order to comprehend the speech of another Wyrmling.

‘w Foaming Fury (Level Three) — The Dancer's mouth
bubbles and foams with a greenish fluid that, when it gets into the
blood, drives even the meekest of individuals into a berserk rage.

System: The Black Spiral Dancer must spend one Gnosis to
activate this Gift fora scene. Anyone bitten by the Dancer must
succeed in a Stamina roll (difficulty 8) or fly into a rabid frenzy.

‘w Icy Chill of Despair (Level Three) — As the Level
Three Shadow Lords Gift.

‘w Skinglide (Level Three) — The Black Spiral Dancer
develops heavy folds of skin under her arms, much like a
pterodactyl or a flying squirrel. By outstretching her arms and
leaping from a height, she glides at 25 miles per hour. The
Dancer gains these patagia in all her forms on learning this Gift;
blending in becomes accordingly improbable.

System: This Gift has no cost, and once learned is perma-
nent. Should her patagia be damaged, the Black Spiral Dancer
must wait until the wound is healed before she can glide again.

te Trackless Waste (Level Three) — As the Level Three Red
Talon Gift, but only effective in caves and tunnels.

*@ Crawling Poison (Level Four) —This Giftallows the Black
Spiral Dancer to infect others with a slow-acting poison via fang or
claw. This venom, when injected intoa victim, inhibitsawerewolfs
regenerative powers and reduces the target's Stamina rating.

System: The Dancer rollshis Gnosis (difficulty of the victim's
Stamina); the effect lasts for one hour per success. Each success
reduces the target's Stamina by one for the remainder of the
scene, as well as negating any regenerative or healing powers
(but not healing Gifts).

w Eyes of the Wyrm (Level Four) —Same as the mockery
Power.

w Wyrm Visions (Level Four) — This Gift allows the Black
Spiral Dancer to distort what a victim sees. The Black Spiral
Dancer must make physical contact with a target and breathe into
her victim's mouth. If successful, the victim's senses are distorted,
giving visions of the madness and suffering caused by the Wyrm.

System: The Dancer rolls Stamina + Medicine (difficulty of
the target's Willpower). Each success reduces the victim's Will-
power and Dexterity by one. This may only be done once on any
target during the course of the scene. The target affected by this
Gift may make a Gnosis roll (difficulty 8) in order to resist the
attack; each success negates one of the Dancer's successes.

‘w Balefire (Level Five) — The Dancer can hurl sickly green
balls of flame at her opponent. If she hits, the target's body is
twisted in some fashion by the strange energies.

System: With the expenditure of a Gnosis point, the Dancer
cantry tostrikean opponent with the energies (difficulty 6; use the
Firearms Chart for complications). The victim must roll Stamina
(difficulty 8) and meet or exceed the Dancer's number of suc-
cesses. If he fails, he is mutated in a manner decided by the
Storyteller. These mutations are always harmful and each level of
the Dancer's success is treated as an aggravated Health Level of
injury for the purpose of healing or rejecting the mutation.

‘w Heart of the Wyrm (Level Five) — This Gift allows the
Black Spiral Dancertosend one target into the "thrallofthe Wyrm."

System: The Black Spiral Dancer must spend two Gnosis
and make a Wits + Manipulation roll (difficulty equal to the
target's Gnosis). Only one success is needed to send an oppo-
nent into a frenzy, but two or more successes are required to
force the target of this Gift into being "ridden by the Wyrm"
(see page 222).

@ Kiss of Entropy (Level Five) — This Gift allows the
Dancer to summon a wave of pure entropy and focus it against
a single foe.

System: The Black Spiral Dancer must hit her opponent
with the black, burning energy (difficulty 6; use the Firearms
Chart for complications). This Gift also costs one Gnosis. If
the Dancer hits his target, the victim must make a successful
Gnosis roll (difficulty 8) to resist the effects of this Gift.

If the target fails to resist the power of this Gift, he ages
10 years for every one of the Dancer's successes.

Werewolf: The Wild West


The Law

With so much open space and so many people spilling into
the area from the East Coast, there's been little chance for
anyone to truly become organized and establish a solid base for
law and order. In many ways, frontier law is virtually the "law
of the jungle," and mob rule is a harsh reality.

While there are actual law-enforcement agencies in place,
there are almost never enough members in the agencies to
make a difference. Each town might havea sheriff anda deputy
sheriff or two, but in most frontier towns — especially the
boom towns builtaround local gold and silver mines — the best
these people manage regularly is catching individual horse
thieves and locking up the local unruly drunks on any given
night. There's also a problem with the honesty of the local law
enforcement. As often as not the sheriff and his men are paid
enough to mind their own business when it comes to vendettas
and illegal activities. In reality, the mine owners and land
barons normally control the law.

Toadd to the confusion, several enterprising men have started
their own private branches of law enforcement...

Armbrusters

The best known of these isthe Armbruster Detective Agency,
owned by none other than Walter Xavier Armbruster of Ama-
illo, Texas. Armbrusters, as the agents are usually called, are
among the best-trained lawmen around. Each is handpicked by
Walter Armbruster and schooled in the fine art of tracking, as
well as in the skills necessary to bring a man in dead or alive. It's
said by many people that the Armbrusters are fearless, and a few
unwary werewolves have learned the hard way how accurate
that statement is. The statistics below represent an average
member of the Armbruster Detective Agency.

Physical: Strength 3, Dexterity 4, Stamina 3

Social: Charisma 2, Manipulation 2, Appearance 1

Mental: Perception 4, Intelligence 3, Wits 2

Talents: Alertness 2, Athletics 2, Brawl 1, Dodge 1, Intimida-
tion 3, Larceny 3, Subterfuge 2

Skills: Etiquette 2, Firearms 3, Leadership 2, Melee 2, Ride 3,
Stealth 2, Survival 2

Knowledges: Culture 1 (Spanish or local Native American
tribe), Enigmas 2, Investigation 3, Law 2, Linguistics 1 (Span-
ish or local Native American tribe), Medicine 1, Politics 1
Backgrounds: Allies 2, Contacts 4, Resources 2

Powers: For reasons unknown to the Garou, the Armbrusters
are unaffected by the Delirium.

Willpower: 7

Image: Armbrusters are always dressed in gray suits with
bowler hats, the signature uniform of the Armbruster Detec-

tive Agency. When they wish to remain more
anonymous, the detectives switch into whatever sort

& of clothes are fashionable around town.

Roleplaying Hints: Always be courteous, as everyone is a
potential employer. Never hesitate to let people know that
you're an Armbruster. Smile, be friendly, and never stop
hunting the target you've been assigned.

History: When Walter Xavier Armbruster was a young man,
he decided he'd had enough of being pushed around and abused
by the local ruffians. He taught himself to fight and to defend
himself from any foe. By the time the Civil War erupted, he'd
already killed a dozen men and was up for election as the sheriff of
Amarillo. Instead, he fought for the Confederacy, because he was
damned if some Yankee was going to tell him what he could and
could not do (that there shouldn't be slaves was a given in his eyes,
but that wasn't the point of the war as far as he is concerned).

When the war ended, Armbruster was a different man. He
came back haunted by the bloody battles he has survived, and
with a reputation for violence that scared many people. He also
came back with the gratitude of an old man whose life he saved,
a man named Arthur Candlewick. After a short time, he
decided to use that reputation to his advantage and founded
the Armbruster Detective Agency.

Unbeknownst to Armbruster, the detective badges he had
Candlewick design for him are actually fetishes that grant the
bearer an immunity to the Delirium. Candlewick is a powerful
mage, who believes the Armbrusters are the best way to enforce
the law in an area where honor seems a trivial matter.

Federal Marshals

/ Texas Rangers

Physical: Strength 3, Dexterity 2, Stamina 3

Social: Charisma 2, Manipulation 2, Appearance 2

Mental: Perception 3, Intelligence 2, Wits 3

Talents: Alertness 2, Athletics 2, Brawl 3, Dodge 2, Expres-
sion 1, Intimidation 3, Larceny 1 (Texas Rangers 3), Tracking
Skills: Crafts 1, Firearms 2-4, Leadership 2, Melee 1-3, Ride 2,
Stealth 2, Survival 3

Knowledges: Culture (Mexican or local Native American
tribe) 3, Enigmas 2, Investigation 3, Law 2, Linguistics (Span-
ish) 1, Medicine 2, Politics 2

Backgrounds: Allies 3, Contacts 2

Willpower: 6

Image: The Federal Marshals and Texas Rangers normally
manage to bring a good amount of the trail with them, in the
form of dirt. Most of these men are constantly on the move,
going from territory to territory and doing what they can to
uphold the law. Most are hardened by incidents in their past
and well-tanned from their time on the move.

Roleplaying Hints: Don't take no guff offa anyone. You've
ajob todo and you aim to take care of the matter, and that's the
end of the discussion. Be polite to the ladies, but don't expect
them to be any better'n the men out here. There's snakes hidin'
in every bush, so always keep your eyes in motion and your
hands close to your pistols.


Local Sheriff

Physical: Strength 2, Dexterity 2, Stamina 2
Social: Charisma 2, Manipulation 2, Appearance 2

Mental: Perception 3, Intelligence 2, Wits 2
Talents: Alertness 1, Brawl 1, Dodge 2, Intimidation 1,
Larceny 3, Streetwise 4
Skills: Etiquette 1, Firearms 2, Leadership 1, Melee 1, Ride 3
Knowledges: Investigation 1, Law 1, Politics 2
Backgrounds: Allies 2, Contacts 3, Resources 1
Willpower: 5
Image: The average sheriff ranges in age and physical descrip-
tion. Mostare armed with at least one pistol and carry a certain air
of authority in the way they walk. Most also wear a tin star. Asa
tule, the sheriff is someone well-known and liked by the commu-
nity — even if he isn't exactly capable of doing his job properly.
Roleplaying Hints: Be courteous; be stern. Never let your
enemies know how nervous you are. You haven't been yourself
since that sidewinder Black Jack Dooley took a shot at you last
week and blew the hat clean off'n yer head.

Naturae

Naturae are the spirits of nature. They are most often found
in the wild, unclaimed areas where mankind has not yet
managed to establish a foothold. Many of these spirits reflect
the areas where they dwell, and help sustain Gaia even as She
gives them life. The Naturae are largely found in the Middle
Lands, but some still exist on the Earth itself.

Animal Spirits

Garou often interact with the spirits of animals, whether to
learn new Gifts from them or to appease them after hunting one
of their kind for food. Animal spirits are similar to their physical
counterparts, but often have a power they can teach a Garou; a
Rabbit-spirit may teach a Garou to leap, or a Cat-spirit might
teach a werewolf tosee in the darkness. There are too many types
of animal spirits to categorize here, and many of the Garou
believe that more existed in the past, when they had living
counterparts, only to fade as their races were exterminated.

The Garou believe that all animal spirits are connected with
their patron totem Incarna, called the Animal Fathers. According
to the lupus, the Animal Fathers regulate the population of the
their children on the Earth. If the Animal Fathers feel
slighted, they can decrease the births of new children,
which can be disastrous for the predatory Garou and
their wolf-kin. The Animal Fathers allegedly live in
great lodges in the Middle Lands. Finding them is
often a very difficult task, as the lodges are
seldom located in the same place twice.
Below are some sample animal
Gafflings.

Desert Winds

Desert Windsare the personification of the hot, often turbulent
winds of the desert. These spirits are as tempestuous as the winds
they control, and have a reputation for a quick temper. In areas
where the railroads have come through, these spirits appear as dark
clouds, smudged with soot and coal dust. In areas where the desert
is untouched by man, they are larger and softer in appearance.

Desert Winds often know when someone, anyone, has come
through the area of the desert that they inhabit. Sometimes, if
the Garou approach them with the proper respect, they will
share the knowledge, knowing full well that the Garou can't
follow a trail they have already swept clean.

Desert Winds are usually Gafflings or Jagglings, though a
very few have reached the power level of Incarna.

Willpower 7, Rage 6, Gnosis 8, Power (varies; between 20 and 80)
Charms: Airt Sense, Cleanse the Blight,
Create Wind, Disorient, Reform, Updraft

Lunes

Lunes are Jagglings of Luna. These
spirits appear as shimmering ribbons of
light surrounded by auras of blue and
gold. Great friends of the Garou,
they communicate empathically,
undulating into various forms as
they silently communicate with
other spirits. This motion be-
comes increasingly unstable as the
moon waxes. The demeanors of
Lunes vary depending on the phase
of the moon and, like their mistress,
these spirits are susceptible to lunar
madness during the full moon.

Lunes have the power to open any
Moon Bridge at will, if they so desire.
They sometimes perform this service for
Garou who successfully petition them (a
taxing task). Should the Lune be suffer-
ing from lunar madness at the time of the
granted request, there's noguarantee that
theGarouwill endup where he requested.

Willpower 8, Rage 4 (8 during the full

moon), Gnosis 7, Power 35
Charms: Airt Sense, Open Moon
& Bridge (cost 5), Reform

Vengeance-spirits
Vengeance-spirits are Jagglings that certain shamans can sum-
mon to do their bidding. Vengeance-spirits take many forms, and
are almost always single-minded in their purpose. Some Garou
believe these spirits are dying out as humans lose their ability to
believe in anything they can't experience with their own five
senses. Most act indirectly against a target, causing mishaps
around them and bringing "bad luck." A few, however, are able to
manifest in the physical world and wreak their havoc directly.

The Wendigo

The avatar of great Wendigo itself, this creature appears as
a twisted humanoid with blue skin, fangs, claws and eyes that
flash like lightning. The Wendigo has large black hooflike
stumps instead of feet, and legs powerful enough to propel it
through the wintry air at 50 miles per hour. The Wendigo is
occasionally called by Garou to act as an engine of vengeance.
Once summoned, it tracks its prey, tears its victim apart and
feeds on the victim's heart. The Wendigo may be summoned
with the Wendigo Gift: Call of the Cannibal Spirit.
Willpower 7, Rage 10, Gnosis 3, Power 45
(Power cost 30; Str 6, Dex 6, Sta 6, Brawl 5,
Dodge 1, Claws: Str +1 Bite: Str +2, Health 12) Create Wind,
Disorient, Freeze, Frozen Breath, Ice Shards

The Howler

The Howlerresembles a werewolf in many ways, but
in truth is in no way related. The Howler is a
powerful spirit of vengeance that serves various
Pure Ones tribes under different guises. Some-
times the Howler takes the form of a bear or
gigantic eagle; at other times it looks like a
walking cloud in the shape of a man.
Whatever its manifestation, the Howler's
a force of destruction unlike any other.
While the Howler doesn'tseem to move
very quickly, its intended victim nor-
mally hears it over the course of three
nights. The first night it sounds very dis-
tant, so faint that it could well be the
wind. The second night it sounds much
closer, as if it were just out of visual
range. The third night the Howler cries
out again, signaling that it will attack
within the hour. When it howls for the
third time, the powerful spirit sounds as
if it's standing only a few yards away.
The cries of the Howler are said to
deafen their enemies; perhaps this is
true of humans, but the Garou who
have survived the creature's attacks
still have their ability to hear. No
human has yet survived its wrath to
prove the point, anyway.

The Howler may be stopped, but it can't be bound or
destroyed. If "killed" in the physical world, the Howler disap-
pears into the Umbra where it waits for one full cycle of the
moon before attacking its prey again.

Willpower 8, Rage 10, Gnosis 5, Power 60

Charms: Materialize (Power Cost 45; Str 9, Dex 6, Sta 10,
Brawl 5, Dodge3, Melee 4, Claws: Str, Bite Str: + 1), Disorient,

Elementals

These spirits are representations of the basic elements of all
life: the four elements of earth, wind, fire and water. Rumors of
other elementals abound, butnoone alive haseverseen them (or
at least provided proof of such spirits' existence). The power of
elementals differs as surely as a single grain of sand differs from
a mountain. Some are no stronger than Gafflings, while others
are as powerful as any of the Incarna. The following examples of
elementals are "average" in strength. They are the most com-
mon, and the most likely to be encountered by Garou Theurges.

Air Elemental

normally unconnected with Gaia,
